## 4.1 Compressive Test Setup Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Universal Testing Machine UTM] --> B[Upper Platen Crosshead]
    B --> C[Load Cell Force Sensor]
    C --> D[Upper Compression Platen]
    D --> E[Polymer Specimen Cylindrical]
    E --> F[Lower Compression Platen]
    F --> G[Fixed Base Column]
    A --> H[Extensometer Strain Measurement]
    H --> I[Data Acquisition System DAS]
    I --> J[Computer with Stress-Strain Plotter]
    J --> K[Output: Compressive Modulus Strength Yield]
    A1[Specimen L0 d] --> E
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style D fill:#ffe1e1
    style E fill:#fff4e1
    style F fill:#ffe1e1
    style J fill:#e1ffe1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Interpretation:** The UTM applies a uniaxial compressive displacement at a constant crosshead speed. The **load cell** measures the resistive force **F**, while the **extensometer** records the change in length **ΔL**. The DAS computes and plots the stress–strain curve in real time.

## 4.2 Compressive Stress–Strain Behavior: Brittle vs. Ductile Polymers

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Compressive Loading Starts] --> B[Linear Elastic Region]
    B --> C{Material Behavior}
    C -->|Brittle: PMMA, PS, Bakelite| D[Sharp Yield at High Stress]
    D --> E[Sudden Fracture along Shear Planes 45 degrees]
    C -->|Ductile: LDPE, PP, Nylon| F[Distinct Yield Point]
    F --> G[Extensive Plastic Flow Plateau]
    G --> H[Strain Hardening and Densification]
    H --> I[No Clean Fracture Buckling or Barrel Cracking]
    style B fill:#e1f5ff
    style D fill:#ffcccc
    style E fill:#ff9999
    style F fill:#ccffcc
    style G fill:#99ff99
    style H fill:#ccff99
```

## 4.3 Specimen Geometry and Slenderness Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Select Specimen Geometry] --> B{Material Type}
    B -->|Rigid Plastic| C[ASTM D695 Right Cylinder]
    B -->|Elastomer Foam| D[ISO 604 Rectangular Prism]
    B -->|Anisotropic Composite| E[Oriented Rectangular Coupon]
    C --> F[Dimensions: d 12.7 mm, L0 25.4 mm]
    D --> G[Dimensions: 50 mm x 50 mm x 25 mm]
    E --> H[Fiber direction specified]
    F --> I{Check Slenderness L0/d}
    I -->|Ratio less than 2| J[Valid No Buckling]
    I -->|Ratio greater than 2| K[Add Lateral Support or Shorten]
    J --> L[Proceed to Compression Test]
    K --> L
    style C fill:#e1f5ff
    style F fill:#fff4e1
    style I fill:#ffe1e1
    style L fill:#ccffcc
```

## 4.4 Barreling Phenomenon Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Specimen Under Compression] --> B{Platen Friction Condition}
    B -->|Lubricated Platen Uniaxial Stress| C[Uniform Lateral Expansion Cylindrical Barrel]
    B -->|No Lubrication Friction at Ends| D[Barreling Bulged Middle Concave Ends]
    C --> E[True Uniaxial Stress State Valid Test]
    D --> F[Triaxial Stress State Invalid Overestimates Modulus]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style C fill:#ccffcc
    style D fill:#ffcccc
    style E fill:#99ff99
    style F fill:#ff9999
```

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight — Barreling Correction:** To minimize barreling, **lubricate the platens** (e.g., with PTFE film or molybdenum disulfide grease) so the ends can expand freely. This ensures a true uniaxial compressive stress state and yields more accurate modulus values.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Short Answer [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
> **Q1.** Differentiate between **tensile** and **compressive** stress–strain behavior of a brittle polymer such as **PMMA**. Why is the compressive strength of brittle polymers typically greater than their tensile strength?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

1. **Tensile Behavior of PMMA:** Linear elastic region followed by brittle fracture at low strain (~1–2%). Failure occurs by **crazing and crack propagation** perpendicular to the tensile axis. Tensile strength ≈ 65–75 MPa.

2. **Compressive Behavior of PMMA:** Linear elastic region followed by yielding and sudden fracture along **shear planes at ~45°** to the loading axis. Compressive strength ≈ 90–110 MPa.

3. **Reason for Higher Compressive Strength:** In tension, microscopic flaws and crazes open up easily, leading to premature failure. In compression, these flaws are **closed** by the hydrostatic pressure component, and crazing is **suppressed**. The polymer must fail by shear yielding, which requires a **higher applied stress**.

*(1 mark for each point — total 3 marks)*

---

### **Question 2: Short Answer [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
> **Q2.** What is **barreling** in a compression test? State **two factors** that contribute to barreling and one practical method to **minimize** it.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

- **Barreling Definition (1 Mark):** Barreling is the **non-uniform lateral bulging** of a cylindrical compression specimen at its mid-height due to friction at the contact surfaces with the platens, which restrains the ends from expanding freely.

- **Two Contributing Factors (1 Mark):**
  1. **High friction coefficient** between platen and specimen ends.
  2. **High aspect ratio (L₀/d)** combined with absence of lubrication.

- **Minimization Method (1 Mark):** Apply a **thin layer of lubricant** (PTFE spray, Molybdenum disulfide grease, or thin PTFE film) between the platen and specimen to promote free lateral expansion and approximate a uniaxial stress state.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]**

> **Q. A (a)** Define **compressive strength** and **compressive modulus** of a polymer. With a neat sketch, describe the **stress–strain behavior** of a ductile polymer (e.g., LDPE) in compression, identifying all key regions. **(7 Marks)**

> **Q. A (b)** A cylindrical polymer specimen of diameter **15 mm** and gauge length **30 mm** is tested in compression per **ASTM D695**. The recorded load–displacement data are:
>
> | Load (N) | Displacement (mm) |
> |---|---|
> | 0 | 0.000 |
> | 1500 | 0.150 |
> | 3000 | 0.300 |
> | 4500 | 0.450 |
> | 6000 | 0.600 |
> | 7000 (yield) | 0.750 |
> | 6800 | 1.000 |
>
> Calculate the **(i) compressive modulus, (ii) engineering compressive stress at yield, and (iii) true compressive stress at 25% strain**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q. A (a):**

**Definition of Compressive Strength (1 Mark):**
> The maximum compressive stress a material can sustain before failure, calculated as the ratio of the maximum compressive load to the original cross-sectional area:
> $\sigma_{cu} = \dfrac{F_{max}}{A_0}$

**Definition of Compressive Modulus (1 Mark):**
> The ratio of compressive stress to corresponding compressive strain within the linear-elastic limit:
> $E_c = \dfrac{\sigma_c}{\varepsilon_c} = \dfrac{F \cdot L_0}{A_0 \cdot \Delta L}$

**Stress–Strain Sketch Description (3 Marks):**

The compressive stress–strain curve of a ductile polymer like **LDPE** has the following key regions:

1. **Region I – Initial Linear Elastic Region:** Steep linear slope; deformation is recoverable; governed by Hooke's law.
2. **Region II – Yield Point:** The curve reaches a local maximum — the **compressive yield strength (σ_yc)**.
3. **Region III – Plastic Flow / Plateau Region:** Stress remains approximately constant while strain increases substantially. Molecular chains slide past one another.
4. **Region IV – Strain Hardening and Densification:** Stress rises again as the polymer densifies due to volume reduction.
5. **Region V – Final Failure:** The specimen may buckle, crack, or split longitudinally (splitting failure mode).

**Neat Sketch (2 Marks):**

```
σ (MPa)
   |
σ_yc|───────────● (yield)
   |          /  \___________________
   |         /                        \____
   |        /                              \__
   |       /                                  \___
   |      /                                       \__
   |     /                                            \____
   |    /                                                  \____
   |   /                                                         \____
   |  /                                                               \____
   | /                                                                    \___
   |/________________________________________________________________________________ ε
        Region I     II    III (plateau)         IV (hardening)         V
```

---

#### **Model Solution for Q. A (b):**

**Step 1 — Calculate Original Cross-Sectional Area (0.5 Mark):**

$$
A_0 = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 15^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \times 225}{4} = 176.71 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Identify Linear Elastic Region (0.5 Mark):**

From the data, the points (0, 0), (1500, 0.150), (3000, 0.300), (4500, 0.450), (6000, 0.600) all lie on a **perfectly straight line**. Therefore, the linear elastic region extends up to 6000 N at 0.600 mm displacement. The yield occurs at **7000 N at 0.750 mm**.

**Step 3 — Calculate Compressive Modulus (2 Marks):**

Use the formula:

$$
E_c = \dfrac{F \cdot L_0}{A_0 \cdot \Delta L}
$$

Using F = 6000 N, L₀ = 30 mm, ΔL = 0.600 mm, A₀ = 176.71 mm²:

$$
E_c = \dfrac{6000 \times 30}{176.71 \times 0.600} = \dfrac{180000}{106.026} = 1697.9 \text{ MPa} \approx 1.698 \text{ GPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate Engineering Compressive Stress at Yield (2 Marks):**

At yield, F_yield = 7000 N:

$$
\sigma_{yc,eng} = \dfrac{F_{yield}}{A_0} = \dfrac{7000}{176.71} = 39.61 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate True Compressive Stress at 25% Strain (2 Marks):**

Engineering stress at 25% strain (ε = 0.25):

$$
\sigma_{eng,0.25} = \dfrac{F}{A_0} = \dfrac{7000}{176.71} = 39.61 \text{ MPa}
$$

(We use the load at the yield point as the representative force at ε = 0.25, since the plateau is approximately flat.)

True stress relationship:

$$
\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng} \cdot (1 - \varepsilon_c)
$$

Substituting:

$$
\sigma_{true} = 39.61 \times (1 - 0.25) = 39.61 \times 0.75 = 29.71 \text{ MPa}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — True vs Engineering Stress Direction:** Under **compression**, the area **increases**, so the true stress is **lower** than the engineering stress at the same strain. Many students incorrectly assume compression behaves like tension (true > engineering) and **invert the formula**, losing 2 marks immediately. **Memorize:** Compression = area grows = true < engineering.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Alternative Choice]**

> **Q. B (a)** Explain the **ASTM D695** standard test method for determining the compressive properties of **rigid plastics**. Include details of **specimen dimensions, test speed, end conditions, and reported parameters**. **(7 Marks)**

> **Q. B (b)** Compare the **compressive and tensile behavior** of the following polymer classes: **(i) brittle amorphous (e.g., PS)**, **(ii) ductile semicrystalline (e.g., HDPE)**, and **(iii) highly crosslinked thermoset (e.g., phenolic resin)**. For each, state the dominant **failure mode** in compression. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q. B (a):**

**ASTM D695 — Standard Test Method for Compressive Properties of Rigid Plastics (7 Marks):**

1. **Scope and Applicability (1 Mark):** ASTM D695 covers the determination of the mechanical compressive properties of unreinforced and reinforced rigid plastics, including **high-modulus composites**, where the deformation in compression is small. The method is limited to materials that do not buckle during the test.

2. **Specimen Geometry (2 Marks):** The preferred specimen is a **right cylinder** of diameter **12.7 mm (0.5 in)** and length **25.4 mm (1.0 in)**. The slenderness ratio L₀/d should be **≤ 2** to prevent buckling. For molded materials, a **prismatic specimen** (12.7 mm × 12.7 mm × 25.4 mm) may also be used. The ends must be **flat, parallel, and perpendicular** to the loading axis.

3. **Loading Conditions (1 Mark):** The test is conducted at a **standard crosshead speed of 1.3 mm/min (0.05 in/min)**, with deviations allowed for specific materials. The load is applied axially through **self-aligning, hardened steel platens**.

4. **End Conditions and Lubrication (1 Mark):** Friction at the platen–specimen interface significantly affects results. To approximate uniaxial stress, **lubrication** (PTFE film, Molybdenum disulfide) is recommended between the platen and specimen.

5. **Reported Parameters (2 Marks):**
   - **Compressive Modulus (E_c)**
   - **Compressive Strength (σ_cu)** at yield or at break
   - **Compressive Strain at break (ε_c)**
   - **Stress–strain curve** (full record)

---

#### **Model Solution for Q. B (b):**

**Comparative Table (7 Marks — distribute 2 + 2 + 2 + 1 for explanation):**

| Polymer Class | Example | Compressive Behavior | Tensile Behavior | Dominant Compression Failure Mode |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Brittle Amorphous** | **Polystyrene (PS)** | High E_c (~3 GPa), brittle, low strain to failure (~1–2%) | Similar modulus, fails by crazing | **Shear fracture along 45° planes**; sudden, catastrophic |
| **Ductile Semicrystalline** | **HDPE** | Moderate E_c (~0.5–1 GPa), extensive plastic flow, large yield plateau | Moderate strength, necking, cold drawing | **Barreling and lateral splitting**; ductile, no sharp fracture |
| **Highly Crosslinked Thermoset** | **Phenolic Resin (Bakelite)** | Very high E_c (~5–10 GPa), brittle, high compressive strength (~200 MPa) | Brittle, lower strength than compression | **Crushing and axial splitting**; high compressive strength due to covalent network |

**Explanatory Notes (1 Mark for synthesis):**

- In **brittle amorphous** polymers, the absence of chain mobility prevents plastic flow; failure occurs by elastic-brittle shear.
- In **ductile semicrystalline** polymers, lamellar slip and amorphous chain uncoiling allow large plastic deformation; compressive strength is moderate but toughness is high.
- In **crosslinked thermosets**, the dense covalent network provides exceptional rigidity and compressive load-bearing capacity, which is why **Bakelite** was historically used for switchboards, insulator housings, and electrical panels.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Mark Distribution:** Many students write excessively long answers for (a) and leave (b) incomplete. **Strategy:** Allocate time proportionally — 12–14 minutes per 7-mark sub-question. Always include a **diagram or table** in compression questions, as visual aids earn easy partial credit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and keep it beside your textbook for last-day revision before KTU exams!**

- [x] **Compression ≠ Tension:** Polymer behavior in compression differs significantly from tensile behavior; compressive strength is generally **higher** than tensile strength for brittle polymers.
- [x] **Core Formulas:** $\sigma_c = F/A_0$, $\varepsilon_c = \Delta L / L_0$, $E_c = \sigma_c / \varepsilon_c$.
- [x] **True vs Engineering Stress:** $\sigma_{true} = \sigma_{eng}(1 - \varepsilon_c)$ — compression INCREASES area, so true < engineering.
- [x] **Standards:** **ASTM D695** (rigid plastics) and **ISO 604** (general plastics) govern compression testing.
- [x] **Preferred Specimen:** Right cylinder, **d = 12.7 mm, L₀ = 25.4 mm**, slenderness ratio **≤ 2**.
- [x] **Barreling:** Caused by platen friction; **minimize by lubrication**.
- [x] **Buckling:** Avoid by limiting slenderness ratio; for tall specimens, use lateral support.
- [x] **Failure Modes in Compression:**
  - *Brittle polymers* → **45° shear fracture**
  - *Ductile polymers* → **Barreling + lateral splitting**
  - *Thermosets* → **Crushing / axial splitting**
- [x] **Factors Increasing E_c and σ_c:** Higher strain rate, lower temperature (below Tg), higher crystallinity, higher crosslink density, addition of stiff fillers (glass, carbon fiber).
- [x] **Poisson's Ratio:** Typically **0.3–0.4** for most polymers; controls lateral expansion under compression.
- [x] **Unit Conversions:** 1 GPa = 1000 MPa = 1000 N/mm² = 10⁹ Pa. Always show units in derivations.
- [x] **Viscoelastic Nature:** Polymers are strain-rate and temperature sensitive — always report test temperature and crosshead speed in your answers.
- [x] **Yield Criterion:** Compressive yield > tensile yield for the same polymer due to suppression of crazing by hydrostatic pressure.
- [x] **Common Exam Trap:** Confusing **compressive strain sign convention** — engineering strain in compression is reported as a **positive magnitude**, but physically represents shortening.
- [x] **Exam Tip:** When asked to "explain with sketch," always include a clearly labeled **stress–strain diagram** showing elastic, yield, plastic flow, and failure regions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flexural Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT602 - POLYMER TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Flexural Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Flexural Properties** are the mechanical properties of a polymeric material that characterize its response to **bending (flexural) loads**. They quantify the ability of a material to resist deformation under flexural stress and are essential indicators of stiffness, strength, and load-bearing capacity in structural polymer applications.

The three primary flexural properties determined experimentally are:

1. **Flexural Stress ($\sigma_f$)** – The maximum fiber stress experienced by the specimen at the moment of failure or at a specified deflection.
2. **Flexural Strain ($\epsilon_f$)** – The nominal fractional change in length of an outer fiber element at the surface of the specimen.
3. **Flexural Modulus ($E_B$)** – The ratio of flexural stress to corresponding flexural strain, representing the stiffness of the material in bending.

The governing standard for unreinforced and reinforced polymers (including high-modulus composites) is **ASTM D790**, while **ISO 178** is the international counterpart. Both prescribe the **three-point loading (3PL)** and **four-point loading (4PL)** configurations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — The diving board:**
> Imagine a person standing at the free end of a diving board. The board **bends** downward — the top surface is being compressed, while the bottom surface is being stretched. Now imagine replacing the diving board with different materials:
>
> * A **wooden board** bends noticeably (low flexural modulus).
> * A **fiberglass reinforced polymer (FRP) board** barely bends (high flexural modulus).
> * A **high-density polyethylene (HDPE) plank** bends a lot and may even take a permanent "set" before breaking.
>
> The dive board is undergoing **flexure**, and the engineering data we obtain from controlled flexural testing tells us exactly how a polymer specimen will behave under similar bending service conditions — think of polymer **shelves, automotive dashboards, pipe hangers, printed circuit board substrates, and structural foam cores**.

A more relevant industrial analogy: a **plastic pallet** resting on two forklift tines and loaded with crates. The pallet beams between the tines are in pure flexure, and the pallet must be designed using the **flexural strength and modulus** of the chosen polymer.

## 1.3 When Is Flexural Testing Preferred Over Tensile Testing?

> [!TIP]
> Flexural testing is particularly valuable when:
> * The polymer is **brittle** (e.g., cured thermosets, PMMA, PS) and tensile specimens are difficult to grip without premature failure at the jaws.
> * The product in service is loaded in **bending** (beams, panels, springs).
> * Only **small, simple rectangular specimens** are available (no machined dog-bone required).
> * The material is **anisotropic** and we need to evaluate directional stiffness (e.g., composites, 3D-printed polymers).

## 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> * **Standard Test Method:** **ASTM D790** (Procedure A: 3-point; Procedure B: 4-point) and **ISO 178**.
> * **Standard Specimen Geometry (ASTM D790):** Length $L$ = 50.8 mm (2 in); Width $b$ = 12.7 mm (0.5 in); Thickness $h$ = 3.2 mm (0.125 in) for injection-molded samples.
> * **Span-to-Depth Ratio ($L/h$):** **16:1** for stiff plastics, **32:1** or **64:1** for less stiff materials (rubbers, foams).
> * **Crosshead Speed:** Calculated to give a strain rate of **0.01 min⁻¹** (Procedure A) or **0.1 min⁻¹** (Procedure A, high-strength option).
> * **Strain rate in outer fiber:** $d\epsilon/dt = 6 D h / L^2 \cdot dD/dt$, where $D$ is deflection and $dD/dt$ is crosshead speed.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework

Flexural testing of polymers is treated as **Euler–Bernoulli beam theory** combined with elementary strength of materials. The following assumptions are inherent:

1. **Linear-elastic behavior** of the material up to the proportional limit (for modulus calculation).
2. **Plane sections remain plane and normal to the neutral axis** after bending (Euler–Bernoulli hypothesis).
3. **Strain is linearly distributed** through the thickness, varying from maximum compression on the top fiber to maximum tension on the bottom fiber; the neutral axis lies at mid-thickness for a symmetric beam.
4. **Deflections are small** compared to the span (otherwise a large-deflection correction factor must be introduced — see Section 2.3).
5. **Shear deformation is negligible** (valid when span-to-depth $L/h \ge 16$).

## 2.2 Stress and Strain Distribution in a Bending Beam

When a beam of rectangular cross-section is loaded in 3-point bending, the **bending moment diagram** is triangular, peaking at the center (where the load nose applies force). The maximum bending moment is:

$$M_{max} = \frac{P L}{4}$$

For 4-point loading (quarter-point), the central region experiences a **constant (pure) bending moment**:

$$M_{max} = \frac{P L}{2} \quad \text{(4-point, quarter-point loading)}$$

The maximum normal stress occurs at the outermost fiber, distance $c = h/2$ from the neutral axis, and is given by the **flexure formula**:

$$\sigma_f = \frac{M_{max} \cdot c}{I}$$

For a rectangular cross-section, the second moment of area is $I = bh^3/12$, and $c = h/2$, giving:

$$\sigma_f = \frac{M_{max} \cdot (h/2)}{bh^3/12} = \frac{6 M_{max}}{b h^2}$$

Substituting $M_{max}$ yields the **flexural stress equations** listed in the cheat sheet.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All flexural test formulas for rectangular specimens — EXAM GOLD**

| Quantity | 3-Point Loading (ASTM D790-A) | 4-Point Loading (Quarter-Point, ASTM D790-B) | Units |
|----------|-------------------------------|------------------------------------------------|-------|
| Maximum bending moment | $M_{max} = \dfrac{PL}{4}$ | $M_{max} = \dfrac{PL}{2}$ | N·mm |
| **Flexural stress (small deflection)** | $\sigma_f = \dfrac{3PL}{2bh^2}$ | $\sigma_f = \dfrac{3PL}{4bh^2}$ | MPa |
| **Flexural stress (large-deflection correction)** | $\sigma_f = \dfrac{3PL}{2bh^2}\left[1 + 6\left(\dfrac{D}{L}\right)^2 - 4\left(\dfrac{D}{L}\right)\dfrac{h}{L}\right]$ | $\sigma_f = \dfrac{3PL}{4bh^2}\left[1 + 4.7\left(\dfrac{D}{L}\right)^2 - 7.04\left(\dfrac{D}{L}\right)\dfrac{h}{L}\right]$ | MPa |
| **Flexural strain (outer fiber)** | $\epsilon_f = \dfrac{6Dh}{L^2}$ | $\epsilon_f = \dfrac{4.36 Dh}{L^2}$ (1/3-point or center strain) | mm/mm |
| **Flexural modulus from initial slope** | $E_B = \dfrac{L^3 \, m}{4 b h^3}$ | $E_B = \dfrac{0.17 L^3 \, m}{b h^3}$ (chord between 0.05 % and 0.25 % strain) | MPa |
| **Crosshead speed (Procedure A)** | $R = \dfrac{0.01 L^2}{6h}$ | $R = \dfrac{0.01 L^2}{4.36 h}$ | mm/min |
| **Span-to-depth ratio** | $L/h$ (16, 32, 64) | $L/h$ (16, 32, 64) | — |

Where:
* $P$ = applied load (N)
* $L$ = support span (mm)
* $b$ = specimen width (mm)
* $h$ = specimen thickness (mm)
* $D$ = deflection of the centerline (mm)
* $m$ = slope of the initial straight-line portion of the load–deflection curve, $m = \Delta P / \Delta D$ (N/mm)
* $R$ = crosshead speed (mm/min)

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Flexural data is the **primary design input** in the following polymer applications:
> * **PVC window profiles** – must withstand wind pressure (transverse bending).
> * **Polycarbonate (PC) roofing sheets** – flexural strength determines snow/wind load rating.
> * **FRP bridge decks** – flexural modulus governs span and deflection limits.
> * **3D-printed polymer parts** – flexural testing is used to compare printing orientations and infill densities.
> * **Wood-polymer composites (WPC)** – flexural properties are the headline mechanical specification in decking boards.

## 2.5 Flexural Strength (Modulus of Rupture, $\sigma_{f,MOR}$)

> [!TIP]
> The **flexural strength** is the **maximum flexural stress sustained by the specimen** before failure, commonly called the **modulus of rupture (MOR)**. In ductile polymers, the true MOR may be reached beyond the proportional limit and the linear-elastic stress formula is no longer strictly valid — the value is therefore a *nominal* strength and should be interpreted with caution.

For **brittle polymers** (e.g., cured epoxy, PMMA, PS), the MOR is generally close to the tensile strength. For **ductile polymers** (e.g., LDPE, PP, PA), the flexural MOR is often **higher than the tensile strength** because the outer fibers in bending reach failure strain before the bulk specimen yields, and the strain distribution in tension is more demanding.

## 2.6 Flexural Modulus: Chord vs. Tangent vs. Secant

* **Tangent modulus** – slope of the load–deflection curve at the origin.
* **Chord modulus** – slope of the chord drawn between two specific strain points (0.05 % and 0.25 % in ASTM D790). This is the **default reported value**.
* **Secant modulus** – slope of a line from the origin to a specific stress/strain point.

The **chord modulus** is preferred for polymer testing because the initial toe region (specimen seating) introduces non-linearity at the start of the curve.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Flexural Stress Formula (3-Point Bending)

### 3.1.1 Setting up the Free-Body and Bending Moment

A simply supported beam of span $L$ carries a concentrated load $P$ at midspan. The vertical reactions at each support, by symmetry, are:

$$R_A = R_B = \frac{P}{2}$$

The bending moment at a section located at a distance $x$ from support A (for $0 \le x \le L/2$) is:

$$M(x) = R_A \cdot x = \frac{P}{2} x$$

At midspan ($x = L/2$):

$$M_{max} = \frac{P}{2} \cdot \frac{L}{2} = \frac{PL}{4}$$

### 3.1.2 Bending Stress from the Flexure Formula

The **flexure formula** (derived from the Euler–Bernoulli assumption that plane sections remain plane and stress is proportional to distance from the neutral axis) is:

$$\sigma = \frac{M y}{I}$$

The outermost fiber is at distance $y_{max} = c = h/2$ from the neutral axis. For a rectangular cross-section, the **second moment of area** about the neutral axis is:

$$I = \int_{0}^{h} y^2 \cdot b \, dy = b \left[ \frac{y^3}{3} \right]_{0}^{h} = \frac{b h^3}{12}$$

Substituting into the flexure formula at $y = h/2$:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{M_{max} \cdot (h/2)}{bh^3/12} = \frac{6 M_{max}}{b h^2}$$

Plugging $M_{max} = PL/4$:

$$\sigma_{max} = \frac{6}{b h^2} \cdot \frac{P L}{4} = \frac{3 P L}{2 b h^2}$$

This is the **flexural stress in 3-point bending**:

$$\boxed{\sigma_f = \frac{3 P L}{2 b h^2}}$$

### 3.1.3 Derivation of the Flexural Strain (3-Point Bending)

The radius of curvature of a beam is related to the bending moment and the flexural rigidity $EI$ as:

$$\frac{1}{\rho} = \frac{M}{E I}$$

The longitudinal strain in a fiber at distance $y$ from the neutral axis is:

$$\epsilon = \frac{y}{\rho} = \frac{M y}{E I}$$

At the outer fiber $y = h/2$:

$$\epsilon_{max} = \frac{M_{max} \cdot (h/2)}{E I}$$

For a beam loaded in 3-point bending, the **midspan deflection** is given by the standard beam result:

$$D = \frac{P L^3}{48 E I}$$

Solving for $E I$:

$$E I = \frac{P L^3}{48 D}$$

Substitute into the strain expression:

$$\epsilon_{max} = \frac{M_{max} (h/2)}{P L^3 / 48 D} = \frac{(PL/4)(h/2) \cdot 48 D}{P L^3} = \frac{48 D h}{8 L^2} = \frac{6 D h}{L^2}$$

Hence:

$$\boxed{\epsilon_f = \frac{6 D h}{L^2}}$$

### 3.1.4 Derivation of the Flexural Modulus (3-Point Bending)

The flexural modulus is defined as the ratio of flexural stress to flexural strain in the linear-elastic region:

$$E_B = \frac{\sigma_f}{\epsilon_f} = \frac{3 P L / (2 b h^2)}{6 D h / L^2} = \frac{3 P L}{2 b h^2} \cdot \frac{L^2}{6 D h} = \frac{P L^3}{4 b h^3 D}$$

Recognizing that the slope of the linear portion of the load–deflection curve is:

$$m = \frac{\Delta P}{\Delta D} = \frac{P}{D}$$

we obtain the **standard form** of the flexural modulus equation used in ASTM D790:

$$\boxed{E_B = \frac{L^3 \, m}{4 b h^3}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Large-Deflection Correction Factor (3-Point Bending)

When the midspan deflection $D$ is **not small** compared to the span $L$ (typically when $D/L > 0.05$ or $D/h > 0.5$), the simple linear-elastic bending formula **overestimates the true stress** because additional moment contributions arise from the geometry (the load arm shortens and the support-to-centerline distance effectively changes).

ASTM D790 prescribes the following correction by introducing a **correction factor $C_f$** such that:

$$\sigma_f^{true} = C_f \cdot \frac{3 P L}{2 b h^2}, \qquad C_f = 1 + 6\left(\frac{D}{L}\right)^2 - 4\left(\frac{D}{L}\right)\left(\frac{h}{L}\right)$$

### 3.2.1 Physical Justification Step-by-Step

The exact moment at midspan, accounting for the vertical component of the load and the geometry of the deformed beam, becomes:

$$M_{max} = \frac{P L}{4} + P \cdot \delta_{geom}$$

where the geometric correction involves the additional bending effect due to the **axial displacement** of the support level relative to the deformed neutral axis. A first-order analysis using the **membrane effect** yields the additional terms proportional to $(D/L)^2$ and $h D / L^2$. The result, after dimensional normalization, gives the standard $C_f$ expression above.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (Dec 2023 pattern):**
> A polymer beam is tested in 3-point bending. Specimen dimensions: $L = 50.8$ mm, $b = 12.7$ mm, $h = 3.2$ mm. At a load of $P = 25$ N, the midspan deflection is $D = 1.0$ mm. Calculate (a) the flexural stress, (b) the flexural strain, and (c) the flexural modulus.

### Solution

**Step 1: Compute the flexural stress.**

$$P = 25 \text{ N}, \quad L = 50.8 \text{ mm}, \quad b = 12.7 \text{ mm}, \quad h = 3.2 \text{ mm}$$

Compute the denominator $2 b h^2$:

$$2 b h^2 = 2 \times 12.7 \times (3.2)^2 = 2 \times 12.7 \times 10.24 = 260.096 \text{ mm}^3$$

Compute the numerator $3 P L$:

$$3 P L = 3 \times 25 \times 50.8 = 3810 \text{ N·mm}$$

Therefore:

$$\sigma_f = \frac{3 P L}{2 b h^2} = \frac{3810}{260.096} = 14.65 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2: Compute the flexural strain.**

$$\epsilon_f = \frac{6 D h}{L^2} = \frac{6 \times 1.0 \times 3.2}{(50.8)^2} = \frac{19.2}{2580.64} = 0.00744 \text{ mm/mm} = 7.44 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3: Compute the flexural modulus.**

$$E_B = \frac{\sigma_f}{\epsilon_f} = \frac{14.65}{7.44 \times 10^{-3}} = 1969 \text{ MPa} \approx 1.97 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 4: Apply large-deflection check.**
$D/L = 1.0/50.8 = 0.0197$, $h/L = 3.2/50.8 = 0.0630$.

$$C_f = 1 + 6(0.0197)^2 - 4(0.0197)(0.0630) = 1 + 0.00233 - 0.00497 = 0.9974$$

Since $C_f \approx 1$, the small-deflection formula is acceptable.

**Final Answer:** $\sigma_f = 14.65$ MPa; $\epsilon_f = 7.44 \times 10^{-3}$ mm/mm; $E_B \approx 1.97$ GPa.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: 3-Point vs. 4-Point vs. Tensile

| Property | 3-Point Bending | 4-Point Bending (Quarter-Point) | Tensile Test |
|----------|-----------------|----------------------------------|----------------|
| Stress state | Linear stress, max at center | Pure bending, max over central span | Uniform uniaxial |
| Failure locus | Always at midspan (under load nose) | Anywhere within central span | Anywhere in gauge length |
| Bending moment profile | Triangular | Rectangular (pure bending) | None (constant stress) |
| Strain formula | $\epsilon_f = 6Dh/L^2$ | $\epsilon_f = 4.36 Dh/L^2$ (at center) | $\epsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ |
| Modulus formula | $E_B = L^3 m / (4 b h^3)$ | $E_B = 0.17 L^3 m / (b h^3)$ | $E = \sigma / \epsilon$ |
| Effect of misalignment | High (load-nose contact errors) | Lower (load distributed over two noses) | Moderate (grip slippage) |
| Preferred for | Quality control, screening | Engineering design data | Fundamental properties |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Three-Point Bending — Block-Level Functional Architecture

This diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a 3-point flexural test, showing the load–deflection data flow and the geometric quantities entering each formula.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Specimen Placement] --> B[Span Setting L = 16h or 32h]
    B --> C[Preload 0.1 to 5 N to seat]
    C --> D[Apply Load P at Crosshead Speed R]
    D --> E[Load-Deflection Curve Recorded]
    E --> F{Linear Elastic Region?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Compute Slope m = dP/dD]
    G --> H[Flexural Modulus EB = L cubed times m over 4 b h cubed]
    F -->|No| I[Use Chord Modulus between 0.05 percent and 0.25 percent strain]
    I --> H
    D --> J[Continue to Maximum Load Pmax]
    J --> K[Compute Flexural Stress sigma f = 3 Pmax L over 2 b h squared]
    D --> L[Record Deflection D at Pmax]
    L --> M[Compute Flexural Strain epsilon f = 6 D h over L squared]
    H --> N[Final Report EB and sigma fMOR and epsilon f]
    K --> N
    M --> N
```

## 4.2 Four-Point Bending — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Specimen Placed on Two Supports] --> B[Load Span set to L over 2 with quarter-point noses]
    B --> C[Two Equal Loads P each Applied]
    C --> D[Constant Pure Bending Moment PL over 2 in central region]
    D --> E[Load vs Deflection Curve Recorded]
    E --> F[Flexural Stress sigma f = 3 P L over 4 b h squared]
    E --> G[Flexural Strain epsilon f = 4.36 D h over L squared at midspan]
    F --> H[Flexural Modulus EB = 0.17 L cubed m over b h cubed using chord slope]
    G --> H
    H --> I[Report EB and sigma fMOR and epsilon f with span to depth ratio noted]
```

## 4.3 Flexural Stress Distribution Through the Thickness

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Top Surface y = plus h over 2] --> B[Maximum Compression stress c]
    B --> C[Linear Stress Distribution]
    C --> D[Neutral Axis y = 0 stress = 0]
    D --> E[Linear Stress Distribution]
    E --> F[Maximum Tension stress t]
    F --> G[Bottom Surface y = minus h over 2]
```

## 4.4 Test Procedure Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Receive Test Request] --> B[Identify Material Type]
    B --> C{Stiffness Known?}
    C -->|No| D[Run Screening at L over h = 32]
    C -->|Yes| E[Choose L over h = 16 if Rigid or 64 if Soft]
    D --> F[Measure b and h at 3 Points]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Condition Specimens at 23 plus minus 2 C and 50 plus minus 5 percent RH for 88 hours per ASTM D618]
    G --> H[Set Span on Fixture and Calibrate]
    H --> I[Mount Specimen and Apply Preload]
    I --> J[Run Test to 5 percent Strain or Failure]
    J --> K[Capture Load-Deflection Data at Minimum 100 Hz]
    K --> L[Compute EB and sigma f and epsilon f]
    L --> M[Generate Report with Mode of Failure and Visual Observations]
```

## 4.5 Span-to-Depth Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Polymer Type] --> B{Stiff Plastic like PC PMMA PA}
    A --> C{Soft Plastic like LDPE PP]
    A --> D{Elastomer or Foam}
    B -->|Yes| E[Use L over h = 16 to maximize moment]
    C -->|Yes| F[Use L over h = 32 to allow measurable deflection]
    D -->|Yes| G[Use L over h = 64 and reduce crosshead speed]
    E --> H[Procedural Choice Procedure A 3 point or B 4 point]
    F --> H
    G --> H
```

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "modulus of rupture" in the context of flexural testing of polymers.**

**Model Answer:**
The *modulus of rupture* (MOR) is the **maximum flexural stress** sustained by a polymer specimen during a bending test, calculated using the linear-elastic flexure formula $\sigma_f = 3PL/(2bh^2)$ for 3-point bending. For ductile polymers, it represents a *nominal* value because the linear-elastic stress formula is no longer strictly valid once the material yields. It is a key indicator of **flexural strength** used in engineering design of beams, panels, and structural plastics. *(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for applicability/limitation.)*

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is the recommended span-to-depth ratio for a stiff polymer in ASTM D790, and why is it important?**

**Model Answer:**
ASTM D790 recommends a span-to-depth ratio ($L/h$) of **16:1** for stiff polymers such as polycarbonate, PMMA, and glass-filled nylon. Smaller ratios lead to significant **shear deformation** contributions and inaccurate flexural modulus values. Larger ratios (32:1 or 64:1) are used for less stiff materials. The $L/h$ ratio must be reported in the final test report because flexural properties depend on it. *(3 marks: 1 mark for the ratio, 1 mark for materials, 1 mark for the reason for choice.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — **[KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the expressions for **flexural stress**, **flexural strain**, and **flexural modulus** for a polymer specimen tested in **three-point bending** according to ASTM D790. State all assumptions clearly.

**(b) [7 marks]** A PVC pipe-grade specimen is tested in 3-point bending. Data: $L = 50.8$ mm, $b = 12.7$ mm, $h = 3.2$ mm. The load–deflection curve is linear in the elastic region with slope $m = 240$ N/mm. The maximum load reached is $P_{max} = 38$ N at deflection $D = 1.5$ mm. Calculate (i) the flexural modulus, (ii) the flexural stress at $P_{max}$, and (iii) the flexural strain at $P_{max}$. Comment on whether a large-deflection correction is necessary.

---

### Question B — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] (Internal Choice)**

**(a) [7 marks]** Compare **three-point bending** and **four-point bending** test methods for polymers with respect to: (i) loading configuration, (ii) bending moment distribution, (iii) failure location, (iv) flexural stress and strain formulas, and (v) preferred application scenarios. State the relevant ASTM/ISO standard.

**(b) [7 marks]** With the help of a **labelled block diagram**, describe the procedure for conducting a 3-point flexural test on a polymer specimen per ASTM D790. Explain the role of conditioning, span-to-depth ratio, and crosshead speed.

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

### Solution to Question A (a)

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. Material behaves as a linear-elastic, homogeneous, isotropic continuum.
2. Plane sections remain plane and normal to the neutral axis (Euler–Bernoulli beam theory).
3. Deflections are small compared to the span (so geometry effects are negligible).
4. Shear deformation is negligible (valid for $L/h \ge 16$).
5. The neutral axis lies at the geometric mid-thickness of a symmetric rectangular section.

**Derivation (5 marks):** The complete derivation is given in Section 3.1 of these notes. The student must present the free-body diagram, compute $M_{max} = PL/4$, use the flexure formula with $I = bh^3/12$ and $c = h/2$ to obtain:

$$\sigma_f = \frac{3 P L}{2 b h^2}$$

Then derive the midspan deflection $D = PL^3/(48EI)$, combine with $\epsilon = y/\rho$ to obtain:

$$\epsilon_f = \frac{6 D h}{L^2}$$

and finally:

$$E_B = \frac{L^3 \, m}{4 b h^3}$$

---

### Solution to Question A (b)

**Given:** $L = 50.8$ mm, $b = 12.7$ mm, $h = 3.2$ mm, $m = 240$ N/mm, $P_{max} = 38$ N, $D_{max} = 1.5$ mm.

**(i) Flexural modulus:**

$$E_B = \frac{L^3 \, m}{4 b h^3}$$

Compute $L^3 = (50.8)^3 = 131096.5$ mm³; $h^3 = (3.2)^3 = 32.768$ mm³; $4 b h^3 = 4 \times 12.7 \times 32.768 = 1664.6$ mm⁴.

$$E_B = \frac{131096.5 \times 240}{1664.6} = \frac{31463160}{1664.6} = 18\,901 \text{ MPa} \approx 18.9 \text{ GPa}$$

**(ii) Flexural stress at $P_{max}$:**

$$\sigma_f = \frac{3 P_{max} L}{2 b h^2} = \frac{3 \times 38 \times 50.8}{2 \times 12.7 \times (3.2)^2} = \frac{5791.2}{260.096} = 22.27 \text{ MPa}$$

**(iii) Flexural strain at $P_{max}$:**

$$\epsilon_f = \frac{6 D_{max} h}{L^2} = \frac{6 \times 1.5 \times 3.2}{(50.8)^2} = \frac{28.8}{2580.64} = 0.01116 \text{ mm/mm} = 1.12 \text{ \%}$$

**Large-deflection check:**

$$D/L = 1.5/50.8 = 0.0295, \quad h/L = 3.2/50.8 = 0.0630$$

$$C_f = 1 + 6(0.0295)^2 - 4(0.0295)(0.0630) = 1 + 0.00522 - 0.00743 = 0.99779 \approx 1$$

**Conclusion:** The small-deflection formulas are valid; the large-deflection correction is **not necessary** for this dataset. The corrected stress would be $22.27 \times 0.998 = 22.23$ MPa, a negligible difference.

**Final Results:** $E_B \approx 18.9$ GPa; $\sigma_f \approx 22.3$ MPa; $\epsilon_f \approx 1.12\%$. *(7 marks: 2 marks for E_B, 2 marks for sigma, 2 marks for epsilon, 1 mark for the large-deflection discussion.)*

---

### Solution to Question B (a) — Comparison Table

| Aspect | 3-Point Bending | 4-Point Bending |
|--------|------------------|------------------|
| (i) Loading configuration | One central load nose, two supports | Two load noses, two supports; load span = $L/2$ (quarter-point) |
| (ii) Bending moment | Triangular, peak $PL/4$ at center | Constant pure moment $PL/2$ in central region |
| (iii) Failure location | Always at center (under load nose) | Anywhere within the constant-moment region |
| (iv) Flexural stress | $\sigma_f = 3PL/(2bh^2)$ | $\sigma_f = 3PL/(4bh^2)$ |
| (v) Flexural strain | $\epsilon_f = 6Dh/L^2$ | $\epsilon_f = 4.36Dh/L^2$ (at midspan) |
| (vi) Preferred use | Quality control, screening | Engineering design data, composite testing |
| (vii) Standard | ASTM D790-A, ISO 178 Method A | ASTM D790-B, ISO 178 Method B |

*(7 marks: 1 mark per aspect, with 1 extra mark for the standard reference.)*

---

### Solution to Question B (b) — Test Procedure

**Step 1 — Specimen preparation (2 marks):**
Prepare rectangular specimens by injection molding or machining. Measure width $b$ and thickness $h$ at three points along the span; use the average values. Condition specimens at $23 \pm 2$ °C and $50 \pm 5$ % RH for not less than **88 hours** per **ASTM D618 Procedure A**.

**Step 2 — Span selection (1 mark):**
Set the support span $L$ to **16 times the thickness** for stiff plastics, **32 times** for less stiff materials, **64 times** for elastomers. Record the actual $L$ and the $L/h$ ratio.

**Step 3 — Fixture and loading (2 marks):**
Place the specimen symmetrically on the two supports with the loading nose centered. Apply a small preload (typically 0.1 % to 1 % of expected maximum load) to seat the specimen. Set the crosshead speed $R = 0.01 L^2 / (6h)$ to achieve an outer-fiber strain rate of 0.01 min⁻¹.

**Step 4 — Data acquisition and computation (2 marks):**
Record load–deflection data continuously. Identify the linear-elastic region and compute the slope $m$. Calculate $E_B = L^3 m / (4 b h^3)$, $\sigma_f = 3PL/(2bh^2)$, and $\epsilon_f = 6Dh/L^2$. Report flexural strength (MOR) and the chord modulus. **Visually classify the failure mode** (tension, compression, shear) per ASTM D790 Fig. X1.1 — a frequent grading criterion.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read this carefully before writing the exam!**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** Marks are deducted if $P$ is in kgf instead of N, or if $L$, $b$, $h$ are in cm instead of mm. KTU insists on a **single consistent unit system (N, mm, MPa)**.
> 2. **Mixing up formulas:** The numerator **3** in the 3-point stress formula and the factor **0.17** in the 4-point modulus formula are commonly swapped. Memorize the cheat sheet verbatim.
> 3. **Forgetting the large-deflection correction:** If the problem gives $D/L > 0.05$, you *must* apply $C_f$. Neglecting it costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Reporting only the chord modulus when the toe region is large:** Always justify the modulus computation by showing the slope and the strain range used.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions** in derivation questions — at least **3 assumptions** must be explicitly written.
> 6. **Failing to comment on $L/h$** in numerical problems — the $L/h$ ratio must be reported, even if not asked.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Flexural Properties of Polymers**
>
> * **Governing Standards:** ASTM D790 (3-point & 4-point) and ISO 178.
> * **Three primary outputs:** Flexural stress $\sigma_f$, flexural strain $\epsilon_f$, and flexural modulus $E_B$.
> * **3-Point Flexural Stress:** $\sigma_f = 3PL / (2 b h^2)$.
> * **3-Point Flexural Strain:** $\epsilon_f = 6 D h / L^2$.
> * **3-Point Flexural Modulus:** $E_B = L^3 m / (4 b h^3)$.
> * **4-Point Flexural Stress (Quarter-Point):** $\sigma_f = 3PL / (4 b h^2)$.
> * **4-Point Flexural Modulus (Chord, 0.05–0.25 %):** $E_B = 0.17 L^3 m / (b h^3)$.
> * **Span-to-Depth Ratio ($L/h$):** **16:1** for stiff, **32:1** for moderate, **64:1** for soft polymers.
> * **Crosshead Speed (Procedure A):** $R = 0.01 L^2 / (6 h)$ mm/min to achieve $\dot{\epsilon} = 0.01$ min⁻¹.
> * **Assumptions:** Linear-elastic, plane sections remain plane, small deflection, shear negligible, symmetric section.
> * **Large-Deflection Correction:** $C_f = 1 + 6(D/L)^2 - 4(D/L)(h/L)$ (3-point) — apply when $D/L > 0.05$.
> * **Chord Modulus:** Default reported modulus; slope of chord between 0.05 % and 0.25 % strain.
> * **Flexural Strength (MOR):** Maximum stress in bending — *nominal* for ductile polymers, *true* for brittle ones.
> * **Conditioning:** $23 \pm 2$ °C, $50 \pm 5$ % RH, ≥ 88 h per ASTM D618.
> * **MOR vs. Tensile Strength:** $\sigma_{f,MOR} > \sigma_{TS}$ for ductile polymers; $\sigma_{f,MOR} \approx \sigma_{TS}$ for brittle polymers.
> * **Practical Use:** Pipes, panels, FRP decks, 3D-printed parts, WPC decking, automotive dashboards.
> * **Watch for in exams:** Units consistency; $L/h$ reporting; large-deflection check; assumptions; failure mode classification.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Shear Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT602 - POLYMER TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Shear Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Shear Properties** of polymers refer to the mechanical response of a polymeric material when subjected to a **shear loading**, i.e., forces acting parallel to the cross-sectional surface of the specimen rather than perpendicular (normal) to it. The shear response is characterized by three primary engineering quantities:

- **Shear Stress ($\tau$)** — The component of stress acting tangentially to a material plane, expressed in **MPa** or **psi**.
- **Shear Strain ($\gamma$)** — The angular deformation (in radians) produced by the shear stress.
- **Shear Modulus ($G$)** — The slope of the linear elastic region of the shear stress–shear strain curve, expressed in **MPa**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under Module 2 of *PCPOT602 – Polymer Testing*, shear properties are studied alongside tensile, flexural, and compressive properties because they jointly describe the **elastic constants** of an isotropic polymer through the well-known relationship between Young's modulus ($E$), shear modulus ($G$), and Poisson's ratio ($\nu$).

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$$

For an **isotropic polymer** (e.g., amorphous thermoplastics like PMMA, PS), only two of the three elastic constants ($E$, $G$, $\nu$) are independent. For **anisotropic polymers** (fibre-reinforced composites, oriented films), the shear response must be described by a full **compliance matrix** with up to five independent shear moduli.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a deck of playing cards lying flat on a table. If you place your palm on the top card and slide it sideways while holding the bottom card fixed, the entire stack deforms into a **parallelogram shape**. The top card has moved sideways relative to the bottom card, and every intermediate card has shifted slightly more than the one below it.

This is exactly what **shear deformation** looks like at the molecular level in a polymer:

- The **fixed bottom** represents the constrained material surface.
- The **sliding top** represents the applied tangential force.
- The **tilted stack** represents the resulting shear strain $\gamma$.
- The **resistance to sliding** between adjacent card layers is analogous to the **internal friction** between polymer chains resisting relative motion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:** Shear testing answers the question, *"How much does the polymer resist sliding deformation when forces push parallel to its surface rather than perpendicular to it?"* This is fundamentally different from tensile testing (which pulls the material apart) and compressive testing (which squeezes it together).

## 1.3 Why Shear Properties Matter in Polymer Engineering

Shear loading is rarely an academic curiosity — it is the **dominant stress mode** in the following real engineering situations:

| Engineering Application | Role of Shear Properties |
|---|---|
| Polymer film extrusion | Melt shear viscosity governs flow rate |
| Injection moulding cavity filling | Shear stress determines mould filling time |
| Adhesive bonded joints | Lap shear strength dictates joint capacity |
| Fibre–matrix interface in composites | Interlaminar shear strength (ILSS) governs delamination |
| Polymer gears & bearings | Surface shear fatigue limits service life |
| Rubber bushings & elastomeric mounts | Dynamic shear modulus controls vibration isolation |

## 1.4 Distinguishing Shear from Related Modes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Boundary:** Students often confuse shear with bending and torsion. The distinction is:

- **Shear** → force parallel to a surface, displacement of that surface relative to a parallel one.
- **Tension** → force perpendicular (normal) to a surface, elongation.
- **Bending** → combination of tension on one face and compression on the opposite face.
- **Torsion** → twisting moment producing **shear stresses** on cross-sectional planes (torsion is a special case of shear loading).

A pure torsion test on a cylindrical polymer rod is, in fact, a **shear test** because the maximum stress generated on the rod surface is purely tangential.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Shear Stress–Strain Curve — A Step-by-Step Walk-Through

When a polymer specimen is loaded in shear, the response curve ($\tau$ vs. $\gamma$) reveals critical information about its **viscoelastic nature**. Unlike metals (which exhibit a sharp yield point), polymers show a **gradual transition** between elastic, viscoelastic, and plastic regimes.

### Stage 1: Linear Viscoelastic Region
- At very small strains ($\gamma < 0.1\%$), the polymer chains deform reversibly.
- The relationship is **linear** and obeys Hooke's law in shear:

$$\tau = G \cdot \gamma$$

- The slope $G$ is **constant** in this region and is called the **shear modulus**.
- For amorphous thermoplastics at room temperature: $G \approx 1$–$3$ GPa.
- For elastomers (natural rubber, silicone): $G \approx 0.1$–$10$ MPa.

### Stage 2: Non-Linear Viscoelastic Region
- Beyond the linear limit, polymer chains begin to **uncoil, orient, and slip past each other**.
- The curve bends, and the apparent modulus decreases.
- **Strain softening** occurs in many glassy polymers; **strain hardening** occurs in semicrystalline polymers (PE, PP) due to chain alignment.

### Stage 3: Yield and Plastic Flow
- For ductile polymers (PC, PA, POM), a **shear yield point** is observed.
- Beyond yield, the polymer flows plastically — this is the basis of **cold forming** and **thermoforming** processes.
- For brittle polymers (PS, PMMA at low temperature), fracture occurs before yield.

### Stage 4: Failure
- Failure mode is either **ductile shear tearing** (withdrawn fibres visible on fracture surface in tough polymers) or **brittle shear fracture** (smooth, glassy surface in brittle polymers).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** The **area under the shear stress–strain curve up to failure** is called the **modulus of resilience in shear** ($U_r$) and represents the energy per unit volume the polymer can absorb in shear before failure:

$$U_r = \int_0^{\gamma_f} \tau \, d\gamma \approx \frac{\tau_y^{\,2}}{2G} \quad \text{(for linear elastic up to yield)}$$

## 2.2 Standard Shear Test Methods for Polymers (KTU Board Favourites)

### 2.2.1 Iosipescu Shear Test (ASTM D5379)
- The **most widely accepted** shear test for fibre-reinforced polymer composites.
- Specimen: small rectangular coupon with **two V-notches** machined on opposite faces.
- Loading: a special fixture applies **counter-acting forces** through the notches, producing a region of **pure, uniform shear** between the notches.
- Strain is measured with **biaxial strain gauges** mounted at $\pm 45^\circ$ to the loading axis.
- Provides: **shear modulus $G_{12}$**, **shear strength $\tau_{12}$**, and **shear stress–strain curve**.

### 2.2.2 V-Notched Rail Shear Test (ASTM D7078)
- Similar concept to Iosipescu but uses a larger specimen and clamping rails.
- Preferred for testing **thick-section composite laminates** (e.g., wind turbine blade root joints).
- Produces a larger volume of material under pure shear → less sensitive to local defects.

### 2.2.3 Short-Beam Shear (Interlaminar) Test (ASTM D2344)
- A three-point bending test on a specimen with **span-to-thickness ratio of 4:1**.
- Failure mode: **interlaminar shear** (delamination between plies).
- Widely used as a **quality control** test in the composites industry due to its simplicity.
- **Limitation:** stress state is not pure shear; results are useful for comparison but not for absolute modulus measurement.

### 2.2.4 Torsion of Solid Cylindrical Rod (ASTM D1043)
- Used for **thermoplastic and elastomeric** polymers.
- Provides the **complex shear modulus $G^* = G' + iG''$** as a function of temperature.
- $G'$ = storage modulus (elastic energy stored).
- $G''$ = loss modulus (energy dissipated as heat).
- The ratio $\tan \delta = G''/G'$ is the **damping factor**.
- This method traces the entire **thermo-mechanical spectrum** from glassy to rubbery state.

### 2.2.5 Block-on-Ring and Pin-on-Disk Tribological Shear
- Measures **shear at the sliding contact interface** — relevant for polymer bearings.
- Not a bulk shear test, but quantifies surface shear strength.

### 2.2.6 Lap Shear Test for Adhesives (ASTM D1002)
- Two polymer or metal substrates bonded by an adhesive, loaded in tension so the bond plane is in shear.
- Reports **lap shear strength** in MPa.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every formula a student must memorize for the **Part A and Part B questions** on Shear Properties in the KTU 2024 ESE.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\tau = F/A$ | Basic shear stress definition | Pa or MPa |
| 2 | $\gamma = \tan\theta \approx \theta$ (small strains) | Shear strain as angular deformation | rad (dimensionless) |
| 3 | $\tau = G \gamma$ | Hooke's law in shear (linear region) | Pa or MPa |
| 4 | $G = E / \left[ 2(1+\nu) \right]$ | Isotropic relation among elastic constants | Pa or MPa |
| 5 | $E = 2G(1+\nu)$ | Inverse of above | Pa or MPa |
| 6 | $\nu = (E / 2G) - 1$ | Poisson's ratio from E and G | dimensionless |
| 7 | $\tau_y = \sigma_y / \sqrt{3}$ | von Mises shear yield criterion (for metals & some polymers) | Pa or MPa |
| 8 | $\tau_{max} = T \cdot r / J$ | Maximum shear stress on surface of a shaft under torsion (radius $r$, polar moment $J$) | Pa or MPa |
| 9 | $\theta = T \cdot L / (G \cdot J)$ | Angle of twist over length $L$ | rad |
| 10 | $J = \pi d^{4}/32$ | Polar moment of inertia for a solid circular shaft of diameter $d$ | m⁴ |
| 11 | $G' = (\tau_0 / \gamma_0) \cos \delta$ | Storage modulus (dynamic) | Pa or MPa |
| 12 | $G'' = (\tau_0 / \gamma_0) \sin \delta$ | Loss modulus (dynamic) | Pa or MPa |
| 13 | $\tan \delta = G'' / G'$ | Damping factor (loss tangent) | dimensionless |
| 14 | $\eta = \tau / \dot{\gamma}$ | Shear viscosity of polymer melt | Pa·s |
| 15 | $U_r = \tau_y^{2} / (2G)$ | Modulus of resilience in shear | J/m³ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Reminder:** Every formula above has been **mathematically verified**. The KTU board expects students to state both the formula and its physical meaning before substituting numerical values.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Where Each Formula Is Used

| Formula | Real-World Use |
|---|---|
| $G = E / [2(1+\nu)]$ | Quick estimation of $G$ when only tensile data is available |
| $\tau_{max} = Tr/J$ | Design of polymer drive shafts, prosthetics, polymer screws |
| $\tan \delta = G''/G'$ | Selection of elastomers for vibration dampers and tyres |
| $\eta = \tau / \dot{\gamma}$ | Rheological characterization of polymer melts for processing |
| $\tau_y = \sigma_y / \sqrt{3}$ | Predicting shear yield in metal-like polymer forming |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Relating Shear Modulus $G$ to Young's Modulus $E$ and Poisson's Ratio $\nu$

### Background
For an **isotropic, homogeneous, linearly elastic material**, the three independent elastic constants ($E$, $G$, $\nu$) are linked by a single equation. This is derived by considering a pure shear stress state and analysing the resulting principal strains.

### Step 1 — Apply a Pure Shear Stress on a Cube
Consider a small cubic element of polymer with edges aligned along the $x$, $y$, $z$ axes. Apply a pure shear stress $\tau_{xy}$ on the faces perpendicular to the $x$ and $y$ axes.

The corresponding shear strain is:

$$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{G}$$

### Step 2 — Decompose Shear into Two Equal and Opposite Principal Stresses
A pure shear stress state is mathematically equivalent to **two equal and opposite normal stresses** acting on planes rotated by $45^\circ$ to the original faces. Using Mohr's circle:

$$\sigma_1 = +\tau_{xy}, \quad \sigma_2 = -\tau_{xy}, \quad \sigma_3 = 0$$

### Step 3 — Compute Principal Strains Using Generalised Hooke's Law
For an isotropic linear elastic solid under triaxial stress, the strain in direction 1 is:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}\left[ \sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3) \right]$$

Substituting $\sigma_1 = +\tau_{xy}$, $\sigma_2 = -\tau_{xy}$, $\sigma_3 = 0$:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{1}{E}\left[ \tau_{xy} - \nu(-\tau_{xy} + 0) \right] = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{E}(1+\nu)$$

Similarly:

$$\varepsilon_2 = \frac{1}{E}\left[ -\tau_{xy} - \nu(+\tau_{xy} + 0) \right] = -\frac{\tau_{xy}}{E}(1+\nu)$$

### Step 4 — Relate Principal Strains to Shear Strain
For small strains, the engineering shear strain is the difference between the two principal strains:

$$\gamma_{xy} = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{E}(1+\nu) - \left(-\frac{\tau_{xy}}{E}(1+\nu)\right)$$

$$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{2\tau_{xy}(1+\nu)}{E}$$

### Step 5 — Equate With Hooke's Law in Shear
By definition, $\tau_{xy} = G \gamma_{xy}$, which means:

$$\gamma_{xy} = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{G}$$

Setting the two expressions for $\gamma_{xy}$ equal:

$$\frac{\tau_{xy}}{G} = \frac{2\tau_{xy}(1+\nu)}{E}$$

### Step 6 — Cancel $\tau_{xy}$ and Solve for $G$

$$\boxed{G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** For an incompressible material ($\nu = 0.5$), $G = E/3$. For steel ($\nu \approx 0.30$), $G \approx 0.385 E$. For a typical amorphous polymer ($\nu \approx 0.35$), $G \approx 0.37 E$. All physically reasonable.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Shear Stress in a Solid Circular Shaft Under Torsion

### Step 1 — Define the Internal Torque and Geometry
Consider a solid circular shaft of diameter $d$, length $L$, subjected to an axial torque $T$. The polar moment of inertia is:

$$J = \int_A r^{2} \, dA = \int_0^{2\pi}\int_0^{d/2} r^{2} \cdot r \, dr \, d\phi = \frac{\pi d^{4}}{32}$$

### Step 2 — State the Elastic Torsion Formula (Assumption: Plane Sections Remain Plane)
For a linearly elastic shaft:

$$\tau(r) = \frac{T \cdot r}{J}$$

The maximum stress occurs at the outer surface where $r = d/2$:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{T \cdot (d/2)}{\pi d^{4}/32} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^{3}}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Angle of Twist
By integrating the shear strain $\gamma = \tau/G = Tr/(GJ)$ along the length:

$$\theta = \frac{T \cdot L}{G \cdot J}$$

### Step 4 — Combine to Find Shear Modulus from Torsion Test
Rearranging:

$$G = \frac{T \cdot L}{J \cdot \theta} = \frac{32 T \cdot L}{\pi d^{4} \cdot \theta}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **operational formula** used in ASTM D1043 torsion testing of polymer rods. The torque $T$ and twist angle $\theta$ are measured directly; the geometric quantities $L$ and $d$ are known.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Style

### Problem (KTU University Exam – June 2023 Style)

A solid cylindrical polymer rod of diameter **15 mm** and gauge length **80 mm** is tested in torsion at room temperature. The torque–twist data in the linear region are:

| Torque $T$ (N·m) | Twist angle $\theta$ (rad) |
|---|---|
| 1.5 | 0.0120 |
| 3.0 | 0.0241 |
| 4.5 | 0.0362 |

Compute:
- (i) The polar moment of inertia $J$
- (ii) The shear modulus $G$
- (iii) The maximum shear stress at a torque of $T = 6.0$ N·m

### Solution

**Step (i) — Polar Moment of Inertia**

Using $J = \pi d^{4}/32$ with $d = 0.015$ m:

$$J = \frac{\pi (0.015)^{4}}{32}$$

$$J = \frac{\pi \times 5.0625 \times 10^{-8}}{32}$$

$$\boxed{J = 4.97 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{m}^{4}}$$

**Step (ii) — Shear Modulus from Slope of T–θ Curve**

Compute $T/\theta$ from the linear data:

| $T$ (N·m) | $\theta$ (rad) | $T/\theta$ (N·m/rad) |
|---|---|---|
| 1.5 | 0.0120 | 125.0 |
| 3.0 | 0.0241 | 124.5 |
| 4.5 | 0.0362 | 124.3 |

Average slope: $\overline{T/\theta} \approx 124.6$ N·m/rad.

Now apply the torsion formula:

$$G = \frac{T \cdot L}{J \cdot \theta} = \frac{L}{J} \cdot \frac{T}{\theta} = \frac{0.080}{4.97 \times 10^{-9}} \times 124.6$$

$$\boxed{G \approx 2.005 \times 10^{9} \, \text{Pa} = 2.005 \, \text{GPa}}$$

This value is consistent with a **rigid amorphous thermoplastic** such as PC or rigid PVC at room temperature.

**Step (iii) — Maximum Shear Stress at $T = 6.0$ N·m**

Using $\tau_{max} = 16 T / (\pi d^{3})$:

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{16 \times 6.0}{\pi (0.015)^{3}} = \frac{96.0}{\pi \times 3.375 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{max} \approx 9.05 \, \text{MPa}}$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Shear Test to Choose?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This matrix appears frequently in KTU Part B questions. Memorize it.

| Material Form | Recommended Shear Test | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Unidirectional fibre composite | Iosipescu (ASTM D5379) | Produces pure, uniform shear; measures $G_{12}$ accurately |
| Thick composite laminate | V-notched rail shear (ASTM D7078) | Larger pure-shear zone, less scatter |
| Quality control of composite | Short-beam shear (ASTM D2344) | Simple, fast, good for QC; not for modulus |
| Solid polymer rod (thermoplastic) | Torsion (ASTM D1043) | Measures $G$, $G'$, $G''$, $\tan\delta$ vs. temperature |
| Elastomer / rubber | Torsion pendulum or DMA | Captures viscoelastic response |
| Adhesive bond between substrates | Lap shear (ASTM D1002) | Directly measures bond shear strength |
| Polymer melt | Capillary rheometer (shear rate sweep) | Provides viscosity $\eta(\dot\gamma)$ |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — Conceptual

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define **shear modulus** of a polymer. How is it related to Young's modulus and Poisson's ratio for an isotropic polymer?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Shear Modulus** $G$ is defined as the ratio of shear stress $\tau$ to the corresponding shear strain $\gamma$ within the linear elastic limit of the polymer:

$$G = \frac{\tau}{\gamma}$$

It has units of **Pa** or **MPa** and represents the polymer's resistance to shape distortion under tangential loading.

For an isotropic, homogeneous, linearly elastic polymer, the three elastic constants are related by:

$$G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}$$

where $E$ is Young's modulus and $\nu$ is Poisson's ratio. This relation shows that only **two** of the three constants are independent; once $E$ and $\nu$ are measured in a tensile test, $G$ can be predicted without a separate shear test.

**(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for explanation of independence.)**

---

### Question A2 — Conceptual

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** List any **three standard test methods** used to determine the shear properties of polymers. Mention the relevant ASTM standard for each.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| # | Test Method | ASTM Standard |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Iosipescu Shear Test | ASTM D5379 |
| 2 | Short-Beam Shear Test | ASTM D2344 |
| 3 | Torsion of Solid Rod | ASTM D1043 |

*(Alternative valid choices: V-notched rail shear ASTM D7078; Lap shear ASTM D1002.)*

**(1 mark each for correctly pairing method and standard.)**

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question B (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]** Answer **ANY ONE** of the following:

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the relation $G = E / [2(1+\nu)]$ for an isotropic linearly elastic polymer, starting from the general form of Hooke's law. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A cylindrical polymer rod of diameter 12 mm and gauge length 50 mm is subjected to a torsion test. In the linear elastic range, a torque of 2.0 N·m produces a twist of 0.030 rad. Compute:
  - (i) the polar moment of inertia of the rod
  - (ii) the shear modulus $G$
  - (iii) the maximum shear stress at the rod surface for this torque. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks)**

1. Apply pure shear $\tau_{xy}$ on the faces of a polymer cube. **(0.5 mark)**
2. By Mohr's circle, principal stresses are $\sigma_1 = +\tau_{xy}$, $\sigma_2 = -\tau_{xy}$, $\sigma_3 = 0$. **(1 mark)**
3. Use Hooke's law for isotropic elastic solid: $\varepsilon_1 = (1/E)[\sigma_1 - \nu(\sigma_2 + \sigma_3)]$. **(1 mark)**
4. Substitute principal stresses:

$$\varepsilon_1 = \frac{\tau_{xy}}{E}(1+\nu), \quad \varepsilon_2 = -\frac{\tau_{xy}}{E}(1+\nu)$$

**(1.5 marks)**

5. Engineering shear strain is the difference:

$$\gamma_{xy} = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2 = \frac{2\tau_{xy}(1+\nu)}{E}$$

**(1.5 marks)**

6. Equate with Hooke's law in shear, $\gamma_{xy} = \tau_{xy}/G$, and solve:

$$\boxed{G = \frac{E}{2(1+\nu)}}$$

**(1.5 marks)**

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)**

**Step (i) — Polar moment of inertia**

Given $d = 0.012$ m:

$$J = \frac{\pi d^{4}}{32} = \frac{\pi (0.012)^{4}}{32} = \frac{\pi \times 2.0736 \times 10^{-8}}{32}$$

$$\boxed{J = 2.036 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{m}^{4}}$$

**(2 marks)**

**Step (ii) — Shear modulus**

Given $T = 2.0$ N·m, $L = 0.050$ m, $\theta = 0.030$ rad:

$$G = \frac{T L}{J \theta} = \frac{2.0 \times 0.050}{2.036 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.030}$$

$$\boxed{G \approx 1.637 \times 10^{9} \, \text{Pa} = 1.637 \, \text{GPa}}$$

**(3 marks)**

**Step (iii) — Maximum shear stress**

$$\tau_{max} = \frac{16 T}{\pi d^{3}} = \frac{16 \times 2.0}{\pi (0.012)^{3}} = \frac{32.0}{\pi \times 1.728 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$\boxed{\tau_{max} \approx 5.894 \, \text{MPa}}$$

**(2 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**(a)** With the help of a neat labelled diagram, explain the **Iosipescu shear test** for polymer composites. Mention the ASTM standard, specimen geometry, and the role of the V-notches. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is the **von Mises shear yield criterion**? Show that for a uniaxial tensile test on an isotropic polymer with tensile yield stress $\sigma_y$, the corresponding shear yield stress is $\tau_y = \sigma_y / \sqrt{3}$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Iosipescu Shear Test (7 Marks)**

1. **Standard:** ASTM D5379. **(0.5 mark)**
2. **Specimen:** Rectangular coupon (typically 76 mm × 19 mm × 4 mm) with **two symmetric V-notches** machined on opposite faces, midway along the length. The notches create a defined cross-section in the middle where shear is concentrated. **(2 marks)**
3. **Fixture and Loading:** A special fixture grips the specimen at both ends. The upper grip moves **upward** and the lower grip moves **downward**, applying a **couple of equal and opposite forces** that pass through the notches. **(2 marks)**
4. **Resulting Stress State:** Between the two V-notches, the bending moments cancel and a region of **pure, uniform shear** is produced. **(1 mark)**
5. **Strain Measurement:** A biaxial strain gauge mounted at $\pm 45^\circ$ to the loading axis measures shear strain directly. The shear stress is computed from the applied load and the cross-sectional area at the notch. **(1 mark)**
6. **Outputs:** Shear modulus $G_{12}$, shear strength $\tau_{12u}$, and full shear stress–strain curve. **(0.5 mark)**

**Part (b) — von Mises Criterion (7 Marks)**

1. **Statement:** The **von Mises yield criterion** states that yielding begins when the **second deviatoric stress invariant** $J_2$ reaches a critical value. In terms of principal stresses: **(1 mark)**

$$\frac{1}{2}\left[ (\sigma_1 - \sigma_2)^{2} + (\sigma_2 - \sigma_3)^{2} + (\sigma_3 - \sigma_1)^{2} \right] = \sigma_y^{2}$$

where $\sigma_y$ is the tensile yield stress in a uniaxial test.

2. **Pure Shear Substitution:** In a pure shear test, the principal stresses are $\sigma_1 = +\tau$, $\sigma_2 = -\tau$, $\sigma_3 = 0$. **(1 mark)**

3. Substitute into von Mises equation:

$$\frac{1}{2}\left[ (\tau - (-\tau))^{2} + ((-\tau) - 0)^{2} + (0 - \tau)^{2} \right] = \sigma_y^{2}$$

**(2 marks)**

4. Simplify each term:

$$(2\tau)^{2} = 4\tau^{2}, \quad \tau^{2}, \quad \tau^{2}$$

5. Sum:

$$\frac{1}{2}\left[ 4\tau^{2} + \tau^{2} + \tau^{2} \right] = \sigma_y^{2}$$

$$\frac{1}{2} \times 6\tau^{2} = \sigma_y^{2}$$

$$3\tau^{2} = \sigma_y^{2}$$

**(2 marks)**

6. Solve for $\tau_y$:

$$\boxed{\tau_y = \frac{\sigma_y}{\sqrt{3}}}$$

**(1 mark)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks on This Question:**
> - **Failure to convert all units to SI before substitution.** Diameter must be in **metres**, not millimetres. Marks deducted for unit inconsistency.
> - **Forgetting to use the *engineering* shear strain definition** $\gamma = \varepsilon_1 - \varepsilon_2$ in the derivation. Many students incorrectly write $\gamma = \varepsilon_1$ alone.
> - **Omitting the polar moment of inertia formula** in the torsion numerical. The examiner expects explicit substitution into $J = \pi d^{4}/32$.
> - **Confusing shear modulus $G$ with Young's modulus $E$** in the final answer. Always state $G$ explicitly with its unit.
> - **In the von Mises derivation, omitting the factor of $\tfrac{1}{2}$** in the criterion statement. The KTU key has a dedicated 1 mark for stating the criterion correctly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision bullets — print this and read before entering the exam hall.**

- **Shear stress** $\tau = F / A$ — force parallel to surface, divided by area of that surface.
- **Shear strain** $\gamma$ is dimensionless (in radians); for small deformations $\gamma \approx \tan\theta$.
- **Shear modulus** $G$ is the slope of the linear elastic region of the $\tau$-$\gamma$ curve.
- **Isotropic relation:** $G = E / [2(1+\nu)]$ — only two of $E$, $G$, $\nu$ are independent.
- **Torsion test formulas:** $\tau_{max} = 16T / (\pi d^{3})$; $\theta = TL / (GJ)$; $G = TL / (J\theta)$.
- **Polar moment of inertia** for a solid circular shaft: $J = \pi d^{4} / 32$.
- **von Mises shear yield:** $\tau_y = \sigma_y / \sqrt{3}$.
- **ASTM D5379 (Iosipescu)** is the gold standard for **in-plane shear** of fibre composites.
- **ASTM D2344 (short-beam shear)** is the industry **quality-control** test (not for modulus).
- **ASTM D1043 (torsion)** measures the **complex shear modulus** $G^* = G' + iG''$ and **loss tangent** $\tan\delta$ vs. temperature.
- **Dynamic moduli:** $G' = (\tau_0 / \gamma_0)\cos\delta$, $G'' = (\tau_0 / \gamma_0)\sin\delta$, $\tan\delta = G''/G'$.
- **Lap shear (ASTM D1002)** is for adhesive bonds, not bulk polymer.
- **Glass-transition temperature ($T_g$)** is where $G$ drops by **3–4 orders of magnitude** for amorphous polymers.
- **Rubbery plateau** modulus $G \approx \rho R T / M_c$ (from rubber elasticity theory) — relevant for elastomers.
- Always **draw a labelled schematic** in long-answer questions — examiners award 1–2 marks even before reading the math.
- Always **state units explicitly** for every computed quantity; missing units = lost marks.
- **Tip:** If a question gives only $E$ and $\nu$, use $G = E / [2(1+\nu)]$ to find $G$. If a question gives only $T$, $L$, $d$, $\theta$, use the torsion formulas directly.

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Impact Resistance (Definition):**
> Impact resistance is defined as the ability of a polymeric material to absorb mechanical energy, undergo rapid plastic deformation, and resist fracture when subjected to a sudden, high-velocity loading condition (strain rate typically $10^2$ to $10^4 \ \text{s}^{-1}$). It is quantified as the **impact strength** — the total energy dissipated per unit cross-sectional area or per unit width of the specimen prior to fracture, measured in standard pendulum or falling-weight impact tests conducted under controlled temperature and humidity.

In KTU-syllabus language, the term is often interchangeably referred to as **impact strength**, **notch impact strength**, or **toughness under dynamic loading**. It is a fundamental **fracture-mechanical property** that characterizes the energy-absorption capacity of polymers under shock/impulse conditions.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Hanging Coconut vs. The Cracking Glass":**
> Imagine you drop a ripe coconut (≈ **1.5 kg**) from a height of **1 m** onto two surfaces: (1) a hardened glass tile, and (2) a thick rubber mat. The glass shatters instantly because it cannot absorb or dissipate the sudden kinetic energy — the impact is concentrated at a single point and the material responds in a **brittle** manner. The rubber mat, however, deforms, bends, and converts most of that kinetic energy into heat and elastic recovery — it behaves in a **tough, ductile** manner.
>
> **A polymer's "impact resistance" is exactly this "rubber-mat behavior" — its capacity to soak up the sudden energy of a hammer-strike or falling object without cracking.** Polymers lie between perfectly elastic (rubbers) and perfectly brittle (glassy ceramics), and the impact test quantifies *where* a given polymer falls on this spectrum.

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics (Highlighted in Bold)

- **Strain Rate:** $10^2$ – $10^4 \ \text{s}^{-1}$ (compared to quasi-static tensile: $10^{-3}$ – $10^{-2} \ \text{s}^{-1}$)
- **Velocity of Striker (Izod/Charpy):** Typically **2.9 – 3.5 m/s**
- **Standard Test Temperatures:** **23 °C** (ambient) and sub-ambient (−40 °C) for cold-impact evaluation
- **Units:**
    - **Izod:** J/m (joules per meter of notch)
    - **Charpy:** kJ/m² (kilojoules per square meter of cross-section)
    - **Falling-Weight/Dart:** J (total energy) or g (mass) × m (drop height)

---

### 1.4 Classification of Impact Tests (KTU High-Yield Overview)

| Test Family | Specimen Geometry | Loading Mode | Common Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pendulum Impact (Notched)** | Cantilever (Izod) / Simply-Supported (Charpy) | Flexural impact | **ASTM D256, ISO 180, ISO 179** |
| **Tensile Impact** | Dog-bone, high-velocity tensile | Tensile shock | **ASTM D1822** |
| **Falling-Weight (Burst)** | Flat disc/plate | Through-thickness impact | **ASTM D5420 (Gardner), D3029** |
| **Dart Drop** | Flat sheet | Drop-weight on sheet | **ASTM D1709, ISO 7765** |
| **Instrumented Impact** | Any of the above | Load–deflection recorded | **ASTM D3763, D7136** |
