| 3 | Rebound Resilience (pendulum) | $R$ | $R = \dfrac{h_2}{h_1} \times 100\,\%$ | dimensionless | $h_1$ = drop height, $h_2$ = rebound height |
| 4 | Rebound Resilience (energy form) | $R$ | $R = \dfrac{E_{\text{recovered}}}{E_{\text{input}}} = \dfrac{m g h_2}{m g h_1}$ | dimensionless | After mass cancels |
| 5 | Hysteresis Loss | $H_L$ | $H_L = \dfrac{U_{\text{input}} - U_{\text{recovered}}}{U_{\text{input}}} = 1 - R$ | dimensionless | Energy dissipated per cycle |
| 6 | Shear Resilience | $U_{r,s}$ | $U_{r,s} = \dfrac{\tau_y^{\,2}}{2G}$ | $\text{J/m}^3$ | $G$ = shear modulus |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** High resilience is critical in **tyre tread compounds**, **vibration dampers**, **sport shoe soles**, and **bouncy balls**. Low resilience is desired in **gaskets**, **anti-vibration mounts**, and **shock absorbers** where energy dissipation is preferred.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Significance

In the polymer industry, resilience testing determines the suitability of an elastomer for **dynamic applications**. A tyre tread rubber must possess high resilience to provide rolling efficiency, while a **mounting bushing** must exhibit low resilience to absorb vibrational shocks. KTU board questions frequently target this contrast.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Modulus of Resilience for a Linearly Elastic Polymer

**Given:**
- Hooke's Law: $\sigma = E \cdot \epsilon$
- Strain energy per unit volume: $U = \displaystyle\int \sigma\, d\epsilon$

**Step 1 — Write the general strain energy expression**

$$
U_r \;=\; \int_{0}^{\epsilon_y} \sigma(\epsilon)\, d\epsilon
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Hooke's Law into the integrand**

$$
U_r \;=\; \int_{0}^{\epsilon_y} (E \epsilon)\, d\epsilon
$$

**Step 3 — Factor out the constant $E$**

$$
U_r \;=\; E \int_{0}^{\epsilon_y} \epsilon\, d\epsilon
$$

**Step 4 — Perform the integration of $\epsilon$**

$$
U_r \;=\; E \left[ \dfrac{\epsilon^{\,2}}{2} \right]_{0}^{\epsilon_y}
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the limits**

$$
U_r \;=\; E \left( \dfrac{\epsilon_y^{\,2}}{2} - \dfrac{0^{\,2}}{2} \right) \;=\; \dfrac{E\,\epsilon_y^{\,2}}{2}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert from strain form to stress form using $\epsilon_y = \dfrac{\sigma_y}{E}$**

$$
U_r \;=\; \dfrac{E}{2} \left( \dfrac{\sigma_y}{E} \right)^{\!2}
$$

**Step 7 — Simplify the squared term**

$$
U_r \;=\; \dfrac{E}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\sigma_y^{\,2}}{E^{\,2}}
$$

**Step 8 — Final reduced form**

$$
\boxed{\,U_r \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_y^{\,2}}{2E}\,}
$$

This shows that resilience is directly proportional to the **square of the yield stress** and inversely proportional to the **stiffness (Young's Modulus)**. A soft, easily-yielded elastomer can therefore exhibit very high modulus of resilience.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Rebound Resilience from a Pendulum Drop

**Given:**
- A pendulum of mass $m$ is released from height $h_1$ above a polymer specimen.
- After impact, it rebounds to height $h_2$.
- Gravitational acceleration $= g$.

**Step 1 — Initial Potential Energy (input energy)**

$$
E_{\text{input}} \;=\; m g h_1
$$

**Step 2 — Final Potential Energy (recovered energy) after rebound**

$$
E_{\text{recovered}} \;=\; m g h_2
$$

**Step 3 — Definition of rebound resilience as the ratio of recovered to input energy**

$$
R \;=\; \dfrac{E_{\text{recovered}}}{E_{\text{input}}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the two potential energy expressions**

$$
R \;=\; \dfrac{m g h_2}{m g h_1}
$$

**Step 5 — Cancel the common factors $m$ and $g$**

$$
R \;=\; \dfrac{h_2}{h_1}
$$

**Step 6 — Express as a percentage (standard convention in polymer testing)**

$$
\boxed{\,R \,\% \;=\; \dfrac{h_2}{h_1} \times 100\,}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the hysteresis loss (energy dissipated as heat / sound)**

$$
H_L \;=\; 1 - R
$$

---

### 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A natural rubber sample is tested on a rebound pendulum. The striker is released from a height $h_1 = 0.50 \text{ m}$ and rebounds to a height $h_2 = 0.40 \text{ m}$. If the yield stress of the rubber is $\sigma_y = 2.5 \text{ MPa}$ and its Young's Modulus is $E = 4.0 \text{ MPa}$, calculate (a) the rebound resilience in percent, and (b) the modulus of resilience.

**Solution (a):**

$$
R \,\% \;=\; \dfrac{0.40}{0.50} \times 100 \;=\; 80\,\%
$$

**Solution (b):**

$$
U_r \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_y^{\,2}}{2E} \;=\; \dfrac{(2.5 \times 10^{6})^{2}}{2 \times 4.0 \times 10^{6}}
$$

**Evaluating the numerator:**

$$
\sigma_y^{\,2} \;=\; (2.5 \times 10^{6})^{2} \;=\; 6.25 \times 10^{12} \;\text{Pa}^{2}
$$

**Evaluating the denominator:**

$$
2E \;=\; 2 \times 4.0 \times 10^{6} \;=\; 8.0 \times 10^{6} \;\text{Pa}
$$

**Final division:**

$$
U_r \;=\; \dfrac{6.25 \times 10^{12}}{8.0 \times 10^{6}} \;=\; 7.8125 \times 10^{5} \;\text{J/m}^{3}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,U_r \;\approx\; 7.81 \times 10^{5} \;\text{J/m}^{3}\,}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Energy Flow During a Rebound Resilience Test

The following Mermaid block maps the energy transformations during a pendulum impact test on a polymer specimen.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initial Height h1] -->|Gravitational PE = m g h1| B[Potential Energy Stored]
    B -->|Striker Released| C[Kinetic Energy at Impact]
    C -->|Impact on Polymer| D[Energy Partition at Specimen]
    D --> E[Elastic Strain Energy Ue Stored in Polymer]
    D --> F[Hysteresis Loss H as Heat and Sound]
    E -->|Polymer Recovers| G[Striker Rebound KE]
    G -->|Converted to PE| H[Final Rebound Height h2]
    F --> I[Dissipated to Surroundings]
    H --> J[Resilience R = h2 / h1 x 100 percent]
    I --> J
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Rebound Resilience Tester

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph T1[Test Chamber Assembly]
        S1[Clamping Jig for Polymer Disc]
        S2[Anvil Base]
        S1 --> S2
    end
    subgraph T2[Striker Assembly]
        P1[Pendulum Arm with Calibrated Mass]
        P2[Release Latch at Height h1]
        P3[Rebound Indicator Scale]
        P2 --> P1
        P1 --> P3
    end
    subgraph T3[Data Computation Unit]
        D1[Height Sensor Optical Encoder]
        D2[Microcontroller for h2 Calculation]
        D3[Digital Display of R percent]
        D1 --> D2
        D2 --> D3
    end
    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> T1
    P3 --> D1
```

### 4.3 Comparison Matrix — High vs Low Resilience Polymers

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    N1[Polymer Type Selection] --> N2{Need Energy Recovery?}
    N2 -->|Yes - High Resilience| P1[Natural Rubber NR]
    N2 -->|Yes - High Resilience| P2[Polyurethane PU]
    N2 -->|Yes - High Resilience| P3[Silicone Rubber]
    N2 -->|No - Energy Dissipation| Q1[Butyl Rubber IIR]
    N2 -->|No - Energy Dissipation| Q2[Neoprene CR]
    N2 -->|No - Energy Dissipation| Q3[Plasticized PVC]
    P1 --> R1[Tyre Tread, Bouncy Balls]
    P2 --> R2[Roller Wheels, Seals]
    P3 --> R3[Medical Implants, Keypads]
    Q1 --> S1[Vibration Dampers, Inner Tubes]
    Q2 --> S2[Mounting Bushings, Hoses]
    Q3 --> S3[Soft Grips, Cable Sheathing]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define resilience. Distinguish between tensile resilience and rebound resilience.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1.5 marks):** Resilience is the ability of a material to absorb energy when deformed elastically and to release that energy upon unloading. Quantitatively, it is the strain energy per unit volume recovered from a material loaded up to its elastic limit.
- **Tensile Resilience (0.75 mark):** Determined statically from the area under the linear (elastic) portion of the tensile stress–strain curve; calculated as $U_r = \sigma_y^{\,2} / 2E$.
- **Rebound Resilience (0.75 mark):** Determined dynamically by measuring the height reached by a pendulum striker after impacting a polymer specimen; calculated as $R = (h_2 / h_1) \times 100\,\%$.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the relationship between resilience and hysteresis loss in a polymer.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Hysteresis loss** is the fraction of input energy dissipated as heat and sound during one loading–unloading cycle due to viscoelastic damping in the polymer (1 mark).
- **Mathematical relationship (1.5 marks):**

$$
H_L \;=\; 1 - R
$$

where $R$ is the rebound resilience expressed as a decimal fraction. A perfectly elastic polymer would have $R = 1$ and $H_L = 0$, while a perfectly plastic polymer would have $R = 0$ and $H_L = 1$.

- **Significance (0.5 mark):** High hysteresis loss means low resilience, which is desirable in damping applications but undesirable in bouncing or rolling applications.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** Answer **any ONE** of the following two full questions (each carries 14 marks and is internally optional per KTU ESE pattern).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Derive an expression for the **modulus of resilience** of a linearly elastic polymer in terms of yield stress and Young's Modulus. State clearly the assumptions used in the derivation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vulcanized rubber specimen has a yield stress of $\sigma_y = 3.0 \text{ MPa}$ and Young's Modulus $E = 5.0 \text{ MPa}$. A pendulum striker of mass $0.250 \text{ kg}$ is released from a height of $40 \text{ cm}$ and rebounds to a height of $32 \text{ cm}$. Calculate:
- (i) The rebound resilience as a percentage. **(2 marks)**
- (ii) The modulus of resilience. **(2 marks)**
- (iii) The energy lost as hysteresis per impact. **(3 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):**

*Assumptions:*
1. Polymer behaves as a **linearly elastic** material within the elastic limit (obeys Hooke's Law).
2. Loading is **quasi-static** (no rate-dependent effects).
3. No **viscoelastic or plastic losses** occur in the elastic region.

*Derivation:*

Strain energy per unit volume is the area under the stress–strain curve up to the elastic limit:

$$
U_r \;=\; \int_{0}^{\epsilon_y} \sigma\, d\epsilon
$$

Substituting Hooke's Law $\sigma = E \epsilon$:

$$
U_r \;=\; \int_{0}^{\epsilon_y} E \epsilon\, d\epsilon \;=\; E \left[ \dfrac{\epsilon^{\,2}}{2} \right]_{0}^{\epsilon_y}
$$

Evaluating limits:

$$
U_r \;=\; \dfrac{E \epsilon_y^{\,2}}{2}
$$

Using $\epsilon_y = \sigma_y / E$:

$$
U_r \;=\; \dfrac{E}{2} \left( \dfrac{\sigma_y}{E} \right)^{\!2} \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_y^{\,2}}{2E}
$$

$$
\boxed{\,U_r \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_y^{\,2}}{2E}\,}
$$

This represents the energy stored per unit volume in the elastic region and is fully recoverable upon unloading.

**Part (b) — Numerical Solutions (7 marks):**

**(i) Rebound resilience (2 marks):**

$$
R \,\% \;=\; \dfrac{h_2}{h_1} \times 100 \;=\; \dfrac{32}{40} \times 100 \;=\; 80\,\%
$$

**(ii) Modulus of resilience (2 marks):**

$$
U_r \;=\; \dfrac{\sigma_y^{\,2}}{2E} \;=\; \dfrac{(3.0 \times 10^{6})^{2}}{2 \times 5.0 \times 10^{6}}
$$

Numerator:

$$
\sigma_y^{\,2} \;=\; 9.0 \times 10^{12} \;\text{Pa}^{2}
$$

Denominator:

$$
2E \;=\; 1.0 \times 10^{7} \;\text{Pa}
$$

$$
U_r \;=\; \dfrac{9.0 \times 10^{12}}{1.0 \times 10^{7}} \;=\; 9.0 \times 10^{5} \;\text{J/m}^{3}
$$

**(iii) Hysteresis energy per impact (3 marks):**

Input energy from initial drop:

$$
E_{\text{input}} \;=\; m g h_1 \;=\; 0.250 \times 9.81 \times 0.40
$$

$$
E_{\text{input}} \;=\; 0.981 \;\text{J}
$$

Recovered energy from rebound:

$$
E_{\text{recovered}} \;=\; m g h_2 \;=\; 0.250 \times 9.81 \times 0.32 \;=\; 0.7848 \;\text{J}
$$

Hysteresis loss per impact:

$$
E_{\text{lost}} \;=\; E_{\text{input}} - E_{\text{recovered}} \;=\; 0.981 - 0.7848
$$

$$
\boxed{\,E_{\text{lost}} \;=\; 0.1962 \;\text{J} \;\approx\; 0.196 \;\text{J}\,}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With the help of a **labeled stress–strain diagram**, explain the graphical meaning of:
- (i) Modulus of resilience
- (ii) Modulus of toughness
- (iii) Hysteresis loop

Clearly state the difference in physical meaning between resilience and toughness. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **Rebound Resilience Test (Pendulum Method)** as per KTU/IS standards. List the factors that influence the rebound resilience of a polymer. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

**Part (a) — Graphical Interpretation (7 marks):**

**Stress-Strain Diagram Description (2 marks):**

The diagram shows stress ($\sigma$) on the y-axis and strain ($\epsilon$) on the x-axis. The curve starts at the origin, rises linearly to the **proportional limit (A)**, continues non-linearly to the **yield point (B)**, and finally to the **fracture point (C)**. A separate **unloading curve** is drawn from a point within the elastic region back to the strain axis, while a **hysteresis loop** is shown when a cycle is completed.

**Modulus of Resilience — Graphical Meaning (1.5 marks):**

The **area under the linear elastic portion** of the stress–strain curve (region O–A) represents the modulus of resilience, $U_r = \sigma_y^{\,2} / 2E$. This is the recoverable strain energy per unit volume stored in the material up to the elastic limit.

**Modulus of Toughness — Graphical Meaning (1.5 marks):**

The **total area under the entire stress–strain curve** (region O–A–B–C) represents the modulus of toughness, $U_t = \displaystyle\int_0^{\epsilon_f} \sigma\, d\epsilon$. This includes both elastic and plastic deformation energy.

**Hysteresis Loop — Graphical Meaning (1 mark):**

When a polymer is loaded and then unloaded, the **unloading path lies below the loading path**, forming a closed loop. The enclosed area equals the **energy lost** as heat per loading cycle.

**Difference between Resilience and Toughness (1 mark):**

| **Resilience** | **Toughness** |
|---|---|
| Recoverable energy (elastic only) | Total energy to fracture (elastic + plastic) |
| Area under **linear elastic** region | Area under **entire** stress–strain curve |
| High for elastomers | High for tough plastics |

**Part (b) — Rebound Resilience Test (7 marks):**

**Principle (1.5 marks):**

A pendulum of known mass is released from a fixed height $h_1$ above a clamped polymer specimen. Upon impact, the specimen deforms elastically and returns energy to the striker, which rebounds to a height $h_2$. The ratio $h_2 / h_1$ (expressed as a percentage) is the **rebound resilience**.

**Apparatus (2 marks):**

1. **Pendulum striker** with calibrated mass and a sharp striking edge.
2. **Clamping jig** to hold the polymer disc firmly.
3. **Graduated scale** to read the drop and rebound heights.
4. **Release mechanism** at a precise drop height.
5. **Anvil/base plate** to support the specimen.

**Procedure (2 marks):**

1. Cut a polymer disc (typically 12–14 mm thick, 35–40 mm diameter) per **IS 3400 Part XI**.
2. Clamp firmly in the jig at a controlled temperature (usually 23 °C or 27 °C).
3. Release the pendulum from a known height $h_1$.
4. Record the rebound height $h_2$.
5. Calculate $R \,\% = (h_2 / h_1) \times 100$.
6. Repeat 3 times and report the mean.

**Factors Influencing Rebound Resilience (1.5 marks):**

- **Temperature** — Higher temperature increases chain mobility and resilience.
- **Hardness / Crosslink density** — Higher crosslink density increases resilience up to an optimum, then decreases.
- **Filler content** — Carbon black and other fillers reduce resilience.
- **Plasticizers** — Generally reduce resilience.
- **Strain rate / impact velocity** — Higher impact velocity reduces observed resilience.
- **Polymer composition** — NR is highly resilient; IIR has low resilience.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Resilience Problems:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch (–1 mark):** Students frequently forget to convert MPa to Pa when computing $U_r = \sigma_y^{\,2}/2E$. Always express $\sigma_y$ and $E$ in identical base SI units (Pa) before squaring.
> 2. **Confusing resilience with toughness (–1 mark):** Resilience corresponds only to the *elastic* area; toughness covers the *entire* stress–strain region. Examiners specifically check this distinction.
> 3. **Forgetting the percent conversion (–0.5 mark):** When reporting rebound resilience, multiply the decimal ratio by 100. Reporting $R = 0.80$ instead of $R = 80\,\%$ is considered incomplete.
> 4. **Skipping assumptions (–0.5 mark):** Derivations of $U_r$ must explicitly state the assumption of **linear elasticity (Hooke's Law)**. Marks are deducted if not mentioned.
> 5. **Hysteresis formula sign error (–0.5 mark):** $H_L = 1 - R$ — students often invert this. Remember: as resilience rises, hysteresis loss falls.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Resilience in Polymers**

- **Resilience** = recoverable elastic strain energy per unit volume, measured in **J/m³** or **Pa**.
- **Modulus of Resilience (linear elastic case):** $U_r = \sigma_y^{\,2} / 2E$.
- **General form:** $U_r = \displaystyle\int_0^{\epsilon_y} \sigma\, d\epsilon$ (area under elastic region of stress–strain curve).
- **Rebound Resilience:** $R \,\% = (h_2 / h_1) \times 100$, where $h_1$ = drop height, $h_2$ = rebound height.
- **Hysteresis Loss:** $H_L = 1 - R$ (fraction of energy dissipated as heat/sound).
- **Toughness $\neq$ Resilience** — toughness includes plastic deformation, resilience does not.
- **Natural rubber** has the highest resilience (~70–85 %); **butyl rubber** has the lowest (~10 %).
- **Higher temperature** and **lower filler content** generally increase rebound resilience.
- **Standard test methods:** IS 3400 Part XI (India), ASTM D7121 (pendulum rebound), ISO 4662.
- **Energy balance:** $E_{\text{input}} = E_{\text{recovered}} + E_{\text{lost}}$.
- **Applications of high resilience:** tyre treads, bouncy balls, shoe soles, conveyor belts.
- **Applications of low resilience:** anti-vibration mounts, gaskets, damping pads, inner tubes.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat Build-up
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT602 - POLYMER TESTING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Heat Build-up ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Heat Build-up** is the phenomenon of progressive temperature rise inside a polymeric material (especially elastomers and rubber compounds) when it is subjected to **repeated cyclic mechanical deformation** under load. The internal temperature increase arises from the mechanical energy that is **not fully recovered** during each loading–unloading cycle, i.e., it is dissipated as **thermal energy** through the viscoelastic **hysteresis loss** of the polymer. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology (PCPOT602 – Polymer Testing), heat build-up is classified as a **dynamic fatigue / viscoelastic dissipation property** and is universally measured using a **Flexometer** (Goodrich-type, Firestone-type, or Vospel-type) under standardized compressive loading and stroke conditions, as per **ASTM D623** and **ISO 4666**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The Wire-Bending Experiment** > Take a soft metal wire (like a copper paperclip) and bend it back and forth rapidly ten times. Within seconds, the bend-point becomes **noticeably hot** to the touch. Why? Because every flex converts a fraction of your mechanical work into heat through internal friction at the microstructural level. Now replace the metal wire with a **rubber block** and bend it cyclically 1,800 times per minute for 20 minutes — the rubber interior can climb to **80 °C – 150 °C** even though the surroundings are at room temperature. **That internal temperature rise is the heat build-up.** In tires, this is precisely why truck tyres running long-haul get hot, leading to blow-outs and tread separation. ### 1.3 Why Heat Build-up Matters in Polymer Engineering - **Service-life prediction** of rubber components (tyres, conveyor belts, engine mounts, bridge bearings). - **Compound formulation** ranking — a high heat build-up compound is unsuitable for dynamic applications. - **Quality control** of carbon-black-filled, silica-reinforced, or oil-extended elastomers. - **Failure diagnosis** — heat build-up is the precursor to **thermal runaway, blow-out, reversion, and fatigue cracking**. > [!TIP] > **Standard Reference Metrics (must memorise for KTU viva):** > - **ΔT (Temperature Rise)**: reported in **°C**, typically measured by a thermocouple embedded at the geometric centre of the specimen. > - **Load**: standard values are **24.5 kgf**, **44.5 kgf**, or **54.5 kgf** (ASTM D623 Method A). > - **Stroke**: **4.45 mm (0.175 in)** or **6.35 mm (0.25 in)** depending on specimen geometry. > - **Frequency**: **1,800 cycles/min (30 Hz)** unless otherwise specified. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Mechanism of Heat Build-up — Step-by-Step Logic When a viscoelastic polymer is subjected to a sinusoidal cyclic strain $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 \sin(\omega t)$, the resulting stress leads the strain by a phase angle $\delta$ (the **loss angle**). This phase lag is the root cause of every energy-dissipation phenomenon in polymers. **Step 1 — Viscoelastic Stress Response** $$ \sigma(t) = \sigma_0 \sin(\omega t + \delta) $$ **Step 2 — Storage and Loss Modulus Decomposition** $$ \sigma(t) = \varepsilon_0 \left[ E' \sin(\omega t) + E'' \cos(\omega t) \right] $$ where: - $E'$ = **Storage modulus** (elastic, recoverable part) — *unit: MPa* - $E''$ = **Loss modulus** (viscous, dissipated part) — *unit: MPa* - $\tan\delta = \dfrac{E''}{E'}$ = **Loss tangent** (dimensionless) **Step 3 — Energy Dissipated per Cycle** The energy converted to heat in one complete cycle is the area of the **hysteresis loop** in the stress–strain diagram: $$ W_{diss} = \pi \cdot \varepsilon_0^{2} \cdot E'' $$ **Step 4 — Average Power Dissipated** $$ \dot{Q} = W_{diss} \cdot f = \pi \cdot \varepsilon_0^{2} \cdot E'' \cdot f $$ where $f$ = cyclic frequency in Hz. **Step 5 — Steady-State Temperature Rise (Energy Balance)** At thermal equilibrium, the rate of heat generation equals the rate of heat loss (conduction to grips + convection to air + radiation): $$ \dot{Q} = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{ss} + k \cdot A \cdot \frac{\Delta T_{ss}}{L} $$ Therefore, the steady-state **temperature rise** is: $$ \Delta T_{ss} = \frac{\pi \cdot \varepsilon_0^{2} \cdot E'' \cdot f}{h A + \dfrac{k A}{L}} $$ ### 2.2 Material Factors Influencing Heat Build-up | Factor | Effect on Heat Build-up | Engineering Insight | |---|---|---| | **Hysteresis (tan δ)** | ↑ tan δ → ↑ Heat build-up | NR has higher H.B. than BR; EPDM has low H.B. | | **Filler type** | Carbon black (small particle, high structure) increases H.B.; silica lowers it | Use **silica-reinforced SBR** for low rolling-resistance tyres | | **Crosslink density** | Optimum crosslink density minimises H.B. | Over-cured or under-cured rubber → higher H.B. | | **Plasticizers / Oils** | Aromatic oils increase; ester plasticizers decrease | Selection of extender oil is critical | | **Frequency & Strain** | Heat ∝ $f \cdot \varepsilon_0^{2}$ | Doubling frequency doubles heat | | **Thermal conductivity (k)** | Higher k → better heat dissipation → lower ΔT | Carbon black raises k slightly | | **Specific heat (Cp)** | Higher Cp → more thermal buffering → lower ΔT for same Q | | | **Ambient temperature** | Higher T_amb → lower ΔT (smaller gradient) | | | **Loading mode** | Compression > tension for the same Δε | | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E^{*} = E' + i E''$ | Complex dynamic modulus | MPa | | 2 | $\tan\delta = E''/E'$ | Loss tangent (hysteresis indicator) | dimensionless | | 3 | $W_{diss} = \pi \varepsilon_0^{2} E''$ | Energy dissipated per cycle | J/m³ | | 4 | $\dot{Q} = \pi \varepsilon_0^{2} E'' f$ | Average heat generation power | W/m³ | | 5 | $\Delta T_{ss} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}}{h A + kA/L}$ | Steady-state temperature rise | °C or K | | 6 | $\dot{Q} = m C_p \dfrac{dT}{dt}$ | Transient heat accumulation rate | W | | 7 | $t_{eq} = \dfrac{m C_p \Delta T_{ss}}{\dot{Q}}$ | Time to reach thermal equilibrium | s | | 8 | $\eta^{*} = E^{*}/(i\omega)$ | Complex dynamic viscosity | Pa·s | | 9 | $G' = \dfrac{E'}{2(1+\nu)}$ | Shear storage modulus (for elastomers, $\nu \approx 0.5$) | MPa | | 10 | $P_{loss} = 2\pi f \cdot \tau_0 \cdot \gamma_0 \cdot \sin\delta$ | Power loss per unit volume in shear | W/m³ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Boundary Conditions / Standard Test Conditions (KTU must-know):** > - ASTM D623 Method A — Goodrich Flexometer: load = 24.5 kgf, stroke = 4.45 mm, freq = 30 Hz, duration = 25 min > - ASTM D623 Method B — Firestone Flexometer: load = 11.4 kgf, stroke = 6.35 mm, freq = 24 Hz > - ISO 4666: cylindrical specimen Ø 17.8 ± 0.2 mm × 25 mm height > - Specimen pre-conditioning: 24 h at 23 ± 2 °C, 50 ± 5 % RH ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility 1. **Tyre Industry** — Heat build-up dictates the maximum service speed of a tyre (load-speed index). Under-inflated or overloaded tyres exhibit runaway heat build-up → **blow-out**. 2. **Anti-vibration Mounts** — Engine mounts must dissipate cyclic strain energy without softening. 3. **Conveyor Belts** — Long-haul mining belts run for years; low heat build-up compounds (e.g., NR/BR blends) are mandatory. 4. **Bridge Bearings** — Elastomeric bearings must withstand seismic cyclic loading with minimal ΔT. 5. **Sports Footwear** — Midsole EVA is engineered for low heat build-up to prevent discomfort. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — From Hysteresis Loop to ΔT We will derive the temperature rise inside a flexometer specimen by combining the **mechanical energy dissipation** with the **lumped-capacitance thermal model**. **Given:** - Cylindrical elastomer specimen of radius $R$ and height $L$. - Subjected to sinusoidal compressive strain $\varepsilon(t) = \varepsilon_0 \sin(\omega t)$. - Constant cyclic frequency $f = \omega/(2\pi)$ in Hz. - Loss modulus of the material is $E''$ at the test temperature and frequency. - Convective heat-transfer coefficient between specimen lateral surface and air = $h$ (W/m²·K). - Thermal conductivity of elastomer = $k$ (W/m·K). - Specific heat = $C_p$ (J/kg·K); density = $\rho$; mass $m = \rho \pi R^{2} L$. **Step 1 — Write the stress response for a viscoelastic solid** For a viscoelastic material under sinusoidal strain, the stress is phase-shifted by loss angle $\delta$: $$ \sigma(t) = E^{*} \cdot \varepsilon_0 \sin(\omega t) $$ Decompose into in-phase (storage) and quadrature (loss) components: $$ \sigma(t) = \varepsilon_0 E' \sin(\omega t) + \varepsilon_0 E'' \cos(\omega t) $$ **Step 2 — Compute instantaneous power density (rate of work done per unit volume)** Power density is the product of stress and strain-rate: $$ p(t) = \sigma(t) \cdot \dot{\varepsilon}(t) $$ Compute strain-rate first: $$ \dot{\varepsilon}(t) = \dfrac{d}{dt}\left[ \varepsilon_0 \sin(\omega t) \right] = \varepsilon_0 \omega \cos(\omega t) $$ Now multiply: $$ p(t) = \left[ \varepsilon_0 E' \sin(\omega t) + \varepsilon_0 E'' \cos(\omega t) \right] \cdot \left[ \varepsilon_0 \omega \cos(\omega t) \right] $$ Distribute the products: $$ p(t) = \varepsilon_0^{2} \omega \, E' \sin(\omega t)\cos(\omega t) + \varepsilon_0^{2} \omega \, E'' \cos^{2}(\omega t) $$ **Step 3 — Average the power density over one full cycle** Use the identity: $$ \langle \sin(\omega t)\cos(\omega t) \rangle = 0 $$ $$ \langle \cos^{2}(\omega t) \rangle = \dfrac{1}{2} $$ Therefore: $$ \langle p \rangle = \varepsilon_0^{2} \omega \, E'' \cdot \dfrac{1}{2} $$ Recognise $\omega = 2\pi f$: $$ \dot{Q}_{vol} = \pi \, \varepsilon_0^{2} \, E'' \, f $$ This is the **average power dissipated per unit volume** in W/m³. **Equation derived ✔** **Step 4 — Total heat generation rate inside the specimen** Multiply by specimen volume $V = \pi R^{2} L$: $$ \dot{Q}_{gen} = \pi \varepsilon_0^{2} E'' f \cdot (\pi R^{2} L) $$ **Step 5 — Energy balance (lumped capacitance)** Rate of temperature rise in specimen (neglecting internal gradients for lumped model): $$ \dot{Q}_{gen} - \dot{Q}_{loss} = m C_p \dfrac{dT}{dt} $$ Heat loss from lateral surface (convection) plus heat loss through end faces (conduction to grips): $$ \dot{Q}_{loss} = h (2\pi R L)(T - T_{\infty}) + \dfrac{k (\pi R^{2})}{L}(T - T_{\infty}) $$ Define an overall heat-loss coefficient $U$ with effective area: $$ \dot{Q}_{loss} = U \, A_{eff} (T - T_{\infty}) $$ **Step 6 — Solve for steady-state temperature** At steady state $dT/dt = 0$: $$ \dot{Q}_{gen} = U A_{eff} (T_{ss} - T_{\infty}) $$ Solve for $\Delta T_{ss}$: $$ \Delta T_{ss} = T_{ss} - T_{\infty} = \dfrac{\dot{Q}_{gen}}{U A_{eff}} $$ Substituting $\dot{Q}_{gen}$: $$ \Delta T_{ss} = \dfrac{\pi \varepsilon_0^{2} E'' f \cdot V}{U A_{eff}} $$ For a cylinder of radius $R$ and height $L$, $V/A_{eff}$ gives the characteristic length: $$ \Delta T_{ss} = \dfrac{\pi \varepsilon_0^{2} E'' f \cdot R}{2 h + k/L} $$ **Step 7 — Time to reach 63.2 % of ΔT_ss (thermal time constant)** From the lumped model: $$ \tau_{th} = \dfrac{m C_p}{U A_{eff}} = \dfrac{\rho \pi R^{2} L \, C_p}{U A_{eff}} $$ Solution of the first-order ODE: $$ \Delta T(t) = \Delta T_{ss} \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau_{th}} \right) $$ At $t = \tau_{th}$, $\Delta T = 0.632 \Delta T_{ss}$. > [!NOTE] > **Why this derivation matters for KTU 14-mark answers:** This derivation directly justifies why **lower $E''$** (i.e., low hysteresis compounds), **higher thermal conductivity**, and **smaller specimen size** all reduce heat build-up. Mentioning this derivation in a board exam immediately signals "Apply-level (Bloom Level 4)" thinking. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Practice) **Problem:** A natural-rubber cylinder ($R = 8.9$ mm, $L = 25$ mm) is tested in a Goodrich flexometer at $\varepsilon_0 = 0.20$, $f = 30$ Hz. Given $E'' = 1.50$ MPa, $h = 15$ W/m²·K, $k = 0.18$ W/m·K. Calculate the steady-state temperature rise. **Solution:** $$ \Delta T_{ss} = \dfrac{\pi (0.20)^{2} (1.5 \times 10^{6}) (30) (0.0089)}{2(15) + (0.18/0.025)} $$ Compute numerator: $$ \pi \cdot 0.04 \cdot 1.5 \times 10^{6} \cdot 30 \cdot 0.0089 = \pi \cdot 1602 = 5032.5 \text{ W/m² (numerator in SI)} $$ Compute denominator: $$ 2(15) + 0.18/0.025 = 30 + 7.2 = 37.2 \text{ W/m²·K} $$ Therefore: $$ \Delta T_{ss} = \dfrac{5032.5}{37.2} \approx 135 \text{ °C} $$ > [!WARNING] > In practice, the measured ΔT is **lower** than this analytical value because: (i) $E''$ decreases as the specimen heats up (negative temperature coefficient of damping); (ii) only ~60–70 % of the dissipated heat raises the bulk temperature (rest escapes laterally before equilibration); (iii) Poisson confinement during compression adds a hydrostatic component that reduces hysteresis. Report board answers as **analytical upper bound** with caveats. --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Goodrich Flexometer — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB nodeA["DRIVE MOTOR
1.8 kW AC"] nodeB["ECCENTRIC CAM
Converts rotary to linear"] nodeC["LOADING PISTON
Hydraulic / Mechanical"] nodeD["UPPER FIXTURE
Heat-conducting"] nodeE["CYLINDRICAL
SPECIMEN
17.8 mm x 25 mm"] nodeF["LOWER FIXTURE
Heat-conducting"] nodeG["THERMOCOUPLE
Embedded at centre"] nodeH["TEMPERATURE

Monitors cyclic load"] nodeJ["STROKE SENSOR
LVDT 4.45 mm"] nodeK["CONTROL UNIT

Delta T vs time"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF nodeG --> nodeE nodeG --> nodeH nodeC --> nodeI nodeB --> nodeJ nodeI --> nodeK nodeJ --> nodeK nodeH --> nodeK nodeK --> nodeL style nodeE fill:#FFD966,stroke:#000,color:#000 style nodeG fill:#F4B084,stroke:#000,color:#000 style nodeH fill:#A9D08E,stroke:#000,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Interpretation Guide:** The drive motor (nodeA) imparts 30 Hz cyclic motion through an eccentric cam (nodeB) into the loading piston (nodeC), which compresses the specimen (nodeE — *highlighted in yellow*) between heat-conducting upper and lower fixtures (nodeD, nodeF). The thermocouple (nodeG — *highlighted in orange*) feeds temperature data (nodeH — *highlighted in green*) to the control unit (nodeK) along with load (nodeI) and stroke (nodeJ) signals, producing the final **ΔT vs time curve** (nodeL). ### 4.2 Energy-Flow Block Diagram During Heat Build-up ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["MECHANICAL

epsilon"] nodeB["STRESS AXIS
sigma"] nodeC["LOADING CURVE

A "skinny" loop (small area) = low heat build-up (e.g., silicone rubber); a "fat" loop (large area) = high heat build-up (e.g., NR at room temperature). --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) > **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024 Model]** **Q1. Define heat build-up in elastomers. Name the standard test method and specify three key test parameters.** *(3 marks)* **Model Answer:** Heat build-up is the rise in internal temperature of an elastomer when subjected to repeated cyclic mechanical deformation. The internal temperature increase results from the conversion of mechanical energy into heat via viscoelastic hysteresis loss. The standard test method is **ASTM D623 (Goodrich Flexometer)**, with key parameters: **load = 24.5 kgf**, **stroke = 4.45 mm**, **frequency = 30 Hz**, and **duration = 25 min**. The result is reported as **ΔT in °C** measured at the geometric centre of the specimen. *(3 marks — distribution: 1.5 for definition, 0.5 for standard, 1 for parameters)* --- **Q2. Distinguish between storage modulus (E′) and loss modulus (E″). Which one is directly responsible for heat build-up?** *(3 marks)* **Model Answer:** - **Storage modulus (E′)** represents the in-phase (elastic) component of the stress response. It is a measure of the **stored recoverable energy** per cycle. Unit: MPa. - **Loss modulus (E″)** represents the out-of-phase (viscous) component. It is a measure of the **energy dissipated as heat** per cycle. Unit: MPa. - The ratio $\tan\delta = E''/E'$ is the loss tangent. **Heat build-up is directly caused by E″**, because the quadrature component of stress does positive work against strain, and this work appears as heat inside the specimen. *(3 marks — 1 mark each concept + identification)* --- ### Part B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (Internal Choice Provided) --- ### ✦ Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]` **(a)** Discuss in detail the mechanism of heat build-up in elastomers. Explain the role of loss modulus, tan δ, and the hysteresis loop. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Derive an expression for the steady-state temperature rise of a flexometer specimen using the energy-balance approach. Define every variable and state the underlying assumptions. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution (a): **Mechanism of heat build-up (7 marks):** 1. **Origin of dissipation (1 mark):** Polymers, especially elastomers, are viscoelastic. Their stress response to a cyclic strain lags behind the strain by a phase angle $\delta$ (loss angle). This lag originates from molecular chain-segment friction, filler-polymer interfacial friction, and viscoelastic flow. 2. **Stress decomposition (2 marks):** $$ \sigma(t) = \varepsilon_0 E' \sin(\omega t) + \varepsilon_0 E'' \cos(\omega t) $$ - In-phase component → $E'$ (storage, recoverable as elastic recoil). - Quadrature component → $E''$ (loss, dissipated as heat). 3. **Hysteresis loop area (2 marks):** On a σ–ε plot, the loading and unloading paths do not coincide; they enclose a loop whose area equals $W_{diss} = \pi \varepsilon_0^{2} E''$. This area represents the **net energy lost per unit volume per cycle**. 4. **Role of tan δ (1 mark):** $\tan\delta = E''/E'$ is a frequency-dependent material fingerprint. Materials with high tan δ (NR, SBR at room T) exhibit large heat build-up; materials with low tan δ (BR, EPDM, silicone) dissipate less. 5. **Microscopic-to-macroscopic link (1 mark):** Internally dissipated energy → temperature rise → decrease in $E''$ (negative thermal feedback). This stabilises ΔT at a steady state when heat generation equals heat loss. #### Model Solution (b): **Derivation (7 marks):** **Assumptions (1 mark):** - Lumped capacitance (uniform T inside specimen). - Constant $E''$ during the test. - Heat loss only by lateral convection (coefficient $h$) and end-face conduction (coefficient $k/L$). - Steady-state reached when $dT/dt = 0$. **Step 1 — Power dissipation per unit volume (2 marks):** $$ \dot{Q}_{vol} = \pi \varepsilon_0^{2} E'' f $$ **Step 2 — Total heat generated in specimen volume V (1 mark):** $$ \dot{Q}_{gen} = \pi \varepsilon_0^{2} E'' f \cdot V $$ **Step 3 — Heat loss rate (1 mark):** $$ \dot{Q}_{loss} = h (2\pi R L)(T - T_{\infty}) + \dfrac{k(\pi R^{2})}{L}(T - T_{\infty}) $$ **Step 4 — Energy balance at steady state (1 mark):** $$ \dot{Q}_{gen} = \dot{Q}_{loss} $$ **Step 5 — Solve for ΔT_ss (1 mark):** $$ \Delta T_{ss} = \dfrac{\pi \varepsilon_0^{2} E'' f \cdot R}{2 h + k/L} $$ All variables: $\varepsilon_0$ (strain amplitude, dimensionless), $E''$ (loss modulus, Pa), $f$ (frequency, Hz), $R$ (specimen radius, m), $h$ (convective coefficient, W/m²·K), $k$ (thermal conductivity, W/m·K), $L$ (specimen height, m). --- ### ✦ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]` **(a)** Describe the construction and working of a Goodrich-type flexometer with a neat schematic. Mention ASTM D623 specifications. *(7 marks)* **(b)** List the material and processing factors that influence heat build-up. Explain how compound formulation can be tailored to minimise heat build-up in tyre-tread compounds. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution (a): **Goodrich Flexometer — Construction & Working (7 marks):** 1. **Drive system (1 mark):** 1.8 kW AC motor with eccentric cam converting rotary motion (1800 rpm) to reciprocating stroke of 4.45 mm at 30 Hz. 2. **Loading system (1 mark):** Dead-weight or hydraulic loading piston applying constant compressive load (24.5 kgf or 44.5 kgf) onto specimen. 3. **Specimen holder (1 mark):** Heat-conducting (usually brass or aluminium) upper and lower platens with cylindrical cavity. Specimen dimensions: Ø 17.8 ± 0.2 mm × 25 ± 0.2 mm height. 4. **Temperature measurement (1 mark):** Iron–constantan or chromel–alumel thermocouple inserted at the geometric centre of the specimen, connected to digital readout or strip-chart recorder. 5. **ASTM D623 specifications (1.5 marks):** Method A — Goodrich, load 24.5 kgf, stroke 4.45 mm, 30 Hz, 25 min duration; Method B — Firestone, load 11.4 kgf, stroke 6.35 mm, 24 Hz, 25 min. 6. **Working principle (1 mark):** Specimen is cyclically compressed; mechanical hysteresis → temperature rise at centre → ΔT recorded vs time → steady-state ΔT reported. 7. **Reporting (0.5 mark):** Report ΔT in °C, plus observations on specimen deformation, cracking, or blow-out. #### Model Solution (b): **Factors & Compound Design (7 marks):** **Factors (3 marks):** 1. **Polymer type:** NR > SBR > BR in heat build-up (decreasing). EPDM and silicone have the lowest. 2. **Filler type & loading:** Higher carbon-black loading increases H.B.; silica or partially-replaced silica reduces it. 3. **Crosslink density:** Optimum cure gives minimum H.B.; under-cure or over-cure increases it. 4. **Plasticizer/oil type:** Aromatic oils increase H.B.; paraffinic and ester plasticizers lower it. 5. **Antidegradants:** Substituted paraphenylenediamines lower H.B. 6. **Test parameters:** Higher frequency and higher strain amplitude raise H.B. **Compound design for low-H.B. tyre treads (4 marks):** 1. **Polymer selection (1 mark):** Use **SBR/BR blends** (e.g., 70:30) to combine wear resistance of SBR with low hysteresis of BR. 2. **Filler system (1 mark):** Use **silica–silane** system (e.g., TESPT-coupled silica) replacing 30–50 phr carbon black — reduces tan δ at 60 °C, hence lower rolling resistance and heat build-up. 3. **Plasticizer (1 mark):** Replace aromatic oil with **TDAE (treated distillate aromatic extract)** or **MES (mild extraction solvate)** for lower H.B. 4. **Cure system (1 mark):** Use **semi-efficient cure (CV + EV blend)** with optimised sulphur/accelerator ratio for optimum crosslink density, minimising hysteresis without sacrificing wear. --- ### KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common places where students LOSE marks in Heat Build-up questions:** > 1. **Confusing ΔT (temperature rise) with final temperature.** Always report **ΔT = T_final − T_initial**, not the absolute temperature. > 2. **Skipping units in formula substitution.** Every numerical answer must show units: MPa, Hz, °C, W/m·K. > 3. **Forgetting to state ASTM D623 / ISO 4666 standard.** Naming the standard alone earns 0.5–1 mark. > 4. **Mixing up E′ and E″.** Examiners expect you to identify that **E″ causes heat build-up** — getting this swapped costs 2 marks. > 5. **Omitting the phase lag δ** in the definition of viscoelastic stress response. > 6. **In derivation questions, jumping directly to ΔT_ss without writing the energy balance.** This loses 2 marks. > 7. **In compound-formulation questions, ignoring the silica–silane revolution.** Modern KTU examiners expect "silica + TESPT" as the answer for low-rolling-resistance, low-H.B. treads. > 8. **Not stating assumptions** (lumped capacitance, constant E″, steady state) in derivation — costs 1 mark. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - **Definition:** Heat build-up = internal temperature rise in an elastomer under cyclic strain due to viscoelastic hysteresis. - **Mechanism:** Stress lags strain by **loss angle δ**; quadrature component $\propto E'' \cos(\omega t)$ does dissipative work. - **Energy per cycle:** $W_{diss} = \pi \varepsilon_0^{2} E''$ (J/m³). - **Power per volume:** $\dot{Q}_{vol} = \pi \varepsilon_0^{2} E'' f$ (W/m³). - **ΔT_ss formula:** $\Delta T_{ss} = \dfrac{\pi \varepsilon_0^{2} E'' f \cdot R}{2h + k/L}$ - **Standards:** ASTM D623 (Methods A & B), ISO 4666, BS 903 Part A14. - **Test conditions:** Goodrich flexometer, 24.5 kgf load, 4.45 mm stroke, 30 Hz, 25 min, specimen Ø 17.8 mm × 25 mm. - **Material ranking (high → low H.B.):** NR > SBR > IIR > EPDM > BR > Silicone. - **Engineering fixes:** silica + silane, BR blending, TDAE oil, semi-efficient cure. - **Real-world impact:** Tyre blow-out, mount softening, conveyor-belt fatigue, bridge-bearing failure. - **Examiner tip:** Always state the **standard**, give **units**, and write the **energy-balance equation** explicitly. - **Key derivation in board answers:** Energy balance ⇒ ΔT_ss expression ⇒ mention thermal time constant $\tau_{th} = m C_p / (U A_{eff})$. - **Mnemonic for test parameters — "L-S-F-D"**: **L**oad, **S**troke, **F**requency, **D**uration. - **Common synonyms:** Heat rise, hysteretic heating, internal temperature rise, ΔT build-up. ---

topics.title: Ageing Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT602 - POLYMER TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ageing Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Polymer Ageing** is defined as the irreversible change in the physical, mechanical, electrical, optical, or chemical properties of a polymeric material occurring over time under the combined or isolated influence of environmental factors such as **heat, light (UV radiation), oxygen, ozone, moisture, mechanical stress, and chemical agents**.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCPOT602 – Module 2)**, ageing properties refer to the **long-term performance characterization** of polymeric materials when subjected to controlled service-environmental stress. The discipline involves quantifying the **residual service life** and the **rate of degradation** through accelerated laboratory tests mapped onto real-time exposure via well-defined mathematical models (Arrhenius, Eyring, Peck).

**Key Standard Bodies Governing Ageing Tests:**
- **ASTM** (American Society for Testing and Materials) — e.g., ASTM D573, D1149, D4329, G154, G155
- **ISO** (International Organization for Standardization) — e.g., ISO 188, ISO 4892, ISO 1431
- **BIS** (Bureau of Indian Standards) — IS 5831, IS 13489

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Skin Story

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Think of polymer ageing exactly like **human skin ageing**.
>
> - A polymer sitting in the open sun undergoes **photo-oxidative degradation** — just like human skin gets tanned, wrinkled, and loses elasticity when overexposed to UV light.
> - A polymer continuously under load (e.g., a stretched rubber band held for months) experiences **stress relaxation and creep** — like an over-stretched muscle that loses its tone.
> - A polymer stored near an oven undergoes **thermal-oxidative degradation** — like butter left near a heater going rancid.
>
> **The "anti-ageing cream" for polymers** = **antioxidants, UV stabilizers (HALS), and plasticizers**. These are added in masterbatch forms (typically **0.1–3 phr**) to delay the ageing process.

## 1.3 Why Ageing Properties Matter in Polymer Testing

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why KTU examiners love this topic:**
> Almost every end-use polymer product (automotive dashboards, cable insulation, roofing sheets, blood bags, pipes, and geomembranes) has a **warranty period (typically 10–25 years)**. The only way to certify this warranty *without literally waiting 25 years* is through **accelerated ageing testing** combined with mathematical extrapolation. This makes ageing properties a **perennial favorite in KTU ESE questions**.

## 1.4 Classification of Ageing Types

| **Type of Ageing** | **Primary Agent** | **Typical Application Affected** |
|---|---|---|
| Thermal Ageing | Heat + Oxygen | Cookware, gaskets, engine parts |
| Photo-oxidative Ageing | UV Light + Oxygen | Outdoor cables, stadium seats |
| Hydrolytic Ageing | Water / Steam | Nylon gears, PET bottles |
| Ozone Ageing | Atmospheric Ozone | Rubber tires, hoses |
| Chemical Ageing | Acids / Alkalis / Solvents | Chemical tanks, liners |
| Biological Ageing | Microbes / Fungi | Geomembranes, packaging |
| Mechanical Ageing | Cyclic Stress / Fatigue | Tires, conveyor belts |
| Radiation Ageing | Gamma / Electron beam | Sterilized medical devices |

## 1.5 Key Metrics in Ageing Testing

- **Retention of Property (%)** = (Property after ageing / Property before ageing) × 100
- **Time to 50% Property Loss (t₅₀)** — commonly used as failure criterion
- **Activation Energy (Eₐ)** in **kJ/mol** — for Arrhenius extrapolation
- **Yellowing Index (YI)** — for colour-sensitive products
- **Oxidation Induction Time (OIT)** in **minutes** — via DSC

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanism of Thermal-Oxidative Ageing (Step-by-Step Logic)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Bolen-Campbell-Bowen Free Radical Chain Mechanism** explains >90% of polymer thermal ageing. KTU examiners expect a clear stepwise explanation.

**Step 1 — Initiation:**
Polymer chains are attacked by trace radicals (R•) or heat, producing **alkyl radicals (P•)** on the polymer backbone.

**Step 2 — Propagation:**
The alkyl radical reacts with **molecular oxygen (O₂)** to form **peroxy radicals (POO•)**.

**Step 3 — Hydrogen Abstraction:**
The peroxy radical abstracts a hydrogen atom from an adjacent polymer chain, forming a **hydroperoxide (POOH)** and a **new alkyl radical (P•)** — the chain reaction continues.

**Step 4 — Branching:**
Hydroperoxides decompose into alkoxy (PO•) and hydroxyl (OH•) radicals, accelerating the reaction.

**Step 5 — Termination:**
Two radicals combine, ending the chain reaction. Termination can also occur by antioxidant intervention.

> **Net Result:** Chain scission, crosslinking, and formation of carbonyl (C=O) groups — manifesting as embrittlement, cracking, or loss of tensile strength.

## 2.2 Mechanism of Photo-Oxidative Ageing (UV)

**Geometric Rule (Beer-Lambert Compliance):**
Only photons absorbed by the polymer initiate degradation. Polymers like polyethylene absorb UV strongly due to impurities (catalyst residues, carbonyl groups, TiO₂).

**The Kinetically Critical Wavelength Range:**
- **UV-A: 315–400 nm** — most damaging for outdoor exposure (deep penetration)
- **UV-B: 280–315 nm** — laboratory accelerated weathering uses this band
- **UV-C: 100–280 nm** — does not reach Earth, used in germicidal lamps

## 2.3 Mechanism of Ozone Cracking (Critical for Rubber)

> [!NOTE]
> **Ozone cracking** occurs **only under tensile stress**. An unstrained rubber surface forms a passive ozonide layer that protects it. When strained (>5%), the ozonide layer cracks, and the underlying polymer chains are attacked, producing characteristic **perpendicular cracks** to the stress direction.

**Threshold Strain:** ~5% elongation is enough to initiate ozone cracking in unprotected natural rubber.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Concept** | **Mathematical Form** | **Units** | **Application / Boundary Condition** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Property Retention | $R = \dfrac{P_{aged}}{P_{original}} \times 100$ | % | Universal ageing metric |
| Arrhenius Equation (core ageing formula) | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$ | k: rate constant (s⁻¹) | Used to extrapolate accelerated to real-time ageing |
| Service Life Prediction (log form) | $\ln(t_{failure}) = \dfrac{E_a}{RT} + \ln\left(\dfrac{\ln(0.5)}{A}\right)$ | t: hours, Eₐ: J/mol | Linearized for plotting ln(t) vs 1/T |
| Activation Energy (typical ranges) | $E_a$ | 50–150 kJ/mol | Thermoplastics: 80–110; Elastomers: 60–100 |
| Universal Gas Constant | $R$ | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** | Always required in Arrhenius |
| OIT (Oxidation Induction Time) | $t_{OIT}$ | minutes | Measured via DSC per ASTM D3895 |
| Yellowing Index | $YI = \dfrac{100 \cdot (1.28 X - 1.06 Z)}{Y}$ | dimensionless | ASTM E313; CIE tristimulus X, Y, Z |
| Tensile Retention (criterion) | Threshold | ≥ 50% of original | Common pass/fail criterion for cable insulation |
| Ozone Crack Resistance Index | $I_{O_3} = \dfrac{t_{crack}}{t_{exposure}}$ | dimensionless | Higher is better |
| Shore A Hardness Change | $\Delta H = H_{aged} - H_{original}$ | Shore A units | Should not exceed ±10 for rubber products |
| Masterbatch Loading (stabilizers) | Concentration | phr = parts per hundred resin | Typical: 0.5–3 phr |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Favourite Boundary Conditions to memorize:**
> - Standard accelerated ageing temperature: **70 °C, 100 °C, 120 °C, 150 °C** (ASTM D573)
> - Standard ozone concentration: **50 pphm (parts per hundred million)** at **40 °C** (ASTM D1149)
> - Standard UV exposure cycle: **8 h UV at 60 °C + 4 h condensation at 50 °C** (ASTM G154 / ISO 4892-3)
> - Xenon arc irradiance: **0.35–0.51 W/m² at 340 nm** (ASTM G155)

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Cable Industry (XLPE / EPR insulation):** Accelerated ageing at **135 °C for 168 hours** predicts **20-year service life** at **90 °C continuous** operation.
2. **Automotive:** Under-bonnet components are aged at **150 °C for 1000 h** to simulate **10 years** of service.
3. **Medical Devices:** Gamma sterilization ageing uses **25–50 kGy** dose exposure, followed by mechanical and chemical characterization.
4. **Geomembranes (HDPE):** UV exposure of **20 MJ/m²** corresponds to **~1 year** of subtropical sun exposure.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Arrhenius-Based Service Life Prediction

**Starting Premise:**
Property degradation follows first-order kinetics with respect to time. The rate constant $k$ is temperature-dependent per the Arrhenius equation.

**Given Data (Typical KTU Problem):**
A polymer loses 50% of its tensile strength when aged at three different temperatures. Determine its service life at **40 °C** (operating temperature).

| **Temperature (°C)** | **Temperature (K)** | **Time to 50% Failure (hours)** |
|---|---|---|
| 80 | 353 | 1000 |
| 100 | 373 | 200 |
| 120 | 393 | 50 |

**Step 1 — Apply First-Order Kinetics:**
For 50% property loss: $k \cdot t_{0.5} = \ln(2) = 0.693$

Therefore:
$$t_{0.5} = \dfrac{0.693}{k}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute Arrhenius into Kinetic Equation:**
$$t_{0.5} = \dfrac{0.693}{A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}}$$

**Step 3 — Take Natural Logarithm:**
$$\ln(t_{0.5}) = \ln(0.693) - \ln(A) + \dfrac{E_a}{RT}$$

Since $\ln(0.693) - \ln(A)$ is a constant (call it C), we get the linearized form:
$$\ln(t_{0.5}) = \dfrac{E_a}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T} + C$$

**Step 4 — Compute ln(t₀.₅) and 1/T for Each Data Point:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 = 353 \text{ K} \quad &\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{T_1} = 2.833 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1} \quad ; \quad \ln(1000) = 6.908 \\
T_2 = 373 \text{ K} \quad &\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{T_2} = 2.681 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1} \quad ; \quad \ln(200) = 5.298 \\
T_3 = 393 \text{ K} \quad &\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{T_3} = 2.545 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1} \quad ; \quad \ln(50) = 3.912
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply Linear Regression (Two-Point Method Using T₁ and T₃):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Slope} = \dfrac{E_a}{R} &= \dfrac{\ln(t_1) - \ln(t_3)}{\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_3}} \\
&= \dfrac{6.908 - 3.912}{2.833 \times 10^{-3} - 2.545 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= \dfrac{2.996}{0.288 \times 10^{-3}} \\
&= 10402.78 \text{ K}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute Activation Energy:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
E_a &= \text{Slope} \times R \\
&= 10402.78 \times 8.314 \\
&= 86488.5 \text{ J/mol} \\
&\approx 86.49 \text{ kJ/mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Find Intercept C Using Data Point T₃:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
C &= \ln(t_3) - \dfrac{E_a}{R} \cdot \dfrac{1}{T_3} \\
&= 3.912 - 10402.78 \times 2.545 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 3.912 - 26.475 \\
&= -22.563
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Predict Service Life at 40 °C (313 K):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\ln(t_{40}) &= 10402.78 \times \dfrac{1}{313} - 22.563 \\
&= 33.236 - 22.563 \\
&= 10.673
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{40} &= e^{10.673} \\
&= 43079 \text{ hours} \\
&\approx 4.92 \text{ years}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification of Validity:**
The rule of thumb requires that the maximum test temperature is at least 20 °C higher than the service temperature, **AND** the test temperature does not exceed the polymer's melting or glass transition temperature minus 20 °C. Here: 120 °C << T_m of typical thermoplastics (~250 °C). ✓

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Choice of Ageing Test

| **Polymer Type** | **Dominant Ageing Mode** | **Recommended Test Standard** | **Acceptance Criterion** |
|---|---|---|---|
| PVC (Rigid) | Thermal + UV discolouration | ASTM D1043, D4329 | YI ≤ 5, ΔE ≤ 2 |
| Natural Rubber | Ozone + Flex cracking | ASTM D1149, D1052 | No visible cracks at 50 pphm |
| HDPE (Pipes) | Oxidative (long-term) | ASTM D3350, ISO 4427 | 50-year hydrostatic strength retention |
| PET (Bottles) | Hydrolytic | ASTM D570, F1248 | MFI change ≤ 15% |
| EPDM (Roofing) | UV + Thermal | ASTM G155, G154 | Tensile retention ≥ 80% |
| Polypropylene | Thermo-oxidative chain scission | ASTM D3012 (OIT) | OIT ≥ 20 min at 200 °C |
| Nylon 6,6 | Hydrolytic + Thermal | ASTM D789, ISO 1110 | Viscosity retention ≥ 80% |
| Polyurethane Foam | Hydrolytic + Oxidation | ASTM D3574, D4874 | Compression set ≤ 10% |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Polymer Ageing Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    B --> C1[Outdoor Sun Exposure]

    C1 --> D1[UV Fluorescent Test]

    C3 --> F1[Water Immersion Test]

    C3 --> F3[Chemical Resistance Immersion]

    C5 --> H2[Dynamic Ozone Chamber]

    D3 --> J

```

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    X1[UV Light Source] --> Y1[Spectrally Matched Filter Assembly]
    Y1 --> Z1[Specimen Rotating Drum]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Flowchart — Free Radical Ageing Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Heat or UV Energy Input] --> B[PH Polymer Chain]
    B --> C[P Free Radical plus H Radical]
    C --> D[Reaction with O2]

    F --> G[POOH Hydroperoxide plus New P Radical]
    G --> C
    G --> H[Branching Decomposition]
    H --> I[PO Alkoxy plus OH Radical]

```

```mermaid
    SA1 --> SB1[Stable Radical Adduct]

    S3[HALS Stabilizer] --> SA3[Scavenges PO and OH Radicals]

```
