# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper Style]**

### **Question A1 (3 Marks)**
**Define polymer ageing. List any four environmental factors responsible for ageing of polymers.**

**Model Answer:**
Polymer ageing is the irreversible deterioration in the physical, mechanical, chemical, or aesthetic properties of a polymer occurring over time due to environmental exposure.

Four environmental factors:
1. Heat (thermal ageing)
2. Ultraviolet radiation (photo-oxidative ageing)
3. Oxygen (oxidative ageing)
4. Moisture / water (hydrolytic ageing)

(Other acceptable factors: ozone, mechanical stress, microbial action, gamma radiation, chemical agents.)

---

### **Question A2 (3 Marks)**
**What is Oxidation Induction Time (OIT)? State its standard test method and significance.**

**Model Answer:**
OIT is the time elapsed between the introduction of oxygen into the heated polymer sample and the onset of detectable oxidation (exothermic peak in DSC), measured at a constant isothermal temperature.

- **Standard:** ASTM D3895 (Differential Scanning Calorimetry)
- **Significance:** Higher OIT = better antioxidant package = longer service life of polyolefins like PP and PE pipes.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question B1 — Choice A (14 Marks)**  `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]`

**(a) Explain in detail the free-radical mechanism of thermal-oxidative ageing of polymers. Illustrate with suitable reaction steps. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Thermal-oxidative ageing of polymers proceeds via the **Bolen-Campbell free radical chain mechanism**, which has four stages:

**Step 1 — Initiation:**
Thermal energy or trace metal impurities cleave a C–H bond on the polymer backbone, generating an alkyl radical.

$$
PH \xrightarrow{\Delta} P^{\bullet} + H^{\bullet}
$$

**Step 2 — Propagation:**
The alkyl radical reacts with dissolved oxygen to form a peroxy radical.

$$
P^{\bullet} + O_2 \rightarrow POO^{\bullet}
$$

The peroxy radical abstracts hydrogen from an adjacent polymer chain, producing a hydroperoxide and a new alkyl radical, sustaining the chain reaction.

$$
POO^{\bullet} + PH \rightarrow POOH + P^{\bullet}
$$

**Step 3 — Branching:**
Hydroperoxides thermally decompose into alkoxy and hydroxyl radicals — both highly reactive.

$$
POOH \rightarrow PO^{\bullet} + {}^{\bullet}OH
$$

These radicals further abstract hydrogen, accelerating degradation.

**Step 4 — Termination:**
Two radicals combine, terminating the chain.

$$
P^{\bullet} + P^{\bullet} \rightarrow P - P
$$

$$
POO^{\bullet} + POO^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{Stable products}
$$

**Net Macroscopic Effects:**
- Chain scission → reduction in molecular weight → embrittlement
- Crosslinking → increase in hardness, loss of flexibility
- Formation of carbonyl groups → yellowing, odour

**Stabilization Strategies:** Addition of primary antioxidants (hindered phenols like Irganox 1010) and secondary antioxidants (phosphites like Irgafos 168).

---

**(b) Describe the Arrhenius method for predicting service life of a polymer from accelerated thermal ageing data. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The Arrhenius method is the **industry-standard technique** for extrapolating short-term accelerated ageing data to long-term service life prediction.

**Principle:**
The rate constant of a degradation reaction follows Arrhenius temperature dependence:

$$
k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}
$$

where:
- $k$ = rate constant (s⁻¹)
- $A$ = pre-exponential factor
- $E_a$ = activation energy (J/mol)
- $R$ = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

**Procedure:**
1. Age samples at **3 or more elevated temperatures** (typically 80, 100, 120 °C).
2. Determine **time to a defined failure criterion** — usually **50% retention of tensile strength or elongation at break**.
3. Compute ln(t) and 1/T for each data point.
4. Plot **ln(t) versus 1/T** — yields a straight line with slope $E_a/R$.
5. Extrapolate the line to the desired service temperature.

**Validity Conditions:**
- Same degradation mechanism across all test temperatures.
- No phase transitions occur in the test temperature range.
- Maximum test temperature ≤ melting temperature minus 20 °C.
- Typically 20–30 °C above service temperature.

**Limitations:**
- Cannot detect multiple failure modes.
- Inaccurate below glass transition temperature (chain mobility issues).
- Single failure criterion may not represent real end-use failure.

---

### **Question B1 — Choice B (14 Marks)**  `[KTU University Exam — Jun 2023 Style]`

**(a) Explain the mechanism of ozone cracking in elastomers. How is it tested as per ASTM standards? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Mechanism:**
Ozone (O₃), even at trace atmospheric levels (5–50 pphm), attacks the C=C double bonds in unsaturated elastomers like natural rubber (NR), SBR, and NBR.

**Step 1:** Ozone reacts with the double bond to form an **ozonide** intermediate.

**Step 2:** Under tensile strain (>5%), the ozonide layer ruptures, exposing fresh polymer surface.

**Step 3:** Cracks propagate **perpendicular** to the direction of applied stress due to chain scission.

**Critical Observation:** Strained rubber cracks; unstrained rubber forms a protective ozonide film and remains intact.

**Prevention:** Use of **ozone-resistant elastomers** (EPDM, butyl, silicone, HNBR) or addition of **antiozonants** (e.g., N-isopropyl-N'-phenyl-p-phenylenediamine, IPPD) that migrate to the surface and sacrificially react with ozone.

**ASTM Test Standards:**

- **ASTM D1149 (Ozone Chamber Test):**
  - Specimens under **static or dynamic strain (20%)**.
  - Ozone concentration: **50 ± 5 pphm** at **40 ± 2 °C**.
  - Exposure time: **70, 96, or 168 hours**.
  - Visual inspection at 2× magnification for cracks.

- **ASTM D1171 (Outdoor-Weathering Test):** Mounted specimens exposed outdoors under controlled strain.

- **ISO 1431:** International equivalent of ASTM D1149.

---

**(b) Discuss accelerated weathering tests using Xenon arc and UV fluorescent apparatus. Compare their merits. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Xenon Arc Weatherometer (ASTM G155, ISO 4892-2):**

- Source: **Xenon arc lamp** with optical filters (Daylight, Window Glass, or Extended UV).
- Spectral match to **natural sunlight** — best representation of real outdoor exposure.
- Controlled parameters: **irradiance (0.35–0.51 W/m² at 340 nm), chamber temperature (38–80 °C), black-panel temperature (up to 110 °C), and relative humidity (50–95%)**.
- Typical cycle: **102 min light + 18 min light with water spray**.
- Use: Automotive, architectural, and outdoor furniture testing.

**UV Fluorescent Apparatus (ASTM G154, ISO 4892-3):**

- Source: **Fluorescent UV-B (313 nm) or UV-A (340/351 nm) lamps**.
- Lower cost, simpler operation, more reproducible.
- Cycles typically alternate UV exposure and **condensation** to simulate dew.
- Common cycle: **8 h UV at 60 °C + 4 h condensation at 50 °C**.
- Use: Coatings, plastics, packaging.

**Comparison Table:**

| **Parameter** | **Xenon Arc** | **UV Fluorescent** |
|---|---|---|
| Spectral match to sunlight | Excellent | Moderate |
| Cost | High | Low |
| Reproducibility | Good | Excellent |
| Test duration equivalence | ~1 year = 1500–2500 h | ~1 year = 1000–2000 h |
| Best for | Automotive, exterior coatings | Quality control, screening |

**Key Insight:** Xenon arc provides the closest simulation of real weathering but is expensive. UV fluorescent is used for routine QC and comparative ranking.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Common Mistake 1:** Writing the Arrhenius equation without stating the **units of R and Eₐ** — costs **1 mark**.
> 2. **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing **OIT (Oxidation Induction Time)** with **OIT (Oxidative Induction Temperature)** — they are NOT the same. OIT is time-based; OIT-temperature is the onset temperature of oxidation.
> 3. **Common Mistake 3:** Failing to mention that **ozone cracking requires tensile strain** — many students write ozone cracks rubber irrespective of stress. Always state the threshold strain of ~5%.
> 4. **Common Mistake 4:** Mixing up **UV-A and UV-B wavelengths** in weathering tests. UV-A is 315–400 nm (outdoor simulation); UV-B is 280–315 nm (accelerated lab).
> 5. **Common Mistake 5:** Not drawing a **ln(t) vs 1/T plot** in the Arrhenus method question — graph is mandatory for **full 7 marks**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Bullet Summary for Last-Minute KTU Revision:**

- [ ] **Definition:** Polymer ageing = irreversible property change over time due to environment.
- [ ] **Eight types:** Thermal, photo-oxidative, hydrolytic, ozone, chemical, biological, mechanical, radiation.
- [ ] **Free radical ageing:** Initiation → Propagation → Branching → Termination.
- [ ] **Critical UV bands:** UV-A (315–400 nm), UV-B (280–315 nm), UV-C (100–280 nm).
- [ ] **Ozone cracking threshold strain:** ~5% for natural rubber; cracks perpendicular to stress.
- [ ] **Arrhenius equation:** $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$, R = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**.
- [ ] **Linearized form:** $\ln(t) = (E_a/R)(1/T) + C$.
- [ ] **Property retention formula:** $R(\%) = (P_{aged} / P_{original}) \times 100$.
- [ ] **Common pass/fail criterion:** Tensile retention ≥ 50%.
- [ ] **Standard test temperatures:** 70, 100, 120, 150 °C (ASTM D573).
- [ ] **Standard ozone concentration:** 50 pphm at 40 °C (ASTM D1149).
- [ ] **Xenon arc irradiance:** 0.35–0.51 W/m² at 340 nm (ASTM G155).
- [ ] **UV fluorescent cycle:** 8 h UV at 60 °C + 4 h condensation at 50 °C (ASTM G154).
- [ ] **OIT measurement:** ASTM D3895 by DSC at constant temperature (typically 200 °C).
- [ ] **Yellowing Index:** ASTM E313 — must specify CIE illuminant (usually D65).
- [ ] **Stabilizer types:** Primary AO (phenols), Secondary AO (phosphites), HALS, UV absorbers, antiozonants (IPPD).
- [ ] **Activation energy range:** Thermoplastics 80–110 kJ/mol; Elastomers 60–100 kJ/mol.
- [ ] **Validity rule:** T_test_max ≤ T_melt − 20 °C; T_test_max ≥ T_service + 20 °C.
- [ ] **Most repeated KTU question:** "Explain the free radical mechanism of thermal-oxidative ageing" — practice writing all four steps.
- [ ] **Most numerical question:** "Predict service life using Arrhenius plot from three temperatures."
- [ ] **Key ozone-resistant rubbers:** EPDM, butyl, silicone, HNBR (no C=C double bonds).
- [ ] **Key photo-oxidative stabilizers:** HALS (Tinuvin 770, 622) + UV absorbers (Tinuvin P, 328).
- [ ] **Cable insulation ageing:** 168 h at 135 °C = ~20-year service at 90 °C.
- [ ] **Yellowness Index formula:** $YI = 100(1.28X - 1.06Z)/Y$ per ASTM E313.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT602 - POLYMER TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometry (FTIR)** is an advanced analytical technique that simultaneously measures all infrared frequencies from a sample by recording the **interferogram** produced by a **Michelson interferometer**, and then mathematically converting this time-domain (or optical-path-difference domain) signal into a frequency-domain infrared spectrum using the **Fourier Transform** algorithm.

In the context of polymer testing, FTIR is the primary spectroscopic technique used for:
- **Qualitative identification** of polymer type (e.g., PE, PP, PS, PET, PVC, Nylon)
- **Detection of functional groups** (C=O, O–H, N–H, C–H, C=C) in the polymer backbone
- **Copolymer composition analysis** (e.g., styrene-butadiene ratio)
- **Monitoring oxidative degradation** (appearance of C=O band at ~1715 cm⁻¹)
- **Additive and filler identification**
- **Degree of crystallinity** estimation (crystallinity-sensitive bands)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** FTIR belongs to the family of *vibrational spectroscopy* methods. The IR region used is typically the **mid-infrared (MIR) region: 4000 cm⁻¹ to 400 cm⁻¹** (wavelength 2.5 μm to 25 μm). The fundamental physical principle is the **absorption of IR radiation by molecular vibrations** when the vibration causes a change in the **dipole moment** of the molecule.

## 1.2 Physical Principle: Why Molecules Absorb IR

Every covalent bond in a polymer chain can be modeled as a **spring** with two masses (atoms) attached, vibrating at a characteristic natural frequency. When infrared radiation of the *same frequency* strikes the molecule, **resonant absorption** occurs — provided that the vibration produces a fluctuating **dipole moment**.

$$ \nu_{\text{vibration}} = \frac{1}{2\pi c}\sqrt{\frac{k}{\mu}} $$

where:
- $\nu$ = wavenumber of vibration (cm⁻¹)
- $k$ = bond force constant (N/m)
- $\mu$ = reduced mass of the two atoms (kg)
- $c$ = speed of light (3 × 10¹⁰ cm/s)

> [!NOTE]
> **Selection Rule (KTU must-know):** A vibration is IR-active **only if** it causes a change in the dipole moment of the molecule. Symmetric, non-polar molecules (e.g., N₂, O₂, homonuclear diatomics) give **no IR absorption**. For polymers, this rule allows discrimination between symmetric (IR-inactive) and asymmetric (IR-active) stretches.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Orchestra Recording Studio"

Imagine you are inside a concert hall with a single microphone that records **every instrument simultaneously** — violins, flutes, drums, all at once — but in a *single, messy, jumbled waveform*. This messy waveform is like the **interferogram** of FTIR.

Now imagine an audio engineer runs this messy recording through a **mathematical filter (the Fourier Transform)** that separates the combined signal back into individual frequency components: 440 Hz (violin A), 1000 Hz (flute), 80 Hz (bass drum), and so on. The result is a clean **frequency spectrum** — exactly analogous to the FTIR spectrum showing absorption versus wavenumber.

> In classical **dispersive IR spectroscopy**, you would have to ask each instrument to play one note at a time (using a grating monochromator to scan through wavelengths) — slow and inefficient. FTIR listens to *the whole orchestra at once* and then mathematically un-mixes it — fast (Fellgett advantage) and more sensitive.

## 1.4 Why FTIR is the Workhorse of Polymer Testing

| Feature | Dispersive IR | FTIR |
|---|---|---|
| Measurement type | Sequential scan | Simultaneous (all frequencies) |
| Time per spectrum | Several minutes | 1–10 seconds |
| Signal-to-noise ratio | Low | Very high (Fellgett advantage) |
| Wavenumber precision | ±1 cm⁻¹ | ±0.01 cm⁻¹ (Connes advantage) |
| Throughput (light) | Low (slits) | High (Jacquinot advantage) |
| Sample requirement | Large | Micrograms possible |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The three classical advantages of FTIR — **Fellgett (multiplex), Jacquinot (throughput), and Connes (precision)** — are heavily emphasized in KTU university examinations and must be memorized by every student.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block Diagram of a Typical FTIR Spectrometer

The optical path of a FTIR instrument, in sequence, is:

1. **IR Source** — typically a Globar (silicon carbide) or Nernst glower, emitting broadband mid-IR radiation.
2. **Interferometer (Michelson type)** — the heart of the instrument. Contains a **beam splitter**, one **fixed mirror**, and one **moving mirror**.
3. **Sample Compartment** — the polymer sample, prepared as a KBr pellet, thin film, or mounted for ATR.
4. **Detector** — commonly DTGS (deuterated triglycine sulfate) for routine work, or MCT (mercury cadmium telluride) for high sensitivity.
5. **Computer with FT algorithm** — performs the Fast Fourier Transform (FFT).

## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step

- **Step 1 (Source):** The Globar emits broadband IR covering the full MIR range (4000–400 cm⁻¹).
- **Step 2 (Beam Splitting):** The beam splitter (a thin film of germanium on KBr substrate) transmits 50% of the IR beam and reflects the other 50%.
- **Step 3 (Path Difference):** One beam travels to the fixed mirror, the other to the moving mirror. The moving mirror oscillates at constant velocity $v$. The optical path difference $\delta$ varies with time as $\delta = 2 v t$.
- **Step 4 (Recombination):** The two beams recombine at the beam splitter and produce **constructive or destructive interference** depending on $\delta$ relative to the wavelength $\lambda$.
- **Step 5 (Interferogram):** The detector records the **interferogram** — signal intensity $I(\delta)$ as a function of optical path difference $\delta$.
- **Step 6 (Fourier Transform):** The computer applies the Fourier Transform to convert $I(\delta)$ into the IR spectrum $B(\nu)$ as a function of wavenumber $\nu$.

## 2.3 The Core FT Equations (KTU Formula Sheet)

| # | Equation | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $B(\nu) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} I(\delta)\, e^{-i 2\pi \nu \delta}\, d\delta$ | Forward Fourier Transform — converts interferogram to spectrum |
| 2 | $I(\delta) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} B(\nu)\, e^{+i 2\pi \nu \delta}\, d\nu$ | Inverse Fourier Transform |
| 3 | $I(\delta) = \int_{0}^{\infty} S(\nu) \cos(2\pi \nu \delta)\, d\nu$ | Real-domain cosine form (typical FTIR) |
| 4 | $\nu_{\text{vib}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi c}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{\mu}}$ | Vibrational frequency (Hooke's Law for diatomic oscillator) |
| 5 | $\Delta \nu = \dfrac{1}{\delta_{\max}}$ | Spectral resolution set by maximum mirror travel |
| 6 | $\mu = \dfrac{m_1 m_2}{m_1 + m_2}$ | Reduced mass of a diatomic pair |
| 7 | $\nu = \dfrac{1}{\lambda} = \dfrac{\tilde{\nu}}{1 \text{ cm}}$ | Relation between wavenumber $\tilde{\nu}$ and wavelength $\lambda$ |
| 8 | $A = -\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I}{I_0}\right)$ | Absorbance (Beer-Lambert) |
| 9 | $T = \dfrac{I}{I_0}$ | Transmittance |
| 10 | $\Delta t = \dfrac{N}{2 \Delta \nu}$ | Time to acquire $N$ co-added scans |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** When writing the FTIR formula, students often write the integration limits incorrectly. The forward Fourier Transform in FTIR spectroscopy uses **full limits from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$** because the interferogram is a symmetric (cosine-based) signal around zero path difference. The inverse transform, when reconstructing the spectrum, integrates over **positive wavenumbers only** because the spectrum is real-valued.

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind the Three FTIR Advantages

- **Fellgett (Multiplex) Advantage:** Because all wavenumbers are measured simultaneously at the detector, the signal-to-noise ratio improves as $\sqrt{N}$ where $N$ is the number of resolution elements. For typical spectra, this yields a 10× to 200× SNR improvement over dispersive instruments for equal measurement time.

- **Jacquinot (Throughput) Advantage:** FTIR uses **circular apertures** (Jacquinot stop) instead of the narrow slits of dispersive spectrometers, allowing much more light to reach the detector — improving SNR further and enabling microsampling.

- **Connes (Precision) Advantage:** The mirror position is precisely tracked by a **helium-neon laser reference interferometer**. Because the wavelength of He-Ne laser is known to extreme precision, the wavenumber scale of the FTIR spectrum is accurate to ±0.01 cm⁻¹.

## 2.5 Sample Preparation Methods for Polymers (High-Yield for KTU)

| Method | Polymer Form | Procedure | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|
| **KBr Pellet** | Powder or ground polymer | Mix ~1 mg polymer with ~100 mg KBr, press at 8–10 tons into transparent disc | Quantitative work, qualitative ID |
| **Cast Film** | Solution-castable polymers | Evaporate solvent on KBr or NaCl window | Transparent thin films |
| **Hot Press Film** | Thermoplastic polymers | Melt-press between heated plates | Polyolefins, PS, PET |
| **ATR (Attenuated Total Reflectance)** | Any solid, liquid, film | Place directly on ZnSe, Ge, or Diamond crystal | Surface analysis, opaque samples, coatings |
| **Pyrolysis** | Insoluble / cross-linked polymer | Heat to 500–700 °C, vaporize, analyze gases | Cured rubbers, thermosets |

> [!NOTE]
> **ATR-FTIR** is the modern workhorse in industrial polymer testing because it requires virtually no sample preparation and is non-destructive. It works on the principle of *evanescent wave* absorption when the IR beam undergoes total internal reflection at the crystal-sample interface.

## 2.6 Characteristic IR Absorption Bands for Common Polymers (MUST MEMORIZE)

| Polymer | Wavenumber (cm⁻¹) | Assignment |
|---|---|---|
| **Polyethylene (PE)** | 2920, 2850 | C–H asymmetric & symmetric stretch (CH₂) |
| | 1460, 720 | CH₂ bending & rocking |
| **Polypropylene (PP)** | 2950, 2920, 2850 | CH₃ and CH₂ stretches |
| | 1375, 970 | CH₃ bending, isotacticity |
| **Polystyrene (PS)** | 3025, 1600, 1490, 1450 | Aromatic C–H stretch, ring vibrations |
| | 700, 750 | Out-of-plane C–H bending of phenyl ring |
| **Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC)** | 2900 | C–H stretch |
| | 1430 | CH₂ bending |
| | 600–700 | C–Cl stretch |
| **Polyethylene Terephthalate (PET)** | 1720 | C=O ester stretch |
| | 1100, 1250 | C–O ester stretch |
| | 720 | Ring out-of-plane |
| **Nylon 6,6** | 3300 | N–H stretch |
| | 1640, 1550 | Amide I, Amide II |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Interferogram for a Monochromatic Source

We begin with the simplest case: an idealized source emitting IR radiation of a **single wavenumber** $\nu_0$. The beam is split by the beam splitter into two beams of equal amplitude $E_0/2$.

**Beam 1 (fixed mirror path):** Electric field after reflection
$$ E_1 = \frac{E_0}{2} e^{i(2\pi \nu_0 t - 2\pi \nu_0 x_1)} $$

**Beam 2 (moving mirror path):** Electric field with extra path length $\delta$
$$ E_2 = \frac{E_0}{2} e^{i(2\pi \nu_0 t - 2\pi \nu_0 (x_2 + \delta))} $$

**Total field at detector:**
$$ E_{\text{total}} = E_1 + E_2 $$

**Intensity at detector** (proportional to the time-average of $\vert E \vert^2$):
$$ I(\delta) = \frac{1}{2} \vert E_{\text{total}} \vert^2 $$

We expand, dropping constant phase terms, and apply $\cos(A-B) = \cos A \cos B + \sin A \sin B$ to obtain the interference term. The DC terms (from $E_1^2$ and $E_2^2$) combine to give $E_0^2/4$. The cross terms give:

$$ I(\delta) = \frac{E_0^2}{4} + \frac{E_0^2}{4} \cos(2\pi \nu_0 \delta) $$

The detector responds only to the **AC component** (the variable part). Therefore:

$$ I(\delta) = I_0 \, \cos(2\pi \nu_0 \delta) $$

**Interpretation:** For a single-frequency source, the interferogram is a **pure cosine wave** in optical path difference $\delta$, with frequency $\nu_0$. This is the building block for understanding polychromatic FTIR.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Spectrum for a Polychromatic Source

A real IR source emits a continuous spectrum $S(\nu)$ — the spectral intensity at each wavenumber $\nu$. The total intensity at the detector is the **superposition (integral)** of contributions from all wavenumbers:

$$ I(\delta) = \int_{0}^{\infty} S(\nu) \, \cos(2\pi \nu \delta) \, d\nu $$

This is a **cosine Fourier transform** of the spectrum $S(\nu)$. To recover the spectrum from the measured interferogram, we apply the **inverse cosine Fourier transform**:

$$ S(\nu) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} I(\delta) \, \cos(2\pi \nu \delta) \, d\delta $$

These two equations are the **fundamental pair of FTIR equations** and form the entire mathematical basis of the technique.

## 3.3 Derivation of Spectral Resolution

The moving mirror can travel only a finite maximum distance $\delta_{\max}$. Beyond this, the interferogram is truncated. For a resolution element centered at wavenumber $\nu$, two close-by wavenumbers $\nu$ and $\nu + \Delta \nu$ can be distinguished only if they produce at least one full oscillation in the interferogram over the range $-\delta_{\max}$ to $+\delta_{\max}$. This requires:

$$ \Delta \nu \cdot 2\delta_{\max} \geq 1 $$

which rearranges to:

$$ \Delta \nu = \frac{1}{2 \delta_{\max}} $$

For a **single-sided interferogram** (0 to $\delta_{\max}$), the resolution is:

$$ \boxed{\Delta \nu = \frac{1}{\delta_{\max}} } $$

**Numerical Example (KTU Exam Standard):** For a maximum mirror displacement of $\delta_{\max} = 2$ cm, the achievable resolution is $\Delta \nu = 1/2 = 0.5$ cm⁻¹.

## 3.4 Apodization — Suppressing Truncation Artifacts

A real interferogram cannot be measured to infinite path difference. The finite $\delta_{\max}$ causes the interferogram to be **truncated**, which mathematically corresponds to multiplying the true interferogram by a **boxcar (rectangular) function** $D(\delta)$:

$$ I_{\text{measured}}(\delta) = I(\delta) \cdot D(\delta) $$

where $D(\delta) = 1$ for $\vert \delta \vert \leq \delta_{\max}$ and $0$ elsewhere.

By the **convolution theorem**, multiplication in the interferogram domain is equivalent to **convolution in the spectral domain**. The boxcar function's Fourier transform is a $\operatorname{sinc}(x) = \sin(x)/x$ function, which has oscillating side-lobes (negative dips next to real absorption peaks). These side-lobes cause **artificial peaks** in the spectrum.

**Solution — Apodization:** Multiply the interferogram by a smooth weighting function (Happ-Genzel, triangular, Hamming, Blackman-Harris) that smoothly tapers to zero at $\delta_{\max}$. This removes the discontinuity and suppresses side-lobes, at a small cost of resolution.

$$ I_{\text{apodized}}(\delta) = I(\delta) \cdot A(\delta) $$

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Numerical: Constructing an Interferogram

**Problem:** A polychromatic IR source contains two spectral lines at $\nu_1 = 1500$ cm⁻¹ and $\nu_2 = 1650$ cm⁻¹, each of intensity $S(\nu) = 1$ (arbitrary units). Compute the interferogram intensity $I(\delta)$ at $\delta = 0$ and $\delta = 0.001$ cm.

**Solution using superposition:**

At $\delta = 0$:
$$ I(0) = \int_{0}^{\infty} S(\nu) \cos(0) \, d\nu = S(\nu_1) + S(\nu_2) = 1 + 1 = 2 $$

At $\delta = 0.001$ cm:

First compute the arguments:
$$ 2\pi \nu_1 \delta = 2\pi \times 1500 \times 0.001 = 3\pi \text{ rad} $$
$$ 2\pi \nu_2 \delta = 2\pi \times 1650 \times 0.001 = 3.3\pi \text{ rad} $$

$$ \cos(3\pi) = \cos(540°) = -1 $$
$$ \cos(3.3\pi) = \cos(594°) = \cos(234°) \approx -0.5878 $$

Therefore:
$$ I(0.001) = (1)(-1) + (1)(-0.5878) = -1.5878 $$

The interferogram is now negative — it has **dropped below the zero baseline** because of destructive interference. This illustrates the central feature of an interferogram: it **oscillates** around a mean value (the DC offset), with the oscillation amplitude and frequency encoding the spectrum.

## 3.6 Co-Added Scans and Signal-to-Noise Ratio

For $N$ co-added scans (averaging), random noise averages out while the true signal adds coherently. The noise level drops by $\sqrt{N}$:

$$ \text{SNR}_{N \text{ scans}} = \sqrt{N} \cdot \text{SNR}_{1 \text{ scan}} $$

**Numerical Example:** To improve SNR by a factor of 10, you need $N = 100$ co-added scans. If one scan takes 1 second, total acquisition time is 100 seconds.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in polymer testing:** Detecting trace degradation products (e.g., carbonyl groups in aged polyethylene) requires high SNR. Co-adding 32 or 64 scans is routine practice.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Michelson Interferometer — Optical Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[IR SOURCE Globar Nernst] --> BS[BEAM SPLITTER Ge on KBr 50 percent transmit 50 percent reflect]
    BS --> FM[FIXED MIRROR stationary]
    BS --> MM[MOVING MIRROR oscillates at velocity v path delta 2vt]
    FM --> BS
    MM --> BS
    BS --> SAMP[SAMPLE COMPARTMENT polymer film KBr pellet ATR crystal]
    SAMP --> DET[DETECTOR DTGS or MCT]
    DET --> FFT[COMPUTER FFT ALGORITHM]
    FFT --> SPEC[IR SPECTRUM absorbance vs wavenumber]
```

## 4.2 Signal Flow — Interferogram to Spectrum

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[IR Light Hits Beam Splitter] --> B[Two Beams Created]
    B --> C[Beam 1 Fixed Mirror]
    B --> D[Beam 2 Moving Mirror]
    C --> E[Recombine at Beam Splitter]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Pass Through Polymer Sample]
    F --> G[Detector Records I delta]
    G --> H[Interferogram in time domain]
    H --> I[Fast Fourier Transform]
    I --> J[IR Spectrum in frequency domain]
    J --> K[Absorbance vs Wavenumber cm inverse]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture of FTIR for Polymer Identification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    POLY[Polymer Sample unknown] --> PREP{Sample Preparation}
    PREP --> P1[KBr Pellet 1 mg in 100 mg KBr]
    PREP --> P2[Cast Film from solution]
    PREP --> P3[Hot Press Film melt pressed]
    PREP --> P4[ATR Direct on crystal]
    PREP --> P5[Pyrolysis for insoluble thermosets]
    P1 --> SPEC[FTIR Spectrometer]
    P2 --> SPEC
    P3 --> SPEC
    P4 --> SPEC
    P5 --> SPEC
    SPEC --> INT[Interferogram captured]
    INT --> FT[FFT Processed]
    FT --> BANDS[Characteristic Bands Identified]
    BANDS --> LIB{Compare with Library}
    LIB --> MATCH[Hit Quality Index above 90 percent confirms polymer identity]
    BANDS --> QUANT[Quantitative Analysis Beer Lambert Law peak height or area]
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Which Sampling Method to Use

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Sample soluble in common solvent} -->|YES| A1[Use Cast Film on KBr window]
    Q1 -->|NO| Q2{Sample is thin film or sheet}
    Q2 -->|YES| A2[Use Transmission mode or ATR directly]
    Q2 -->|NO| Q3{Sample is rubbery or filled}
    Q3 -->|YES| A3[Use ATR with Diamond Crystal]
    Q3 -->|NO| Q4{Sample is cross linked thermoset}
    Q4 -->|YES| A4[Use Pyrolysis FTIR or TGA FTIR coupling]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`**
**Q:** *Define Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometry (FTIR) and list its three classical advantages over dispersive IR spectroscopy.*

**Model Answer:**
FTIR is an infrared spectroscopic technique in which a Michelson interferometer is used to produce an interferogram — a signal in the optical-path-difference domain — which is then mathematically converted to a frequency-domain (wavenumber) spectrum using the Fourier Transform algorithm.

The three classical advantages are:
1. **Fellgett advantage (multiplex)** — all wavenumbers measured simultaneously, improving signal-to-noise ratio by a factor proportional to $\sqrt{N}$.
2. **Jacquinot advantage (throughput)** — circular apertures permit higher light throughput than dispersive slits.
3. **Connes advantage (precision)** — use of an internal He-Ne laser reference gives wavenumber accuracy of ±0.01 cm⁻¹.

*(Marks distribution: Definition 1 mark + 3 advantages × 0.5 mark + explanation = 3 marks)*

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sessional]`**
**Q:** *Explain the principle of ATR (Attenuated Total Reflectance) in FTIR. Why is it preferred for polymer testing?*

**Model Answer:**
ATR is a sampling technique in which the IR beam undergoes **total internal reflection** at the interface between a high-refractive-index crystal (ZnSe, Ge, or Diamond) and the lower-refractive-index polymer sample. At each reflection, a small portion of the light — called the **evanescent wave** — penetrates a few microns (0.5–5 μm) into the sample and is selectively absorbed at frequencies corresponding to the polymer's vibrational modes.

ATR is preferred for polymer testing because:
- **No sample preparation** required — the polymer is placed directly on the crystal.
- **Non-destructive** — the sample can be recovered.
- Suitable for **opaque, thick, or highly filled polymers** that cannot be cast as thin films.
- Excellent for **surface analysis** (depth profiling by varying crystal or angle).

*(Marks: 1 for principle + 2 for advantages = 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks (Choice Option A)

**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Paper]`**

**Q:** *With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a Michelson interferometer-based FTIR spectrometer. Discuss in detail (a) the function of each component, and (b) the mathematical steps involved in converting the interferogram to an IR spectrum using the Fourier Transform.*

#### Part (a) — Components and Their Functions [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

A FTIR spectrometer consists of the following components arranged in sequence:

**1. IR Source (1 mark):** A broadband thermal emitter such as a **Globar** (silicon carbide rod heated to ~1300 K) or a **Nernst glower** (mixture of rare-earth oxides). It emits mid-IR radiation covering 4000–400 cm⁻¹.

**2. Beam Splitter (1 mark):** A thin film of **germanium on a KBr substrate** that transmits 50% and reflects 50% of the incident IR beam. This is the critical element that creates the two interfering beams.

**3. Fixed Mirror (0.5 mark):** A flat, front-surface mirror that reflects the transmitted beam back to the beam splitter at constant position.

**4. Moving Mirror (0.5 mark):** A flat mirror mounted on a precision translation stage that moves at constant velocity $v$, varying the optical path difference $\delta = 2vt$.

**5. Sample Compartment (0.5 mark):** Holds the polymer sample — as KBr pellet, cast film, or ATR crystal — through which the recombined beam passes.

**6. Detector (1 mark):** Converts IR intensity to electrical signal. Common types:
- **DTGS** (deuterated triglycine sulfate) — routine, room temperature, moderate sensitivity.
- **MCT** (mercury cadmium telluride) — high sensitivity, requires liquid-nitrogen cooling.

**7. He-Ne Laser Reference (0.5 mark):** A red laser (632.8 nm) co-propagating through the interferometer whose fringes precisely track mirror position — providing the Connes wavenumber-precision advantage.

**8. Computer + FFT Processor (0.5 mark):** Performs the Fast Fourier Transform of the interferogram and displays the IR spectrum.

**Working Sequence (1.5 marks):**
- Source emits broadband IR → beam splitter splits into two beams → one goes to fixed mirror, the other to moving mirror → both reflect back and recombine at the beam splitter → interference pattern depends on path difference δ → beam passes through sample (some frequencies absorbed) → detector records the interferogram $I(\delta)$ → computer applies FFT → output IR spectrum $S(\nu)$ is plotted as absorbance or transmittance versus wavenumber.

#### Part (b) — Fourier Transform Mathematics [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** For a single-frequency source of wavenumber $\nu_0$, the interferogram is a pure cosine:
$$ I(\delta) = I_0 \cos(2\pi \nu_0 \delta) $$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** For a polychromatic source with spectral distribution $S(\nu)$, the interferogram is the superposition (integral) of all cosine components:
$$ I(\delta) = \int_{0}^{\infty} S(\nu) \cos(2\pi \nu \delta) \, d\nu $$

This is a **cosine Fourier transform** of $S(\nu)$.

**Step 3 (1 mark):** The detector records $I(\delta)$ as a function of mirror displacement. At $\delta = 0$ (zero path difference, ZPD), all cosine terms equal 1, producing a sharp central maximum — the *centerburst*.

**Step 4 (1.5 marks):** To recover the spectrum $S(\nu)$ from the measured interferogram, apply the **inverse Fourier transform**:
$$ S(\nu) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} I(\delta) \cos(2\pi \nu \delta) \, d\delta $$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** In practice, a discrete Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) algorithm (Cooley-Tukey, 1965) is used. The interferogram is digitized at sampling intervals $\Delta \delta$ determined by the Nyquist criterion: $\Delta \delta \leq 1/(2\nu_{\max})$.

**Step 6 (1 mark):** The mirror is moved to a maximum displacement $\delta_{\max}$, giving a spectral resolution of:
$$ \Delta \nu = \frac{1}{\delta_{\max}} $$

The interferogram is typically multiplied by an **apodization function** (Happ-Genzel, triangular) before FFT to suppress truncation side-lobes, then phase-corrected.

**Step 7 (0.5 mark):** The final output is the IR spectrum — a plot of absorbance $A = -\log_{10}(I/I_0)$ versus wavenumber $\nu$ in cm⁻¹, ready for interpretation against reference polymer libraries.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Choice Option B)

**`[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]`**

**Q:** *(a) Describe the sample preparation techniques used in FTIR analysis of polymers. Compare the merits of KBr pellet, cast film, and ATR methods. (b) With the help of a sketch of an FTIR spectrum, explain the identification of polyethylene (PE) and polyethylene terephthalate (PET) using their characteristic absorption bands.*

#### Part (a) — Sample Preparation Techniques [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**1. KBr Pellet Method (2 marks):**
- Procedure: ~1 mg of finely ground polymer is mixed with ~100 mg of spectroscopic-grade KBr powder. The mixture is pressed in a hydraulic press at 8–10 tons for 1–2 minutes to form a transparent disc of diameter 13 mm.
- **Merits:** Excellent for quantitative work (Beer-Lambert law), gives sharp, well-defined peaks, suitable for powders.
- **Demerits:** Destructive, time-consuming, requires grinding, moisture-sensitive (KBr absorbs water), unsuitable for water-containing or rubbery polymers.

**2. Cast Film Method (1.5 marks):**
- Procedure: Polymer is dissolved in a volatile solvent (e.g., chloroform for PS, TFA for PET). A few drops are placed on a KBr or NaCl window and the solvent is evaporated, leaving a thin film.
- **Merits:** Excellent for quantitative comparison, easy to vary thickness, no grinding needed.
- **Demerits:** Requires soluble polymer, residual solvent may interfere (e.g., water peaks near 3400 cm⁻¹), unsuitable for insoluble cross-linked polymers.

**3. ATR Method (2 marks):**
- Procedure: Polymer is placed in direct contact with a high-refractive-index crystal (Diamond, ZnSe, or Ge). The IR beam undergoes multiple total internal reflections, and the evanescent wave selectively absorbs.
- **Merits:** **No sample preparation**, non-destructive, works on solids, liquids, films, gels, powders, opaque samples, suitable for surface analysis. Diamond ATR is rugged and chemically inert.
- **Demerits:** Smaller absorption intensity than transmission (corrected by software), penetration depth varies with wavelength (slight spectral distortion at low wavenumbers), quantitative work requires careful control of contact pressure.

**4. Hot-Press Film Method (0.5 mark):** Polymer melted between two heated platens and pressed into a thin film — ideal for thermoplastics like PE, PP.

**5. Pyrolysis Method (1 mark):** For insoluble cross-linked polymers (cured rubbers, thermosets), the sample is heated to 500–700 °C in a pyrolysis unit; the volatile decomposition products are analyzed. Often coupled with TGA-FTIR.

#### Part (b) — Identification of PE and PET [7 Marks]

**Model Solution:**

**Sketch description (2 marks for spectra):** A typical FTIR spectrum shows absorbance (y-axis) versus wavenumber (y-axis cm⁻¹, decreasing left to right) from 4000 to 400 cm⁻¹. Sharp peaks point downward (in transmittance mode) or upward (in absorbance mode).

**Polyethylene (PE) identification — 2.5 marks:**

The PE spectrum is exceptionally simple, dominated by the aliphatic C–H vibrations:
- **2920 cm⁻¹** — strong peak: C–H **asymmetric stretching** of CH₂ groups.
- **2850 cm⁻¹** — strong peak: C–H **symmetric stretching** of CH₂ groups.
- **1460 cm⁻¹** — medium peak: CH₂ **bending** (scissoring).
- **720 cm⁻¹** — medium peak: CH₂ **rocking** (diagnostic of long alkyl chains, ≥4 CH₂ groups).

The absence of any peak between 1700 and 1800 cm⁻¹ (no C=O) and between 1500 and 1600 cm⁻¹ (no aromatic ring) immediately rules out oxygen-containing or aromatic polymers. This simple "C–H only" pattern is characteristic of polyolefins.

**Polyethylene Terephthalate (PET) identification — 2.5 marks:**

The PET spectrum is rich and distinctive, with multiple diagnostic bands:
- **1720 cm⁻¹** — strong, sharp peak: **C=O ester carbonyl stretching** (the most diagnostic band for PET).
- **1265 cm⁻¹ and 1100 cm⁻¹** — strong peaks: **C–O ester asymmetric and symmetric stretching**.
- **870 cm⁻¹** — peak: aromatic C–H out-of-plane bending of *para*-disubstituted benzene ring.
- **725 cm⁻¹** — peak: aromatic ring out-of-plane deformation.
- **2960 cm⁻¹** — weak: C–H stretch of CH₂ groups in the ethylene glycol segment.

The simultaneous presence of (i) C=O at 1720 cm⁻¹, (ii) aromatic ring band near 870 cm⁻¹, and (iii) C–O ester doublet near 1100–1265 cm⁻¹ uniquely identifies PET among common polymers.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Students frequently confuse **absorbance vs transmittance spectra** — remember, in transmittance mode, peaks point **downward**; in absorbance mode, peaks point **upward**. Always state which mode is used.
> 2. Students often give band positions without specifying whether they are **stretching, bending, scissoring, rocking, or wagging** modes. Examiner awards marks only when vibration type is correctly named.
> 3. **Do not** state vague ranges like "around 1700 cm⁻¹" — KTU expects **specific values** like **1715 ± 10 cm⁻¹** for PET C=O.
> 4. When asked to **compare** polymers, use a **tabular comparison** rather than long paragraphs — examiners scan tabular answers faster and award more marks.
> 5. Always mention the **selection rule** (dipole moment change) when explaining why some vibrations are IR-active and others are not — this is a high-value KTU point.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **FTIR = Fourier Transform + Infrared Spectroscopy** — measures all IR frequencies simultaneously, then applies Fourier Transform.
- ✅ **Mid-IR region used: 4000–400 cm⁻¹** (most important range for organic/polymer functional groups).
- ✅ **Selection Rule:** IR absorption requires a **change in dipole moment** during vibration.
- ✅ **Hooke's Law for vibrations:** $\nu = \dfrac{1}{2\pi c}\sqrt{\dfrac{k}{\mu}}$ — heavier atoms and weaker bonds give lower wavenumbers.
- ✅ **FTIR spectrum is the FT of the interferogram** — the fundamental pair of equations is memorized.
- ✅ **Resolution equation:** $\Delta \nu = \dfrac{1}{\delta_{\max}}$ — longer mirror travel = better resolution.
- ✅ **Three classical advantages:** Fellgett (multiplex), Jacquinot (throughput), Connes (precision).
- ✅ **Components in order:** Source → Beam splitter → Fixed/Moving mirrors → Sample → Detector → FFT computer.
- ✅ **Sampling methods:** KBr pellet (quantitative), cast film (soluble polymers), ATR (no prep, preferred for routine analysis), hot press (thermoplastics), pyrolysis (cross-linked).
- ✅ **Apodization** is applied to suppress truncation side-lobes in the spectrum.
- ✅ **Key polymer bands:**
  - PE: 2920, 2850, 1460, 720 cm⁻¹
  - PP: 2950, 1375, 970 cm⁻¹
  - PS: 3025, 1600, 1490, 700 cm⁻¹
  - PVC: 2900, 600–700 cm⁻¹
  - PET: 1720 (C=O), 1265, 1100 (C–O), 870 (aromatic) cm⁻¹
  - Nylon: 3300 (N–H), 1640 (Amide I), 1550 (Amide II) cm⁻¹
- ✅ **SNR improvement by co-adding $N$ scans** = $\sqrt{N}$.
- ✅ **FTIR detects oxidative degradation** by the appearance of carbonyl C=O band near 1715 cm⁻¹ in aged polymers.
- ✅ **Library search (Hit Quality Index > 90%)** confirms polymer identity against reference spectra.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ultraviolet-Visible Spectrometry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT602 - POLYMER TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ultraviolet-Visible Spectrometry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ultraviolet-Visible (UV-Vis) Spectrometry** is an analytical spectroscopic technique that quantitatively measures the absorption (or transmission) of ultraviolet (**200 nm – 400 nm**) and visible (**400 nm – 700 nm**) electromagnetic radiation by a sample, typically a polymer solution or thin film. It is governed fundamentally by the **Beer-Lambert Law** and is used to probe electronic transitions (π → π*, n → π*) in chromophoric groups.

In the context of **KTU PCPOT602 – Polymer Testing**, UV-Vis spectrometry is classified as a **non-destructive optical characterization technique** primarily used for:
- Quantitative determination of additives, stabilizers, and unreacted monomers in polymers.
- Monitoring **photo-oxidative degradation** of polymers (yellowing index, carbonyl index correlations).
- Determination of optical properties (transparency, haze, cut-off wavelength) of polymer films.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Stained Glass Window" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Check:** Imagine sunlight passing through a stained-glass window in a church. White light (containing all colors) enters from one side, but only specific colors emerge on the other side because the glass *absorbs* particular wavelengths and *transmits* the rest. The deeper/thicker the colored glass, the more intense the absorption.

**Mapping this analogy to UV-Vis:**
- **White light** → Polychromatic UV-Vis radiation from the source (Deuterium + Tungsten lamps).
- **Stained glass** → Polymer sample containing **chromophores** (molecular groups that absorb specific wavelengths).
- **Emerging colored light** → Transmitted light measured by the detector.
- **Glass thickness** → Optical path length (ℓ).
- **Color intensity of glass** → Molar concentration (c) and absorptivity (ε).

The spectrometer essentially asks: *"Which wavelengths did the polymer swallow, and how hungry was it?"*

## 1.3 Key Terminology Glossary

| Term | Symbol | Definition |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Transmittance | **T** | Ratio of transmitted intensity to incident intensity, $T = I \mid I_0$ |
| Absorbance | **A** | Negative logarithm (base 10) of transmittance, $A = -\log_{10} T$ |
| Molar Absorptivity | **ε** | Intrinsic absorption capacity of a species at a given wavelength (**L mol⁻¹ cm⁻¹**) |
| Path Length | **ℓ** | Internal width of the sample cuvette (**cm**) |
| Chromophore | — | Atomic or molecular group responsible for electronic absorption |
| Auxochrome | — | Saturated group with lone pairs that intensifies chromophore absorption |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Heuristic:** When defining UV-Vis, **always state the wavelength ranges** explicitly (UV: 200–400 nm; Vis: 400–700 nm) — omitting these is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction in Part A.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Electronic Transitions

UV-Vis absorption occurs when incident photons possess energy exactly matching the **HOMO → LUMO** electronic transition energy gap of chromophores. The fundamental energy equation is:

$$E_{photon} = h \nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$

where $h$ is **Planck's constant (6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s)**, $c$ is the speed of light (3 × 10⁸ m/s), and $\lambda$ is the wavelength.

### 2.1.1 Primary Electronic Transitions (in decreasing energy / increasing wavelength)

1. **σ → σ\*** : Highest energy, requires λ < 150 nm (vacuum UV, not observed in standard instruments). Found in saturated alkanes (C–C, C–H bonds).
2. **n → π\*** : Lowest energy of common transitions. Non-bonding electrons (lone pairs on O, N, S) jump to anti-bonding π* orbital. *Example: carbonyl groups (C=O) in degraded PET.*
3. **π → π\*** : Medium-high energy. Electrons in π-bonding orbitals transition to π* anti-bonding orbitals. *Example: aromatic rings in polystyrene, conjugated double bonds in polyacetylene.*
4. **n → σ\*** : Higher energy than n→π*. Common in alcohols, amines, ethers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Polymer Insight:** Photodegradation of polymers is detected by the emergence of new n → π* absorption bands around **280–320 nm** due to carbonyl (C=O) formation, even when the virgin polymer showed none.

### 2.1.2 The Chromophore and Auxochrome Concept

- **Chromophore:** The core functional group responsible for absorption (e.g., –C=C–, –C=O, –NO₂, benzene ring).
- **Auxochrome:** A substituent (e.g., –OH, –NH₂, –Cl) that **does not absorb** in the UV region alone but shifts the chromophore's absorption to longer wavelengths (**bathochromic / red shift**) and increases intensity (**hyperchromic effect**).

## 2.2 Instrumentation — Block-Level Architecture

A UV-Vis spectrometer operates on a **single-beam or double-beam** configuration. The optical path is:

**Source → Monochromator → Beam Splitter → Sample Compartment → Detector → Signal Processor → Display**

| Component | Function | Typical Material / Spec |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Light Source | Emits continuous UV-Vis spectrum | Deuterium lamp (UV, 190–375 nm); Tungsten-Halogen lamp (Vis, 350–2500 nm) |
| Monochromator | Isolates monochromatic light | Quartz prism or diffraction grating |
| Sample Holder | Houses the polymer solution/film | Quartz cuvettes (transparent to UV); path length typically 1 cm |
| Detector | Converts photons → electrical signal | Photomultiplier Tube (PMT) or Photodiode Array (PDA) |
| Recorder/PC | Plots Absorbance vs Wavelength | X-Y plotter or digital interface |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Quick-Reference Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains **all derivation-ready equations** for this topic. Memorize the inter-relationships between transmittance, absorbance, and Beer-Lambert's law.

| # | Equation | Description | Variables & Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $T = \dfrac{I}{I_0}$ | Transmittance definition | $I$ = transmitted intensity; $I_0$ = incident intensity |
| 2 | $A = -\log_{10} T = \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{I_0}{I}\right)$ | Absorbance from transmittance | $A$ is dimensionless |
| 3 | $A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot \ell$ | **Beer-Lambert Law** | $\varepsilon$ in L mol⁻¹ cm⁻¹; $c$ in mol/L; $\ell$ in cm |
| 4 | $\%T = T \times 100$ | Percentage transmittance | Expressed as % |
| 5 | $A + \log_{10} T = 0$ | Identity (since $A = -\log T$) | — |
| 6 | $\varepsilon = \dfrac{M}{\rho \cdot \ell} \cdot A$ | For solid films (concentration by mass) | $M$ = molar mass; $\rho$ = density |
| 7 | $E_{photon} = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Photon energy | Joules (J) |
| 8 | $\tilde{\nu} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Wavenumber (often used in IR, sometimes in UV) | cm⁻¹ |

## 2.4 Why UV-Vis Matters for Polymers — Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Quality Control (QC):** Verifying concentration of UV-stabilizers (e.g., benzotriazoles, hindered amine light stabilizers / HALS) in commercial polymer masterbatches.
- **Degradation Studies:** Accelerated weathering tests (QUV, Xenon arc) coupled with UV-Vis track the growth of **carbonyl peak at ~280 nm** as a degradation marker.
- **Conjugated Polymer Analysis:** Conducting polymers (polyaniline, PEDOT:PSS, polythiophene) exhibit strong visible-region absorptions directly correlated with electronic conductivity.
- **Copolymer Composition:** Using characteristic monomer absorptions to determine reactivity ratios via Fineman-Ross or Kelen-Tudos methods.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Beer-Lambert Law (Bouguer–Beer–Lambert Law)

The law is built incrementally by combining two empirical observations: **Bouguer's Law** (path length dependence) and **Beer's Law** (concentration dependence).

### Step 1 — Bouguer's Law (1729): Absorbance is proportional to optical path length

Consider a thin slab of absorbing medium of thickness $d\ell$ perpendicular to a monochromatic light beam of intensity $I$. The fractional decrease in intensity $dI \mid I$ is proportional to the slab thickness:

$$\frac{dI}{I} = -k_1 \, d\ell$$

where $k_1$ is a proportionality constant characteristic of the medium.

### Step 2 — Integrate across the entire path length

Integrating from the entry face ($\ell = 0$, intensity $I_0$) to the exit face ($\ell = \ell$, intensity $I$):

$$\int_{I_0}^{I} \frac{dI}{I} = -k_1 \int_{0}^{\ell} d\ell$$

$$\left[\ln I\right]_{I_0}^{I} = -k_1 \ell$$

$$\ln I - \ln I_0 = -k_1 \ell$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = -k_1 \ell$$

Converting natural log to base-10 log by substituting $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$:

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = -\frac{k_1}{2.303} \, \ell$$

Define the constant $k_2 = k_1 \mid 2.303$:

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = k_2 \, \ell$$

The left side is recognized as the **absorbance A**:

$$A = k_2 \, \ell$$

This proves **absorbance is directly proportional to path length** (Bouguer's Law).

### Step 3 — Beer's Law (1852): Absorbance is proportional to concentration

By analogous reasoning, when a collimated beam passes through a solution of concentration $c$, the fractional intensity loss per unit path length is proportional to the number of absorbing molecules encountered:

$$\frac{dI}{I} = -k_3 \, c \, d\ell$$

Integrating yields:

$$A = k_4 \, c$$

This proves **absorbance is directly proportional to concentration** (Beer's Law).

### Step 4 — Combining both laws (Beer-Lambert Law)

Since absorbance is additive in both path length and concentration, we combine the two constants $k_2$ and $k_4$ into a single composite constant **ε (epsilon)**, the molar absorptivity (also called molar extinction coefficient):

$$A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot \ell$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Form:**
> $$\boxed{\,A = \varepsilon \, c \, \ell\,}$$
> where $\varepsilon$ has units of **L mol⁻¹ cm⁻¹**, $c$ has units of **mol L⁻¹**, and $\ell$ has units of **cm**. The product $A$ is **dimensionless**.

### Step 5 — Recovering the Transmittance Form

Starting from $A = -\log_{10} T$ and substituting Beer-Lambert:

$$-\log_{10} T = \varepsilon \, c \, \ell$$

$$T = 10^{-\varepsilon c \ell}$$

$$I = I_0 \cdot 10^{-\varepsilon c \ell}$$

This is the **exponential decay form** of Beer-Lambert Law, mathematically equivalent to the linear absorbance form.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Polymer Additive Quantification

> [!EXAMPLE]
> **Problem:** A polystyrene sample is dissolved in chloroform at a concentration of **2.5 × 10⁻⁴ mol L⁻¹**. A UV-stabilizer (Tinuvin P) present in the polymer has a molar absorptivity of **1.2 × 10⁴ L mol⁻¹ cm⁻¹** at 300 nm. The measurement is performed in a **1.0 cm** quartz cuvette. Calculate:
> (a) The absorbance at 300 nm.
> (b) The percentage transmittance.
> (c) The transmitted intensity if $I_0 = 1.00$ (arbitrary units).

### Solution

**(a) Absorbance calculation:**

Given: $c = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ mol L⁻¹; $\varepsilon = 1.2 \times 10^{4}$ L mol⁻¹ cm⁻¹; $\ell = 1.0$ cm.

$$A = \varepsilon \, c \, \ell$$

$$A = (1.2 \times 10^{4}) \times (2.5 \times 10^{-4}) \times (1.0)$$

$$A = 1.2 \times 2.5 = 3.0$$

$$\boxed{A = 3.0 \text{ (dimensionless)}}$$

**(b) Percentage transmittance:**

$$A = -\log_{10} T \implies T = 10^{-A}$$

$$T = 10^{-3.0} = 0.001$$

$$\%T = T \times 100 = 0.001 \times 100 = 0.1\%$$

$$\boxed{\%T = 0.1\%}$$

**(c) Transmitted intensity:**

$$T = \frac{I}{I_0} \implies I = T \cdot I_0 = 0.001 \times 1.00$$

$$\boxed{I = 0.001 \text{ (arbitrary units)}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Many students compute $\%T$ as $T \times 10$ instead of $T \times 100$. Always multiply by **100**, not 10.

## 3.3 Limitations of Beer-Lambert Law (High-Yield for 7-Mark Sub-Parts)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU frequently tests the **deviations** and **limitations** of Beer-Lambert Law for 7-mark questions. The following matrix organizes the key failure modes:

| # | Limitation | Physical Cause | Practical Consequence |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **High concentrations** ($> 0.01$ M) | Intermolecular interactions; refractive index changes | ε becomes concentration-dependent; non-linear A vs c plot |
| 2 | **Stray radiation** | Imperfect monochromator; scattered light reaching detector | Apparent absorbance deviates, especially at high A values |
| 3 | **Chemical deviations** | Solute dissociation, association, or reaction with solvent | New species absorb at different λ; ε is no longer constant |
| 4 | **Polychromatic radiation** | Non-monochromatic slit bandwidth (Δλ) | Different wavelengths have different ε values, causing non-linearity |
| 5 | **Scattering by particulates** | Polymer aggregates, dust, micro-bubbles in solution | Spurious "absorbance" appears due to scattering losses |
| 6 | **Fluorescence of sample** | Sample re-emits absorbed photons | Detector sees extra signal; measured A is artificially low |

## 3.4 Mathematical Relationship Between Transmittance and Absorbance — Line of Best Fit Derivation

For a series of standard solutions with concentrations $c_1, c_2, c_3, \dots, c_n$, the absorbance values should fall on a straight line when plotted against concentration:

$$A_i = \varepsilon \, \ell \cdot c_i$$

The slope of the $A$ vs $c$ plot gives $\varepsilon \ell$, and the y-intercept should ideally be zero (no absorption at $c = 0$).

Using **least-squares regression** on $n$ calibration points:

$$\text{slope} = \varepsilon \ell = \frac{n \sum c_i A_i - \sum c_i \sum A_i}{n \sum c_i^2 - \left(\sum c_i\right)^2}$$

This formula is **directly applicable to KTU numerical problems** asking for the determination of unknown concentration from a calibration curve.

## 3.5 Conjugated Polymer Wavelength Extension — Empirical Rule

For linear conjugated polyenes (e.g., polyacetylene, polythiophene), the absorption wavelength follows the empirical **Free-Electron Model** relation:

$$\lambda_{max} \propto \frac{n^2}{N}$$

where $n$ is the number of conjugated double bonds and $N$ is a normalization constant. As conjugation length increases, $\lambda_{max}$ shifts toward the visible region — explaining why polythiophene (long conjugation) is **visibly colored**, while short oligothiophenes are colorless.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Double-Beam UV-Vis Spectrometer Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram represents the **block-level functional architecture** of a typical double-beam UV-Vis spectrometer, as is standard in modern instruments (e.g., Shimadzu UV-1900, PerkinElmer Lambda 365).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SrcL["LIGHT SOURCE A\nDEUTERIUM LAMP\n190 nm to 375 nm"] --> M1["MONOCHROMATOR 1\nENTRANCE SLIT\nDIFFRACTION GRATING"]
    M1 --> BS["BEAM SPLITTER\nQUARTST PRISM\n50:50 DIVIDER"]
    BS --> SmpPath["SAMPLE BEAM PATH\nTO SAMPLE CUVETTE\n1 CM QUARTZ CELL"]
    BS --> RefPath["REFERENCE BEAM PATH\nTO BLANK CUVETTE\nSOLVENT ONLY"]
    SmpPath --> Chop["BEAM CHOPPER\nALTERNATES SAMPLE\nAND REFERENCE PATHS"]
    RefPath --> Chop
    Chop --> M2["MONOCHROMATOR 2\nEXIT SLIT\nWAVELENGTH SELECTION"]
    M2 --> Det["PHOTOMULTIPLIER TUBE\nDETECTOR\nCONVERTS PHOTONS TO SIGNAL"]
    Det --> Amp["SIGNAL AMPLIFIER\nAND LOG CONVERTER\nCOMPUTES A EQUALS LOG I0 BY I"]
    Amp --> Disp["DISPLAY AND PC\nPLOTS A VS LAMBDA\nRECORDS SPECTRUM"]
    Det -.FEEDBACK.-> SrcL
```

## 4.2 Process Flow — How a UV-Vis Spectrum Is Acquired

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start(["START EXPERIMENT"]) --> P1["PREPARE POLYMER SOLUTION\nDISSOLVE IN SUITABLE SOLVENT\nFILTER THROUGH 0.45 MICRON MEMBRANE"]
    P1 --> P2["FILL QUARTZ CUVETTE\nRINSE WITH SAMPLE\nENSURE NO AIR BUBBLES"]
    P2 --> P3["PREPARE BLANK\nPURE SOLVENT IN MATCHED CUVETTE\nREFERENCE BASELINE"]
    P3 --> P4["SET INSTRUMENT PARAMETERS\nWAVELENGTH RANGE 200 NM TO 800 NM\nSCAN SPEED AND SLIT WIDTH"]
    P4 --> P5["ACQUIRE BASELINE\nRUN BLANK FIRST\nSTORE AS I0 AT ALL LAMBDA"]
    P5 --> P6["ACQUIRE SAMPLE SPECTRUM\nRECORD I AT EACH LAMBDA\nCOMPUTE A EQUALS LOG I0 BY I"]
    P6 --> P7["IDENTIFY LAMBDA MAX\nFROM SPECTRUM PEAK\nRECORD ABSORBANCE VALUE"]
    P7 --> P8["QUANTIFY USING CALIBRATION CURVE\nPLOT A VS C FOR STANDARDS\nINTERPOLATE UNKNOWN CONCENTRATION"]
    P8 --> Stop(["END AND REPORT"])
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    App["UV-VIS APPLICATIONS IN POLYMER TESTING"]
    App --> App1["QUALITY CONTROL"]

    App2 --> App2b["THERMAL OXIDATION\nMONITORING VIA UV ABSORPTION"]

    App3 --> App3a["UV STABILIZER CONCENTRATION\nHALS AND BENZOTRIAZOLES"]
    App3 --> App3b["ANTIOXIDANT QUANTIFICATION\nPHENOLIC TYPE ADDITIVES"]

    App4 --> App4a["BANDGAP ESTIMATION\nFROM ABSORPTION EDGE"]
```

## 4.4 Beer-Lambert Law — Mathematical Dependency Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["A EQUALS EPSILON C L\nBEER LAMBERT LAW"]
    E["EPSILON\nMOLAR ABSORPTIVITY\nL PER MOL PER CM"]
    C["C\nCONCENTRATION\nMOL PER L"]
    L["L\nPATH LENGTH\nCM"]
    T["T EQUALS 10 TO MINUS A\nTRANSMITTANCE"]
    P["PERCENT T\nT TIMES 100"]

    E --> A
    C --> A
    L --> A
    A --> T
    T --> P
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define Beer-Lambert Law. State its mathematical form and the units of each term.**

**Model Answer:**

Beer-Lambert Law states that the absorbance of a monochromatic light beam passing through a homogeneous absorbing medium is directly proportional to both the concentration of the absorbing species and the optical path length through the medium.

The mathematical form is:

$$A = \varepsilon \, c \, \ell$$

where:
- $A$ = absorbance (dimensionless)
- $\varepsilon$ = molar absorptivity or molar extinction coefficient (**L mol⁻¹ cm⁻¹**)
- $c$ = concentration of the absorbing species (**mol L⁻¹**)
- $\ell$ = optical path length through the sample (**cm**)

> **Examiner's Note (3 marks):** Law statement (1) + Equation (1) + Units (1).

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between chromophore and auxochrome with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Chromophore | Auxochrome |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Definition | Functional group directly responsible for electronic absorption | Saturated group that modifies chromophore absorption |
| Intrinsic Absorption | Yes, absorbs UV-Vis light on its own | No, does not absorb appreciably on its own |
| Effect on λ_max | Determines the primary absorption wavelength | Shifts λ_max to longer wavelength (bathochromic shift) |
| Effect on Intensity | Sets the base molar absorptivity | Increases intensity (hyperchromic effect) |
| Example | **–C=O (carbonyl)** or **benzene ring** | **–OH (hydroxyl)** or **–NH₂ (amino)** attached to a chromophore |

> **Examiner's Note (3 marks):** Definition of each (1) + Differentiating property (1) + One example each (1).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In 14-mark Part B answers, the typical mark split is **7 + 7** for sub-parts (a) and (b). Students commonly lose marks by (i) **omitting diagrams** (lose 2 marks), (ii) writing only the principle without **instrumentation details** (lose 3 marks), and (iii) **forgetting units** in formulas (lose 1 mark). Always include a labeled diagram.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a) Explain the instrumentation of a UV-Vis Spectrometer with the help of a block diagram. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The UV-Vis spectrometer consists of the following essential components arranged in the optical path:

**1. Light Sources (2 marks)**
- **Deuterium Lamp (D₂):** Emits continuous UV radiation in the range **190 nm – 375 nm**. Operates by electrical discharge through deuterium gas at low pressure.
- **Tungsten-Halogen Lamp (W):** Emits continuous visible and near-IR radiation in the range **350 nm – 2500 nm**. A halogen cycle maintains filament evaporation.
- A mirror or beam-combiner selects the appropriate lamp based on the wavelength region being scanned.

**2. Monochromator (1 mark)**
- A **diffraction grating** (or quartz prism) disperses the polychromatic light into its constituent wavelengths.
- An **entrance slit** and **exit slit** collimate and select a narrow band of wavelengths (typical bandwidth: 0.5–2 nm).

**3. Sample Compartment (1 mark)**
- Houses a **quartz cuvette** (path length 1 cm) containing the polymer solution.
- Quartz is used because it is transparent down to ~190 nm (glass would absorb below 320 nm).

**4. Detector (1 mark)**
- A **Photomultiplier Tube (PMT)** or **Photodiode Array (PDA)** converts incoming photons into an amplified electrical signal.
- PMT offers high sensitivity for low-absorbance measurements.

**5. Signal Processor & Recorder (1 mark)**
- A logarithmic amplifier converts the transmittance ratio to absorbance via $A = \log_{10}(I_0 / I)$.
- The output is plotted as **Absorbance (y-axis) vs Wavelength (x-axis)**.

**Block Diagram (1 mark):** Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes.

---

**(b) Discuss the various electronic transitions occurring in organic molecules during UV-Vis absorption. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

When a molecule absorbs UV-Vis radiation, electrons in lower-energy molecular orbitals are promoted to higher-energy anti-bonding orbitals. The primary transitions, in order of **decreasing energy requirement** (i.e., increasing wavelength of absorption), are:

**1. σ → σ\* Transition (1 mark)**
- Promotes an electron from a σ-bonding orbital to a σ* anti-bonding orbital.
- Requires high energy (λ < 150 nm, vacuum UV).
- **Example:** C–C and C–H bonds in polyethylene and polypropylene.

**2. n → σ\* Transition (1 mark)**
- Promotes a non-bonding (lone pair) electron to σ* anti-bonding orbital.
- λ ≈ 150–250 nm.
- **Example:** O–H, N–H, S–H bonds in polymer side chains.

**3. π → π\* Transition (2 marks)**
- Promotes a π-bonding electron to π* anti-bonding orbital.
- λ ≈ 200–400 nm, with high molar absorptivity ($\varepsilon = 10{,}000$ L mol⁻¹ cm⁻¹).
- **Example:** C=C bonds in polybutadiene, aromatic rings in polystyrene and PET, conjugated backbones in polythiophene.

**4. n → π\* Transition (2 marks)**
- Promotes a non-bonding electron to π* anti-bonding orbital.
- Lowest energy of the four; λ ≈ 270–350 nm, low intensity ($\varepsilon < 100$ L mol⁻¹ cm⁻¹).
- **Example:** Carbonyl (C=O) groups formed during photo-oxidative degradation of polyolefins.

**5. Charge-Transfer Transitions (1 mark)**
- Electron transfers from a donor atom/molecule to an acceptor across the complex.
- High intensity ($\varepsilon$ up to 50,000 L mol⁻¹ cm⁻¹).
- **Example:** Complexes of iodine with poly(vinyl pyrrolidone).

> **Examiner's Note:** Sub-part (b) carries 7 marks; allocate ~1.5 marks per transition type, with extra weight on π → π* and n → π* as they are the most commonly observed in polymer systems.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) Derive Beer-Lambert Law. State and explain any four of its limitations. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (3.5 marks):**

The derivation proceeds in three steps:

**Step 1:** For a thin slab of absorbing medium of thickness $d\ell$, the fractional decrease in intensity is proportional to thickness:

$$\frac{dI}{I} = -k_1 \, d\ell$$

**Step 2:** Integrating from entry ($\ell = 0$, $I = I_0$) to exit ($\ell = \ell$, $I = I$):

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) = -k_1 \ell$$

Converting to base-10 logarithm ($\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$):

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I_0}{I}\right) = \frac{k_1}{2.303} \, \ell = k_2 \, \ell$$

**Step 3:** Recognizing the left side as absorbance $A$, and noting that $A$ is also proportional to concentration (Beer's Law), we combine both:

$$\boxed{\,A = \varepsilon \, c \, \ell\,}$$

**Limitations (3.5 marks; ~0.9 marks each):**

1. **Deviation at high concentrations** (>0.01 M): Molecules interact with each other, changing their effective ε. The plot of A vs c becomes curved.
2. **Scattering by suspended particles:** Polymer aggregates, dust, or micro-bubbles in the solution scatter light, producing a false-positive absorbance.
3. **Stray light in the instrument:** Imperfect monochromator design allows unwanted wavelengths to reach the detector, causing non-linearity especially at A > 1.
4. **Chemical reactions of the solute:** Dissociation, association, or reaction with the solvent changes the absorbing species, making ε concentration-dependent.

---

**(b) Explain the applications of UV-Vis spectrometry in polymer testing with suitable examples. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Identification of Polymers and Additives (2 marks)**
Each polymer/additive has a characteristic absorption spectrum. By comparing the spectrum of an unknown with standard reference spectra, qualitative identification is possible. UV-stabilizers like benzotriazoles show strong absorption around **300–350 nm**.

**2. Determination of Additive Concentration (2 marks)**
Using Beer-Lambert Law, the concentration of an absorber in a polymer matrix is quantified. For example, the concentration of HALS (Hindered Amine Light Stabilizer) in a polypropylene masterbatch can be determined by dissolving the polymer in a solvent and measuring absorbance at the characteristic wavelength.

**3. Monitoring Polymer Degradation (2 marks)**
During accelerated weathering (QUV or Xenon arc), new chromophoric groups form. For example:
- **Carbonyl group formation** during photo-oxidation of polyethylene is monitored by growth of the **n → π\*** absorption at **~280 nm**.
- **Yellowing index** of aged polystyrene or polycarbonate is tracked via increased absorption in the **400–450 nm** region.

**4. Optical Property Evaluation (1 mark)**
UV-Vis is used to determine the **transparency**, **haze**, and **cut-off wavelength** of polymer films per ASTM D1003. Conjugated conducting polymers (polyaniline, PEDOT:PSS) are characterized by their **bandgap** determined from the absorption edge.

> **Examiner's Note (7 marks):** Applications carry 7 marks. Allocate ~1.5–2 marks per major application, ensuring each has a specific example with numerical wavelength data where possible.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Rapid-Revision Checklist for UV-Vis Spectrometry (PCPOT602 — Module 3)**

- [ ] UV-Vis covers **200–700 nm**: UV (200–400 nm) + Visible (400–700 nm).
- [ ] **Beer-Lambert Law** in all three equivalent forms: $A = \varepsilon c \ell$, $T = 10^{-A}$, $I = I_0 \cdot 10^{-\varepsilon c \ell}$.
- [ ] **Units Mastery:** $\varepsilon$ in L mol⁻¹ cm⁻¹; $c$ in mol/L; $\ell$ in cm; $A$ is dimensionless.
- [ ] **Transition Hierarchy (highest to lowest energy):** σ→σ* > n→σ* > π→π* > n→π*.
- [ ] **Conjugation increases λ_max** — long conjugation shifts absorption into visible region (colored polymers).
- [ ] **Auxochromes** (–OH, –NH₂, –Cl) cause bathochromic (red) shift and hyperchromic effect.
- [ ] **Instrumentation Sequence:** Source → Monochromator → Sample → Detector → Recorder.
- [ ] **Light Sources:** Deuterium lamp (UV) + Tungsten-Halogen lamp (Visible).
- [ ] **Cuvettes** must be **quartz** (not glass or plastic) for UV region measurements.
- [ ] **Key Limitation:** Beer-Lambert Law fails at high concentrations (>0.01 M) and in presence of scattered light.
- [ ] **Polymer-Specific Application:** Photo-oxidative degradation is monitored by growth of carbonyl absorption at **~280 nm**.
- [ ] **Calculation Tricks:** If $A = 1$, $T = 10\%$; $A = 2$, $T = 1\%$; $A = 3$, $T = 0.1\%$.
- [ ] **Photodegradation Detection:** Carbonyl Index = $A_{1710 \text{ cm}^{-1}} \mid A_{reference}$ (IR-coupled) or $A_{280 \text{ nm}}$ (UV-Vis).
- [ ] **ASTM Standard for Transparency/Haze:** ASTM D1003 — frequently cited in KTU polymer optics questions.

> **End of Topic Notes — Ultraviolet-Visible Spectrometry**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
