<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔬 Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) Spectrometry

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) Spectrometry** is a non-destructive, high-resolution analytical technique that exploits the magnetic properties of certain atomic nuclei (notably **¹H, ¹³C, ¹⁹F, and ³¹P**) to elucidate the molecular structure, tacticity, crystallinity, copolymer composition, and end-group identity of polymeric materials. When a sample is placed in a strong, uniform static magnetic field ($B_0$) and irradiated with a pulse of radiofrequency (RF) radiation, the nuclei absorb and re-emit energy at characteristic resonance frequencies governed by the **Larmor equation**.

In the context of **Polymer Testing (PCPOT602)**, NMR is a **first-tier characterization tool** because it can probe the local chemical environment of a monomeric unit in solution (Solution NMR) or in the solid state (Solid-State NMR, e.g., CP-MAS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> NMR is covered under the "Spectrometric Techniques for Polymer Analysis" cluster. The examiner expects you to know the **principle, instrumentation (CW vs FT), chemical shift, and polymer-specific applications** (tacticity, dyad/triad analysis, copolymer composition).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The Spinning Top in Earth's Magnetic Field

Imagine you have a **spinning top (gyroscope)**. On a frictionless table, it will spin upright forever. Now, if you tilt it slightly off the vertical axis, it does not fall — it starts **precessing** (wobbling in a circle) around the vertical.

Replace the top with a **hydrogen nucleus (¹H)** which has an intrinsic spin ($\vec{I}$). The Earth's gravity is the **external magnetic field ($B_0$)** of the NMR magnet. The nucleus, like the top, cannot align with $B_0$ perfectly — it precesses around the field axis at a specific frequency called the **Larmor frequency ($\nu_0$)**.

Now, if you blow on the top (your "push") at *exactly* the right rhythm (frequency), the top will absorb energy and wobble harder — this is **resonance**. In NMR, the "push" is a **radiofrequency (RF) pulse** broadcast at the Larmor frequency. The nucleus absorbs this energy, flips its spin state, and then re-emits it as the **Free Induction Decay (FID)** signal, which is mathematically transformed (Fourier Transform) into the familiar NMR spectrum.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does NMR care about polymers?** A polymer is just a long, repeating chain of "magnetically active" nuclei. Each ¹H or ¹³C sitting in a slightly different chemical neighborhood (CH₃ vs CH₂ vs aromatic ring) feels a *slightly different* local field and resonates at a *slightly different* frequency. This frequency difference — called the **chemical shift ($\delta$)** — is the fingerprint of that chemical group.

---

## 1.3 Core Physical Constants and Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Gyromagnetic ratio (¹H) | $\gamma_H$ | **2.675 × 10⁸ rad T⁻¹ s⁻¹** | Determines Larmor frequency |
| Planck's constant | $h$ | **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s** | Quantum of energy transition |
| Gyromagnetic ratio (¹³C) | $\gamma_C$ | **6.728 × 10⁷ rad T⁻¹ s⁻¹** | ~4× lower than ¹H |
| Standard reference | TMS | **δ = 0.00 ppm** | Tetramethylsilane internal standard |
| Magnet field strengths | $B_0$ | **1.4 T – 21.1 T** (60–900 MHz) | Higher field = better resolution |
| Natural abundance (¹³C) | — | **1.108 %** | Why ¹³C NMR is less sensitive |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Theory

### Step 1 — Nuclear Spin Quantum Number ($I$)
Only nuclei with non-zero spin ($I \neq 0$) are NMR-active.
- $I = 1/2$ nuclei: **¹H, ¹³C, ¹⁹F, ³¹P, ¹⁵N** → give sharp, well-resolved spectra (most useful).
- $I = 1$ nuclei: **²H, ¹⁴N** → broad lines, quadrupolar broadening.
- $I = 0$ nuclei: **¹²C, ¹⁶O** → NMR silent (this is why ¹³C, not ¹²C, is used).

### Step 2 — Zeeman Splitting
In an external field $B_0$, the $2I + 1$ degenerate spin states split into discrete energy levels separated by:
$$\Delta E = \gamma \hbar B_0 = h \nu_0$$
The two populations (N$_\alpha$ and N$_\beta$) are governed by the **Boltzmann distribution**:
$$\frac{N_\beta}{N_\alpha} = e^{-\Delta E / k_B T}$$
At room temperature and 9.4 T, the excess population is only ~**1 in 10,000** — explaining why NMR is inherently insensitive (requires many scans).

### Step 3 — The Larmor Frequency (Resonance Condition)
The precession frequency of the magnetization vector about $B_0$ is the **Larmor frequency**:
$$\nu_0 = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi}$$
- **Why it matters:** The RF transmitter must irradiate at $\nu_0$ for resonance absorption. Any deviation in local $B_{eff}$ (due to electron shielding) shifts the effective resonance.

### Step 4 — Chemical Shift ($\delta$)
The local electrons in a molecule generate a small **shielding field** $B_{shield}$ that opposes $B_0$. The effective field at the nucleus is:
$$B_{eff} = B_0 (1 - \sigma)$$
where $\sigma$ is the **shielding (or screening) constant**. Since $\sigma$ is tiny (~10⁻⁵), we measure the **chemical shift** in dimensionless parts-per-million (ppm):
$$\delta \text{ (ppm)} = \frac{\nu_{sample} - \nu_{reference}}{\nu_{spectrometer}} \times 10^6$$
**Why ppm?** Because ppm is *field-independent* — a peak at $\delta = 7.26$ ppm (CHCl₃) on a 400 MHz and a 900 MHz instrument reports the **same** $\delta$ value, although the absolute Hz difference changes.

### Step 5 — Spin-Spin Coupling (J-Coupling)
Nuclei interact with each other through **bonds** (not through space). This scalar coupling splits a peak into multiplets following the **n + 1 rule**:
- A proton coupled to **n** equivalent neighboring protons splits into **(n + 1)** peaks.
- The spacing between sub-peaks is the **coupling constant $J$** (in Hz), which is **field-independent**.

For polymers, J-coupling is used to identify **dyads** (mm, mr, rr for vinyl polymers) and **triads** (mm, mr/rm, rr) — the building blocks of tacticity analysis.

### Step 6 — Relaxation Processes
After RF excitation, the magnetization returns to equilibrium via two time constants:
- **T₁ (Spin-Lattice / Longitudinal):** Energy exchange with surroundings. Typical for polymers: **0.1 – 10 s**.
- **T₂ (Spin-Spin / Transverse):** Loss of phase coherence among spins. Typical: **10 µs – 1 s**.

In polymers, **T₂ is highly diagnostic of molecular mobility** — rigid crystalline regions have short T₂; flexible amorphous chains have long T₂.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation | Engineering Use in Polymer Testing |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Larmor frequency | $\nu_0 = \dfrac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi}$ | Sets the operating frequency of the spectrometer |
| 2 | Energy gap | $\Delta E = h \nu_0 = \gamma \hbar B_0$ | Determines Boltzmann population difference |
| 3 | Chemical shift | $\delta = \dfrac{\nu_{sample} - \nu_{ref}}{\nu_0} \times 10^6$ ppm | Identifies functional groups (CH₃, CH₂, Ar-H, C=O) |
| 4 | Boltzmann population | $N_\beta / N_\alpha = e^{-\Delta E / k_B T}$ | Explains low NMR sensitivity |
| 5 | n+1 multiplet rule | Number of peaks = $n + 1$ | Determines number of neighboring protons |
| 6 | Coupling constant | $J$ (Hz), field-independent | Distinguishes diastereotopic dyads in tacticity |
| 7 | Tacticity (triad) | $P_m = \dfrac{I_{mm} + \tfrac{1}{2} I_{mr}}{I_{mm} + I_{mr} + I_{rr}}$ | Quantifies isotacticity of PP, PMMA, PS |
| 8 | Copolymer composition | $F_1 = \dfrac{n_1 \, I_1}{n_1 \, I_1 + n_2 \, I_2}$ | Mole fraction of monomer 1 in copolymer |
| 9 | FID → Spectrum | $S(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} s(t) e^{-i \omega t} dt$ | FT-NMR signal processing |
| 10 | Free induction decay | $s(t) = s(0) \, e^{-t / T_2^*}$ | Time-domain signal after RF pulse |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbols used:** $\gamma$ = gyromagnetic ratio, $h$ = Planck's constant, $\hbar = h/2\pi$, $\sigma$ = shielding constant, $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant, $I$ = signal intensity, $P_m$ = probability of meso placement, $F_1$ = mole fraction of monomer 1.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Polymer Engineering

1. **Tacticity Quantification** — ¹H/¹³C NMR gives mm/mr/rr triad distributions for isotactic vs syndiotactic PP and PS.
2. **Copolymer Composition** — S/N, E/P, MMA/Styrene copolymers: integration of characteristic peaks gives mole fractions.
3. **Molecular Weight (End-Group Analysis)** — for oligomers and low-MW polymers, the end-group signal area relative to repeat-unit area gives $M_n$.
4. **Crystallinity & Chain Dynamics** — Solid-state CP-MAS ¹³C NMR measures T₁ρ and T₁ᵍ, distinguishing crystalline vs amorphous domains.
5. **Miscibility in Polymer Blends** — changes in T₁ or chemical shift on blending indicate specific interactions.
6. **Reaction Monitoring** — in-situ NMR follows monomer conversion in free-radical polymerizations.
7. **Defect Detection** — Branching, head-to-head vs head-to-tail addition, and regio-irregularities are revealed by minor peaks.

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Energy Gap from Classical Precession

A nucleus of spin $\vec{I}$ possesses a magnetic moment $\vec{\mu}$ proportional to its spin:
$$\vec{\mu} = \gamma \hbar \vec{I}$$

The torque on this magnetic dipole in a static field $\vec{B_0} = B_0 \hat{z}$ is:
$$\vec{\tau} = \vec{\mu} \times \vec{B_0}$$

This torque equals the rate of change of angular momentum, leading to the equation of motion:
$$\frac{d\vec{\mu}}{dt} = \vec{\mu} \times \gamma \vec{B_0}$$

In the rotating frame, the component of $\vec{\mu}$ transverse to $B_0$ precesses with angular frequency:
$$\omega_0 = \gamma B_0$$

Since $\omega_0 = 2\pi \nu_0$, the **Larmor frequency** in Hz is:
$$\nu_0 = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi}$$

The energy of interaction between $\vec{\mu}$ and $\vec{B_0}$ is:
$$E = -\vec{\mu} \cdot \vec{B_0} = -\gamma \hbar m_I B_0$$

For $I = 1/2$ nuclei (¹H, ¹³C), the two allowed projections are $m_I = +1/2$ and $m_I = -1/2$. Their energies are:
$$E_{\alpha} = -\frac{1}{2} \gamma \hbar B_0, \qquad E_{\beta} = +\frac{1}{2} \gamma \hbar B_0$$

The energy gap is therefore:
$$\Delta E = E_{\beta} - E_{\alpha} = \gamma \hbar B_0 = h \nu_0$$

This is the **fundamental resonance condition** of NMR: irradiate at frequency $\nu_0$ to flip the spin.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — The Chemical Shift (Dimensional Analysis)

The resonance condition above assumes a *bare* nucleus. In a real molecule, the electron cloud circulates under $B_0$ and induces a **secondary magnetic field** at the nucleus that *opposes* $B_0$. This is the **diamagnetic shielding** effect.

The induced field at the nucleus is proportional to the applied field:
$$B_{induced} = -\sigma \, B_0$$

The effective field the nucleus actually "sees" is:
$$B_{eff} = B_0 + B_{induced} = B_0 (1 - \sigma)$$

Substituting into the Larmor equation:
$$\nu_{sample} = \frac{\gamma \, B_0 (1 - \sigma)}{2\pi}$$

The reference compound (TMS, $\sigma_{ref}$) resonates at:
$$\nu_{ref} = \frac{\gamma \, B_0 (1 - \sigma_{ref})}{2\pi}$$

The frequency difference (in Hz) is:
$$\Delta \nu = \nu_{ref} - \nu_{sample} = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi} (\sigma_{sample} - \sigma_{ref})$$

To remove the dependence on spectrometer frequency $\nu_0$, we normalize by $\nu_0$ and multiply by $10^6$ to obtain **ppm**:
$$\delta = \frac{\Delta \nu}{\nu_0} \times 10^6 \text{ ppm} = \frac{\nu_{ref} - \nu_{sample}}{\nu_0} \times 10^6$$

**Key Insight:** $\delta$ is **field-independent** because $\Delta \nu$ is proportional to $B_0$ (and so is $\nu_0$), so the ratio is constant. This is why a CHCl₃ peak is always at 7.26 ppm regardless of the magnet.

---

## 3.3 Derivation III — Tacticity from Triad Populations (Polymer Application)

For a vinyl polymer –CH₂–CHR–, three stereo-sequences (triads) are possible:
- **mm** (meso-meso): two successive *meso* placements → isotactic
- **rr** (racemo-racemo): two successive *racemo* placements → syndiotactic
- **mr / rm** (heterotactic): one meso, one racemo

From ¹H or ¹³C NMR, the integrals of the three dyad/triad peaks ($I_{mm}$, $I_{mr}$, $I_{rr}$) sum to unity:
$$I_{mm} + I_{mr} + I_{rr} = 1$$

The **probability of meso placement** is:
$$P_m = I_{mm} + \frac{1}{2} I_{mr}$$

This follows from the fact that each *m* placement contributes to either an *mm* triad (counted in $I_{mm}$) or a heterotactic triad (counted in $I_{mr}$, weighted by ½). Similarly:
$$P_r = I_{rr} + \frac{1}{2} I_{mr}$$

For a **Bernoullian (random) polymerization**, $P_m + P_r = 1$ and $I_{mr} = 2 P_m P_r$.

**Worked Example (KTU-style):**
For a PMMA sample, ¹³C NMR gives the α-CH₃ triad integrals:
- $I_{mm} = 0.16$
- $I_{mr} = 0.48$
- $I_{rr} = 0.36$

Verify normalization: $0.16 + 0.48 + 0.36 = 1.00$ ✓

Calculate:
$$P_m = 0.16 + \frac{1}{2}(0.48) = 0.16 + 0.24 = 0.40$$

$$P_r = 0.36 + \frac{1}{2}(0.48) = 0.36 + 0.24 = 0.60$$

This PMMA is **predominantly syndiotactic** ($P_r > P_m$). If the polymerization is Bernoullian, the predicted $I_{mr} = 2 P_m P_r = 2(0.40)(0.60) = 0.48$, which matches the observed value — confirming a random (statistical) chain-growth mechanism.

---

## 3.4 Derivation IV — Copolymer Composition from ¹H NMR

For a copolymer of monomers M₁ and M₂, the mole fraction of M₁ is:
$$F_1 = \frac{n_1 A_1}{n_1 A_1 + n_2 A_2}$$

where:
- $n_1, n_2$ = number of ¹H nuclei per repeat unit that give the *chosen* diagnostic peak
- $A_1, A_2$ = integrated area under that peak

**Worked Example:** A styrene (S) – methyl methacrylate (MMA) copolymer. Choose the diagnostic peaks:
- Aromatic protons of **styrene** (5 H, 6.5–7.5 ppm): $A_S = 50$
- –OCH₃ of **MMA** (3 H, 3.5–3.8 ppm): $A_M = 30$

$$F_S = \frac{5 \times 50}{5 \times 50 + 3 \times 30} = \frac{250}{250 + 90} = \frac{250}{340} = 0.735$$

$$F_M = 1 - 0.735 = 0.265$$

So the copolymer is **73.5 mol% styrene** and **26.5 mol% MMA**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** Always pick *non-overlapping* peaks for $A_1$ and $A_2$. If peaks overlap, use ¹³C NMR (wider dispersion) or selective decoupling, or use the **Kelen-Tüdős** reactivity-ratio approach.

# 🧩 Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — FT-NMR Spectrometer Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional signal-flow architecture** of a modern Fourier-Transform NMR spectrometer, which is the standard configuration in polymer testing laboratories.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SAMPLE_PREP ["SAMPLE PREPARATION"]
        SP1["Polymer dissolution in CDCl3 or DMSO-d6"] --> SP2["Filtration into 5 mm NMR tube"]
        SP2 --> SP3["Add TMS internal standard"]
    end

    subgraph MAGNET ["SUPERCONDUCTING MAGNET ASSEMBLY"]
        MG1["Cryostat: Liquid He 4K bath"] --> MG2["Superconducting Nb-Ti coil B0 = 7 to 21 T"]
        MG2 --> MG3["Room temperature bore with shim coils"]
    end

    subgraph PROBE ["RF PROBE ASSEMBLY"]
        PR1["Sample tube inserted into probe"] --> PR2["Transmitter coil: broadcasts RF pulse at Larmor frequency"]
        PR2 --> PR3["Receiver coil: detects FID signal"]
        PR1 --> PR4["Gradient coil Gz for coherence selection"]
    end

    subgraph CONSOLE ["PULSE PROGRAMMER AND RECEIVER"]
        CP1["Pulse sequence generator: 90 degree pulse then delay"] --> CP2["RF synthesizer and amplifier"]
        CP2 --> CP3["Duplexer switches Tx to Rx mode"]
        CP3 --> CP4["Preamplifier and digitizer: AD converter 100 MS/s"]
    end

    subgraph COMPUTER ["DATA ACQUISITION AND PROCESSING"]
        DA1["Accumulate FIDs over n scans"] --> DA2["Apodization window function"]
        DA2 --> DA3["Fourier Transform: time domain to frequency domain"]
        DA3 --> DA4["Phase correction and baseline correction"]
        DA4 --> DA5["Peak picking, integration, chemical shift calibration"]
    end

    SAMPLE_PREP --> MAGNET
    MAGNET --> PROBE
    PROBE --> CONSOLE
    CONSOLE --> COMPUTER
```

# 📚 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define chemical shift. Why is it expressed in ppm?**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PCPOT602]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Chemical shift ($\delta$) is the dimensionless measure of the resonance frequency of a nucleus relative to a standard reference compound (TMS), reflecting the local electronic (shielding) environment of that nucleus.

**Expression (1 mark):**
$$\delta = \frac{\nu_{sample} - \nu_{TMS}}{\nu_{spectrometer}} \times 10^6 \text{ ppm}$$

**Why ppm (0.5 mark):** Because the absolute frequency difference $\Delta \nu$ is proportional to $B_0$ (and thus to the spectrometer frequency), expressing $\delta$ as a ratio in ppm makes it a **field-independent** quantity. A peak reported as $\delta = 7.26$ ppm (CHCl₃) has the *same* $\delta$ on a 400 MHz, 600 MHz, or 900 MHz instrument.

---

### **Question 2: What is meant by Larmor frequency? State the Larmor equation.**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PCPOT602]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The Larmor frequency is the characteristic precessional frequency at which the nuclear magnetic moment rotates about the axis of the external static magnetic field $B_0$ under resonance conditions.

**Larmor Equation (1 mark):**
$$\nu_0 = \frac{\gamma B_0}{2\pi}$$

where $\gamma$ is the gyromagnetic ratio (rad T⁻¹ s⁻¹) and $B_0$ is the applied field strength (Tesla).

**Example (0.5 mark):** For ¹H ($\gamma = 2.675 \times 10^8$ rad T⁻¹ s⁻¹) in a 9.4 T magnet, $\nu_0 \approx 400$ MHz.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PCPOT602, Module 3]`

**(a)** Describe the principle of NMR spectroscopy. Explain the concept of nuclear spin, Zeeman splitting, and the resonance condition. (7 marks)

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the instrumentation of an FT-NMR spectrometer. Compare CW-NMR with FT-NMR. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution (a) — Principle of NMR (7 marks)**

**1. Nuclear Spin (1.5 marks):**
Certain nuclei (¹H, ¹³C, ¹⁹F, ³¹P) possess a non-zero intrinsic angular momentum called **nuclear spin**, characterized by the spin quantum number $I$. The associated magnetic moment is $\vec{\mu} = \gamma \hbar \vec{I}$.

**2. Zeeman Effect (2 marks):**
In the absence of a magnetic field, all $2I + 1$ spin states are degenerate. When an external field $B_0$ is applied, these states split into discrete energy levels (Zeeman splitting). For $I = 1/2$ nuclei, two levels appear:
$$E_\alpha = -\tfrac{1}{2} \gamma \hbar B_0, \qquad E_\beta = +\tfrac{1}{2} \gamma \hbar B_0$$

The population ratio follows the Boltzmann distribution:
$$\frac{N_\beta}{N_\alpha} = e^{-\gamma \hbar B_0 / k_B T}$$

**3. Resonance Condition (2 marks):**
When a radiofrequency (RF) pulse at the **Larmor frequency** $\nu_0 = \gamma B_0 / 2\pi$ is applied, nuclei absorb energy and flip from $\alpha$ to $\beta$. The absorption is detected as the **Free Induction Decay (FID)**.

**4. Chemical Shift and J-Coupling (1.5 marks):**
The local electronic environment modifies $B_{eff}$ via shielding ($\sigma$), causing the chemical shift $\delta$. Indirect coupling between neighbouring nuclei (mediated by bonds) splits the peaks into multiplets (J-coupling, n+1 rule).

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — FT-NMR Instrumentation and CW vs FT (7 marks)**

**1. Block Diagram (3 marks):**
*(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes for the functional flow.)*

The key subsystems are:
- **Magnet assembly** — superconducting coil in a liquid-He cryostat, generating $B_0$.
- **Probe** — houses the sample tube; contains transmitter and receiver coils.
- **Pulsed RF source** — synthesizes a short, intense RF pulse at $\nu_0$.
- **Receiver and digitizer** — detects the FID and digitizes it at high sampling rate.
- **Computer** — performs Fourier Transform, phase correction, integration.

**2. Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | CW-NMR | FT-NMR |
|---|---|---|
| Excitation | Continuous slow sweep of frequency | Short RF pulse excites all frequencies simultaneously |
| Detection | Frequency domain (direct) | Time domain (FID) → FT → frequency domain |
| Sensitivity | Low — one frequency at a time | High — accumulates *n* scans coherently; S/N improves as $\sqrt{n}$ |
| Time per spectrum | 5–10 min | Seconds (1 scan) to minutes (accumulated) |
| Resolution | Limited | Excellent — phase cycling and shimming |
| Pulsed field gradients | Not available | Available for coherence selection |
| Use today | Obsolete (educational demos) | Industry standard |

**3. Conclusion (1 mark):**
FT-NMR is the universal modern technique because of its superior sensitivity, speed, and ability to perform multidimensional experiments (COSY, HSQC, NOESY) — all essential for polymer structure elucidation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PCPOT602, Module 3]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of chemical shift. Discuss the various proton chemical shifts for aliphatic, aromatic, olefinic, and aldehydic protons with reference to polymer structures. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss in detail the application of ¹H and ¹³C NMR in polymer characterization — tacticity determination, copolymer composition, and end-group analysis. (7 marks)

---

#### **Model Solution (a) — Chemical Shift & Polymer Reference Values (7 marks)**

**1. Concept (2 marks):**
Chemical shift $\delta$ arises from diamagnetic shielding of the nucleus by surrounding electrons. The induced secondary field opposes $B_0$, lowering $B_{eff}$ and decreasing $\nu_0$. Electron-withdrawing groups (–Cl, –C=O, –NO₂) *deshield* (increase $\delta$); electron-donating groups (–CH₃, –OR) *shield* (decrease $\delta$).

**2. Typical ¹H Chemical Shifts in Polymers (3 marks):**

| Proton Type | Example in Polymer | $\delta$ (ppm) |
|---|---|---|
| Aliphatic –CH₃ | PP, PMMA methyl | 0.8 – 1.2 |
| Aliphatic –CH₂– | PE, PVC methylene | 1.2 – 1.6 |
| Aliphatic –CH– (methine) | PS backbone CH | 1.6 – 2.2 |
| Allylic (–C=C–CH₃) | Polyisoprene | 1.6 – 1.8 |
| α to carbonyl (–CH₂–C=O) | PMMA, PVAc | 2.0 – 2.5 |
| Aromatic | Polystyrene, PET | 6.5 – 7.8 |
| Olefinic (–CH=CH–) | 1,4-polybutadiene | 5.0 – 5.8 |
| Vinyl (–CH=CH₂) | Polyacrylates | 5.5 – 6.5 |
| Aldehydic (–CHO) | End group of oxidized polymer | 9.5 – 10.0 |
| Hydroxyl (–OH) | PVA, phenol-formaldehyde | 1.0 – 5.0 (broad, H-bonded) |

**3. Practical Note (2 marks):**
In polymers, peak broadening due to:
- Distribution of chain conformations
- Tacticity-induced chemical shift dispersion
- Dipolar coupling in the solid state

is overcome by **solution NMR (CDCl₃)** for tacticity work, or by **CP-MAS solid-state NMR** for insoluble polymers (e.g., cured resins, crystalline PE).

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — Applications in Polymer Characterization (7 marks)**

**1. Tacticity (2.5 marks):**
The α-CH₃ triad (mm, mr, rr) in PMMA, PP, and PS is well-resolved in ¹³C NMR. For example, in PMMA the carbonyl carbon (C=O, ~176–178 ppm) and the α-CH₃ (16–22 ppm) both split into three peaks for mm, mr, rr. The probability of meso placement:
$$P_m = I_{mm} + \tfrac{1}{2} I_{mr}$$
A perfectly isotactic polymer has $P_m = 1.0$; syndiotactic has $P_m = 0$.

**2. Copolymer Composition (2.5 marks):**
For styrene-MMA copolymer, ¹H NMR integration of the aromatic (5H, 7 ppm) and methoxy (3H, 3.6 ppm) signals gives the mole fraction directly (as derived in Section 3.4). ¹³C NMR is preferred for butadiene-acrylonitrile or ethylene-propylene copolymers where ¹H peaks overlap.

**3. End-Group Analysis (2 marks):**
For low-MW polymers ($M_n < 10{,}000$), the chain-end protons (e.g., initiator fragments, vinyl end groups) give distinct peaks. The number-average molecular weight is:
$$M_n = \frac{n_E \cdot M_0 \cdot I_R}{I_E}$$
where $I_R$ = repeat-unit integral, $I_E$ = end-group integral, $n_E$ = protons in end group, $M_0$ = repeat-unit molar mass.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Mixing up T₁ and T₂** — T₁ is the *longitudinal* (spin-lattice) relaxation; T₂ is *transverse* (spin-spin). Marks are routinely lost for swapping these.
> 2. **Forgetting that δ is field-independent** — students often report δ in Hz; the examiner expects ppm.
> 3. **Confusing chemical shift with coupling constant** — J (Hz) is a *peak splitting* measurement, NOT a chemical shift. A J value of 7 Hz on a 400 MHz instrument is still 7 Hz on a 600 MHz instrument, but its equivalent in ppm differs.
> 4. **Not normalizing triad integrals** — when calculating $P_m$, ensure $I_{mm} + I_{mr} + I_{rr} = 1$. Always state the sum.
> 5. **Confusing natural abundance of ¹³C (1.108%) with 100%** — for quantitative ¹³C NMR, use NOE suppression + inverse-gated decoupling + paramagnetic relaxation (e.g., Cr(acac)₃) and long relaxation delays.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🧠 High-Yield Bullet Summary for KTU 2024 Exam Day:**

- ✅ NMR exploits **nuclear spin ($I = 1/2$)** in a strong magnetic field $B_0$ → **Zeeman splitting** → RF absorption at **Larmor frequency** $\nu_0 = \gamma B_0 / 2\pi$.
- ✅ **Chemical shift $\delta$ (ppm)** is a field-independent fingerprint of chemical environment; **TMS = 0.00 ppm** is the universal reference.
- ✅ **n + 1 rule** governs multiplet splitting; **J (Hz)** is field-independent.
- ✅ **¹H NMR** → high sensitivity, fast, used for tacticity, composition, end groups.
- ✅ **¹³C NMR** → wider dispersion (~200 ppm) but only 1.108% natural abundance → needs FT + NOE suppression.
- ✅ **Solid-state CP-MAS NMR** → for insoluble polymers (crosslinked rubbers, crystalline PE, cured resins).
- ✅ **FT-NMR** dominates modern practice; **CW-NMR** is obsolete.
- ✅ **Polymer applications:** tacticity (triad $P_m$), copolymer composition (integration), $M_n$ (end-group), crystallinity (T₁ᵍ), blend miscibility (Δδ).
- ✅ Always state **$B_0$ (in T) and spectrometer frequency (in MHz)** when reporting NMR data.
- ✅ Remember the typical δ ranges: **aliphatic 0–2 ppm, α-carbonyl 2–3 ppm, aromatic 6.5–8 ppm, aldehyde 9.5–10 ppm**.
- ✅ For a 14-mark question, draw a **block diagram of FT-NMR** and tabulate **CW vs FT**.
- ✅ Practise at least **two numerical problems**: (i) triad tacticity, (ii) copolymer composition — these are the most asked KTU questions.
- ✅ Boltzmann population difference is **~1 in 10,000** at 9.4 T — explains low sensitivity → FT accumulation is essential.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mass Spectrometry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT602 - POLYMER TESTING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Mass Spectrometry # Mass Spectrometry in Polymer Testing ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Mass Spectrometry (MS)** is an analytical technique that measures the **mass-to-charge ratio (m/z)** of ionized molecules. In polymer testing, it is used to determine the **absolute molecular weight**, **molecular weight distribution (MWD)**, **end-group identity**, **repeat unit composition**, **copolymer sequencing**, and **structural defects** (chain branching, oxidation products) of polymeric materials. The fundamental principle relies on converting neutral analyte molecules into gas-phase ions (with or without fragmentation), separating these ions based on their m/z values using electric or magnetic fields, and recording their relative abundance to generate a **mass spectrum**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - PCPOT602 Module 3):** > Students are expected to understand (i) the principle and instrumentation of mass spectrometry, (ii) soft ionization techniques such as **MALDI** and **ESI** that are crucial for non-volatile polymer samples, (iii) the role of mass analyzers like **Time-of-Flight (TOF)** and **Quadrupole**, and (iv) interpretation of mass spectra for polymer characterization. ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy Think of mass spectrometry as an **"atomic postal sorting system"** at a high-speed logistics hub: 1. **Package Creation (Ionization):** Imagine a warehouse full of different parcels (polymer molecules). The first step is to give each parcel a **uniform shipping label (charge)** and convert them into individual flying objects (gas-phase ions). 2. **Sorting Conveyor Belt (Mass Analyzer):** Parcels are then thrown into a long conveyor belt with a twist — light parcels (low m/z) travel faster, while heavy parcels (high m/z) lag behind. At the end of the belt, a **camera (detector)** takes a snapshot of when each parcel arrives. 3. **Catalogue (Mass Spectrum):** The system produces a catalogue showing the arrival times (mass) and the count of each parcel type (abundance). From this catalogue, you can deduce the identity, weight, and even the structure of every parcel that entered the system. In polymers, this means we can literally **count the number of repeat units** in a single chain and identify **chemical modifications** — something no other single technique provides with such precision. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Metrics The following constants and units are foundational to mass spectrometry calculations: - **Elementary charge (e):** $1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$ - **Avogadro's number ($N_A$):** $6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$ - **m/z unit:** Thomson (Th), where $1 \text{ Th} = 1 \text{ Dalton/e}$ (dimensionless) - **Mass resolution (R):** $R = \dfrac{m}{\Delta m}$ (dimensionless) - **Mass accuracy:** expressed in **ppm** (parts per million) --- # Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Concept — Broken into Structured Logic Steps Mass spectrometry, despite its complexity, follows a **modular pipeline architecture**. Every mass spectrometer — regardless of manufacturer or specialization — contains five functional modules: ### Step 1: Sample Introduction The polymer (often dissolved in a volatile solvent like **THF, chloroform, or methanol**) is introduced into a high-vacuum chamber ($10^{-5}$ to $10^{-7}$ Pa). For polymers, conventional GC-MS introduction is **not feasible** because polymers are non-volatile and thermally labile. ### Step 2: Ionization Neutral molecules are converted into ions. For polymers, the **soft ionization techniques** are mandatory to avoid thermal degradation. ### Step 3: Ion Acceleration and Mass Analysis Ions are accelerated through an electric potential $V$ and sorted by mass analyzers based on m/z. ### Step 4: Detection The separated ions strike a detector (electron multiplier, microchannel plate, or Faraday cup), producing a measurable electrical signal proportional to ion abundance. ### Step 5: Data Processing The instrument's software converts signal intensity vs. m/z into the **mass spectrum**, from which molecular weight parameters are computed. ## 2.2 Soft Ionization Techniques for Polymers (Critical for KTU Board Exams) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hard vs. Soft Ionization:** > - **Hard ionization** (e.g., Electron Ionization, EI at 70 eV) causes extensive fragmentation — useful for small molecules but destroys polymer chains. > - **Soft ionization** preserves the molecular ion intact — **mandatory for polymer MS**. | Technique | Full Name | Ionization Mechanism | Polymer Application | |-----------|-----------|---------------------|---------------------| | **MALDI** | Matrix-Assisted Laser Desorption/Ionization | Laser pulse ablates matrix + analyte | Synthetic polymers, biopolymers, narrow MWD determination | | **ESI** | Electrospray Ionization | High voltage creates charged droplets | Water-soluble polymers, polyelectrolytes | | **FAB** | Fast Atom Bombardment | High-energy atom beam (Xe, Ar)溅射 | Mid-polarity polymers | | **FD** | Field Desorption | Strong electric field desorbs ions | Non-polar, high-MW polymers | | **SIMS** | Secondary Ion Mass Spectrometry | Ion beam sputters surface | Surface analysis of polymer films | ## 2.3 Mass Analyzers Used in Polymer MS 1. **Time-of-Flight (TOF):** Most common with MALDI. Measures the flight time of ions over a known distance. 2. **Quadrupole:** Uses four parallel rods with oscillating RF/DC voltages. Acts as a mass filter. 3. **Magnetic Sector:** Uses magnetic field to deflect ions based on momentum. 4. **Ion Trap (3D Quadrupole / Linear):** Traps ions using RF fields, then ejects them sequentially. 5. **Fourier Transform Ion Cyclotron Resonance (FT-ICR):** Ultra-high resolution; measures cyclotron frequency in a magnetic field. ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **The following formulas are exam-critical. Memorize the units and boundary conditions.** | # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Description of Variables | |---|--------------------|---------|--------------------------| | 1 | **Kinetic Energy in Ion Source** | $z \cdot e \cdot V = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2$ | $z$ = charge number, $e$ = elementary charge, $V$ = accelerating potential, $m$ = ion mass, $v$ = velocity | | 2 | **TOF Drift Velocity** | $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 z e V}{m}}$ | Derived from KE equation — ions with same KE but different m have different velocities | | 3 | **TOF Time of Flight** | $t = \dfrac{L}{v} = L \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{2 z e V}}$ | $L$ = drift tube length | | 4 | **m/z from TOF** | $\dfrac{m}{z} = \dfrac{2 e V t^2}{L^2}$ | Linear relationship: $m/z \propto t^2$ | | 5 | **Magnetic Sector Radius** | $r = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 V (m/z)}}{B}$ | $B$ = magnetic field strength; rearranged for mass analyzers | | 6 | **Cyclotron Frequency (FT-ICR)** | $\omega_c = \dfrac{z B}{m}$ | Used in ICR for ultra-high resolution | | 7 | **Resolution** | $R = \dfrac{m}{\Delta m} = \dfrac{m}{m_{0.5\,\text{peak}}}$ | Defines ability to separate two adjacent masses | | 8 | **Number Average MW from MS** | $M_n = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i}{\sum N_i}$ | $N_i$ = number of chains of mass $M_i$ | | 9 | **Weight Average MW from MS** | $M_w = \dfrac{\sum N_i M_i^2}{\sum N_i M_i}$ | Requires abundance-weighted data | | 10 | **Polydispersity Index (PDI)** | $PDI = \dfrac{M_w}{M_n}$ | Monodisperse polymers (synthesized by living polymerization) have PDI $\approx 1.00$ | | 11 | **Repeat Unit Count (MALDI)** | $n = \dfrac{M_{\text{peak}} - M_{\text{end groups}}}{M_0}$ | $M_0$ = molar mass of repeat unit; $M_{\text{end groups}}$ = mass of initiator + terminating groups | | 12 | **Copolymer Composition (MS)** | $\dfrac{n_A}{n_B} = \dfrac{I_A / M_A}{I_B / M_B}$ | $I_A, I_B$ = MS peak intensities of comonomer units | | 13 | **Mass Accuracy** | $\text{Accuracy (ppm)} = \dfrac{(m_{\text{measured}} - m_{\text{true}})}{m_{\text{true}}} \times 10^6$ | Lower ppm = higher accuracy | | 14 | **Reduced Mass (FT-ICR)** | $\mu = \dfrac{m_1 m_2}{m_1 + m_2}$ | Not commonly used in standard polymer MS but appears in ion-molecule reactions | > [!WARNING] > **Critical Exam Pitfall (KTU Valuation):** > When writing $m/z$ in a markdown table, use $\dfrac{m}{z}$ or $m/z$ as a label — never use the literal pipe symbol $\vert$ inside a table row, as it breaks the markdown syntax. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility In the polymer industry, mass spectrometry is no longer a research-only tool — it is **routine quality control**: - **Pharmaceutical polymers** (PLGA, PEG-drug conjugates): MALDI-TOF confirms drug loading efficiency. - **Recycled plastics**: MS detects oxidation products, chain scission by-products, and contaminants. - **Forensic polymer analysis**: Identifies polymer type from micro-traces. - **Failure analysis**: Identifies additive degradation products causing discoloration or embrittlement. - **Copolymer sequencing**: Tandem MS (MS/MS) reveals monomer arrangement in block vs. random copolymers. --- # Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Time-of-Flight Equation for MALDI-TOF The MALDI-TOF analyzer is the **single most important instrument for polymer MS** in the KTU syllabus. We will derive its governing equation from first principles. ### Starting Assumption An ion of mass $m$ and charge $z \cdot e$ (where $z$ is an integer charge state, $e$ is the elementary charge) is accelerated from rest through a potential difference $V$ in a uniform electric field. ### Step 1 — Energy Conservation The work done by the electric field on the ion equals its kinetic energy at the exit of the acceleration region: $$ W = z \cdot e \cdot V $$ $$ \text{KE} = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2 $$ By the work-energy theorem: $$ z \cdot e \cdot V = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2 $$ ### Step 2 — Solve for Velocity Rearranging the energy balance equation for velocity $v$: $$ v^2 = \dfrac{2 z e V}{m} $$ $$ v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 z e V}{m}} $$ This step explicitly shows that **velocity is inversely proportional to the square root of mass** — the foundational insight of TOF analyzers. ### Step 3 — Uniform Field-Free Drift After acceleration, the ion enters a field-free drift tube of length $L$. Assuming negligible collisions (high vacuum), the ion moves at constant velocity $v$. The time-of-flight $t$ is therefore: $$ t = \dfrac{L}{v} $$ ### Step 4 — Substitute $v$ into $t$ $$ t = \dfrac{L}{\sqrt{\dfrac{2 z e V}{m}}} $$ Inverting the denominator: $$ t = L \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{m}{2 z e V}} $$ ### Step 5 — Solve for m/z Squaring both sides: $$ t^2 = \dfrac{L^2 \cdot m}{2 z e V} $$ Rearranging: $$ \dfrac{m}{z} = \dfrac{2 e V t^2}{L^2} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Result:** > $$ \boxed{\dfrac{m}{z} = \dfrac{2 e V t^2}{L^2}} $$ > **Interpretation:** The mass-to-charge ratio is **directly proportional to the square of the flight time**. Doubling the flight time quadruples the m/z. This is why MALDI-TOF spectra are displayed with a non-linear $t \to m/z$ calibration. ### Step 6 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style Problem) > **Problem:** A polystyrene (PS) sample ($M_0 = 104.15$ Da) is analyzed by MALDI-TOF. The accelerating voltage is $V = 20{,}000$ V, drift length $L = 1.20$ m. A peak appears at flight time $t = 22.40 \ \mu s$. Assuming singly charged ions ($z = 1$) and $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C, calculate: > (a) The m/z of the ion. > (b) The number of repeat units $n$ if the end-group contribution is 58 Da (sodium-cationized proton end group). **Solution (a):** $$ \dfrac{m}{z} = \dfrac{2 \cdot (1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}) \cdot (20{,}000 \text{ V}) \cdot (22.40 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s})^2}{(1.20 \text{ m})^2} $$ Computing the numerator step-by-step: - $2 \cdot 1.602 \times 10^{-19} = 3.204 \times 10^{-19}$ - $3.204 \times 10^{-19} \cdot 2.0 \times 10^{4} = 6.408 \times 10^{-15}$ - $(22.40 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 5.0176 \times 10^{-10}$ - Numerator $= 6.408 \times 10^{-15} \cdot 5.0176 \times 10^{-10} = 3.215 \times 10^{-24}$ Denominator: - $(1.20)^2 = 1.44$ Therefore: $$ \dfrac{m}{z} = \dfrac{3.215 \times 10^{-24}}{1.44} = 2.233 \times 10^{-24} \text{ kg} $$ Converting from kg to Daltons (1 Da = $1.6605 \times 10^{-27}$ kg): $$ m/z \ (\text{Da}) = \dfrac{2.233 \times 10^{-24}}{1.6605 \times 10^{-27}} \approx 1345 \text{ Da} $$ **Solution (b):** $$ n = \dfrac{M_{\text{peak}} - M_{\text{end}}}{M_0} = \dfrac{1345 - 58}{104.15} \approx \dfrac{1287}{104.15} \approx 12.36 $$ Since $n$ must be an integer for a well-defined oligomer distribution, the closest integer is **$n = 12$**, giving a calculated mass of $12 \times 104.15 + 58 = 1307.8$ Da. The slight deviation (~37 Da) indicates this peak belongs to the next oligomer ($n = 13$, predicted 1411.95 Da) or the spectrum contains isotopic distributions. ## 3.2 Derivation: Resolution of a TOF Analyzer The resolution depends on the **initial spatial spread** ($\Delta x_0$) and **initial kinetic energy spread** ($\Delta E_0$) of ions at the moment of acceleration. ### Energy Spread Contribution The energy spread $\Delta E_0$ introduces a velocity spread $\Delta v$ at the source exit: $$ \Delta v = \dfrac{\Delta E_0}{m v} $$ ### Spatial Spread Contribution The initial spatial spread $\Delta x_0$ also contributes a time spread. Combining both effects, the total time spread at the detector is: $$ \Delta t = \dfrac{\Delta x_0}{v} + \dfrac{\Delta E_0 \cdot L}{m v^3} $$ ### Resolution Definition Using $\Delta t / t = \Delta m / (2m)$ (since $t \propto \sqrt{m}$): $$ \dfrac{\Delta m}{m} = 2 \left( \dfrac{\Delta x_0}{L} + \dfrac{\Delta E_0}{2 z e V} \right) $$ Thus, the resolution is: $$ \boxed{R = \dfrac{m}{\Delta m} = \dfrac{1}{2 \left( \dfrac{\Delta x_0}{L} + \dfrac{\Delta E_0}{2 z e V} \right)}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** Resolution increases with (i) longer drift tube $L$, (ii) higher accelerating voltage $V$, and (iii) smaller initial energy spread $\Delta E_0$. This is why modern MALDI-TOF instruments use **delayed extraction** and **ion mirrors (reflectrons)** to compensate for initial energy spread. ## 3.3 Derivation: Number-Average Molecular Weight from MS Data Suppose a polymer sample produces the following MS peaks (MALDI-TOF of polystyrene with Na⁺ cationization, end-group mass = 58 Da, $M_0$ = 104 Da): | Peak Index $i$ | $m/z$ (Da) | Relative Intensity $I_i$ | |-----------------|------------|---------------------------| | 1 | 162 | 5 | | 2 | 266 | 12 | | 3 | 370 | 25 | | 4 | 474 | 40 | | 5 | 578 | 35 | | 6 | 682 | 20 | | 7 | 786 | 8 | **Calculate $M_n$ and $M_w$:** Number of chains $N_i \propto I_i$ (assuming equal ionization efficiency across the distribution). ### Step 1 — Sum of Intensities $$ \sum I_i = 5 + 12 + 25 + 40 + 35 + 20 + 8 = 145 $$ ### Step 2 — Calculate Number Fractions $$ x_i = \dfrac{I_i}{\sum I_i} $$ For example, $x_1 = 5/145 = 0.0345$, $x_4 = 40/145 = 0.2759$, etc. ### Step 3 — Compute $M_n$ $$ M_n = \sum x_i M_i $$ Performing term-by-term: $$ \begin{aligned} M_n &= (0.0345)(162) + (0.0800)(266) + (0.1724)(370) \\ &\quad + (0.2759)(474) + (0.2414)(578) \\ &\quad + (0.1379)(682) + (0.0552)(786) \end{aligned} $$ Computing each product: - $0.0345 \times 162 = 5.59$ - $0.0800 \times 266 = 21.28$ - $0.1724 \times 370 = 63.79$ - $0.2759 \times 474 = 130.78$ - $0.2414 \times 578 = 139.53$ - $0.1379 \times 682 = 94.05$ - $0.0552 \times 786 = 43.39$ Summing: $$ M_n = 5.59 + 21.28 + 63.79 + 130.78 + 139.53 + 94.05 + 43.39 = 498.4 \text{ Da} $$ ### Step 4 — Compute $M_w$ $$ M_w = \sum x_i M_i^2 = \sum \dfrac{I_i M_i^2}{\sum I_i} $$ Computing each $I_i M_i^2$: - $5 \times 162^2 = 5 \times 26{,}244 = 131{,}220$ - $12 \times 266^2 = 12 \times 70{,}756 = 849{,}072$ - $25 \times 370^2 = 25 \times 136{,}900 = 3{,}422{,}500$ - $40 \times 474^2 = 40 \times 224{,}676 = 8{,}987{,}040$ - $35 \times 578^2 = 35 \times 334{,}084 = 11{,}692{,}940$ - $20 \times 682^2 = 20 \times 465{,}124 = 9{,}302{,}480$ - $8 \times 786^2 = 8 \times 617{,}796 = 4{,}942{,}368$ Sum: $$ \sum I_i M_i^2 = 131{,}220 + 849{,}072 + 3{,}422{,}500 + 8{,}987{,}040 + 11{,}692{,}940 + 9{,}302{,}480 + 4{,}942{,}368 = 39{,}327{,}620 $$ Therefore: $$ M_w = \dfrac{39{,}327{,}620}{145} \approx 271{,}225 \text{ Da} $$ ### Step 5 — Polydispersity Index $$ PDI = \dfrac{M_w}{M_n} = \dfrac{271{,}225}{498.4} \approx 5.44 $$ > [!WARNING] > **Note:** The high PDI (~5.44) is consistent with a **free-radical polymerization**, not a living polymerization. Living polymerization (e.g., anionic) typically gives PDI $< 1.1$. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Ionization Technique | Polymer Type | Recommended Technique | Reason | |--------------|----------------------|--------| | Polystyrene (PS) | MALDI-TOF | Aromatic, absorbs UV matrix well | | Polyethylene (PE) | LD-TOF / APPI | Saturated, no chromophore; needs atmospheric pressure photoionization | | Poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) | MALDI-TOF or ESI | Both work; ESI for low MW | | PEG (water-soluble) | ESI | Highly polar, ionizes in solution | | Polyamides (Nylon) | MALDI-TOF | Aromatic matrices (DHB) effective | | Polyfluorinated polymers | APCI or APPI | Low polarity; ESI inefficient | | Conducting polymers (polyaniline) | ESI or MALDI | Charge inherently present | --- # Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Modular Architecture of a Mass Spectrometer ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["Sample
Introduction
Module"] nodeB["Ionization
Source
MALDI or ESI"] nodeC["Mass
Analyzer
TOF or Quadrupole"] nodeD["Detector
Electron
Multiplier"] nodeE["Data
Processing
Workstation"] nodeF["Mass
Spectrum
Output"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF nodeG["High Vacuum
System
10 to 5 Pa"] nodeG -.powers.-> nodeB nodeG -.powers.-> nodeC nodeG -.powers.-> nodeD ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: MALDI-TOF Ion Path ```mermaid flowchart LR nodeA["UV Laser
Pulse 337 nm"] nodeB["Sample
Matrix
Co crystals"] nodeC["Acceleration
Region
20 kV"] nodeD["Field Free
Drift Tube
L meters"] nodeE["Ion Mirror
Reflectron
Optional"] nodeF["Detector
MCP"] nodeG["Time to Digital
Converter TDC"] nodeA --> nodeB nodeB --> nodeC nodeC --> nodeD nodeD --> nodeE nodeE --> nodeF nodeF --> nodeG ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram: Decision Tree for Polymer MS Method Selection ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeStart["Polymer Sample"] nodeQ1{"Soluble in
Polar Solvent?"} nodeQ2{"MW Range?"} nodeQ3{"Chromophore
Present?"} nodeMALDI["Use MALDI-TOF"] nodeESI["Use ESI MS"] nodeAPPI["Use APPI or LD"] nodeStart --> nodeQ1 nodeQ1 -- Yes --> nodeQ3 nodeQ1 -- No --> nodeAPPI nodeQ3 -- Yes --> nodeQ2 nodeQ3 -- No --> nodeAPPI nodeQ2 -- "Less than 10 kDa" --> nodeMALDI nodeQ2 -- "10 to 100 kDa" --> nodeMALDI nodeQ2 -- "Greater than 100 kDa" --> nodeESI ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram: Mass Spectrum Interpretation Workflow for Polymers ```mermaid flowchart TD nodeStart["Raw Mass Spectrum"] node1["Identify Major Peak Series"] node2["Determine Repeat Unit Mass
M0"] node3["Calculate Peak Spacing"] node4["Subtract End Group Mass"] node5["Determine Polymerization
Degree n"] node6["Compute Mn and Mw"] node7["Determine PDI"] node8["Assign Structural Identity"] nodeStart --> node1 node1 --> node2 node2 --> node3 node3 --> node4 node4 --> node5 node5 --> node6 node6 --> node7 node7 --> node8 ``` --- # KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]` **Define mass spectrometry and explain why soft ionization techniques are preferred for polymer analysis.** **Model Answer:** Mass spectrometry is an analytical technique that measures the mass-to-charge ratio (m/z) of ionized molecules to determine their molecular weight, structure, and composition. Soft ionization techniques such as MALDI (Matrix-Assisted Laser Desorption/Ionization) and ESI (Electrospray Ionization) are preferred for polymer analysis because: 1. Polymers are non-volatile and thermally degrade before evaporating. 2. Hard ionization (like EI at 70 eV) fragments polymer chains into small pieces, destroying molecular weight information. 3. Soft ionization transfers minimal excess energy, preserving the intact molecular ion and allowing detection of the whole polymer chain. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]` **What is the principle of a Time-of-Flight (TOF) mass analyzer?** **Model Answer:** A Time-of-Flight (TOF) mass analyzer separates ions based on the time they take to traverse a field-free drift tube of known length after being accelerated through a fixed potential difference. The governing equation is: $$ \dfrac{m}{z} = \dfrac{2 e V t^2}{L^2} $$ Since $m/z \propto t^2$, lighter ions reach the detector first, while heavier ions arrive later. The flight time is measured precisely (typically to nanosecond resolution) and converted to m/z using the calibration equation above. --- ## Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** > Each Part B question contains two sub-parts: **(a) for 7 marks** and **(b) for 7 marks**. Both sub-parts must be answered. There is **internal choice** between two full questions (Q1 or Q2, Q3 or Q4, etc.). ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]` **Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the instrumentation of a MALDI-TOF mass spectrometer. Explain the role of (i) the matrix, (ii) the laser, and (iii) the reflectron in achieving high-resolution polymer spectra. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A polymer sample analyzed by MALDI-TOF gives a peak at $t = 18.50 \ \mu s$ using $V = 25{,}000$ V and $L = 1.50$ m. Assuming singly charged ions ($z = 1$) and $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C, calculate the m/z and identify the polymer if the repeat unit mass is 44 Da (likely polypropylene glycol, PPG) with an end-group mass of 39 Da (sodium + hydroxyl). **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution (a) The MALDI-TOF instrument consists of the following blocks in series: 1. **Sample Plate (Target):** The polymer is co-crystallized with a small organic matrix molecule (e.g., DHB, HABA, dithranol) at a molar ratio of typically 1:1000 to 1:10,000. 2. **Laser Source:** A pulsed nitrogen UV laser ($\lambda = 337$ nm, pulse width ~3 ns) is fired at the sample. 3. **Acceleration Region:** Ions are accelerated through a potential of 15–25 kV. 4. **Field-Free Drift Tube:** Ions travel at constant velocity over length $L$. 5. **Reflectron (Ion Mirror):** A series of electrostatic lenses that reflect ions back, compensating for initial kinetic energy spread. 6. **Detector:** A microchannel plate (MCP) records ion arrival times. 7. **TDC (Time-to-Digital Converter):** Converts arrival times into digital signals for spectrum generation. **Role of Matrix:** Absorbs UV laser energy, transfers it to the analyte, and provides protons or metal cations (Na⁺, K⁺) for ionization. The matrix isolates polymer chains, preventing aggregation. **Role of Laser:** Provides the energy pulse for desorption and ionization without excessive thermal damage. **Role of Reflectron:** Corrects the kinetic energy distribution of ions formed at slightly different positions in the plume, dramatically improving resolution to >20,000. #### Model Solution (b) Using the TOF equation: $$ \dfrac{m}{z} = \dfrac{2 e V t^2}{L^2} $$ Substituting values: - $2 e V = 2 \times 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \times 25{,}000 = 8.01 \times 10^{-15}$ - $t^2 = (18.50 \times 10^{-6})^2 = 3.4225 \times 10^{-10}$ - Numerator $= 8.01 \times 10^{-15} \times 3.4225 \times 10^{-10} = 2.741 \times 10^{-24}$ - $L^2 = (1.50)^2 = 2.25$ $$ \dfrac{m}{z} = \dfrac{2.741 \times 10^{-24}}{2.25} = 1.218 \times 10^{-24} \text{ kg} $$ Converting to Daltons: $$ m/z \ (\text{Da}) = \dfrac{1.218 \times 10^{-24}}{1.6605 \times 10^{-27}} \approx 733.4 \text{ Da} $$ Determining $n$: $$ n = \dfrac{M_{\text{peak}} - M_{\text{end}}}{M_0} = \dfrac{733.4 - 39}{44} = \dfrac{694.4}{44} \approx 15.78 $$ Since $n$ must be an integer, the closest value is **$n = 16$**, giving an expected mass of $16 \times 44 + 39 = 743$ Da. The slight discrepancy is due to rounding in the flight time. **Conclusion:** The peak corresponds to a **PPG oligomer with $n \approx 16$ repeat units, sodium-cationized** with an –OH end group. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023 Model]` **Q. (a)** Compare MALDI and ESI ionization techniques with respect to (i) ionization mechanism, (ii) applicable polymer types, (iii) mass range, and (iv) multiply-charged ion formation. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The MALDI-TOF spectrum of a PS sample shows peaks at m/z values of 1010, 1114, 1218, 1322, and 1426 Da with relative intensities of 10, 25, 40, 18, and 7, respectively. The end-group contribution is 58 Da. Calculate $M_n$, $M_w$, and PDI. Comment on the polymerization type. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution (a) | Criterion | MALDI | ESI | |-----------|-------|-----| | (i) Mechanism | Laser desorption from matrix-analyte co-crystals | Charged droplet evaporation from a Taylor cone | | (ii) Polymer type | Synthetic, hydrophobic, aromatic | Water-soluble, polar, polyelectrolytes | | (iii) Mass range | Up to ~500 kDa | Typically < 100 kDa (limited by multiple charging) | | (iv) Multiple charges | Mostly singly charged (simpler spectrum) | Multiple charging (z = 2, 3, 4...) — broad m/z range | #### Model Solution (b) Step 1: Verify that peaks are spaced by 104 Da (PS repeat unit): $$ 1114 - 1010 = 104; \quad 1218 - 1114 = 104; \quad 1322 - 1218 = 104; \quad 1426 - 1322 = 104 \ \checkmark $$ Step 2: Compute $n$ for each peak: - $n_1 = (1010 - 58)/104 = 952/104 = 9.15 \approx 9$ (expected 9 × 104 + 58 = 994 Da — discrepancy 16 Da) - Adjusted values: assume peaks correspond to oligomers $n = 9, 10, 11, 12, 13$ with theoretical masses 994, 1098, 1202, 1306, 1410 Da. The data is offset by ~16 Da (likely Na⁺ instead of H⁺ or isotope effect). Step 3: For computation, use the measured m/z values directly. Number fractions $x_i = I_i / \sum I_i$: - $\sum I_i = 10 + 25 + 40 + 18 + 7 = 100$ - $x_i$: 0.10, 0.25, 0.40, 0.18, 0.07 Step 4: Calculate $M_n$: $$ M_n = (0.10)(1010) + (0.25)(1114) + (0.40)(1218) + (0.18)(1322) + (0.07)(1426) $$ $$ M_n = 101 + 278.5 + 487.2 + 237.96 + 99.82 = 1204.48 \text{ Da} $$ Step 5: Calculate $M_w$: $$ M_w = \dfrac{\sum I_i M_i^2}{\sum I_i} $$ - $10 \times 1010^2 = 10{,}201{,}000$ - $25 \times 1114^2 = 31{,}024{,}900$ - $40 \times 1218^2 = 59{,}340{,}960$ - $18 \times 1322^2 = 31{,}449{,}192$ - $7 \times 1426^2 = 14{,}234{,}332$ Sum $= 146{,}250{,}384$ $$ M_w = 146{,}250{,}384 / 100 = 1462.5 \text{ Da} $$ Step 6: PDI: $$ PDI = \dfrac{1462.5}{1204.5} = 1.214 $$ **Conclusion:** PDI = 1.21 indicates a **moderately controlled polymerization** — narrower than free-radical (PDI > 1.5) but not as narrow as living anionic polymerization (PDI < 1.10). This is consistent with **reversible-deactivation radical polymerization** (e.g., ATRP, RAFT, NMP). --- ## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Loss Areas in Mass Spectrometry Questions (KTU Board Pattern):** > 1. **Forgetting to convert units:** Students often write kg instead of Da. Always convert $m/z$ from kg to Daltons by dividing by $1.6605 \times 10^{-27}$ kg. > 2. **Confusing $m$ with $m/z$:** Always remember the formula gives m/z, not m. For singly charged ions, m/z = m. > 3. **Wrong peak spacing interpretation:** In a polymer spectrum, peaks are spaced by the **repeat unit mass $M_0$**, not by the polymer MW. Spacing of 44 = PEG/PPG, 104 = PS, 28 = PE/PP oxide, 58 = polyacrylamide. > 4. **End-group errors:** Always subtract end-group mass before dividing by $M_0$. Failure to do this gives non-integer $n$. > 5. **Using intensity instead of $N_i$:** Without proper normalization, $M_n$ calculations become wrong. > 6. **Confusing $M_n$ and $M_w$ definitions:** $M_n$ weights by number; $M_w$ weights by mass squared. Switching them inflates PDI errors. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Mass spectrometry measures m/z**, not mass directly. For $z = 1$, $m/z = m$. - **MALDI-TOF** is the workhorse for synthetic polymers; **ESI** for water-soluble polymers; **APPI** for non-polar saturated polymers like PE. - **Soft ionization** preserves the molecular ion — essential for polymers. - **TOF equation:** $m/z = 2eVt^2 / L^2$. Lighter ions arrive first. - **Resolution** improves with higher $V$, longer $L$, and reflectron use. - **Peak spacing** in a polymer spectrum equals the **repeat unit mass** $M_0$. - **$M_n = \sum x_i M_i$** and **$M_w = \sum x_i M_i^2$** computed from MS peak intensities. - **PDI = $M_w / M_n$**: ~1.00 for living, ~1.2 for controlled radical, >1.5 for free-radical polymerization. - **Common matrices:** DHB (2,5-dihydroxybenzoic acid), HABA, dithranol, sinapinic acid. - **Common cationizing agents:** Na⁺, K⁺, Ag⁺ (for olefins), Cu²⁺ (for polyolefins). - **FT-ICR** gives the highest resolution (>1,000,000); used for complex polymer mixtures and exact mass determination. - **Tandem MS (MS/MS)** enables copolymer sequencing by fragmenting selected oligomer ions. - **Mass accuracy** in modern instruments: <5 ppm (FT-ICR), <50 ppm (TOF). - **Sample prep rule:** Polymer concentration ~1–10 mg/mL; matrix:analyte ratio 100:1 to 10,000:1.

topics.title: X-ray Diffraction Spectrometry
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT602 - POLYMER TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** X-ray Diffraction Spectrometry

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**X-ray Diffraction Spectrometry (XRD)** is a non-destructive analytical characterization technique that exploits the constructive interference of monochromatic X-rays with the periodic, ordered atomic or molecular planes within a material to determine its crystalline structure, phase composition, unit cell dimensions, and degree of crystallinity. In polymer science, it is the definitive tool for quantifying the proportion and arrangement of crystalline regions within a semi-crystalline macromolecular solid.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** XRD is listed under Module 3 of PCPOT602 as a primary *structural* testing method, distinguishing it from mechanical (tensile, impact) and thermal (DSC, TGA) tests covered in other modules. The KTU examiner expects students to connect Bragg's Law to the *physical* arrangement of polymer chains.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine throwing tennis balls at a neatly arranged honeycomb fence:

- If you throw the balls **straight on**, most bounce back at random — this is what happens when X-rays hit an **amorphous polymer region** (no long-range order, producing a diffuse "halo").
- If you throw the balls at a **specific angle** equal to the spacing between the wooden slats, the balls ricochet and converge at a single bright spot — this is **constructive interference**, analogous to X-rays striking the parallel planes of atoms in a **crystalline polymer region**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Box:** The "slats" of the honeycomb are the ordered polymer lamellae (crystallites). The "bounce-back angle" is the diffraction angle **2θ**, measured by the detector. The wavelength of the "tennis ball" is the X-ray wavelength **λ** (typically 1.5418 Å for Cu Kα radiation).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Speed of light | $c$ | $3.00 \times 10^{8}$ | m/s |
| Planck's constant | $h$ | $6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ | J·s |
| Cu Kα wavelength | $\lambda_{Cu}$ | **1.5418** | Å |
| Wavelength for d-spacing calculation | $\lambda$ | $0.15418$ | nm |
| Typical 2θ scan range for polymers | $2\theta$ | **5° – 60°** | degrees |

> [!NOTE]
> The Cu Kα wavelength is the **most commonly used** target in polymer XRD because its energy (~8.04 keV) is ideal for resolving inter-planar spacings in the 1–10 Å range typical of polymer crystallites.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Physics — Stepwise Logic

1. **X-ray Generation:** Electrons strike a Cu target in an X-ray tube. Inner-shell (K-shell) electrons are ejected, and outer electrons drop down, emitting characteristic **Kα and Kβ** X-rays.
2. **Monochromatization:** A nickel filter or crystal monochromator isolates the **Cu Kα** radiation ($\lambda = 1.5418$ Å), removing Kβ and white (Bremsstrahlung) radiation.
3. **Collimation:** The monochromatic beam is narrowed by slits and directed at the polymer sample.
4. **Interaction with the Sample:** When the X-ray beam encounters periodic electron density in crystalline regions, the waves are **scattered** by atoms. If scattered waves are in-phase, **constructive interference** occurs at specific angles satisfying **Bragg's Law**.
5. **Detection:** The goniometer rotates the detector (and sample) to record intensity vs. 2θ. The result is a **diffractogram**.
6. **Interpretation:** Sharp peaks → crystalline domains. Broad humps (halos) → amorphous regions.

### 2.2 Why and How — Engineering Rationale

- **Why use XRD for polymers?** Polymers are *never* 100% crystalline; they are semi-crystalline. XRD is the only direct, quantitative way to measure the **degree of crystallinity ($X_c$)**, which directly governs mechanical stiffness, optical clarity, density, and barrier properties.
- **How is it achieved?** By comparing the integrated area under the crystalline peaks with the total area under the diffractogram (crystalline + amorphous).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$ | **Bragg's Law** (n = order, usually 1) | $\lambda$ and $d$ in Å or nm |
| 2 | $d = \dfrac{\lambda}{2\sin\theta}$ | Inter-planar spacing (rearranged Bragg) | nm or Å |
| 3 | $\sin\theta = \dfrac{n\lambda}{2d}$ | Critical angle for diffraction | dimensionless |
| 4 | $X_c = \dfrac{A_c}{A_c + A_a} \times 100\%$ | **Degree of crystallinity** | % |
| 5 | $D = \dfrac{K\lambda}{\beta \cos\theta}$ | **Scherrer Equation** (crystallite size) | nm |
| 6 | $q = \dfrac{4\pi \sin\theta}{\lambda}$ | Scattering vector (SAXS formalism) | Å$^{-1}$ |
| 7 | $\rho_{polymer} = X_c \cdot \rho_c + (1-X_c) \cdot \rho_a$ | Two-phase density model | g/cm³ |
| 8 | $E_{X-ray} = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | X-ray photon energy | keV |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU exams, examiners *frequently* test **Scherrer Equation** alongside Bragg's Law. Memorize the constant **K (Scherrer constant = 0.9)** — students routinely lose marks for omitting it. **β** is the Full Width at Half Maximum (FWHM) of the peak in **radians**.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Polymer Processing:** Determines optimal annealing temperatures to maximize crystallinity in PET bottles, increasing barrier properties.
- **Quality Control:** Verifies lot-to-lot consistency in HDPE pipes (essential for KTU Civil/Mechanical engineers).
- **Pharmaceutical Polymers:** Confirms polymorphic form of drug-loaded polymer matrices.
- **Failure Analysis:** Identifies whether brittle failure originated from excessive crystallinity (a common industrial case study).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Bragg's Law (The Foundation of XRD)

**Starting Premise:** Consider two parallel crystallographic planes, separated by an inter-planar distance **d**. An incident X-ray beam (wavelength $\lambda$) strikes the planes at a glancing angle $\theta$.

Let the two rays be **Ray 1** and **Ray 2**:
- Ray 1 reflects off the **top plane** at point A.
- Ray 2 penetrates to the **second plane** and reflects at point B.

For constructive interference, the **path difference** between Ray 2 and Ray 1 must equal an integer multiple of the wavelength, $n\lambda$.

**Step 1 — Construct the geometry:**
The extra distance Ray 2 travels (compared to Ray 1) is split into two right-angled segments, each opposite to the angle $\theta$ on the inter-planar spacing $d$.

$$
\text{Path difference} = AB + BC
$$

**Step 2 — Apply trigonometry:**
Each segment has length $d \sin\theta$:

$$
\text{Path difference} = d\sin\theta + d\sin\theta
$$

$$
\text{Path difference} = 2d\sin\theta
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the constructive interference condition:**

$$
2d\sin\theta = n\lambda
$$

where $n$ is a positive integer ($1, 2, 3, \dots$) representing the diffraction order.

**Step 4 — Simplify for the first-order case (n = 1):**

$$
\lambda = 2d\sin\theta
$$

This is **Bragg's Law** in its most commonly used form for KTU problems.

**Step 5 — Solve for d-spacing (the actual experimental goal):**

$$
d = \frac{\lambda}{2\sin\theta}
$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Degree of Crystallinity ($X_c$)

**Starting Premise:** The total intensity of X-rays scattered by a semi-crystalline polymer is the sum of contributions from crystalline domains and amorphous domains.

$$
I_{total}(2\theta) = I_{crystalline}(2\theta) + I_{amorphous}(2\theta)
$$

**Step 1 — Define the integrated areas:**
The total integrated intensity (area under the diffractogram) over the scan range is:

$$
A_{total} = \int_{2\theta_1}^{2\theta_2} I_{total}(2\theta) \, d(2\theta)
$$

**Step 2 — Separate the contributions:**

$$
A_{total} = A_c + A_a
$$

where:
- $A_c$ = integrated area of crystalline peaks (sharp reflections).
- $A_a$ = integrated area of the amorphous halo (broad hump).

**Step 3 — Define crystallinity as the ratio of crystalline scattering to total scattering:**

$$
X_c = \frac{A_c}{A_c + A_a}
$$

**Step 4 — Express as a percentage:**

$$
X_c (\%) = \frac{A_c}{A_c + A_a} \times 100
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):** A polymer diffractogram has $A_c = 850$ counts·deg and $A_a = 1150$ counts·deg. Calculate $X_c$.

**Solution:**

$$
X_c = \frac{850}{850 + 1150} \times 100 = \frac{850}{2000} \times 100 = 42.5\%
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Scherrer Equation (Crystallite Size)

**Starting Premise:** In a perfect infinite crystal, diffraction peaks would be infinitesimally sharp (Dirac delta functions). In real polymer crystallites, finite size causes **peak broadening**.

The broadening $\beta$ (FWHM) is inversely proportional to the crystallite dimension $D$ along the direction perpendicular to the diffracting planes.

**Step 1 — Empirical proportionality:**

$$
D \propto \frac{\lambda}{\beta \cos\theta}
$$

**Step 2 — Introduce the Scherrer constant K:**

$$
D = \frac{K\lambda}{\beta \cos\theta}
$$

**Step 3 — Units and standard values:**
- $K = 0.9$ (typical for spherical crystallites).
- $\lambda = 0.15418$ nm (for Cu Kα).
- $\beta$ must be in **radians** (convert from degrees: $\beta_{rad} = \beta_{deg} \times \pi/180$).
- $D$ comes out in **nm**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The FWHM $\beta$ used in Scherrer must be the **pure broadening** (sample broadening), not the raw measured FWHM. Instrumental broadening must be subtracted: $\beta_{sample} = \sqrt{\beta_{measured}^2 - \beta_{instrument}^2}$. Many students lose 2 marks by using raw values.

---

### 3.4 Worked Analytical Problem — Combining Bragg + Scherrer

**Problem:** A polymer sample is analyzed using Cu Kα radiation ($\lambda = 0.15418$ nm). A sharp diffraction peak appears at $2\theta = 23.5°$ with a FWHM of $0.45°$. Calculate the d-spacing and the approximate crystallite size.

**Step 1 — Convert 2θ to θ:**

$$
\theta = \frac{23.5°}{2} = 11.75°
$$

**Step 2 — Apply Bragg's Law for d-spacing (n=1):**

$$
d = \frac{\lambda}{2\sin\theta} = \frac{0.15418}{2 \times \sin(11.75°)}
$$

$$
\sin(11.75°) = 0.2037
$$

$$
d = \frac{0.15418}{2 \times 0.2037} = \frac{0.15418}{0.4074} = 0.3785 \text{ nm}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert FWHM to radians for Scherrer:**

$$
\beta = 0.45° \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 0.00785 \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Scherrer Equation (K = 0.9):**

$$
D = \frac{0.9 \times 0.15418}{0.00785 \times \cos(11.75°)}
$$

$$
\cos(11.75°) = 0.9790
$$

$$
D = \frac{0.13876}{0.00785 \times 0.9790} = \frac{0.13876}{0.007685} = 18.05 \text{ nm}
$$

**Final Answer:** $d = 0.3785$ nm; $D \approx 18.05$ nm.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram of an X-ray Diffractometer (Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[X-ray Tube Power Supply] --> B[X-ray Tube Cu Target]
    B --> C[K alpha Filter Nickel Foil]
    C --> D[Divergence Slit Collimator]
    D --> E[Polymer Sample Holder Flat Plate or Film]
    E --> F[Receiving Slit]
    F --> G[Scintillation Detector]
    G --> H[Preamplifier and Pulse Height Discriminator]
    H --> I[Goniometer Control and Stepper Motor]
    I --> J[Data Acquisition System DAS]
    J --> K[Computer Software for Diffractogram Plot]
    K --> L[Degree of Crystallinity Calculation Module]
    K --> M[Crystallite Size Scherrer Module]
    K --> N[Phase ID Search Match JCPDS Database]
    L --> O[Final Report and Quantitative Analysis]
    M --> O
    N --> O
```

### 4.2 Workflow Schematic — XRD Analysis of a Polymer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Step 1 Sample Preparation Compression Molding or Thin Film] --> B[Step 2 Mount in Sample Holder]
    B --> C[Step 3 Set Scan Parameters 2 theta Range Step Size]
    C --> D[Step 4 Run XRD Scan Generate Diffractogram]
    D --> E[Step 5 Identify Peaks and Halo]
    E --> F[Step 6 Apply Braggs Law for d spacing]
    E --> G[Step 7 Apply Scherrer Equation for Crystallite Size]
    E --> H[Step 8 Calculate Area under Peaks for Xc]
    F --> I[Final Structural Profile of Polymer]
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

### 4.3 Comparison Block — Crystalline vs Amorphous XRD Signature

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Polymer Sample] --> B{Order at Long Range?}
    B -- Yes Crystalline --> C[Sharp Bragg Peaks at Specific 2 theta]
    B -- No Amorphous --> D[Broad Diffuse Halo]
    C --> E[High Intensity Low FWHM]
    D --> F[Low Intensity High FWHM]
    E --> G[Interpret using Braggs and Scherrer]
    F --> G
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Define Bragg's Law and explain the physical significance of the inter-planar spacing d.*

**Model Answer:**
Bragg's Law states that for constructive interference of X-rays reflected from successive planes of a crystal lattice, the path difference must be an integer multiple of the wavelength:

$$n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$$

The inter-planar spacing **d** is the perpendicular distance between two adjacent, parallel crystallographic planes. Physically, it represents the *repeat unit of the atomic/molecular arrangement* along a given direction in the crystal. In polymers, d corresponds to the stacking distance of polymer chain segments within the crystallite lamellae, typically in the range of 0.3–0.5 nm. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Distinguish between the XRD patterns of a fully amorphous and a semi-crystalline polymer.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Fully Amorphous | Semi-Crystalline |
|---|---|---|
| 2θ peaks | None | Sharp peaks present |
| Halo | One broad hump | Halo + peaks |
| Crystallinity | 0% | > 0% (typically 10–80%) |
| Example | Atactic PS, PMMA | HDPE, isotactic PP, Nylon 6 |

The sharp peaks arise from ordered crystallites obeying Bragg's Law; the broad halo arises from disordered amorphous chains producing diffuse scattering. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper]**

**(a)** *Derive Bragg's Law for X-ray diffraction and explain the role of monochromatization in XRD instrumentation.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation:**
Consider two parallel crystal planes separated by distance d, with X-rays of wavelength λ incident at glancing angle θ.

Step 1: Constructive interference requires:

$$
2d\sin\theta = n\lambda
$$

Step 2: Detailed geometric construction (as shown in SECTION 3.1 above).

Step 3: For first order (n = 1):

$$
\lambda = 2d\sin\theta
$$

Step 4: Solving for d:

$$
d = \frac{\lambda}{2\sin\theta}
$$

**Monochromatization (3 of the 7 marks):**
The X-ray tube produces a spectrum containing the desired Cu Kα (λ = 1.5418 Å), the Cu Kβ (λ = 1.392 Å), and a continuous white background (Bremsstrahlung). A **nickel filter** preferentially absorbs the Kβ (Ni K-edge at 1.488 Å), passing primarily Kα. Alternatively, a **crystal monochromator** (e.g., graphite) physically diffracts and selects only the Kα beam. Monochromatization is essential because a polychromatic beam would produce overlapping diffraction patterns, making peak indexing impossible. **(7 marks total)**

---

**(b)** *A polymer sample exhibits an XRD peak at 2θ = 21.6° using Cu Kα radiation. Calculate the inter-planar spacing d. If the FWHM of this peak is 0.38°, estimate the crystallite size using the Scherrer equation (K = 0.9).* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert 2θ to θ:**

$$
\theta = \frac{21.6°}{2} = 10.8°
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate d-spacing (Bragg's Law, n = 1):**

$$
d = \frac{\lambda}{2\sin\theta} = \frac{0.15418 \text{ nm}}{2 \times \sin(10.8°)}
$$

$$
\sin(10.8°) = 0.1874
$$

$$
d = \frac{0.15418}{0.3748} = 0.4114 \text{ nm}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert FWHM to radians for Scherrer:**

$$
\beta = 0.38° \times \frac{\pi}{180} = 6.63 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Scherrer Equation:**

$$
D = \frac{K\lambda}{\beta \cos\theta} = \frac{0.9 \times 0.15418}{6.63 \times 10^{-3} \times \cos(10.8°)}
$$

$$
\cos(10.8°) = 0.9823
$$

$$
D = \frac{0.13876}{6.51 \times 10^{-3}} = 21.31 \text{ nm}
$$

**Final Answer:** $d = 0.4114$ nm; $D \approx 21.3$ nm. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Supplementary]**

**(a)** *Explain the concept of degree of crystallinity in polymers. Describe how it is determined from an XRD diffractogram, with a suitable mathematical expression and one numerical example.* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept (2 marks):** Degree of crystallinity ($X_c$) is the volume or mass fraction of ordered (crystalline) regions in a semi-crystalline polymer. It governs mechanical strength, optical transparency, density, melting behavior, and chemical resistance.

**Determination (3 marks):** The XRD diffractogram is integrated over a chosen 2θ range. The total area $A_{total}$ is decomposed into:
- $A_c$ = area of sharp crystalline peaks.
- $A_a$ = area of the broad amorphous halo (typically a smooth curve fitted beneath the peaks).

A software tool (e.g., Origin, Jade) or the Ruland/Vonk method is used for deconvolution.

**Mathematical Expression:**

$$
X_c (\%) = \frac{A_c}{A_c + A_a} \times 100
$$

**Numerical Example (2 marks):** If $A_c = 720$ units and $A_a = 1080$ units:

$$
X_c = \frac{720}{1800} \times 100 = 40\%
$$

**(7 marks total)**

---

**(b)** *With a neat block diagram, describe the components and working of an X-ray diffractometer used for polymer testing. What sample preparation methods are commonly used for polymeric samples?* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Components (4 marks) — Refer to Mermaid block in SECTION 4.1:**

1. **X-ray Source (Tube):** High-voltage tube with Cu target producing Cu Kα radiation ($\lambda = 1.5418$ Å).
2. **Monochromator/Filter:** Ni foil or graphite crystal to isolate Kα.
3. **Collimator Slits:** Define beam geometry.
4. **Sample Stage:** Rotating or fixed flat-plate holder; goniometer.
5. **Detector:** Scintillation counter or solid-state detector.
6. **Data Acquisition System:** Records intensity vs. 2θ.
7. **Computer & Software:** Generates diffractogram, peak identification, crystallinity calculation.

**Working (1 mark):** The X-ray beam strikes the sample; the detector rotates to scan 2θ from ~5° to 60°. Constructive interference at Bragg-angles produces peaks; amorphous halos appear elsewhere.

**Sample Preparation (2 marks):**
- **Compression Molding:** Polymer pellet pressed into a flat disc (1–2 mm thick).
- **Solution Casting:** Polymer dissolved, cast on glass slide, dried.
- **Melt Casting:** Polymer melted on a hot stage, cooled to a film.
- **Film/Fiber Mounting:** Thin films clamped; fibers bundled parallel.
- **Powder:** For insoluble crosslinked polymers, ground to fine powder.

**(7 marks total)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert FWHM to radians** in the Scherrer equation → **–2 marks**
> 2. **Using n = 2 or higher** in Bragg's Law without justification (always state n = 1 unless specified) → **–1 mark**
> 3. **Confusing 2θ with θ** in the calculator — a classic blunder. θ is *half* of 2θ → **–2 marks**
> 4. **Omitting the Scherrer constant K = 0.9** → **–1 mark**
> 5. **Not stating the radiation source** (e.g., "Cu Kα, 1.5418 Å") in numerical problems → **–1 mark**
> 6. **Mixing up A_c and A_a** in the crystallinity formula → **direct 0 on the sub-part**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Bragg's Law** is the *heart* of XRD: $n\lambda = 2d\sin\theta$. Use $\lambda = 1.5418$ Å (Cu Kα) unless stated otherwise.
- **Always convert 2θ to θ** before any calculation. The detector measures 2θ; the formula needs θ.
- **d-spacing** is the *perpendicular distance* between two adjacent lattice planes; in polymers, it represents chain-stacking periodicity (typically 0.3–0.5 nm).
- **Degree of crystallinity** $X_c = \dfrac{A_c}{A_c + A_a} \times 100\%$ — must sum crystallite peak areas + amorphous halo area.
- **Scherrer Equation** $D = \dfrac{K\lambda}{\beta \cos\theta}$ gives crystallite size; **β in radians**, **K = 0.9**.
- **Crystalline polymer** → sharp peaks at Bragg angles. **Amorphous** → broad diffuse halo, no sharp peaks.
- **Cu Kα radiation** is the polymer-XRD standard because its wavelength matches the scale of polymer chain spacings.
- **Components to remember (in order):** X-ray tube → Monochromator → Collimator → Sample → Detector → DAS → Computer.
- **Sample prep for polymers:** compression molding, solution casting, melt casting, or powder (for insoluble crosslinked).
- **Instrumentation constant:** Goniometer rotates sample and detector to scan 2θ; θ–2θ scan is the standard mode.
- **SAXS vs WAXS:** WAXS gives d-spacings (Bragg peaks, 2θ > 5°); SAXS gives long-period lamellar stacking (q < 1 nm⁻¹).
- **Real-world applications:** Quality control of HDPE, PET bottle crystallinity, Nylon 6 polymorphism, polymer blend phase analysis.
- **Examiner's hot keywords:** "monochromatization," "constructive interference," "inter-planar spacing," "degree of crystallinity," "Scherrer constant," "goniometer," "Cu Kα."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gas Chromatography
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT602 - POLYMER TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Gas Chromatography

# Gas Chromatography — Foundational Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Gas Chromatography (GC)** is a high-resolution **physico-chemical separation technique** in which a vaporized sample is transported by a gaseous mobile phase (carrier gas) through a column containing a stationary phase (liquid film coated on an inert solid support, or a solid adsorbent). Components of the mixture partition differentially between the two phases based on their volatility and affinity, eluting at different times and being recorded by a detector.

In the context of the **PCPOT602 — Polymer Testing** syllabus, GC is primarily employed for:

- Quantitative determination of **residual unreacted monomers** in polymers.
- Identification and quantification of **low-molecular-weight additives** (plasticizers, antioxidants, stabilizers, slip agents).
- **Purity analysis** of polymer synthesis solvents.
- **Pyrolysis-GC (Py-GC)** for fingerprinting and structural characterization of polymeric materials.
- Headspace analysis of **volatile organic compounds (VOCs)** emitted by polymers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** Students must know GC instrumentation, the role of the stationary and mobile phases, retention parameters, separation efficiency equations, and application relevance to polymer characterization.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Polymer Race Track"

Imagine a **race track** with two surfaces:

- **The track itself (stationary phase)** — a sticky, waxy surface where certain runners struggle to advance.
- **The wind blowing behind the runners (carrier gas)** — pushing everyone forward at a constant pace.

Runners who **dislike sticking to the track** (low-affinity / highly volatile monomers like vinyl chloride) glide quickly and finish the race first — they have a **short retention time**. Runners who **love clinging to the track** (polar monomers or hydrogen-bonding additives) move slowly and finish much later — they have a **long retention time**.
