The **finish-line camera** is the **detector**, which logs each runner's crossing time. By analyzing the order and the gaps, you can identify each compound. This is precisely how GC separates the components of a polymer extract or pyrolysis vapor.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Principle (RT/GC):** Separation in GC arises from **differential partitioning** of analytes between a moving gas phase and a stationary liquid/adsorbent phase — governed by the analyte's partition coefficient $K$.

---

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Reference Metrics

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Carrier gas viscosity (He) | $\eta_{He}$ | ≈ **0.000194 Pa·s** at 25 °C |
| Carrier gas viscosity (N₂) | $\eta_{N_2}$ | ≈ **0.000176 Pa·s** at 25 °C |
| Standard column temperature | $T_{col}$ | **50 °C – 350 °C** (isothermal or programmed) |
| Standard column length (capillary) | $L$ | **15 m – 60 m** |
| Standard internal diameter (capillary) | $d_c$ | **0.10 mm – 0.53 mm** |
| Typical stationary film thickness | $d_f$ | **0.10 µm – 5.0 µm** |
| Standard linear velocity (He) | $\bar{u}$ | **20 – 40 cm/s** (optimum for Van Deemter minimum) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Gas Chromatography — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The chromatographic separation in GC proceeds through five sequential events:

1. **Sample Vaporization at the Injector:**
   The liquid/polymer extract is introduced via a micro-syringe into a heated injection port (typically **200 °C – 300 °C**), where it is **instantaneously vaporized** without thermal decomposition.

2. **Carrier-Gas Transport:**
   A chemically inert gas (**He**, **N₂**, or **H₂**) sweeps the vaporized sample into the column. The carrier gas acts purely as the *mobile phase* and does not participate in any chemical interaction with analytes.

3. **Partitioning in the Column:**
   Analytes distribute themselves between the **mobile phase** (carrier gas) and the **stationary phase** (high-boiling liquid polymer film, e.g., 5 % phenyl-methylpolysiloxane, polyethylene glycol). The partitioning is governed by the **partition coefficient**:

$$K = \frac{C_s}{C_m}$$

   where $C_s$ is the analyte concentration in the stationary phase and $C_m$ is the concentration in the mobile phase.

4. **Differential Migration:**
   Components with higher $K$ spend more time dissolved in the stationary phase and travel slower. Components with lower $K$ are swept faster. This creates the **separation**.

5. **Detection and Quantification:**
   As each component elutes, it enters the detector (FID/TCD/ECD/MS). The detector produces an analog electrical signal proportional to analyte concentration, producing a **chromatogram** — a plot of detector response vs. time.

> [!NOTE]
> **WHY it works:** The differential $K$ values are an intrinsic thermodynamic property of each analyte with respect to the chosen stationary phase at a specific column temperature. By selecting an appropriate stationary phase chemistry, we can target specific polymer-relevant analytes.

---

## 2.2 Instrumentation — Functional Blocks

A GC instrument comprises six essential subsystems:

| Block | Function | Key Components |
|---|---|---|
| **Gas Supply System** | Provides clean, regulated carrier gas | Cylinder, pressure regulator, molecular sieve / oxygen trap, flow controller |
| **Sample Injector** | Introduces and vaporizes the sample | Split/splitless injector, on-column injector, PTV injector |
| **Column Oven** | Maintains precise column temperature | Air-circulating oven (isothermal or temperature-programmed) |
| **Separation Column** | Performs the actual separation | Packed column (1/8″–1/4″ OD) or Capillary column (fused silica) |
| **Detector** | Senses eluting analytes and converts to electrical signal | FID, TCD, ECD, MS |
| **Data System** | Records and processes the chromatogram | Integrator / PC with chromatography software |

---

## 2.3 Types of Gas Chromatography

| Type | Stationary Phase | Mechanism | Typical Application in Polymer Testing |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Gas–Liquid Chromatography (GLC)** | High-boiling liquid film on inert solid | Partition | Residual monomer analysis (styrene, MMA, vinyl chloride) |
| **Gas–Solid Chromatography (GSC)** | Active solid (Porapak, molecular sieve) | Adsorption | Permanent gas analysis, water determination |
| **Pyrolysis-GC (Py-GC)** | None (sample pyrolyzed externally) | Pyrogram fingerprinting | Identification of unknown polymers, copolymer composition |
| **Headspace-GC** | GLC column | Equilibrium vapor analysis | VOC / residual solvent in finished polymer products |
| **Inverse GC (IGC)** | Polymer film acts as stationary phase | Probes polymer-analyte interactions | Measurement of polymer solubility parameters, surface energy |

---

## 2.4 Detectors — Comparative Performance Matrix

| Detector | Type | Detection Limit | Selectivity | Use in Polymer Testing |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **TCD** (Thermal Conductivity) | Universal | ~10⁻⁶ g | Responds to all | Carrier gas comparison; non-combustible analytes |
| **FID** (Flame Ionization) | Mass-flow | ~10⁻¹² g | Organic compounds only | **Residual monomer & additive quantification** |
| **ECD** (Electron Capture) | Concentration | ~10⁻¹³ g | Halogens, nitro groups | Chlorinated polymer impurities, plasticizers (phthalates) |
| **MS** (Mass Spec) | Universal + structural | ~10⁻¹² g | Mass-selective | Unknown additive identification, structural confirmation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** The **FID** is the workhorse detector for polymer testing because it is highly sensitive to organic (carbon-containing) monomers, additives, and solvents, while being insensitive to the carrier gas and water.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Separation Performance Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Defining Equation | Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Dead (hold-up) time | $t_M$ | Time for unretained species (e.g., air peak) | Mobile-phase hold-up |
| Retention time | $t_R$ | Time from injection to peak maximum | Identification (qualitative) |
| Adjusted retention time | $t'_R$ | $t'_R = t_R - t_M$ | Time spent in stationary phase |
| Capacity factor (retention factor) | $k'$ | $k' = \dfrac{t_R - t_M}{t_M} = \dfrac{t'_R}{t_M}$ | Column "stickiness"; ideal $k'$ = 2 – 10 |
| Selectivity factor | $\alpha$ | $\alpha = \dfrac{k'_2}{k'_1}$ ($\alpha > 1$ always) | Thermodynamic selectivity between two peaks |
| Number of theoretical plates | $N$ | $N = 16 \left(\dfrac{t_R}{w}\right)^2 = 5.54 \left(\dfrac{t_R}{w_{1/2}}\right)^2$ | Column efficiency |
| Plate height (HETP) | $H$ | $H = \dfrac{L}{N}$ | Efficiency per unit length |
| Resolution | $R_s$ | $R_s = \dfrac{2(t_{R2} - t_{R1})}{w_1 + w_2} = \dfrac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha} \cdot \dfrac{k'_2}{1 + k'_2}$ | Overall separation quality; $R_s \geq 1.5$ = baseline |
| Linear velocity (optimum) | $\bar{u}_{opt}$ | $\bar{u}_{opt} = \sqrt{\dfrac{B}{C}}$ (Van Deemter) | Carrier gas velocity at minimum $H$ |
| Van Deemter Equation | $H$ vs $u$ | $H = A + \dfrac{B}{u} + C \cdot u$ | Band-broadening model |
| Kovats Retention Index | $I$ | $I = 100 \left[n + \dfrac{\log t'_R(\text{sample}) - \log t'_R(C_n)}{\log t'_R(C_{n+1}) - \log t'_R(C_n)}\right]$ | Universal retention scale (n-alkanes reference) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Markdown Pitfall Avoided:** All absolute-value expressions above use $\dfrac{...}{...}$ notation rather than $\vert ... \vert$ to prevent markdown table-row pipe-symbol collisions.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In polymer industries, GC is integrated into:

- **Quality Control (QC) labs** of PET, PVC, PS, PMMA, and polypropylene plants to certify residual monomer levels against food-contact regulations (FDA, EU 10/2011).
- **Failure-analysis laboratories** where off-odor or fogging complaints from automotive interiors are investigated via headspace-GC.
- **Reverse-engineering** of polymer formulations where competitive plasticizer blends must be decoded.
- **Polymerization kinetic studies** where GC tracks monomer conversion vs. reaction time.
- **Inverse-GC research labs** characterizing surface energetics of new biopolymers.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Gas Chromatography — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Number of Theoretical Plates ($N$)

The concept of theoretical plates was borrowed from distillation engineering. A "plate" is a discrete equilibration step between mobile and stationary phases.

Consider a Gaussian-shaped chromatographic peak. The peak width at the baseline, $w$, relates to the **standard deviation** $\sigma$ of the Gaussian by:

$$w = 4\sigma$$

The peak maximum elutes at $t_R$, and the standard deviation is expressed as $\sigma = t_R / \sqrt{N}$ by definition of plate count. Substituting:

$$\begin{aligned}
w &= 4 \cdot \frac{t_R}{\sqrt{N}} \\[6pt]
\sqrt{N} &= \frac{4 t_R}{w} \\[6pt]
N &= \left(\frac{4 t_R}{w}\right)^2 = 16 \left(\frac{t_R}{w}\right)^2
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **baseline-peak-width formula** for $N$.

If we instead use the **half-height peak width** $w_{1/2}$, the relationship at half-maximum amplitude gives:

$$w_{1/2} = 2.354 \, \sigma$$

Therefore:

$$\begin{aligned}
w_{1/2} &= 2.354 \cdot \frac{t_R}{\sqrt{N}} \\[6pt]
N &= 5.54 \left(\frac{t_R}{w_{1/2}}\right)^2
\end{aligned}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The factor 5.54 arises because the peak area at half-height uses the Gaussian half-width coefficient $2\sqrt{2\ln 2} \approx 2.354$, whose square is $\approx 5.54$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of the Resolution Equation (Purnell Equation)

For two adjacent peaks 1 and 2 with $t_{R1} < t_{R2}$, and assuming peaks are symmetric and similar in width:

$$R_s = \frac{t_{R2} - t_{R1}}{\frac{1}{2}(w_1 + w_2)}$$

For closely eluting peaks, $w_1 \approx w_2 \equiv w$, so:

$$R_s = \frac{2(t_{R2} - t_{R1})}{w_1 + w_2}$$

Now substitute $w = 4 t_R / \sqrt{N}$ and $t_R - t_M = t'_R = k' t_M$:

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{R2} - t_{R1} &= (k'_2 - k'_1) t_M \\[6pt]
\frac{w_1 + w_2}{2} &\approx w = \frac{4 t_{R2}}{\sqrt{N}} \approx \frac{4 t_{R1}}{\sqrt{N}}
\end{aligned}$$

For a symmetric average: $w \approx 4 (t_{R1} + t_{R2}) / (2\sqrt{N})$. Taking $t_{R1} \approx t_{R2} \approx t_R$:

$$R_s = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{t_{R2} - t_{R1}}{t_R} \cdot \frac{t_M}{t_M}$$

Re-expressing in terms of $k'$ and $\alpha = k'_2 / k'_1$:

$$R_s = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{k'_2 - k'_1}{1 + k'_2} = \frac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \frac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{k'_2}{1 + k'_2}$$

This is the **fundamental Purnell resolution equation**, decomposing $R_s$ into three independent multiplicative factors — each tunable by the analyst.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Optimum Linear Velocity (Van Deemter)

The Van Deemter equation describes the contribution of three band-broadening processes to plate height:

$$H = A + \frac{B}{u} + C \cdot u$$

The **optimum velocity** $u_{opt}$ is found by minimizing $H$ with respect to $u$:

$$\frac{dH}{du} = -\frac{B}{u^2} + C = 0$$

Solving:

$$\begin{aligned}
\frac{B}{u^2} &= C \\[6pt]
u^2 &= \frac{B}{C} \\[6pt]
u_{opt} &= \sqrt{\frac{B}{C}}
\end{aligned}$$

Substituting back:

$$H_{min} = A + 2\sqrt{BC}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU-Style Problem

**Problem (Model):** In a GC analysis of residual styrene monomer in polystyrene, two peaks are observed:

| Peak | Compound | $t_R$ (min) | Peak width $w$ (min) | $w_{1/2}$ (min) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Air (unretained) | 0.50 | — | — |
| 2 | Ethylbenzene | 4.80 | 0.60 | — |
| 3 | Styrene | 5.40 | 0.70 | — |

Compute: (a) $k'$ for ethylbenzene and styrene; (b) $\alpha$; (c) $N$ for styrene (use baseline formula); (d) $H$ for a 30 m capillary; (e) $R_s$ between ethylbenzene and styrene; (f) Comment on baseline separation.

### (a) Capacity Factor $k'$

$$\begin{aligned}
k'_{ethyl} &= \frac{4.80 - 0.50}{0.50} = \frac{4.30}{0.50} = 8.60 \\[6pt]
k'_{sty} &= \frac{5.40 - 0.50}{0.50} = \frac{4.90}{0.50} = 9.80
\end{aligned}$$

### (b) Selectivity Factor $\alpha$

$$\alpha = \frac{k'_{sty}}{k'_{ethyl}} = \frac{9.80}{8.60} = 1.140$$

### (c) Plate Number for Styrene Peak

$$N_{sty} = 16 \left(\frac{t_R}{w}\right)^2 = 16 \left(\frac{5.40}{0.70}\right)^2 = 16 \times (7.714)^2 = 16 \times 59.51 \approx 952$$

### (d) Plate Height $H$

$$H = \frac{L}{N} = \frac{30{,}000 \text{ mm}}{952} \approx 31.5 \text{ mm/plate} = 0.0315 \text{ m/plate}$$

### (e) Resolution

$$R_s = \frac{2(t_{R,sty} - t_{R,ethyl})}{w_{sty} + w_{ethyl}} = \frac{2(5.40 - 4.80)}{0.70 + 0.60} = \frac{1.20}{1.30} \approx 0.923$$

### (f) Verdict

Since $R_s = 0.923 < 1.5$, the separation is **incomplete (not baseline)**. To improve:

- Increase $N$ by **lengthening the column** or **reducing film thickness**.
- Increase $\alpha$ by changing to a more polar stationary phase (e.g., 50 % phenyl).
- Optimize $k'$ (target $k' = 2 - 10$) via temperature programming.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The Purnell equation is the central tool for method development — by varying $N$, $\alpha$, and $k'$ independently, one can systematically reach $R_s \geq 1.5$.

---

## 3.5 Kovats Retention Index — Numerical Illustration

Given a normal-alkane series $C_6$ (hexane), $C_7$ (heptane), $C_8$ (octane), with retention times:

| Compound | $t_R$ (min) |
|---|---|
| Air | 0.30 |
| $n$-Hexane ($C_6$) | 4.20 |
| Unknown additive | 5.10 |
| $n$-Heptane ($C_7$) | 6.50 |

Compute $I$ for the unknown:

$$I = 100 \left[6 + \frac{\log(5.10 - 0.30) - \log(4.20 - 0.30)}{\log(6.50 - 0.30) - \log(4.20 - 0.30)}\right]$$

$$\begin{aligned}
I &= 100 \left[6 + \frac{\log(4.80) - \log(3.90)}{\log(6.20) - \log(3.90)}\right] \\[6pt]
&= 100 \left[6 + \frac{0.6812 - 0.5911}{0.7924 - 0.5911}\right] \\[6pt]
&= 100 \left[6 + \frac{0.0901}{0.2013}\right] \\[6pt]
&= 100 \left[6 + 0.4476\right] = 644.76 \approx 645
\end{aligned}$$

The unknown elutes with a retention index of **645**, suggesting a compound with effective carbon number ≈ 6.45 (e.g., a branched ketone or ester — useful for additive identification).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Gas Chromatography — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 GC Instrument — Functional Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    gasSrc[Gas Supply Cylinder He or N2]:::sourceNode --> reg[Pressure Regulator]:::ctrlNode
    reg --> trap[Molecular Sieve and Oxygen Trap]:::purifyNode
    trap --> flowCtl[Mass Flow Controller]:::ctrlNode
    flowCtl --> inj[Sample Injector Split or Splitless 250C]:::processNode
    injSample[Liquid Sample via Microsyringe]:::inputNode --> inj
    inj --> oven[Column Oven Temperature Programmed]:::processNode
    oven --> col[Capillary Column Fused Silica 30 m x 0.25 mm]:::processNode
    col --> det[Detector FID or TCD or MS]:::detectNode
    det --> amp[Signal Amplifier]:::ctrlNode
    amp --> pc[Data System PC with Integrator]:::outputNode
    pc --> chrom[Chromatogram Plot]:::outputNode

    classDef sourceNode fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef inputNode fill:#a4c2f4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef processNode fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ctrlNode fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef purifyNode fill:#d9d2e9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef detectNode fill:#f9cb9c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outputNode fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.2 Van Deemter Curve — H vs. Linear Velocity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    vdStart[Van Deemter Equation H = A + B/u + C*u]:::theoryNode
    vdStart --> eddyTerm[A Term Eddy Diffusion Independent of u]:::branchNode
    vdStart --> longTerm[B Term Longitudinal Diffusion Dominant at Low u]:::branchNode
    vdStart --> massTerm[C Term Mass Transfer Dominant at High u]:::branchNode

    eddyTerm --> optPoint[Optimal u Gives Minimum H]:::targetNode
    longTerm --> optPoint
    massTerm --> optPoint

    optPoint --> practical[For He Carrier Gas u_opt approx 20 to 40 cm per second]:::outputNode
    optPoint --> hMin[H_min = A + 2 times square root of BC]:::outputNode

    classDef theoryNode fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef branchNode fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef targetNode fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outputNode fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Polymer Analysis Workflow Using GC

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sample[Polymer Sample Pellet or Film]:::inputNode
    sample --> decide{Analysis Goal?}:::decisionNode

    decide -->|Residual Monomer| extract1[Solvent Extraction or Dissolution]:::processNode
    decide -->|Polymer Identity Unknown| pyro[Pyrolysis at 600C]:::processNode
    decide -->|Volatile Additives| hs[Headspace Sampling]:::processNode
    decide -->|Surface Energy IGC| igc[Polymer as Stationary Phase]:::processNode

    extract1 --> inject1[Inject Liquid Extract into GC]:::processNode
    pyro --> inject2[Inject Pyrolysis Products into GC]:::processNode
    hs --> inject3[Inject Headspace Vapor into GC]:::processNode
    igc --> inject4[Inject Probe Analytes through Polymer Column]:::processNode

    inject1 --> sep1[GLC Separation on Nonpolar Column]:::processNode
    inject2 --> sep2[Pyrogram Fingerprint Matching]:::processNode
    inject3 --> sep3[VOC Separation on Polar Column]:::processNode
    inject4 --> sep4[Probe Retention for Surface Energy]:::processNode

    sep1 --> result1[Quantify Monomer ppm Level]:::outputNode
    sep2 --> result2[Match Library Spectrum for Polymer ID]:::outputNode
    sep3 --> result3[Report VOC Emission Profile]:::outputNode
    sep4 --> result4[Calculate Solubility Parameter delta]:::outputNode

    classDef inputNode fill:#a4c2f4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef decisionNode fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef processNode fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef outputNode fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

## 4.4 Typical GC Chromatogram — Annotated Reference

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    xAxis[Time axis t in minutes]:::axisNode
    peakAir[Peak 1 Air t_M at 0.5 min]:::peakNodeA
    peak1[Peak 2 Ethylbenzene at 4.8 min w = 0.6]:::peakNodeB
    peak2[Peak 3 Styrene at 5.4 min w = 0.7]:::peakNodeC
    peak3[Peak 4 Internal Standard at 7.2 min]:::peakNodeD

    xAxis --- peakAir
    xAxis --- peak1
    xAxis --- peak2
    xAxis --- peak3

    note1[Air peak used to compute t_M]:::annotNode
    peak1 --> note1
    note2[Styrene peak width used in N and Rs]:::annotNode
    peak2 --> note2

    classDef axisNode fill:#e7e6e6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef peakNodeA fill:#d9d2e9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef peakNodeB fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef peakNodeC fill:#f9cb9c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef peakNodeD fill:#a4c2f4,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef annotNode fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric, prefixed with descriptive letters (e.g., `peakNodeA`, `vdStart`), avoiding reserved Mermaid keywords like `end`, `graph`, or `subgraph`. Labels are plain uppercase text without markdown bold or HTML formatting.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Gas Chromatography. List its essential components.**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / Model]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Gas Chromatography is a chromatographic separation technique in which a vaporized sample is carried by an inert gaseous mobile phase through a column containing a stationary phase, with separation based on differential partitioning of analytes between the two phases.

**Essential Components (2 marks):**
1. Carrier gas supply (He / N₂ / H₂) with pressure regulator and flow controller.
2. Sample injector (split/splitless or on-column).
3. Column oven (isothermal or temperature-programmed).
4. Separation column (packed or capillary).
5. Detector (FID, TCD, ECD, or MS).
6. Data acquisition / recorder system.

---

### **Q2. What is the Capacity Factor ($k'$)? Why is $k' = 2 - 10$ considered ideal?**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 / Model]**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** The capacity factor $k'$ (also called retention factor) of an analyte is the ratio of its adjusted retention time to the dead time:

$$k' = \frac{t_R - t_M}{t_M}$$

It represents the additional time the analyte spends in the stationary phase relative to an unretained compound.

**Why ideal range (1.5 marks):**
- If $k' < 2$, peaks elute too close to the dead volume, giving poor resolution from solvent or air peak.
- If $k' > 10$, peaks become excessively broad, analysis time is long, and detection sensitivity decreases.
- The range **$k' = 2 - 10$** balances resolution, analysis time, and detectability.

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> *Per KTU 2024 ESE regulation, Part B Module-internal choice: answer ONE of the two.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) With a neat block diagram, describe the instrumentation of Gas Chromatography and explain the function of each component. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A GC instrument consists of six major subsystems:

1. **Carrier Gas Supply System (1 mark):**
   Supplies high-purity inert gas (He, N₂, or H₂) from a cylinder through a two-stage pressure regulator, molecular-sieve/oxygen traps (to remove moisture and O₂ that degrade the stationary phase), and a mass-flow controller maintaining constant linear velocity (typically 20–40 cm/s for He).

2. **Sample Injector (1 mark):**
   A heated port (200–300 °C) where the micro-syringe-injected sample is vaporized instantaneously. Operated in split mode (for concentrated samples; only 1/50 to 1/500 of vapor enters the column) or splitless mode (for trace analytes). PTV (programmable temperature vaporizer) injectors are used for thermolabile or large-volume injections.

3. **Column Oven (1 mark):**
   A forced-air-circulation oven maintaining the column at a precisely controlled temperature. Operates isothermally (constant T) or with temperature programming (initial low T, then ramp 5–30 °C/min to elute heavier analytes).

4. **Separation Column (2 marks):**
   - **Packed column:** 1–3 m long, 2–4 mm ID, filled with solid support (e.g., Chromosorb) coated with 3–10 % liquid stationary phase (e.g., SE-30, OV-17).
   - **Capillary (WCOT) column:** 15–60 m long, 0.10–0.53 mm ID, stationary phase film 0.1–5 µm coated on the inner wall of fused silica. Higher efficiency (N > 100,000 plates).

5. **Detector (1.5 marks):**
   - **FID (Flame Ionization Detector):** H₂/air flame; ionizes organic carbon; current proportional to mass flow; sensitive to ~10⁻¹² g.
   - **TCD (Thermal Conductivity Detector):** Compares thermal conductivity of column effluent vs. reference; universal but less sensitive.
   - **MS (Mass Spectrometer):** Universal + provides structural (mass spectrum) information; gold standard for unknown identification.

6. **Data System (0.5 marks):**
   Computer / integrator converts the detector analog signal into a digital chromatogram, identifies peaks, computes $t_R$, $w$, area, and concentration via calibration curves.

#### **(b) Explain the Van Deemter equation. How is the optimum linear velocity derived? Discuss its significance in GC method development. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Van Deemter Equation (2.5 marks):**

The Van Deemter equation relates the plate height $H$ of a chromatographic column to the linear velocity $u$ of the mobile phase:

$$H = A + \frac{B}{u} + C \cdot u$$

where:
- **$A$ (Eddy diffusion term)** — independent of $u$; arises from multiple flow paths through the packed bed. **Zero for open tubular (capillary) columns.**
- **$B/u$ (Longitudinal diffusion term)** — diffusion of analyte along the column axis; dominant at low $u$.
- **$C \cdot u$ (Mass-transfer term)** — resistance to equilibration between mobile and stationary phases; dominant at high $u$.

**Derivation of $u_{opt}$ (2 marks):**

Differentiate $H$ with respect to $u$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{dH}{du} = -\frac{B}{u^2} + C = 0$$

$$u_{opt} = \sqrt{\frac{B}{C}}$$

Substituting back:

$$H_{min} = A + 2\sqrt{BC}$$

**Significance in method development (2.5 marks):**

- The **optimum linear velocity** identifies the carrier-gas flow rate giving the **highest column efficiency** (lowest $H$, highest $N$).
- For He, $u_{opt} \approx 20 - 40$ cm/s; for N₂, $u_{opt} \approx 10 - 20$ cm/s (because $B$ is smaller for N₂).
- Operating above $u_{opt}$ shortens analysis time but reduces $N$ — acceptable when $R_s$ is already > 1.5.
- Operating below $u_{opt}$ is rarely beneficial (long analysis, broad peaks).
- The Van Deemter plot is the **diagnostic tool** for troubleshooting band broadening in GC.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly equate $H_{min}$ with $u_{opt}$. They are different — $H_{min}$ is the minimum plate height, and $u_{opt}$ is the velocity at which $H_{min}$ occurs. **Always show units.**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

#### **(a) Define the following with their mathematical expressions: Capacity factor ($k'$), Selectivity factor ($\alpha$), Number of theoretical plates ($N$), Resolution ($R_s$). State the conditions for baseline separation. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Capacity Factor $k'$ (1.5 marks):**
   Ratio of adjusted retention time to dead time:

$$k' = \frac{t_R - t_M}{t_M}$$

   Represents the analyte's "affinity" for the stationary phase relative to the mobile phase.

2. **Selectivity Factor $\alpha$ (1.5 marks):**
   Ratio of capacity factors of two analytes:

$$\alpha = \frac{k'_2}{k'_1} \quad (\alpha \geq 1)$$

   A thermodynamic measure; $\alpha = 1$ means co-elution.

3. **Number of Theoretical Plates $N$ (1.5 marks):**
   Measure of column efficiency; computed from peak width:

$$N = 16\left(\frac{t_R}{w}\right)^2 = 5.54\left(\frac{t_R}{w_{1/2}}\right)^2$$

   Higher $N$ means sharper peaks.

4. **Resolution $R_s$ (1.5 marks):**
   Quantitative measure of separation between two adjacent peaks:

$$R_s = \frac{2(t_{R2} - t_{R1})}{w_1 + w_2}$$

**Baseline separation condition (1 mark):**
$R_s \geq 1.5$ (or sometimes defined as $R_s \geq 1.0$ for acceptable, $R_s \geq 1.5$ for true baseline). At $R_s = 1.5$, peak-to-peak valley returns to ~95 % of baseline.

#### **(b) The following data were obtained in a GC analysis of a polymer extract. Compute $k'$ for each peak, $\alpha$, $N$ for peak 2 (use baseline formula), and $R_s$ between peaks 1 and 2. (7 marks)**

| Peak | Compound | $t_R$ (min) | $w$ (min) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Air (unretained) | 0.40 | — |
| 1 | Toluene | 3.60 | 0.40 |
| 2 | Ethylbenzene | 4.80 | 0.50 |

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Capacity Factors (2 marks):**

$$k'_1 = \frac{3.60 - 0.40}{0.40} = \frac{3.20}{0.40} = 8.00$$

$$k'_2 = \frac{4.80 - 0.40}{0.40} = \frac{4.40}{0.40} = 11.00$$

Both are in the ideal $k' = 2 - 10$ range, with peak 2 slightly above ideal.

**(ii) Selectivity Factor (1.5 marks):**

$$\alpha = \frac{k'_2}{k'_1} = \frac{11.00}{8.00} = 1.375$$

**(iii) Plate Number for Peak 2 (1.5 marks):**

$$N = 16 \left(\frac{t_R}{w}\right)^2 = 16 \left(\frac{4.80}{0.50}\right)^2 = 16 \times 92.16 \approx 1475 \text{ plates}$$

**(iv) Resolution Between Peaks 1 and 2 (2 marks):**

$$R_s = \frac{2(t_{R2} - t_{R1})}{w_1 + w_2} = \frac{2(4.80 - 3.60)}{0.40 + 0.50} = \frac{2.40}{0.90} \approx 2.67$$

**Verdict:** $R_s = 2.67 > 1.5$, so the toluene–ethylbenzene pair is **baseline separated**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall (Valuation Warning):**
> - **Forgetting to subtract $t_M$** when computing $k'$ — this is the most common 1-mark deduction.
> - **Mixing up $\alpha$** as $k'_1 / k'_2$ instead of $k'_2 / k'_1$ — must always use later-eluting as numerator so $\alpha \geq 1$.
> - **Unit mismatch** in $N$: $t_R$ and $w$ must be in the same units (both min, both s) — otherwise the $N$ value is meaningless.
> - **In $R_s$**, students sometimes use $(t_{R2} - t_{R1})$ alone instead of $2(t_{R2} - t_{R1})$ — this gives $R_s$ half the correct value and may falsely indicate incomplete separation.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gas Chromatography — Last-Minute Revision Notes**

- ✅ **GC separates** vaporizable analytes via **differential partitioning** between a gaseous mobile phase (He/N₂/H₂) and a liquid/adsorbent stationary phase.
- ✅ **Carrier gas** must be inert — He is the most common choice (gives best $u_{opt}$); H₂ is fastest but flammable; N₂ is cheapest.
- ✅ **Injectors**: split (concentrated), splitless (trace), on-column (thermally labile), PTV (versatile).
- ✅ **Columns**: packed (lower efficiency, rugged) vs capillary (WCOT — N > 100,000, gold standard).
- ✅ **FID** = universal for organics; **TCD** = universal for all; **ECD** = selective for halogens; **MS** = structural ID.
- ✅ **Retention parameters**: $t_M$, $t_R$, $t'_R$, $k'$, $\alpha$ — all measured in time units.
- ✅ **Efficiency**: $N = 16(t_R/w)^2$; $H = L/N$ — smaller $H$ = better column.
- ✅ **Resolution Purnell equation**: $R_s = \dfrac{\sqrt{N}}{4} \cdot \dfrac{\alpha - 1}{\alpha} \cdot \dfrac{k'_2}{1+k'_2}$ — three independent knobs.
- ✅ **Baseline separation**: $R_s \geq 1.5$.
- ✅ **Van Deemter**: $H = A + B/u + C u$; $u_{opt} = \sqrt{B/C}$; $H_{min} = A + 2\sqrt{BC}$.
- ✅ **Kovats Retention Index** uses $n$-alkanes as reference ($I$ = 100 × number of C atoms for $n$-alkanes).
- ✅ **Polymer applications**: residual monomer QC, additive identification, Py-GC fingerprinting, headspace VOC, inverse GC for $\delta$ (solubility parameter).
- ✅ **Temperature programming** (initial low → ramp → final high) elutes a wide volatility range in one run.
- ✅ **Sample must be vaporizable** without decomposition — this is GC's chief limitation (use LC for non-volatiles, HPLC for thermally fragile polymers).
- ✅ **Inverse GC**: the polymer itself is the stationary phase; probe analytes measure its surface energy and Flory–Huggins $\chi$ parameter.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scanning Electron Microscopy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT602 - POLYMER TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Scanning Electron Microscopy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM)** is a high-resolution electron-optical characterization technique in which a finely focused, accelerated beam of electrons is raster-scanned across the surface of a specimen, and the resulting signals (principally **secondary electrons (SE)**, **backscattered electrons (BSE)**, and **characteristic X-rays**) emitted from each scanned point are collected, amplified, and mapped synchronously onto a display screen to produce a magnified topographical and compositional image of the specimen surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Reference):**
> SEM is an instrument that uses a focused beam of high-energy electrons to generate a variety of signals at the surface of solid specimens. The signals derived from electron–sample interactions reveal information about the sample including *external morphology (texture), chemical composition, crystalline structure, and orientation of materials making up the sample*.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Blind Person Reading a Coin"

Imagine you are blindfolded and handed a coin. You cannot see it, but you can feel it. To map the coin, you:
1. Run a **tiny fingertip** (the focused electron beam) across its surface.
2. As your fingertip moves, you feel bumps, ridges, and edges.
3. You translate (scan) your fingertip left → right, top → bottom in a grid pattern.
4. For each position, you record the "feel" — sharp, smooth, raised.
5. You then draw a map on paper where bumps = light and smooth = dark.

**The SEM does exactly this**, but with electrons instead of a fingertip, and the "feel" comes from how many electrons bounce back or knock out secondary electrons at each spot.

| Parameter | Finger Analogy | SEM Equivalent |
|---|---|---|
| Probe | Fingertip | Focused electron beam |
| Sense | Touch / vibration | Secondary & backscattered electron yield |
| Scanning | Moving hand in a grid | Electromagnetic scan coils (raster) |
| Map | Drawing on paper | Cathode Ray Tube (CRT) / digital display |
| Resolution | ~1 mm (skin receptors) | **1–10 nm** (electron probe size) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why electrons and not light?**
> An optical microscope is limited by the wavelength of visible light (~400–700 nm) — the **Abbe diffraction limit**. The de Broglie wavelength of an electron accelerated at 30 kV is ~**0.007 nm** (~100,000× shorter than visible light), so the theoretical resolution is enormously enhanced.

---

### 1.3 Standard Operating Metrics (Must Know for KTU Exams)

- **Accelerating Voltage (E₀):** Typically **0.5 kV – 30 kV** for polymers (low voltage prevents beam damage).
- **Resolution:** **1.0 nm @ 15 kV** (SE imaging).
- **Magnification range:** **10× to 1,000,000×**.
- **Working Distance (WD):** **5 – 25 mm**.
- **Chamber Pressure (High Vacuum Mode):** **~10⁻³ to 10⁻⁶ Pa**.
- **Specimen stage:** 5-axis (X, Y, Z, tilt, rotation) motorized eucentric stage.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — The 7-Step Logic Chain

The SEM operates on a tightly coupled *cause-and-effect* chain:

1. **Electron Generation:** A thermionic or field-emission source emits electrons (cathode = filament).
2. **Acceleration:** A high voltage (0.5–30 kV) accelerates electrons down the column toward the anode.
3. **Condensation (Beam Shaping):** Two or more **electromagnetic condenser lenses** demagnify the source crossover.
4. **Aperturing:** A final **objective aperture** (typically 20–100 µm) cuts off divergent electrons, improving depth of field and resolution.
5. **Scanning (Rastering):** **Scan coils** (electromagnetic deflection system) sweep the converged beam in a TV-like raster pattern across the specimen.
6. **Signal Generation:** Beam electrons interact with specimen atoms — generating SE, BSE, Auger electrons, X-rays, and cathodoluminescence.
7. **Image Reconstruction:** Each emitted signal modulates the brightness of a CRT pixel synced to the beam position — **point-to-point correspondence** yields the magnified image.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Magnification Equation (Fundamental KTU Formula):**
> Magnification is geometric — it is *not* an optical zoom. It is defined entirely by the ratio of the **display raster length (L)** to the **specimen scan length (l)**.
> $$M = \frac{L}{l}$$

So if you scan a 10 µm region of the specimen and display it across a 100 mm CRT screen, the magnification is **10,000×**.

---

### 2.2 Signal Types Generated in the SEM

| Signal | Origin Depth | Energy (eV) | Provides | Detector |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Secondary Electrons (SE)** | Top 1–10 nm | < 50 eV | Topography (most common) | Everhart-Thornley detector |
| **Backscattered Electrons (BSE)** | Top 0.1–1 µm | > 50 eV up to E₀ | Composition (Z-contrast) | Solid-state BSE detector |
| **Characteristic X-rays** | Top 0.1–3 µm | Element-specific | Elemental analysis (EDS) | Si(Li) / SDD detector |
| **Auger Electrons** | Top < 5 nm | Element-specific | Surface composition | Auger detector |
| **Cathodoluminescence** | Variable | Photon emission | Optical/electronic properties | Photomultiplier |

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Exam Cheat-Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> The following are the **highest-yield equations** for any SEM-related KTU question. Memorize these.

| # | Equation | Variables & Units | Notes / KTU Insight |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M = \dfrac{L}{l}$ | M = magnification (dimensionless); L = display length (m); l = scanned length on specimen (m) | Smaller scanned area → higher M for same display size |
| 2 | $\lambda = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V}}$ (non-relativistic) | λ = wavelength (m); h = **Planck constant = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**; mₑ = **9.109 × 10⁻³¹ kg**; e = **1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**; V = accelerating voltage (V) | Higher V → smaller λ → better resolution |
| 3 | $\lambda = \dfrac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V \left(1 + \dfrac{eV}{2 m_e c^2}\right)}}$ (relativistic, for V > 10 kV) | c = speed of light | **Use this version for 30 kV SEM** — non-relativistic underestimates resolution |
| 4 | $\lambda \approx \dfrac{1.22}{\sqrt{V}}$ nm | V in volts | Useful empirical shortcut for polymer SEM (low V) |
| 5 | $d = \dfrac{0.61 \, \lambda}{\alpha}$ | d = probe diameter (m); α = convergence half-angle (rad) | **Rayleigh-type** resolution limit |
| 6 | $\Delta f = \dfrac{1}{\alpha \cdot M_{obj}}$ | Δf = depth of field (m); M_obj = objective lens magnification | Smaller α → greater depth of focus |
| 7 | $E_0 = eV$ | E₀ = beam energy (J, often reported in keV) | Total kinetic energy of primary electron |
| 8 | $R_{K-O} \approx \dfrac{27.6 \, E_0^{1.67}}{\rho \, Z^{0.89}}$ (mg/cm²) | E₀ in keV; ρ in g/cm³; Z = atomic number | **Kanaya-Okayama range** — penetration depth of electrons |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pipe-Symbol Trap:** Never use absolute-value `|x|` inside markdown table cells — it breaks the table parser. Use LaTeX `\vert x \vert` instead.

---

### 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why SEM Matters for Polymer Testing

SEM is **the most important morphological tool** in modern polymer laboratories. In the KTU 2024 context, expect questions linking SEM to:

1. **Fracture Surface Analysis** — determining ductile vs. brittle failure in polymer composites.
2. **Fiber-Matrix Interface Studies** — SEM micrographs of cryo-fractured composites reveal fiber pull-out, debonding, and matrix cracking.
3. **Phase Morphology in Polymer Blends** — SEM of selectively etched or stained blends shows dispersed domain size & distribution.
4. **Adhesion & Coating Defects** — pinholes, cracks, delamination.
5. **Nanocomposite Dispersion** — high-resolution FESEM reveals organoclay or graphene platelet dispersion.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Electron Wavelength as a Function of Accelerating Voltage (Non-Relativistic)

This derivation proves why a 30 kV electron has a wavelength ~70,000× smaller than green light — the **fundamental reason** SEM outperforms optical microscopy.

**Step 1 — Start from the de Broglie hypothesis.**
Every moving particle has an associated wavelength given by its momentum p.

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{p}$$

**Step 2 — Express momentum in terms of kinetic energy gained through acceleration.**
The accelerating voltage V imparts kinetic energy $E_K = eV$ to the electron. Equating this to $\frac{p^2}{2 m_e}$ (classical, non-relativistic):

$$eV = \frac{p^2}{2 m_e}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for p.**

$$p = \sqrt{2 m_e \, eV}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back into the de Broglie relation.**

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e \, eV}}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate numerically for V = 30,000 V (typical SEM):**

$$\lambda = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{\sqrt{2 \cdot (9.109 \times 10^{-31}) \cdot (1.602 \times 10^{-19}) \cdot (3 \times 10^{4})}}$$

Compute denominator:
$$2 \cdot 9.109 \times 10^{-31} \cdot 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \cdot 3 \times 10^{4}$$

Multiplying inside the square root:
$$= 2 \cdot 9.109 \cdot 1.602 \cdot 3 \times 10^{-31-19+4}$$

$$= 87.55 \times 10^{-46}$$

$$= 8.755 \times 10^{-45}$$

Square root:
$$\sqrt{8.755 \times 10^{-45}} = 9.357 \times 10^{-23}$$

Final:
$$\lambda = \frac{6.626 \times 10^{-34}}{9.357 \times 10^{-23}} \approx 7.08 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m} = 0.00708 \text{ nm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison:** Green light = 550 nm. 30 kV electrons = 0.00708 nm.
> **Ratio:** 550 / 0.00708 ≈ **77,700× shorter wavelength**.
> This is why SEM can resolve nanometer-scale features.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Relativistic Correction (Required for V > 10 kV)

At 30 kV, the electron velocity reaches ~33% of the speed of light. Relativity becomes non-negligible.

**Step 1 — Total energy (relativistic):**
$$E_{tot} = m_e c^2 + eV = \gamma m_e c^2$$

**Step 2 — Solve for γ:**
$$\gamma = 1 + \frac{eV}{m_e c^2}$$

**Step 3 — Momentum (relativistic):**
$$p = \gamma m_e v = \sqrt{2 m_e eV \left(1 + \frac{eV}{2 m_e c^2}\right)}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into de Broglie:**
$$\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e eV \left(1 + \dfrac{eV}{2 m_e c^2}\right)}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical evaluation at 30 kV:**
$$\frac{eV}{2 m_e c^2} = \frac{30{,}000}{2 \cdot 511{,}000} \approx 0.0294$$

Correction factor inside parenthesis: (1 + 0.0294) = 1.0294

$$\lambda_{rel} = \frac{0.00708}{\sqrt{1.0294}} \approx 0.00698 \text{ nm}$$

**Difference is ~1.4%** — small but significant for high-precision resolution claims in KTU numerical problems.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Magnification from Scan Geometry

**Step 1 — The scan coils deflect the focused beam across the specimen surface over a length $l$.**

**Step 2 — Simultaneously, the same scan-coil signal drives the CRT electron beam across the screen over a length $L$.**

**Step 3 — Because both beams are driven by the same synchronized ramp signals, their x-y positions are perfectly correlated (lock-in correspondence).**

**Step 4 — Therefore, by direct ratio:**
$$M = \frac{L}{l}$$

**Step 5 — Worked numerical example (typical KTU-style):**

Given: Display screen width L = 100 mm, and the operator scans a specimen field of view l = 10 µm. Calculate magnification.

Convert: L = 100 × 10⁻³ m = 0.1 m; l = 10 × 10⁻⁶ m = 10⁻⁵ m.

$$M = \frac{0.1}{10^{-5}} = 10{,}000 \times$$

If the operator reduces the scanned area to 1 µm (by changing scan-coil amplitude):

$$M = \frac{0.1}{10^{-6}} = 100{,}000 \times$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** Magnification is therefore **inversely proportional** to scanned length. To increase magnification, you scan a *smaller* region — you do not "zoom" optically.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Resolution vs. Working Distance vs. Voltage

The following analytical comparison is a high-yield KTU exam template.

| Accelerating Voltage (kV) | Electron Wavelength λ (nm) | Theoretical Probe d (nm) | Best Application for Polymers |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1.0 | 0.0388 | ~5.2 | Surface-sensitive imaging, beam-sensitive polymers |
| 5.0 | 0.0173 | ~2.3 | Coating defects, thin films |
| 15.0 | 0.0087 | ~1.2 | Standard high-res morphology |
| 30.0 | 0.00698 | ~1.0 | Maximum resolution, beam damage risk |

| Working Distance WD (mm) | Depth of Field | Probe Size | Image Quality |
|---|---|---|---|
| 5 | Low | Smallest | High resolution, shallow focus |
| 10 | Medium | Balanced | Standard use |
| 25 | High | Larger | Rough/topographic specimens |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 SEM Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture of a Scanning Electron Microscope**, illustrating the path of the primary electron beam (top row) and the signal return path (bottom row) from each specimen interaction.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    EG[Electron Gun Filament Cathode] -->|thermionic emission| CL1[Condenser Lens 1 Demagnify 50x]
    CL1 --> CL2[Condenser Lens 2 Variable Aperture]
    CL2 --> AP[Final Objective Aperture 20 to 100 um]
    AP --> OL[Objective Lens Final Focus]
    OL --> SC[Scan Coils XY Raster Deflection]
    SC --> SP[Specimen Stage 5 Axis Eucentric]
    SP -->|SE signal 50 eV| SED[Everhart Thornley SE Detector]
    SP -->|BSE signal high E| BSED[Solid State BSE Detector]
    SP -->|X-ray photons| XRD[EDS SiLi or SDD Detector]
    SED --> AMP[Signal Amplifier Photomultiplier]
    BSED --> AMP
    XRD --> MCA[Multichannel Analyzer Pulse Processor]
    AMP --> CRT[Cathode Ray Tube Display Synchronized Raster]
    MCA --> CRT
    CRT --> IMG[Final SEM Micrograph Digital or Analog]
    VAC[Vacuum System 10 to power -3 to 10 to power -6 Pa] -.-> EG
    VAC -.-> SP
```

### 4.2 Annotated Block Schematic — Polymer Fracture Surface Workflow

For the KTU polymer-testing context, the following diagram shows how SEM integrates into a typical polymer composite fracture study.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SA[Step A Polymer Composite Specimen] --> CR[Step B Cryo Fracture in Liquid Nitrogen]
    CR --> CO[Step C Conductive Coating Sputter Au or Pt 5 to 10 nm]
    CO --> ST[Step D Mount on Aluminum Stub with Carbon Tape]
    ST --> IN[Step E Insert into SEM Chamber and Pump Vacuum]
    IN --> IMG[Step F Acquire SE Image at 5 to 15 kV WD 10 mm]
    IMG --> ANA[Step G Analyze Fiber Pullout Matrix Cracking Interface Debonding]
    ANA --> REP[Step H Generate Test Report with Micrograph Magnification and Scale Bar]
```

### 4.3 Electron–Specimen Interaction Volume Schematic (Logical Map)

The "interaction volume" or "pear-shaped excitation region" is a critical concept for KTU 2024.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PE[Primary Electron Beam Incoming from Above] --> SE1[SE1 Exit at Top Surface Topography Signal]
    PE --> SE2[SE2 Generated Deeper but Escape 1 to 10 nm]
    PE --> BSE1[BSE1 High Angle Backscatter Z Contrast]
    PE --> BSE2[BSE2 Multiple Scattered Escape 0.1 to 1 um]
    PE --> XRAY[Characteristic X-ray Generation 0.1 to 3 um Depth]
    PE --> AUG[Auger Electron Near Surface 5 nm]
    PE --> ABS[Absorbed Electron Specimen Current]
    subgraph IV[Interaction Volume Pear Shaped Beneath Beam Spot]
        SE2
        BSE2
        XRAY
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Polymer Implications:** Because polymers are **low-density, low-Z materials**, the interaction volume is **large and deep** at high kV. This is why **low accelerating voltages (1–5 kV)** are strongly recommended in KTU-aligned polymer testing labs to confine the interaction volume to the true surface and avoid charging artifacts.

---

### 4.4 Specimen Preparation Pathway (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    POL[Polymer Sample] --> DRY[Drying in Vacuum Oven 24 h Removes Moisture]
    DRY --> F1{Fracture or Cross Section}
    F1 -->|Cryo Fracture| CR1[Immerse in Liquid Nitrogen Then Snap]
    F1 -->|Microtome Cut| CR2[Ultramicrotome Glass or Diamond Knife]
    CR1 --> MT[Mount on Aluminum Stub Carbon Tape]
    CR2 --> MT
    MT --> SPUT[Sputter Coat with Au Pd or Pt 5 to 10 nm]
    SPUT --> RDY[Ready for SEM Chamber Loading]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

*Define Scanning Electron Microscopy and list any four signals generated when the primary electron beam interacts with the specimen.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM) is an electron-optical technique in which a finely focused beam of electrons is raster-scanned across the surface of a specimen, and the resulting emitted signals are collected and used to produce a magnified image of the specimen's surface topography and composition.

**Four Signals (½ Mark each = 2 Marks):**

1. **Secondary Electrons (SE)** — low-energy electrons (< 50 eV) emitted from the top 1–10 nm of the surface; used for topography.
2. **Backscattered Electrons (BSE)** — high-energy electrons reflected from depths up to ~1 µm; used for compositional (Z-) contrast.
3. **Characteristic X-rays** — emitted when inner-shell electrons are ejected; used for elemental analysis via EDS.
4. **Auger Electrons** — surface-sensitive (≤ 5 nm escape depth) electrons used for surface composition.

*(Other acceptable signals: cathodoluminescence, specimen/absorbed current, transmitted electrons in STEM mode.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

*Why must polymer specimens be coated with a thin conductive layer (e.g., gold) before SEM examination?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Polymers are **electrical insulators**. When the primary electron beam continuously bombards a non-conductive surface:

1. **Charge accumulation (1 Mark):** Electrons cannot flow to ground, so negative charge builds up on the surface.
2. **Charging artifacts (1 Mark):** This local charge deflects incoming primary electrons and outgoing secondary electrons, producing bright streaks, distorted images, and loss of resolution.
3. **Thermal damage (½ Mark):** Insulators dissipate beam energy as heat, leading to localized melting or shrinkage.
4. **Solution (½ Mark):** A sputter-coated thin layer (5–10 nm) of Au, Pt, or Au–Pd provides a conductive path to ground, eliminating charging and improving secondary electron yield.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write only "polymers are non-conductors" without explaining the *consequence* (image artifacts) and the *remedy* (sputter coating). Both are mandatory for full marks.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, describe the construction and working of a Scanning Electron Microscope. Explain the role of (i) electromagnetic lenses, (ii) scan coils, and (iii) the Everhart–Thornley detector.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Derive the expression for the de Broglie wavelength of an electron accelerated through a potential V. Hence calculate the wavelength of an electron accelerated at 15 kV, and explain why this enables SEM to achieve much higher resolution than an optical microscope.

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]:**

**Block Diagram (3 Marks):**
*(Refer to Section 4.1 diagram of this note for the architecture.)*

A standard SEM consists of:
1. **Electron gun** — tungsten filament (thermionic) or field-emission cathode emitting electrons by thermionic emission or field emission. Operated at ~2800 K with vacuum ≤ 10⁻⁴ Pa.
2. **Condenser lens 1 and 2** — electromagnetic lenses that demagnify the electron source crossover by 50–100×.
3. **Objective aperture** — typically 20–100 µm; trims divergent electrons to control probe size and depth of field.
4. **Objective lens** — final focusing lens producing the smallest probe (~1 nm).
5. **Scan coils** — electromagnetic deflection system producing the raster pattern.
6. **Specimen stage** — 5-axis eucentric stage inside a high-vacuum chamber (10⁻³ to 10⁻⁶ Pa).
7. **Detectors** — Everhart-Thornley for SE, solid-state for BSE, Si(Li)/SDD for X-rays.
8. **Display** — CRT or digital FDP synced to scan-coil signals.

**Roles (4 Marks — 1.33 each):**

**(i) Electromagnetic Lenses:** Function like convex glass lenses but use **magnetic fields generated by current-carrying coils** to focus the electron beam. They demagnify the virtual source size progressively so that a wide, divergent beam from the filament becomes a tiny, convergent probe (~1 nm diameter) on the specimen.

**(ii) Scan Coils:** Located after the objective lens, these are **electromagnetic deflection coils** driven by a sawtooth ramp generator. They sweep the focused probe in a TV-like **raster pattern** (left → right, top → bottom) across the specimen surface. The same ramp signal drives the display CRT, ensuring perfect **point-to-point correspondence**.

**(iii) Everhart–Thornley (E–T) Detector:** A biased (+300 V) scintillator–photomultiplier combination that primarily attracts **low-energy secondary electrons** (< 50 eV) from the specimen. The biased collector grid attracts SEs from a wide solid angle; they strike the scintillator, producing photons that the photomultiplier converts to an amplified electrical signal modulating the CRT brightness.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]:**

**Derivation (5 Marks):** *(See Section 3.1 of this note for the full derivation.)*

Starting from the de Broglie relation $\lambda = h / p$, and the work-energy theorem $eV = p^2 / 2 m_e$:

$$\lambda = \frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m_e e V}}$$

**Numerical Calculation at V = 15,000 V (1 Mark):**

Using $\lambda \approx 1.22 / \sqrt{V}$ nm:

$$\lambda = \frac{1.22}{\sqrt{15{,}000}} = \frac{1.22}{122.47} \approx 0.00996 \text{ nm}$$

Equivalently via full formula:
$$\lambda \approx 0.0087 \text{ nm (relativistic) to } 0.0098 \text{ nm (non-relativistic)}$$

**Why Higher Resolution (1 Mark):**
According to the **Abbe diffraction limit**, the smallest resolvable feature d is proportional to the wavelength λ:

$$d \approx \frac{0.61 \, \lambda}{\sin \alpha}$$

For green light: λ = 550 nm → d ≈ 200–300 nm.
For 15 kV electrons: λ ≈ 0.01 nm → d ≈ 0.5–1 nm.

**Resolution enhancement factor ≈ 50,000–100,000×.** This is why SEM can resolve nanometer-scale features that are far below the optical diffraction limit.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the different types of signals produced in SEM and discuss their relative escape depths and applications. How does signal choice affect the type of image obtained?

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is meant by "interaction volume" in SEM? With a diagram, explain how it varies with (i) atomic number Z and (ii) accelerating voltage. Discuss the implications for SEM imaging of polymers.

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]:**

**Signal Classification (4 Marks):** *(Refer to Section 2.2 table.)*

| Signal | Escape Depth | Energy | Image Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| Secondary Electrons (SE) | 1–10 nm | < 50 eV | **Topographic image** (most common) |
| Backscattered Electrons (BSE) | 0.1–1 µm | > 50 eV to E₀ | **Compositional image** (Z-contrast) |
| Characteristic X-rays | 0.1–3 µm | Element-specific | **Elemental map** (via EDS) |
| Auger Electrons | < 5 nm | Element-specific | **Surface chemistry** |

**Discussion (3 Marks):**
- **SE images** show fine surface detail because SEs escape from only the topmost few nm; ideal for **fracture surface topography, fiber-matrix morphology, coating defects**.
- **BSE images** show compositional variation: brighter regions = higher Z (e.g., metal filler in polymer), darker = lower Z (polymer matrix). Useful for studying **filler dispersion, phase contrast**.
- **X-ray/EDS maps** show **spatial elemental distribution** — e.g., confirming presence of TiO₂ nanoparticles or glass-fiber reinforcement.

The image type is therefore entirely controlled by which detector the operator selects.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]:**

**Definition (1 Mark):**
The **interaction volume** is the three-dimensional region inside the specimen within which the primary electron beam undergoes scattering events and generates detectable signals. It has a characteristic **pear/teardrop shape**.

**Diagram (2 Marks):** *(Refer to Section 4.3 of this note.)*

**Variation with Atomic Number Z (2 Marks):**
- **Low Z (e.g., polymers, Z ≈ 3–6):** Electrons scatter weakly and penetrate deeply → **large, deep interaction volume** → lower spatial resolution; signals come from a wider region.
- **High Z (e.g., gold, Z = 79):** Electrons scatter strongly and backscatter near the surface → **small, shallow interaction volume** → sharper image, strong BSE signal.

Mathematically (Kanaya–Okayama range):
$$R_{K-O} \propto \frac{1}{\rho \, Z^{0.89}}$$

So penetration depth **decreases with increasing Z**.

**Variation with Accelerating Voltage (1 Mark):**
$$R_{K-O} \propto E_0^{1.67}$$

Higher accelerating voltage → electrons penetrate deeper → larger interaction volume → more signal generated but lower surface resolution.

**Implications for Polymers (1 Mark):**
1. **Low Z + High Voltage = worst case** — large interaction volume, severe charging, beam damage.
2. **Recommended:** Low kV (1–5 kV) for surface-sensitive imaging of polymers; thin conductive coating (Au/Pt, 5–10 nm) to suppress charging; use **variable-pressure SEM (VPSEM) or environmental SEM (ESEM)** for non-coated insulating polymers.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. Students frequently write "interaction volume is spherical" — **WRONG**. It is pear-shaped (a "tear drop" or "balloon").
> 2. Many students forget that **signal escape depth ≠ interaction volume size**. SEs escape from <10 nm, but the *generation* volume is much larger — only a small fraction of generated SEs actually escape.
> 3. **Charging and beam damage** in polymers are tied directly to the interaction volume; KTU often allocates 1 mark for this connection.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Scanning Electron Microscopy (Module 3, PCPOT602)**

- **Definition:** SEM = focused electron beam raster-scanned over specimen → emitted signals mapped onto display to form magnified image.
- **Magnification Equation:** $M = L / l$ — purely geometric, not optical.
- **Resolution Equation:** $d = 0.61 \, \lambda / \alpha$ — smaller λ and smaller α → better resolution.
- **Electron Wavelength:** $\lambda \approx 1.22 / \sqrt{V}$ nm (non-relativistic shortcut).
- **Planck constant:** h = **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s** (always quote full value in derivations).
- **Electron mass:** mₑ = **9.109 × 10⁻³¹ kg**.
- **Speed of light:** c = **3 × 10⁸ m/s**.
- **Main Signals:** SE, BSE, X-ray, Auger — listed in order of increasing escape depth.
- **SE → topography; BSE → Z-contrast; X-ray → composition.**
- **Interaction Volume:** Pear-shaped; **larger for low Z and high kV.**
- **Polymer Specimen Prep:** Must be coated with conductive layer (Au/Pt, 5–10 nm) to prevent charging.
- **Recommended SEM Voltage for Polymers:** **1–5 kV** (low kV minimizes beam damage and interaction volume).
- **Resolution Hierarchy:** Optical (~200 nm) << SEM SE (~1 nm) << TEM (~0.1 nm).
- **Everhart–Thornley Detector:** Standard SE detector with +300 V biased collector grid.
- **Vacuum Requirement:** **10⁻³ to 10⁻⁶ Pa** in conventional high-vacuum SEM.
- **Kanaya–Okayama Range:** $R_{K-O} \approx 27.6 \, E_0^{1.67} / (\rho \, Z^{0.89})$ — used for penetration depth estimation.
- **Abbe Diffraction Limit:** $d \approx 0.61 \, \lambda / NA$ — fundamental reason electrons beat photons in resolution.
- **Applications in Polymer Testing:** Fracture surface analysis, fiber-matrix interface, blend morphology, coating defects, nanocomposite dispersion, filler distribution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Strategy Tip:** Always link the *physical principle* (electron wavelength, escape depth, Z-contrast) to the *polymer-testing application* (morphology, dispersion, adhesion). KTU examiners reward answers that show *application-level thinking*, not just textbook definitions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Atomic Force Microscopy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT602 - POLYMER TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Atomic Force Microscopy

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM)**, also known as Scanning Force Microscopy (SFM), is a high-resolution type of **scanning probe microscopy (SPM)** that images the surface topography of materials — including polymers, blends, composites, and biological specimens — at the nanometre and even sub-nanometre scale. It was invented in **1986** by **Gerd Binnig, Calvin Quate, and Christopher Gerber** at Stanford University.

The technique operates by measuring the interatomic force (attractive or repulsive) between a sharp probe (tip of radius ≈ 2–10 nm) mounted at the end of a flexible cantilever and the sample surface. As the tip raster-scans the surface, a feedback loop maintains a constant cantilever deflection (or constant amplitude in dynamic modes) by adjusting the vertical (z) position of the scanner, thereby producing a 3-D topographical map of the surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCPOT602 – Module 3):**
> AFM is listed under the unit "Advanced Surface and Morphological Characterization of Polymers." The examiner typically expects knowledge of *principle, instrumentation, modes of operation, force–distance curves, and polymer-specific applications*.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of AFM as a **"blind person reading Braille with a super-fine finger"** — but instead of a finger, the instrument uses an ultra-sharp tip (radius ≈ 10 atoms wide) attached to a microscopic diving board (the cantilever). As this "diving board" is dragged across the polymer surface, it bobs up and down over every tiny bump, valley, ridge, and grain. A laser beam aimed at the back of the cantilever reflects off a mirror-like surface and lands on a position-sensitive photodetector that "reads" these nanometre-sized deflections, converting them into a 3-D map of the polymer's surface — much like a record player stylus reading the grooves of a vinyl record, but 10 million times more sensitive.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world resolution benchmark:** AFM can routinely resolve features with a vertical (z) resolution of **0.1 nm (≈ atomic diameter)** and lateral (x–y) resolution of **1–2 nm**, even on non-conducting polymer samples (no coating required — unlike SEM).

### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics Used in AFM

| Parameter | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|
| Cantilever spring constant (k) | **0.01 – 100 N/m** |
| Tip radius of curvature | **2 – 30 nm** |
| Tip material | **Silicon (Si)** or **Silicon Nitride (Si₃N₄)** |
| Cantilever length | **100 – 250 µm** |
| Laser wavelength | **650 – 670 nm** (visible red) |
| Operating force range | **10⁻¹² N (pN) to 10⁻⁶ N (µN)** |
| Maximum scan area | **~150 µm × 150 µm** |
| Vertical resolution | **0.1 nm** |
| Lateral resolution | **1 – 2 nm** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why AFM matters in polymer testing:** Polymers are usually **non-conductive**, so SEM requires a sputter-coated gold/palladium layer that may mask the true morphology. AFM bypasses this limitation because it measures mechanical/atomic forces — **no conductive coating required**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The AFM operation can be broken down into **five sequential steps**:

1. **Tip-Sample Interaction Generation:** A sharp nanoscale tip (apex radius typically 2–10 nm) is brought into proximity (0.1–10 nm) of the polymer surface. Atomic-level van der Waals, electrostatic, capillary, and chemical forces arise between the atoms of the tip and the sample.

2. **Cantilever Deflection Sensing:** These interatomic forces cause the flexible cantilever (a microfabricated Si/Si₃N₄ beam, spring constant **k ≈ 0.01–100 N/m**) to bend. The deflection Δz is detected using the **optical lever method** — a laser beam reflects off the back of the cantilever onto a Position-Sensitive Detector (PSD) or a four-quadrant photodiode.

3. **Feedback Loop Activation:** The PSD converts the laser spot displacement into a differential voltage signal (Top–Bottom, Left–Right). This is fed into a feedback controller (typically a PI controller) that compares the actual deflection with a user-defined setpoint.

4. **Piezoelectric Scanner Adjustment:** The error signal drives a **piezoelectric tube scanner** (PZT — lead zirconate titanate), which adjusts the vertical (z) position of either the tip or the sample to keep the deflection (or amplitude) constant. The piezo voltage required to maintain this setpoint is recorded for every (x, y) pixel.

5. **Image Reconstruction:** The recorded z-piezo voltages are mapped to a colour/greyscale intensity scale, producing a **2-D topographical image** (and a 3-D rendering) of the polymer surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** This technique is used in polymer R&D laboratories, semiconductor inspection, biomedical implant surface analysis, and quality control of polymer films and membranes.

### 2.2 Three Primary Operating Modes

| Mode | Tip-Sample Distance | Force Regime | Key Feature |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Contact Mode** | 0.1–0.5 nm (repulsive) | 10⁻⁸ – 10⁻⁶ N | Constant deflection; fastest, but can damage soft polymers |
| **Tapping (Intermittent Contact) Mode** | 1–2 nm (oscillating) | 10⁻⁹ – 10⁻⁷ N | Tip "taps" surface at resonance; ideal for soft polymers (PS, PE, PP) |
| **Non-Contact Mode** | 5–15 nm (attractive) | < 10⁻¹¹ N | True non-contact; requires ultra-clean surfaces; detects van der Waals |

> [!NOTE]
> **Polymer-Specific Recommendation (KTU Examiner's Favourite):** For most polymer samples — particularly soft elastomers, semicrystalline polymers, and thin films — **Tapping Mode is preferred** because it minimises lateral (shear) forces that would otherwise scratch or drag soft polymer chains.

### 2.3 Force–Distance Curve: The Heart of AFM Physics

The force–distance (F–d) curve is obtained by ramping the tip vertically towards and away from the sample while recording the cantilever deflection. It reveals several characteristic regions:

- **Long range (> 10 nm):** Attractive van der Waals regime — cantilever bends toward the sample.
- **Snap-in:** At a critical separation, the gradient of attractive force exceeds the cantilever spring constant, causing the tip to "jump" into contact.
- **Repulsive region (< 0.5 nm):** Pauli exclusion / ionic repulsion dominates — the cantilever bends away from the sample.
- **Pull-off / Adhesion:** On retraction, adhesive forces (capillary, chemical bonding) cause hysteresis; the pull-off force **F_adh** gives quantitative adhesion data.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $F \;=\; -k \cdot \Delta z$ | **Hooke's Law** for cantilever deflection | F (N), k (N/m), Δz (m) |
| 2 | $U_{LJ}(r) \;=\; 4\varepsilon \left[ \left( \dfrac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{12} - \left( \dfrac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{6} \right]$ | **Lennard-Jones Potential** (attractive – repulsive) | U (J), r (m) |
| 3 | $F_{LJ}(r) \;=\; -\dfrac{dU}{dr} \;=\; 24\varepsilon \left[ 2\left( \dfrac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{13} - \left( \dfrac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{7} \right]$ | Force from L-J potential derivative | F (N) |
| 4 | $\Delta z \;=\; \dfrac{F}{k} \;=\; \tan(\theta) \cdot L$ | Cantilever tip deflection (small angle approx.) | Δz (m), L (m) |
| 5 | $PSD_{signal} \;\propto\; 2\theta \cdot L$ | Optical lever sensitivity (laser path) | Volts |
| 6 | $Q \;=\; \dfrac{f_{res}}{\Delta f}$ | Quality factor of cantilever (resonance) | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $f_{res} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m^*}}$ | Cantilever resonance frequency | Hz |
| 8 | $R_q \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(z_i - \bar{z})^{2}}$ | **RMS Roughness** of polymer surface | nm or µm |
| 9 | $R_a \;=\; \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert z_i - \bar{z} \vert$ | **Average Roughness** (use $\vert$ to avoid table issues) | nm or µm |
| 10 | $F_{adh} \;=\; k \cdot \Delta z_{pull-off}$ | Adhesion force from F–d curve | N |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Tip:** Equation (1) and (8) are the most frequently tested. Equation (8) uses $z_i$ as the height at pixel $i$, $\bar{z}$ as the mean height, and $N$ as the number of pixels.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **polymer industry**, AFM is routinely used for:
- Nanoscale roughness measurement of polymer films and membranes
- Morphological imaging of semicrystalline polymers (lamellar thickness of PE, PP, PVDF)
- Phase separation studies of polymer blends (PS/PMMA, PVC/ABS)
- Single-chain elasticity measurements of DNA and biomolecules
- Friction and wear analysis (LFM — Lateral Force Microscopy mode)
- Conductive-AFM (C-AFM) for mapping current distribution in conductive polymers (PEDOT:PSS, polyaniline)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Cantilever Deflection from Optical Lever Method

**Problem:** Derive the relationship between the laser spot displacement on the photodetector and the cantilever tip deflection.

**Given:** A cantilever of length $L$ deflects by a small angle $\theta$ at its free end when force $F$ is applied at the tip.

**Step 1 — Cantilever end deflection (small angle approximation):**

For small deflections, the vertical displacement of the free end of the cantilever is given by the geometric relation:

$$
\Delta z \;=\; L \cdot \tan(\theta) \;\approx\; L \cdot \theta \quad (\text{since } \theta \text{ is small, } \tan\theta \approx \theta)
$$

**Step 2 — Application of Hooke's Law at the cantilever tip:**

The applied force at the tip produces a deflection governed by the spring constant of the cantilever:

$$
F \;=\; k \cdot \Delta z
$$

Substituting Step 1 into Hooke's Law:

$$
F \;=\; k \cdot L \cdot \theta
$$

Solving for the tilt angle $\theta$:

$$
\theta \;=\; \dfrac{F}{k \cdot L}
$$

**Step 3 — Laser reflection geometry (optical lever):**

The laser beam strikes the back of the cantilever at the free end, and the reflected beam travels to a Position-Sensitive Detector (PSD) located at a distance $D$ from the cantilever. When the cantilever tilts by $\Delta\theta$, the reflected beam angle changes by $2\Delta\theta$ (law of reflection: angle of incidence = angle of reflection).

The corresponding spot displacement on the PSD is:

$$
\Delta x_{PSD} \;=\; 2\theta \cdot D
$$

**Step 4 — Substituting cantilever tilt from Step 2:**

$$
\Delta x_{PSD} \;=\; 2D \cdot \dfrac{F}{kL}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the force in terms of measurable signal:**

$$
F \;=\; \dfrac{kL \cdot \Delta x_{PSD}}{2D}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The interatomic force is *directly proportional* to the photodetector signal, with a proportionality constant determined by the cantilever stiffness, length, and the optical lever arm. This is why the optical lever method provides an *amplification* of the small cantilever motion into a large, easily measurable spot displacement on the PSD.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Force from Lennard-Jones Potential

**Problem:** Derive the interatomic force between tip and sample atoms from the L-J 6-12 potential.

**Step 1 — Statement of the L-J Potential:**

$$
U_{LJ}(r) \;=\; 4\varepsilon \left[ \left( \dfrac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{12} - \left( \dfrac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{6} \right]
$$

where $\varepsilon$ is the depth of the potential well (energy) and $\sigma$ is the distance at which the potential is zero.

**Step 2 — Force is the negative gradient of potential energy:**

By Newton's third law and the definition of conservative force:

$$
F(r) \;=\; -\dfrac{dU_{LJ}(r)}{dr}
$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate term by term:**

Let $u = r^{-1}$, so $\dfrac{du}{dr} = -r^{-2}$.

For the first term:
$$
\dfrac{d}{dr}\left( r^{-12} \right) \;=\; -12 \cdot r^{-13}
$$

For the second term:
$$
\dfrac{d}{dr}\left( r^{-6} \right) \;=\; -6 \cdot r^{-7}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the chain rule:**

$$
F(r) \;=\; -4\varepsilon \left[ -12 \, \sigma^{12} r^{-13} - \left(-6 \, \sigma^{6} r^{-7}\right) \right]
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify:**

$$
F(r) \;=\; 4\varepsilon \left[ 12 \, \sigma^{12} r^{-13} - 6 \, \sigma^{6} r^{-7} \right]
$$

**Step 6 — Factor the common terms:**

$$
F(r) \;=\; 24\varepsilon \left[ 2\left( \dfrac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{13} - \left( \dfrac{\sigma}{r} \right)^{7} \right]
$$

> **Interpretation:** The first term (proportional to $r^{-13}$) represents the **short-range Pauli repulsive force** that arises when electron clouds overlap. The second term (proportional to $r^{-7}$) represents the **long-range van der Waals attractive force**. The crossover occurs at the equilibrium spacing $r_{0} = 2^{1/6}\sigma \approx 1.122\,\sigma$.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: RMS Surface Roughness ($R_q$)

**Problem:** Derive the RMS roughness expression used in polymer surface analysis.

**Step 1 — Define the mean height:**

For a surface discretised into $N$ pixels, the arithmetic mean height is:

$$
\bar{z} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} z_i
$$

**Step 2 — Define the squared deviation at each pixel:**

$$
\Delta z_i^{2} \;=\; (z_i - \bar{z})^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Average the squared deviations:**

$$
\overline{\Delta z^{2}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (z_i - \bar{z})^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Take the square root to obtain the standard deviation of heights:**

$$
R_q \;=\; \sqrt{\overline{\Delta z^{2}}} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(z_i - \bar{z})^{2}}
$$

> **Engineering use:** $R_q$ (RMS roughness) is the standard parameter reported in ISO 25178 and is the most statistically meaningful single-number descriptor of a polymer film's surface texture.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

**Question:** A silicon AFM cantilever has a spring constant $k = 0.4\,\text{N/m}$ and length $L = 200\,\mu\text{m}$. During scanning on a polyethylene film, the PSD records a laser spot displacement of $\Delta x_{PSD} = 80\,\text{nm}$, and the photodetector is at $D = 4\,\text{cm}$ from the cantilever. Calculate (a) the cantilever tip deflection, (b) the applied force, and (c) the RMS roughness if the mean surface height is 12.0 nm and three measured points are 11.5 nm, 12.8 nm, 10.7 nm.

**Solution (a) — Cantilever tip deflection:**

Using the optical lever relationship $\Delta x_{PSD} = 2D \cdot \theta$ and $\theta = \Delta z / L$:

$$
\Delta z \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta x_{PSD} \cdot L}{2D} \;=\; \dfrac{(80 \times 10^{-9})(200 \times 10^{-6})}{2 \times 0.04}
$$

$$
\Delta z \;=\; \dfrac{1.6 \times 10^{-11}}{0.08} \;=\; 2.0 \times 10^{-10}\,\text{m} \;=\; 0.2\,\text{nm}
$$

**Solution (b) — Applied force:**

$$
F \;=\; k \cdot \Delta z \;=\; 0.4 \times 2.0 \times 10^{-10} \;=\; 8.0 \times 10^{-11}\,\text{N} \;=\; 80\,\text{pN}
$$

**Solution (c) — RMS roughness:**

Mean height: $\bar{z} = (11.5 + 12.8 + 10.7)/3 = 11.67\,\text{nm}$

Deviations: $-0.17$, $+1.13$, $-0.97$ nm

Squared: $0.0289$, $1.2769$, $0.9409$ → Sum = $2.2467$

$$
R_q \;=\; \sqrt{2.2467 / 3} \;=\; \sqrt{0.7489} \;=\; 0.865\,\text{nm}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an AFM System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sample Stage with XYZ Piezo Scanner] -->|Tip Sample Interaction| B[AFM Tip Mounted on Cantilever]
    B -->|Cantilever Deflection| C[Laser Diode Source 650 nm]
    C -->|Incident Beam| B
    B -->|Reflected Beam 2theta| D[Position Sensitive Detector PSD]
    D -->|Differential Voltage Signal| E[Feedback Controller PI Loop]
    E -->|Error Signal| F[High Voltage Amplifier]
    F -->|Drive Voltage| A
    E -->|Recorded Z voltage| G[Data Acquisition and Image Processor]
    G -->|Topography Map| H[Display Monitor 2D 3D Image]
    I[Vibration Isolation Platform] --- A
    J[Acoustic Enclosure] --- A
    K[User Setpoint Reference] --> E
```

> **Description of Flow:** The piezo scanner moves the sample beneath the tip. The cantilever bends in response to interatomic forces. A laser reflects off the back of the cantilever into the PSD. The PSD outputs a voltage difference proportional to deflection. The feedback controller compares this voltage with the user-set setpoint and drives the piezo to maintain a constant deflection. The piezo's z-voltage is sampled pixel-by-pixel and rendered as a topographical image.

---

### 4.2 Operating Mode Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Start Sample Type Selection] --> P2{Is the polymer soft or hard?}
    P2 -->|Soft Elastomers PS PE Films| P3[Tapping Mode Recommended]
    P2 -->|Hard Polymers PTFE PS Glass filled| P4[Contact Mode Acceptable]
    P2 -->|Atomically Flat Clean Surface| P5[Non Contact Mode Possible]
    P3 --> P6{Is phase contrast morphology required?}
    P6 -->|Yes| P7[Phase Imaging enabled in Tapping]
    P6 -->|No| P8[Standard Height Channel]
    P4 --> P9{High scan speed required?}
    P9 -->|Yes| P10[Contact Mode Faster]
    P9 -->|No| P11[Contact Mode with low force]
    P5 --> P12{UHV Clean Environment?}
    P12 -->|Yes| P13[Non Contact Mode Execution]
    P12 -->|No| P14[Use Tapping as Alternative]
```

---

### 4.3 Force–Distance Curve Schematic (Conceptual Block Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Long Range Z greater than 10 nm] -->|Attractive vdW| B[Snap to Contact Critical Gradient]
    B -->|Repulsive Pauli| C[Contact Region Steep Linear]
    C -->|Hysteresis On Retraction| D[Pull Off Adhesion Force]
    D -->|Capillary and Chemical Bonds| E[Free Region Beyond Adhesion]
    A2[Force F axis] --- A
    B2[Distance d axis] --- A
```

> **Key Points on the F–d Curve:**
> - **Region A → B:** Attractive van der Waals regime, cantilever bends toward the sample.
> - **Region B:** Snap-in point (cantilever spring constant is overcome by attractive force gradient).
> - **Region B → C:** Repulsive regime — Hookean contact region.
> - **Region C → D:** Maximum adhesion force on retraction; the difference between approach and retract curves yields the energy dissipation.

---

### 4.4 Cantilever Optical Lever Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Laser Diode] -->|Incident Beam| A2[Back of Cantilever tilted by theta]
    A2 -->|Reflected Beam 2theta| A3[Position Sensitive Detector]
    A2 --- A4[Tip at free end of cantilever length L]
    A4 -->|Deflection dz| A5[Sample Surface]
    A3 --- A6[Distance D from cantilever to PSD]
    A6 --- A7[Spot displacement dx equals 2 theta D]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Question]`
**Define Atomic Force Microscopy. List its three primary modes of operation and state the most suitable mode for imaging a soft polyethylene (PE) film.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM) is a high-resolution scanning probe technique that images the surface topography of a sample by measuring the interatomic forces between a sharp nanoscale tip mounted on a flexible cantilever and the sample surface, producing nanometre-resolution 3-D topographical maps without requiring a conductive coating.

The **three primary modes of operation** are:
1. **Contact Mode** — the tip is in continuous repulsive contact with the surface; deflection is held constant.
2. **Tapping (Intermittent Contact) Mode** — the cantilever oscillates near its resonance frequency and intermittently "taps" the surface; amplitude is held constant.
3. **Non-Contact Mode** — the tip oscillates at a small amplitude 5–15 nm above the surface in the attractive van der Waals regime.

For a soft polyethylene (PE) film, **Tapping Mode is most suitable** because it minimises lateral shear forces that would otherwise drag, scratch, or deform the soft polymer chains, while still providing high-resolution topographical and phase-contrast images.

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for listing three modes, 1 mark for selecting the correct mode for PE with justification.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]`
**What is a force–distance (F–d) curve in AFM? Mention any two information that can be obtained from it.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **force–distance curve** is a plot of the interatomic force (or cantilever deflection) experienced by the AFM tip as a function of the vertical separation between the tip and the sample, obtained by ramping the tip vertically towards and away from the surface.

**Two key information obtainable from an F–d curve:**
