1. **Cantilever spring constant / system calibration:** The slope of the linear repulsive contact region provides a direct measure of the cantilever's effective spring constant, enabling quantitative force measurements.

2. **Adhesion force between tip and polymer:** The pull-off (snap-back) force on retraction gives the work of adhesion, capillary force, or chemical bonding strength at the nanoscale — a critical parameter for polymer adhesion, coating, and biocompatibility studies.

*(1 mark for F–d curve definition, 1 mark each for two pieces of information.)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

**Q3A.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024 / Dec 2024, Model Question]`
**(a)** With a neat labelled block diagram, describe the **instrumentation and working principle** of Atomic Force Microscopy. Mention the function of each component. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the principle of the **optical lever method** for cantilever deflection detection. Derive the relationship between the photodetector signal and the tip-sample force. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution Q3A:

**(a) Instrumentation and Working Principle (7 marks):**

The major components of an AFM system are:

1. **Sharp Tip (Probe):** A microfabricated Si or Si₃N₄ tip of apex radius 2–10 nm, mounted on a flexible cantilever; interacts with the sample surface through interatomic forces.

2. **Cantilever:** A microfabricated beam (length 100–250 µm) with a low spring constant (0.01–100 N/m) that bends in response to the tip–sample force.

3. **Piezoelectric Scanner (PZT tube):** A lead zirconate titanate (PZT) ceramic that expands/contracts proportionally to applied voltage, providing sub-Ångström precision in x, y, and z motion. It raster-scans the sample and adjusts z to maintain a constant setpoint.

4. **Laser Diode and Mirror:** A visible (650–670 nm) laser focused on the back of the cantilever; the reflected beam's angular change encodes the cantilever's deflection.

5. **Position-Sensitive Detector (PSD) / Four-Quadrant Photodiode:** Detects the position of the reflected laser spot; outputs differential voltages (Top–Bottom, Left–Right) proportional to deflection.

6. **Feedback Controller (PI controller):** Compares the PSD signal with a user-set setpoint; generates an error signal that drives the piezo z-motion.

7. **Vibration Isolation Platform & Acoustic Enclosure:** A damped stage and acoustic hood that isolate the AFM from mechanical vibrations and acoustic noise (essential for sub-nm resolution).

**Working Principle:**
The tip is brought close to the sample (~ 0.1–10 nm). Interatomic forces cause the cantilever to bend. A laser reflected from the cantilever's back falls on the PSD, which generates a signal proportional to the deflection. This signal is compared to a setpoint in the feedback loop. The error signal drives the PZT scanner to adjust the z-position so that the deflection (or amplitude) remains constant. The z-piezo voltage at each (x, y) point is recorded and converted into a topographical image.

*(Block diagram 2 marks, list of components 3 marks, working principle 2 marks = 7 marks.)*

---

**(b) Optical Lever Method Derivation (7 marks):**

The optical lever amplifies the tiny cantilever deflection into a measurable spot displacement on a remote PSD.

**Setup:** A laser beam strikes the back of the cantilever at its free end, at a perpendicular incidence when the cantilever is undeflected. The reflected beam is detected by a PSD placed at a distance $D$ from the cantilever.

**Step 1:** When the cantilever tilts by a small angle $\theta$ (rad), the reflected beam angle changes by $2\theta$ (law of reflection).

**Step 2:** The corresponding spot displacement on the PSD is:

$$
\Delta x_{PSD} \;=\; 2\theta \cdot D
$$

**Step 3:** The cantilever's free-end vertical deflection is related to its tilt by:

$$
\Delta z \;=\; L \cdot \theta \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta \;=\; \dfrac{\Delta z}{L}
$$

**Step 4:** Substituting into Step 2:

$$
\Delta x_{PSD} \;=\; \dfrac{2D \cdot \Delta z}{L}
$$

**Step 5:** Using Hooke's Law $F = k \cdot \Delta z$ to express the tip–sample force:

$$
F \;=\; k \cdot \Delta z \;=\; \dfrac{kL \cdot \Delta x_{PSD}}{2D}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The PSD signal is *linearly proportional* to the tip–sample force. Because $D \gg L$, the optical lever amplifies a 0.1 nm cantilever deflection into a spot displacement on the order of 10–100 nm on the PSD — easily measurable with high signal-to-noise ratio.

*(Geometry setup 2 marks, equations 3 marks, force-signal relationship 2 marks = 7 marks.)*

---

**Q3B.** `[KTU University Exam – Model Question – Alternative Choice]`
**(a)** Explain the **three operating modes** of AFM (Contact, Tapping, Non-Contact) with their typical force regimes and **suitability for polymer analysis**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **force–distance curve** in detail. Sketch and explain the regions: long-range attraction, snap-to-contact, repulsive contact, and pull-off adhesion. Indicate how the **adhesion force between a polymer surface and the AFM tip is measured** from this curve. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution Q3B:

**(a) Three Operating Modes of AFM (7 marks):**

| Mode | Tip-Sample Distance | Force Regime | Working | Polymer Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Contact Mode** | 0.1–0.5 nm | 10⁻⁸ to 10⁻⁶ N (repulsive) | Tip remains in continuous contact; deflection held constant | Suitable only for **hard, rigid polymers** (e.g., glass-filled nylon, PTFE, cured epoxy) because high lateral forces can scratch soft polymers. |
| **Tapping Mode** | 1–2 nm (peak) | 10⁻⁹ to 10⁻⁷ N | Cantilever oscillates at resonance; amplitude held constant | **Most preferred mode for polymers** — soft elastomers, semicrystalline polymers, thin films; provides both height and phase images. |
| **Non-Contact Mode** | 5–15 nm | < 10⁻¹¹ N (attractive) | Oscillates just above surface; detects van der Waals | Used for **ultra-clean, atomically flat samples**; rarely used in routine polymer testing because contamination layers on polymers swamp the weak signal. |

> **Key Polymer Insight:** In **Tapping Mode**, the phase lag between the driving oscillation and the cantilever's response reveals the **local stiffness / viscoelasticity** variation across a polymer blend or a semicrystalline morphology — this is known as **Phase Imaging** and is unique to tapping-mode AFM.

*(3 modes × 2 marks each + 1 mark for polymer-specific recommendation = 7 marks.)*

---

**(b) Force–Distance Curve (7 marks):**

**Procedure:** The tip is ramped vertically (z-piezo extended and then retracted) while the cantilever deflection (force) is recorded.

**Regions of the F–d Curve:**

1. **Long-Range Attraction Region (Z > 10 nm):** As the tip approaches, weak van der Waals forces dominate; the cantilever bends *toward* the sample (negative deflection). This region follows a $1/r^{6}$ attractive behaviour consistent with the L-J potential.

2. **Snap-to-Contact (Critical Gradient Point):** As the tip approaches closer, the attractive force gradient $\partial F / \partial z$ eventually exceeds the cantilever spring constant $k$. At this point, the cantilever becomes mechanically unstable and the tip *jumps* into contact with the surface — visible as a sudden vertical step in the F–d curve. The snap-in distance provides information on the long-range attractive force.

3. **Repulsive Contact Region (Z < 0.5 nm):** After snap-in, the Pauli exclusion principle produces strong short-range repulsion. The cantilever now bends *upward*, and the F–d curve becomes linear with slope $k$ (Hookean contact). This region is used to determine the cantilever spring constant and local mechanical stiffness of the polymer.

4. **Pull-off / Adhesion Region (Retraction):** On retraction, the tip remains in contact beyond the original snap-in distance because of adhesive forces — capillary forces (from adsorbed water on hydrophilic polymer surfaces), chemical bonding, and van der Waals. The cantilever bends strongly downward until the elastic restoring force exceeds the maximum adhesion. At this point, the tip "pulls off" — the *pull-off force* gives the work of adhesion.

**Measurement of Adhesion Force:**

The adhesion force is calculated from the maximum downward cantilever deflection at pull-off:

$$
F_{adh} \;=\; k \cdot \Delta z_{pull-off}
$$

where $\Delta z_{pull-off}$ is the cantilever's downward deflection at the moment of release and $k$ is the cantilever's spring constant.

> **Polymer Application:** F–d curves on different regions of a polymer blend (e.g., PS vs PMMA) reveal differences in adhesion, stiffness, and surface energy at the nanoscale — crucial for understanding the mechanical integrity of polymer interfaces and adhesive joints.

*(Long-range 1.5 marks, snap-to-contact 1.5 marks, repulsive contact 1.5 marks, pull-off / adhesion calculation 2.5 marks = 7 marks.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Conflating SEM and AFM resolution:** Do *not* state that AFM achieves resolution "like SEM" — AFM's lateral resolution is lower (1–2 nm) than SEM (≈ 0.5 nm), but AFM *does not require a conductive coating* and provides 3-D height data.
> 2. **Confusing contact mode with non-contact mode:** Students frequently swap their force regimes. Memorise: **Contact = repulsive (nN range), Non-Contact = attractive (pN range), Tapping = oscillating (intermittent)**.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention the optical lever method** when asked about deflection detection — this is the *single most-missed* 2-mark item in KTU valuation.
> 4. **Sign error in Lennard-Jones derivation:** The repulsive term is the $r^{-13}$ term; the attractive is $r^{-7}$ — do not swap them.
> 5. **In Part B 14-mark answers, every sub-part MUST contain a diagram or tabulated form** — KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for visual presentation alone.
> 6. **Spelling "Piezoelectric"** correctly — the KTU board *does* penalise scientific terminology errors by 0.5 mark each.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ AFM was invented in **1986** by **Binnig, Quate, and Gerber** at Stanford.
- ✅ AFM uses a **sharp tip on a flexible cantilever** to measure interatomic forces (van der Waals, Pauli repulsion, capillary, chemical).
- ✅ Deflection is detected using the **optical lever method** — laser reflected off the cantilever's back onto a **Position-Sensitive Detector (PSD)**.
- ✅ **Three operating modes:** Contact (repulsive, hard polymers), Tapping (intermittent, soft polymers — preferred), Non-Contact (attractive, atomically clean surfaces).
- ✅ **Tapping mode** is the *most widely used mode for polymer analysis* because it minimises lateral force damage and provides both height and phase data.
- ✅ **Force–distance curve** gives: cantilever spring constant, adhesion force, surface energy, and local stiffness of polymer regions.
- ✅ **Key equations to memorise:**
  - Hooke's law: $F = k \cdot \Delta z$
  - Lennard-Jones force: $F = 24\varepsilon [2(\sigma/r)^{13} - (\sigma/r)^{7}]$
  - Optical lever: $\Delta x_{PSD} = 2\theta D$
  - RMS roughness: $R_q = \sqrt{(1/N)\sum (z_i - \bar{z})^{2}}$
  - Adhesion force: $F_{adh} = k \cdot \Delta z_{pull-off}$
- ✅ **No conductive coating required** — AFM can image non-conducting polymers directly (advantage over SEM).
- ✅ **Resolution:** vertical ≈ **0.1 nm**, lateral ≈ **1–2 nm**; maximum scan area ≈ **150 µm × 150 µm**.
- ✅ **Polymer applications:** surface roughness ($R_q, R_a$), crystalline lamellar thickness, blend phase separation, single-chain elasticity, adhesion measurements, conductive polymer mapping (C-AFM), and LFM for friction.
- ✅ **Critical components:** Sharp tip, cantilever, PZT scanner, laser, PSD, feedback controller, vibration isolation.
- ✅ **Phase Imaging (Tapping Mode)** is unique to AFM and provides nanoscale maps of stiffness / viscoelasticity in polymer blends and semicrystalline morphologies.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 Hot Topics:** optical lever derivation, F–d curve interpretation, comparison of three modes, Lennard-Jones potential derivation, polymer-specific applications.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrical Properties: Volume Resistivity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT602 - POLYMER TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electrical Properties: Volume Resistivity**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Volume Resistivity (ρᵥ)** is defined as the electrical resistance offered by a unit cube of the material when an electric current passes through the bulk of the specimen, measured between two opposite faces of the cube. It is an intrinsic property that quantifies how strongly a polymer material opposes the flow of electric current *through its body (volume)*.

Mathematically, it is the reciprocal of **volume conductivity (σᵥ)**.

$$\rho_v = \frac{1}{\sigma_v}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Volume resistivity is the *primary parameter* used to classify a polymer as an **insulator**, **semiconductor**, or **conductor**. Most commodity and engineering polymers fall in the insulator category with ρᵥ in the range of **10¹² to 10¹⁸ Ω·cm**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a polymer block as a **dense, dry sponge**:
- The sponge itself does not conduct water (electricity) well — it is a poor conductor.
- If you try to push water (current) from the top face to the bottom face, the water has to travel *through* the entire thickness of the sponge, encountering resistance at every internal pore.
- The thicker the sponge, the more resistance (this is why **length (L)** appears in the denominator).
- The wider the face you push through, the more pathways are available, so the resistance drops (this is why **area (A)** appears in the numerator).

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Takeaway:** Volume resistivity answers the question — *"If I take a 1 cm × 1 cm × 1 cm cube of this plastic and try to push electricity through it from one face to the opposite face, how much resistance will the current face inside the bulk of the material?"*

## 1.3 SI Units and Standard Metrics

- **SI Unit of ρᵥ:** Ohm-metre (**Ω·m**)
- **CGS / Practical Unit commonly used in polymer testing:** Ohm-centimetre (**Ω·cm**)
- **Conversion:** 1 Ω·m = 100 Ω·cm
- **Measurement Standards:** ASTM D257, IEC 60093, IS 3396
- **Typical values for polymers:** **10⁸ to 10¹⁸ Ω·cm**

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners frequently test the **order-of-magnitude** classification. Memorize: Insulators > 10⁹ Ω·cm; Semiconductors 10³–10⁹ Ω·cm; Conductors < 10³ Ω·cm.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — The Physics of Volume Resistivity

When a DC voltage is applied across the faces of a polymer specimen, the flow of current through the volume can be broken down into structured logic steps:

**Step 1 — Ohm's Law Foundation**
A voltage **V** applied across the specimen drives a steady current **I** through its bulk. By Ohm's Law:
$$R = \frac{V}{I}$$

**Step 2 — Resistance Depends on Geometry**
The resistance **R** of a homogeneous body is proportional to its length **L** (in the direction of current) and inversely proportional to its cross-sectional area **A**:
$$R = \rho_v \cdot \frac{L}{A}$$

**Step 3 — Defining Volume Resistivity**
Rearranging gives the fundamental working equation:
$$\rho_v = R \cdot \frac{A}{L}$$

**Step 4 — Practical Measurement Cell**
In the laboratory, a circular disc specimen of diameter **D** and thickness **t** is used. The active area becomes:
$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$
Hence:
$$\rho_v = R \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4t}$$

**Step 5 — Why "Volume" and not "Surface"?**
The current is forced to travel *through* the bulk via the main electrode (top) and the bottom electrode. Surface leakage is suppressed using a **guard electrode**, ensuring that only *volume* current is measured.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\rho_v = R \cdot \dfrac{A}{L}$ | Fundamental volume resistivity | Ω·m |
| 2 | $\rho_v = \dfrac{V}{I} \cdot \dfrac{A}{L}$ | Using Ohm's Law substitution | Ω·m |
| 3 | $\rho_v = R \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4t}$ | For circular disc specimen (D = diameter, t = thickness) | Ω·m |
| 4 | $\sigma_v = \dfrac{1}{\rho_v}$ | Volume conductivity | S/m |
| 5 | $G = \dfrac{1}{R} = \dfrac{I}{V}$ | Conductance of specimen | Siemens (S) |
| 6 | $J = \sigma_v \cdot E$ | Current density (E = electric field) | A/m² |
| 7 | $\rho_v = \rho_0 \cdot e^{\dfrac{E_a}{kT}}$ | Temperature dependence (Arrhenius-type) | Ω·m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary conditions to remember for KTU viva/papers:**
> 1. Temperature is held at **23 ± 1 °C** and relative humidity at **50 ± 2 %** as per ASTM D257.
> 2. Electrification time is standardized at **60 seconds** (1 minute).
> 3. Applied voltage is typically **100 V DC** for insulating polymers.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Volume resistivity is not a theoretical number — it directly dictates engineering applications:

- **Cable & Wire Insulation:** Polyethylene (PE), PVC, and XLPE are selected based on their ρᵥ to prevent current leakage in high-voltage cables.
- **Printed Circuit Board (PCB) Substrates:** FR-4 epoxy laminates must have ρᵥ > 10¹² Ω·cm to avoid leakage between tracks.
- **High-Voltage Insulators:** Silicone rubber and EPDM insulators for overhead transmission lines.
- **Capacitor Dielectrics:** Polypropylene, PET films — low conductivity (high ρᵥ) is essential to minimize dielectric losses.
- **Electrostatic Discharge (ESD) Protection:** Anti-static polymers are *engineered* to have reduced ρᵥ (10⁶–10⁹ Ω·cm) for safe charge dissipation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — From Ohm's Law to the Standard Test Equation

**Given:**
- A polymer disc of diameter **D** and uniform thickness **t**.
- A DC voltage **V** is applied across the two flat faces.
- The resulting current is **I**.
- The radius of the active electrode is **r = D/2**.

**To derive:** The expression for volume resistivity **ρᵥ**.

---

### Step 1 — Apply Ohm's Law

By definition of resistance from Ohm's Law:
$$R = \frac{V}{I}$$

*Explanation:* The ratio of the applied potential difference **V** to the resulting current **I** through the bulk gives the resistance **R** in ohms.

---

### Step 2 — Resistance of a Homogeneous Block

For a homogeneous, isotropic body of length **L** and uniform cross-section **A**:
$$R = \rho_v \cdot \frac{L}{A}$$

*Explanation:* The resistance is directly proportional to the path length (longer path → more collisions with molecules → more resistance) and inversely proportional to the area (wider path → more parallel pathways → less resistance). The proportionality constant is the intrinsic volume resistivity **ρᵥ**.

---

### Step 3 — Equating the Two Expressions

From Steps 1 and 2:
$$\frac{V}{I} = \rho_v \cdot \frac{L}{A}$$

Solving for **ρᵥ**:
$$\rho_v = \frac{V}{I} \cdot \frac{A}{L}$$

*Explanation:* This is the most general form. The factor A/L is the *cell constant* of the test geometry.

---

### Step 4 — Geometric Substitution for a Circular Disc

The cross-sectional area of a circular electrode:
$$A = \pi r^2 = \pi \left(\frac{D}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

The length in the current direction is the thickness of the specimen **t = L**.

Substituting:
$$\rho_v = \frac{V}{I} \cdot \frac{\pi D^2 / 4}{t}$$

Simplifying:
$$\rho_v = \frac{\pi D^2 V}{4 \, t \, I}$$

*Explanation:* This is the working equation used in laboratory calculations. Note the appearance of the geometric factor πD²/4, which is constant for a given specimen and electrode.

---

### Step 5 — Explicit Numerical Demonstration

**Worked Example:**

A circular polymer disc of diameter **D = 10 cm** and thickness **t = 0.3 cm** is tested. A voltage of **V = 100 V** DC produces a current of **I = 5 × 10⁻¹² A** (5 pico-amperes) through the volume.

**Solution:**

Area of electrode:
$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.10)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

Resistance:
$$R = \frac{V}{I} = \frac{100}{5 \times 10^{-12}} = 2.0 \times 10^{13} \text{ Ω}$$

Volume resistivity:
$$\rho_v = R \cdot \frac{A}{t} = (2.0 \times 10^{13}) \cdot \frac{7.854 \times 10^{-3}}{3.0 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\rho_v = (2.0 \times 10^{13}) \cdot (2.618) = 5.236 \times 10^{13} \text{ Ω·m}$$

Converting to Ω·cm:
$$\rho_v = 5.236 \times 10^{13} \times 100 = 5.236 \times 10^{15} \text{ Ω·cm}$$

*Conclusion:* This is a **high-quality insulator** (typical of cross-linked polyethylene, PTFE, or epoxy).

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Factors Affecting Volume Resistivity

| Factor | Effect on ρᵥ | Physical Reason |
|--------|--------------|-----------------|
| ↑ Temperature | ↓ ρᵥ (decreases) | Thermal energy excites charge carriers, increasing ionic/electronic conduction |
| ↑ Humidity | ↓ ρᵥ | Absorbed water introduces mobile ions and plasticizes the polymer |
| ↑ Voltage | ↓ ρᵥ (slightly) | Non-ohmic behaviour, field-assisted ionization |
| ↑ Time of electrification | ↑ ρᵥ reading | Polarization current decays; only true conduction current remains |
| ↑ Filler (conductive) loading | ↓ ρᵥ sharply | Percolation threshold — above critical loading, conductivity jumps |
| ↑ Crystallinity | ↑ ρᵥ | Ordered regions restrict ion mobility |
| ↑ Cross-link density | ↑ ρᵥ | Restricts segmental motion, reduces ion transport |
| ↑ Hydrophobicity of polymer | ↑ ρᵥ | Water absorption minimized |
| ↑ Plasticizer content | ↓ ρᵥ | Mobile plasticizer molecules carry ionic impurities |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** A frequent conceptual question asks *"Why does volume resistivity decrease with temperature?"* — Answer: According to the Arrhenius-type relation $\rho_v = \rho_0 \cdot e^{E_a / kT}$, increasing **T** reduces the exponent denominator, lowering ρᵥ.

---

## 3.3 Conduction Mechanisms in Polymers — A Simplified Picture

Polymer conduction is governed by three dominant mechanisms:

1. **Ionic Conduction:** Mobile ions (from catalyst residues, impurities, absorbed moisture) drift under the applied field.
2. **Electronic Conduction:** Electrons hop between localized states — this is dominant in conjugated polymers (e.g., polyaniline, polypyrrole).
3. **Hopping / Tunneling Conduction:** Charge carriers tunnel through insulating barriers between conductive domains — dominant near the percolation threshold in composites.

The total current density is:
$$J_{total} = J_{ionic} + J_{electronic} + J_{hopping}$$

For a *good insulator* like PTFE, all three terms are extremely small, giving ρᵥ > 10¹⁸ Ω·cm.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Volume Resistivity Test Setup

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["DC VOLTAGE SOURCE 100V"] --> nodeB["HIGH RESISTANCE METER ELECTROMETER"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["MAIN ELECTRODE TOP FACE"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["POLYMER SPECIMEN DISC OF THICKNESS t AND DIAMETER D"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["BOTTOM ELECTRODE"]
    nodeD -.-> nodeF["GUARD ELECTRODE RING SURROUNDS MAIN ELECTRODE"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["GUARD TERMINAL GROUNDED"]
    nodeE --> nodeH["CURRENT MEASUREMENT I PICOAMMETER"]
    nodeH --> nodeI["CALCULATION MODULE RHOv = V x A / I x t"]
    nodeI --> nodeJ["OUTPUT VOLUME RESISTIVITY IN OHM METRE"]
    nodeK["ENVIRONMENTAL CHAMBER 23 DEG C AND 50 PERCENT RH"] -.-> nodeD
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The specimen is sandwiched between the *main electrode* (top) and *bottom electrode*. A *guard electrode* encircles the main electrode and is grounded — it intercepts any surface leakage current, ensuring that the ammeter reads **only the volume current**.

---

## 4.2 Specimen Geometry and Current Path

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["MAIN ELECTRODE TOP"] -->|"Current I enters"| nodeB["POLYMER DISC Thickness t"]
    nodeB -->|"Current I exits"| nodeC["BOTTOM ELECTRODE"]
    nodeD["GUARD RING"] -.->|"Intercepts surface leakage"| nodeB
    nodeE["Diameter D"] -.-> nodeB
    nodeF["Area A equals pi D squared divided by 4"] -.-> nodeB
```

---

## 4.3 Volume Resistivity Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["MEASURED RHOv OHM cm"] --> nodeB{"VALUE RANGE"}
    nodeB -->|"Less than 10 cubed"| nodeC["CONDUCTOR METAL GRAPHENE"]
    nodeB -->|"10 cubed to 10 to the 9"| nodeD["SEMICONDUCTOR ANTISTATIC POLYMER"]
    nodeB -->|"10 to the 9 to 10 to the 18"| nodeE["INSULATOR PVC PE EPOXY PTFE"]
    nodeB -->|"Greater than 10 to the 18"| nodeF["EXCELLENT INSULATOR HIGH PURITY CERAMICS PTFE"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. Define volume resistivity of a polymer. State its SI unit and the standard test method used.**
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Volume resistivity (ρᵥ) of a polymer is defined as the electrical resistance offered by a unit cube (1 m × 1 m × 1 m) of the material when current flows through its bulk between two opposite faces. It is a measure of how effectively a polymer resists the flow of electric current through its volume.

**SI Unit (0.5 mark):** The SI unit is **Ohm-metre (Ω·m)**. In practice, Ohm-centimetre (Ω·cm) is also widely used.

**Standard Test Method (1 mark):** As per **ASTM D257** (equivalent to **IEC 60093**), using a two-electrode or guarded-electrode setup with a DC voltage source and a sensitive electrometer/picoammeter.

---

### **Q2. List any four factors that affect the volume resistivity of polymers.**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks — 0.75 each):**

1. **Temperature:** Increase in temperature decreases ρᵥ due to enhanced ionic mobility.
2. **Humidity:** Moisture absorption reduces ρᵥ by introducing mobile ions.
3. **Voltage:** Higher applied fields cause field-assisted ionization, lowering ρᵥ.
4. **Time of electrification:** Longer electrification time gives higher (more accurate) ρᵥ as polarization currents decay.

*(Acceptable alternatives: crystallinity, cross-link density, plasticizer content, filler loading.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for volume resistivity of a polymer specimen in terms of applied voltage, current, and specimen geometry. (7 marks)

**(b)** A polymer disc of diameter 50 mm and thickness 2 mm gives a current of 2 × 10⁻¹¹ A when 200 V DC is applied across its faces. Calculate the volume resistivity in Ω·m and Ω·cm. Comment on the insulating nature of the material. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Apply Ohm's Law to relate voltage and current:
$$R = \frac{V}{I}$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks):** For a homogeneous block of length L and area A:
$$R = \rho_v \cdot \frac{L}{A}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Equate and solve for ρᵥ:
$$\rho_v = \frac{V}{I} \cdot \frac{A}{L}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Substitute geometric expressions for a circular disc specimen (diameter D, thickness t):
$$A = \frac{\pi D^2}{4}, \quad L = t$$

Therefore:
$$\rho_v = \frac{\pi D^2 V}{4 \, t \, I}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Conclude with the physical meaning: ρᵥ is the resistance of a unit cube and is independent of specimen dimensions, characterizing the material intrinsically.

---

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- D = 50 mm = 0.050 m
- t = 2 mm = 0.002 m
- V = 200 V
- I = 2 × 10⁻¹¹ A

**Step 1 — Calculate area (1.5 marks):**
$$A = \frac{\pi (0.050)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3}}{4} = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Calculate resistance (1 mark):**
$$R = \frac{V}{I} = \frac{200}{2 \times 10^{-11}} = 1.0 \times 10^{13} \text{ Ω}$$

**Step 3 — Apply volume resistivity formula (2 marks):**
$$\rho_v = \frac{\pi D^2 V}{4 \, t \, I} = \frac{\pi \times (0.050)^2 \times 200}{4 \times 0.002 \times 2 \times 10^{-11}}$$

$$\rho_v = \frac{\pi \times 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 200}{1.6 \times 10^{-13}}$$

$$\rho_v = \frac{1.5708}{1.6 \times 10^{-13}} = 9.817 \times 10^{12} \text{ Ω·m}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to Ω·cm (1 mark):**
$$\rho_v = 9.817 \times 10^{12} \times 100 = 9.817 \times 10^{14} \text{ Ω·cm}$$

**Step 5 — Comment on insulation (1.5 marks):**
The value **9.817 × 10¹⁴ Ω·cm** lies in the insulator range (10⁹–10¹⁸ Ω·cm). The material is therefore a **good electrical insulator**, suitable for applications such as cable insulation, switchgear housings, and PCB substrates.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the principle of measurement of volume resistivity of polymers using a guarded electrode. Draw a neat labelled schematic. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the effect of temperature, humidity, and conductive filler loading on the volume resistivity of polymers. (7 marks)

---

#### Model Solution

**(a) Principle and Schematic (7 marks):**

**Principle (4 marks):**
A DC voltage is applied between the **main electrode (top)** and the **bottom electrode** of a polymer disc. The current flowing through the bulk of the specimen is measured using a highly sensitive electrometer. The **guard electrode** (a concentric ring around the main electrode) is grounded and intercepts any surface leakage current — only the *true volume current* passes through the measuring circuit.

**Cell constant (K):** K = A / t (for disc geometry, K = πD² / 4t)

$$\rho_v = R \cdot K = \frac{V}{I} \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4t}$$

**Schematic (3 marks):** Refer to the diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes — the student should draw:
1. Specimen disc with thickness t
2. Main electrode on top
3. Bottom electrode
4. Guard ring around the main electrode, grounded
5. DC source (V) and ammeter (I) connected in series
6. Label all parts clearly

---

**(b) Effects of Three Parameters (7 marks — ~2.3 marks each):**

**1. Temperature Effect (2.3 marks):**
Volume resistivity of polymers decreases with increasing temperature. This is because thermal energy provides activation to charge carriers (ions, electrons), increasing their mobility. The relationship follows an Arrhenius-type equation:
$$\rho_v = \rho_0 \cdot e^{E_a / kT}$$
where Eₐ is the activation energy, k is Boltzmann's constant, and T is absolute temperature. As T rises, the exponential term decreases, lowering ρᵥ.

**2. Humidity Effect (2.3 marks):**
Absorbed moisture from the environment diffuses into the polymer matrix, especially in hydrophilic polymers (e.g., nylon, epoxy, cellulose). Water molecules introduce mobile ionic species (H⁺, OH⁻) and plasticize the polymer, both of which enhance ionic conduction, lowering ρᵥ. Hydrophobic polymers like PTFE and PE are largely unaffected.

**3. Conductive Filler Loading Effect (2.4 marks):**
Addition of conductive fillers (carbon black, graphite, metal powders, CNTs) progressively reduces ρᵥ. Initially, filler particles are isolated, and the polymer remains insulating. At a critical concentration called the **percolation threshold (φc)**, the fillers form a continuous conductive network, causing ρᵥ to drop sharply by 6–10 orders of magnitude. Above φc, the composite becomes semi-conductive or conductive.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students LOSE marks in Volume Resistivity questions:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Mixing up **m** and **cm** in the geometric factor — 1 Ω·m = 100 Ω·cm. Always carry units in every step.
> 2. **Forgetting the guard electrode concept:** Examiners specifically test understanding of *why* the guard is used. If you omit its role, expect 1–2 marks deducted.
> 3. **Confusing surface resistivity (ρₛ) with volume resistivity (ρᵥ):** Surface is measured *across* the surface in Ω; Volume is measured *through* the bulk in Ω·m.
> 4. **Forgetting standard test conditions:** 23 °C, 50 % RH, 60 s electrification, 100 V DC. KTU expects you to quote these in any procedural question.
> 5. **Misapplying the Arrhenius equation direction:** Higher T → lower ρᵥ, *not* higher. Many students write the trend backwards.
> 6. **Percolation threshold terminology:** Saying "resistivity decreases linearly with filler" — wrong. It is **sigmoidal**, with a sharp drop at φc.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Volume Resistivity**

- ✅ **Definition:** Resistance of a unit cube of polymer measured through its bulk between opposite faces.
- ✅ **Symbol:** ρᵥ (rho subscript v)
- ✅ **SI Unit:** Ω·m; **Practical Unit:** Ω·cm (1 Ω·m = 100 Ω·cm)
- ✅ **Master Formula:** $\rho_v = \dfrac{V \cdot A}{I \cdot L} = \dfrac{\pi D^2 V}{4 t I}$ (for disc)
- ✅ **Standards:** ASTM D257 / IEC 60093
- ✅ **Test Conditions:** 23 °C, 50 % RH, 100 V DC, 60 s electrification
- ✅ **Typical Polymer Range:** 10⁹ to 10¹⁸ Ω·cm (insulators)
- ✅ **Temperature Effect:** ρᵥ ↓ as T ↑ (Arrhenius)
- ✅ **Humidity Effect:** ρᵥ ↓ as humidity ↑ (water = mobile ions)
- ✅ **Filler Effect:** Sigmoidal drop at percolation threshold φc
- ✅ **Guard Electrode Purpose:** Eliminates surface leakage → measures true volume current
- ✅ **Three Conduction Mechanisms:** Ionic + Electronic + Hopping
- ✅ **Engineering Use:** Cable insulation, PCB substrates, capacitor dielectrics, HV insulators
- ✅ **Reciprocal relation:** $\sigma_v = 1/\rho_v$ (conductivity)
- ✅ **Classification rule:** Insulator > 10⁹; Semiconductor 10³–10⁹; Conductor < 10³ (Ω·cm)

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Surface Resistivity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT602 - POLYMER TESTING > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Surface Resistivity ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) **Surface Resistivity (ρ_s)** is defined as the ratio of the direct current voltage drop per unit length along the surface of a material to the current per unit width passing through the surface. It is an intrinsic electrical property of an insulating material that quantifies the resistance offered by the material's surface to the lateral flow of leakage current. Mathematically, it is the electrical resistance measured between two opposite edges of a square formed on the surface of the material, with the current flowing along the surface (not through the bulk). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Terminology — KTU Board Definition** > - **Surface Resistivity (ρ_s):** Measured in **ohms (Ω)** or commonly expressed as **ohms per square (Ω/□)**. It is *dimensionally independent* of the size of the square — a remarkable property of sheet materials. > - **Volume Resistivity (ρ_v):** The corresponding bulk property measured through the thickness (Ω·cm). > - **Surface Conductivity (σ_s):** The reciprocal, measured in **siemens (S)**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **flat, wide sidewalk made of polished marble** during a light drizzle. Water (representing electric current) does not soak *into* the marble — instead, it flows as a thin film *along the top surface*. If the marble is clean and dry, very little current flows. But if the surface is dirty, slightly wet, or coated with a salt residue, water flows easily. **Surface resistivity works exactly like this:** - The **polymer surface** = the marble sidewalk - **Leakage current** = the thin film of water - **Surface resistivity** = the "slipperiness" or "stickiness" of the surface to electrical charge - A **high ρ_s** = the surface "repels" current flow (good insulator) - A **low ρ_s** = the surface "conducts" current easily (could be antistatic or contaminated) > [!NOTE] > **Why should a polymer engineer care?** > In real applications — cable insulation, computer chip housings, antistatic flooring, medical device casings — current often leaks *across* the surface from one terminal to another. Surface resistivity directly tells you whether your polymer will safely resist this leakage or fail catastrophically. ### 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Industry Benchmarks for Polymers (Typical Orders of Magnitude)** > - **Excellent Insulators** (PTFE, LDPE, Polystyrene): **ρ_s > 10¹⁶ Ω** > - **Good Insulators** (PVC, Nylon, Polyester): **ρ_s ≈ 10¹² – 10¹⁵ Ω** > - **Antistatic / Dissipative Polymers** (Carbon-filled PE, ESD plastics): **ρ_s ≈ 10⁶ – 10⁹ Ω** > - **Conductive Polymers** (Metallized, heavily filled): **ρ_s < 10⁵ Ω** ### 1.4 Governing Standard The international standard governing this test is **ASTM D257** — *"Standard Test Methods for DC Resistance or Conductance of Insulating Materials."* This defines the electrode geometry, applied voltage (typically **500 V DC**), electrification time (1 minute standard), and environmental conditioning (23 °C, 50% RH). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Surface Resistivity is "Per Square" A defining peculiarity of surface resistivity is that its numerical value is **independent of the size of the square** you measure, *as long as the material is uniform*. This is because: - If you **double the side length** of the square → the resistance path **doubles** (proportional to length). - If you **double the width** of the square (using a parallel path) → the resistance **halves** (proportional to width). - These two effects **cancel exactly**, leaving a constant value — hence "per square." This is the same principle that allows engineers to specify the sheet resistance of thin films in microelectronics in **Ω/□** without worrying about the chip's geometric area. ### 2.2 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic 1. **Sample Preparation:** A flat, uniform polymer specimen (typically a disc or square plaque, 50–100 mm wide, 1–3 mm thick) is conditioned at standard laboratory atmosphere. 2. **Electrode Application:** Two (or more) electrodes are placed in contact with the surface — either by conductive silver paint, vacuum-deposited metal, conductive rubber, or mercury contacts. 3. **DC Voltage Application:** A stable DC voltage (commonly **100 V, 500 V, or 1000 V**) is applied across the electrodes. 4. **Current Measurement:** After the standard electrification time (1 min per ASTM D257), the steady-state leakage current is measured using a high-sensitivity electrometer (capable of measuring 10⁻¹⁴ A or lower). 5. **Resistance Calculation:** Using Ohm's law, R_s = V / I. 6. **Geometry Correction:** The measured resistance is multiplied by a geometry factor (g) to yield the **intrinsic surface resistivity**, independent of electrode dimensions. ### 2.3 Electrode Configurations | Configuration | Geometry | Geometry Factor (g) | Typical Use | |---------------|----------|--------------------|-------------| | **Two parallel strips** | Length L, gap D | L / D | Flat sheets, films | | **Concentric ring** (circular) | Outer ring (R₁), inner disc (R₂) | 2π / ln(R₁/R₂) | Standard ASTM D257 | | **Guard ring** | Inner electrode, outer guard | Eliminates surface path | Combined with volume measurement | > [!NOTE] > **Guard Electrode Principle** > A guard electrode intercepts *unwanted* leakage currents (through the volume or along the back surface) and diverts them to ground. This ensures that **only the true surface leakage current** between the two measuring electrodes is captured — a critical KTU exam point. ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------| | 1 | $R_s = \dfrac{V}{I}$ | Ohm's Law applied to surface | Ω | | 2 | $\rho_s = R_s \cdot \dfrac{W}{D}$ | Surface resistivity (two-strip) | Ω | | 3 | $\rho_s = \dfrac{V}{I} \cdot \dfrac{2\pi}{\ln(R_1/R_2)}$ | Surface resistivity (concentric ring) | Ω | | 4 | $\rho_s = R_s \cdot g$ | General form with geometry factor $g$ | Ω | | 5 | $\sigma_s = \dfrac{1}{\rho_s}$ | Surface conductivity | S (siemens) | | 6 | $I_{leak} = \dfrac{V}{\rho_s} \cdot \dfrac{D}{W}$ | Leakage current per unit width | A | | 7 | $\tau = \varepsilon \cdot \rho_s$ | Dielectric relaxation time (where $\varepsilon$ is permittivity) | s | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Units Clarification for KTU Board Exam** > - **ρ_s in SI** = Ω (because the W/D term is dimensionless for a square geometry) > - **ρ_s in cgs/practical polymer literature** = Ω/□ (ohms per square) — *the □ symbol explicitly states the geometry is unit square* > - Do **not** confuse with **Volume Resistivity (ρ_v)**, which is reported in **Ω·cm** because it has a third dimension (thickness). ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **High-Voltage Cable Insulation:** Surface resistivity > 10¹³ Ω prevents surface flashover and tracking failure, especially in humid/polluted environments. - **Antistatic Packaging (IC Trays, HDD Cases):** Surface resistivity in the 10⁶–10⁹ Ω range safely dissipates static charge, preventing ESD damage to microelectronics. - **Medical Implants:** Biocompatible polymers (e.g., silicone) must have high surface resistivity to avoid leakage currents interfering with cardiac/neural stimulation devices. - **Aerospace Composites:** Carbon-fiber reinforced polymers need controlled surface resistivity to manage lightning strike damage. - **Photovoltaic Encapsulants:** EVA films in solar modules require stable ρ_s over decades of UV/thermal exposure. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: From Resistance to Surface Resistivity (Two-Strip Geometry) **Given:** - A polymer sheet of width $W$ and length $L$ (where $L \gg W$) - Two parallel strip electrodes of length $L$ separated by a gap $D$ - Applied DC voltage $V$, measured current $I$ **Step 1 — Express Resistance from Ohm's Law** By definition, the resistance measured between the two electrodes is: $$R_s = \dfrac{V}{I}$$ **Step 2 — Express Resistance in Terms of Geometry** For a uniform sheet, resistance is proportional to the length of the current path (the gap $D$) and inversely proportional to the width of the current path (the strip length $L$). Thus: $$R_s = \rho_s \cdot \dfrac{D}{L \cdot t_{surface}}$$ where $t_{surface}$ is the *effective thickness* of the surface conduction layer (a very thin layer, typically nanometers thick, where leakage current flows). **Step 3 — Define Surface Resistivity** Rearranging to isolate the material property: $$\rho_s = R_s \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} \cdot t_{surface}$$ **Step 4 — Note the "Per Square" Property** For a **unit square** (where $L = D$), the geometric factor becomes: $$\rho_s = R_s \cdot 1 \cdot t_{surface} = R_s \cdot t_{surface}$$ But for the surface measurement, the effective thickness is taken as unit thickness (a convention), so: $$\rho_s = R_s \quad \text{(when } L = D\text{)}$$ This proves why **ρ_s has the same numerical value as R_s for a square of any size** — a foundational concept for KTU board questions. **Step 5 — The General Working Formula** For non-square geometries, we apply the geometry factor: $$\rho_s = \dfrac{V}{I} \cdot \dfrac{L}{D}$$ This is the formula KTU examiners most often test. --- ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style) **Problem:** A polymer sheet is tested using two parallel strip electrodes, each of length **L = 50 mm**, separated by a gap **D = 10 mm**. A voltage of **500 V DC** is applied, and after 1 minute, a steady current of **I = 2.5 × 10⁻¹² A** is measured. Calculate the surface resistivity. **Solution — Step-by-Step:** **Step 1:** Calculate the measured resistance. $$R_s = \dfrac{V}{I} = \dfrac{500 \text{ V}}{2.5 \times 10^{-12} \text{ A}} = 2.0 \times 10^{14} \text{ Ω}$$ **Step 2:** Apply the surface resistivity formula. $$\rho_s = R_s \cdot \dfrac{L}{D} = 2.0 \times 10^{14} \text{ Ω} \cdot \dfrac{50 \text{ mm}}{10 \text{ mm}}$$ $$\rho_s = 2.0 \times 10^{14} \cdot 5 = 1.0 \times 10^{15} \text{ Ω}$$ **Step 3:** Express in standard units. $$\boxed{\rho_s = 1.0 \times 10^{15} \text{ Ω (or Ω/□)}}$$ **Step 4:** Classify the material. > [!NOTE] > Since ρ_s > 10¹⁴ Ω, this polymer qualifies as an **excellent insulator** — suitable for high-voltage cable insulation or transformer applications. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Factors Affecting Surface Resistivity | Factor | Effect on ρ_s | Engineering Implication | KTU Exam Tip | |--------|---------------|------------------------|--------------| | **↑ Humidity** | ↓ ρ_s (drops by 1–3 orders) | Water film forms conductive layer | Always condition at 50% RH | | **↑ Temperature** | ↓ ρ_s (Arrhenius-type) | Thermal activation of carriers | Report test temperature | | **Surface contamination** | ↓ ρ_s dramatically | Fingerprints, dust act as leakage paths | Clean with isopropanol before test | | **Electrode pressure** | ↑ Contact area, ↓ ρ_s (apparent) | Use standardized contact force | Use conductive paint or vacuum deposition | | **Polar groups in polymer** | ↓ ρ_s | Nylon, PVC are more conductive than PE | Discuss in terms of dipolar relaxation | | **Carbon black / metallic filler** | ↓ ρ_s by 6–10 orders | Used to make ESD-safe plastics | Percolation threshold concept | | **UV exposure / aging** | ↓ ρ_s | Surface oxidation creates polar groups | Important for outdoor insulation | | **Applied voltage (high field)** | ↓ ρ_s (non-Ohmic) | Carrier injection at high fields | Limit to ≤1000 V/mm | ### 3.4 Distinction: Surface vs. Volume Resistivity | Property | Surface Resistivity (ρ_s) | Volume Resistivity (ρ_v) | |----------|---------------------------|--------------------------| | **Current path** | Along the surface | Through the bulk thickness | | **Units** | Ω (or Ω/□) | Ω·cm | | **Electrode setup** | Two electrodes on same face | Two electrodes on opposite faces | | **Guard electrode** | Diverts bulk current | Diverts surface current | | **Application relevance** | Surface cleanliness, tracking | Bulk insulation integrity | | **Typical insulator value** | 10¹² – 10¹⁶ Ω | 10¹⁴ – 10¹⁸ Ω·cm | > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistake (KTU Valuation Note):** > Confusing the units — students often write ρ_s in Ω·cm or ρ_v in Ω. This is a **direct 1-mark penalty** in KTU answer scripts. Memorize: **surface = ohms; volume = ohms × length**. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Test Setup Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TB DCPS[DC Voltage Source
100 V to 1000 V] VM[Voltmeter
High Impedance] AM[Electrometer / Picoammeter
Sensitivity 10 to the power minus 14 A] E1[Electrode 1
High Voltage Side] E2[Electrode 2
Current Measurement Side] EG[Guard Electrode
Ground Potential] SP[Polymer Specimen
Flat Disc or Square] ENC[Faraday Cage / Shielded Chamber] COND[Environmental Chamber
23 degree C 50 percent RH] DCPS -->|applies V| E1 E1 -->|contact| SP SP -->|surface leakage| E2 E2 -->|measures I| AM VM -->|monitors V| E1 E2 -->|diverts bulk current| EG EG -->|shields| ENC ENC -->|conditioned by| COND SP -->|placed inside| ENC ``` ### 4.2 Concentric Ring Electrode Geometry ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CR[Concentric Ring Electrode Top View] IE[Inner Electrode
Disc radius r2] GE[Guard Ring Electrode
Inner radius r1 Outer radius r3] SP2[Polymer Surface
Current flows radially between IE and GE] end IE -->|current I measured| AM2[Ammeter] GE -->|grounded| GND[Ground] SP2 -->|annular gap ln r1 by r2| IE SP2 -->|annular gap ln r1 by r2| GE ``` ### 4.3 Surface Resistivity Measurement Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Step 1 Specimen Preparation] --> B[Step 2 Conditioning 24 hours at 23 C 50 percent RH] B --> C[Step 3 Apply Electrodes Silver Paint or Vacuum Deposit] C --> D[Step 4 Place in Shielded Test Chamber] D --> E[Step 5 Apply DC Voltage 500 V standard] E --> F[Step 6 Wait 60 seconds Electrification Time] F --> G[Step 7 Read Steady State Current I] G --> H[Step 8 Calculate R sub s equals V over I] H --> I[Step 9 Multiply by Geometry Factor g] I --> J[Step 10 Report rho sub s in Ohms per square] J --> K[Final Result Classification Insulator or Conductive] ``` ### 4.4 Conceptual Model — Current Pathways in a Polymer ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph POLY[Polymer Sample Cross Section] SURF[Surface Layer
Conducts leakage current I sub s] BULK[Bulk Volume
Conducts current I sub v] BOTT[Bottom Surface
Usually neglected] end TOP[Top Electrode] -->|I total| SURF SURF -->|I sub s| BOTM[Bottom Electrode] TOP -->|I sub v| BULK BULK -->|I sub v| BOTM GUARD[Guard Ring Diverts I sub v to ground leaving pure I sub s] -.->|intercepts| SURF ``` > [!NOTE] > The guard ring is the key engineering feature that makes the ASTM D257 method reliable. Without it, volume and surface currents mix, and the reading is meaningless. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define surface resistivity. State its SI unit and mention the standard test method used for its determination.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(1 mark)** **Surface resistivity (ρ_s)** is defined as the resistance to leakage current along the surface of an insulating material, measured between two opposite edges of a unit square formed on the surface. - **(1 mark)** It is expressed in **ohms (Ω)** or **ohms per square (Ω/□)**. - **(1 mark)** The governing standard is **ASTM D257** (DC Resistance or Conductance of Insulating Materials), using a concentric ring electrode with a guard electrode. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *Distinguish between surface resistivity and volume resistivity. Give the units of both.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **(1.5 marks)** **Surface resistivity (ρ_s)** measures the resistance to current flow *along the surface* of the material, between two electrodes on the same face. It depends on surface contamination, humidity, and finish. **Unit: Ω (or Ω/□).** - **(1.5 marks)** **Volume resistivity (ρ_v)** measures the resistance to current flow *through the bulk thickness*, between two electrodes on opposite faces. It depends on the polymer's intrinsic molecular structure. **Unit: Ω·cm.** --- ### 5.2 Part B Question (14 Marks with Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]** **(a)** *Explain in detail the principle, electrode configuration (with a neat sketch), and procedure for the determination of surface resistivity of a polymer using ASTM D257 standard. Discuss the role of the guard electrode.* **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **(2 marks) Principle:** Surface resistivity is determined by applying a known DC voltage between two electrodes placed on the same face of the polymer specimen and measuring the steady-state leakage current flowing *along the surface* between them. The ratio of voltage to current gives the surface resistance, which is then multiplied by an appropriate geometry factor to obtain the intrinsic surface resistivity of the material. **(2 marks) Electrode Configuration:** The ASTM D257 standard specifies a **concentric ring electrode system** consisting of: - An **inner solid disc electrode** (radius $r_2$) - An **outer annular ring electrode** (inner radius $r_1$, outer radius $r_3$) - A **guard electrode** surrounding the outer ring (or forming the outer ring itself) The current flows radially outward from the inner disc, through the annular surface gap, into the outer ring. The geometry factor is: $$g = \dfrac{2\pi}{\ln(r_1/r_2)}$$ **(2 marks) Procedure:** 1. Condition the specimen for 24 hours at 23 °C and 50% RH. 2. Apply conductive silver paint or vacuum-deposit metal electrodes on the cleaned surface. 3. Place the specimen in a shielded (Faraday cage) test chamber. 4. Connect the electrometer and apply **500 V DC** (or per the material's expected resistivity range). 5. Wait **60 seconds** for steady-state, then record the current. 6. Calculate $\rho_s = (V/I) \cdot g$. **(1 mark) Role of Guard Electrode:** The guard electrode **diverts the bulk (volume) leakage current to ground**, ensuring that the measured current is *purely* the surface leakage current between the inner and outer measuring electrodes. This eliminates cross-talk and makes the measurement accurate. --- **(b)** *A polymer sheet is tested using a concentric ring electrode with inner radius r₂ = 25 mm and outer ring inner radius r₁ = 50 mm. A voltage of 250 V DC produces a current of 5.0 × 10⁻¹³ A after 1 minute. Calculate the surface resistivity and classify the material.* **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Step 1 (2 marks):** Calculate the surface resistance. $$R_s = \dfrac{V}{I} = \dfrac{250}{5.0 \times 10^{-13}} = 5.0 \times 10^{14} \text{ Ω}$$ **Step 2 (2 marks):** Calculate the geometry factor. $$g = \dfrac{2\pi}{\ln(r_1/r_2)} = \dfrac{2\pi}{\ln(50/25)} = \dfrac{2\pi}{\ln(2)} = \dfrac{2 \times 3.1416}{0.6931} = \dfrac{6.2832}{0.6931} \approx 9.0647$$ **Step 3 (2 marks):** Calculate surface resistivity. $$\rho_s = R_s \cdot g = 5.0 \times 10^{14} \times 9.0647 \approx 4.53 \times 10^{15} \text{ Ω}$$ $$\boxed{\rho_s \approx 4.53 \times 10^{15} \text{ Ω (or Ω/□)}}$$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Classification. Since $\rho_s > 10^{15}$ Ω, this polymer is an **excellent electrical insulator**, suitable for **high-voltage insulation, transformer components, and cable sheathing** applications. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2** *(Internal Choice)* **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3]** **(a)** *Discuss the various factors that affect the surface resistivity of polymers. How does humidity influence the test, and how is this controlled in standard testing?* **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **(1 mark) Definition Recap:** Surface resistivity is highly sensitive to surface and environmental conditions. **(2 marks) Major Influencing Factors:** 1. **Humidity** — Adsorbed water forms a thin conductive film, dramatically lowering ρ_s. 2. **Temperature** — Increased thermal energy activates charge carriers; ρ_s typically drops by a factor of 2–10 per 20 °C rise. 3. **Surface contamination** — Dust, oils, fingerprints, and salts provide leakage paths. 4. **Polymer chemistry** — Polar groups (–OH, –CONH, –Cl) increase surface conductivity. 5. **Filler content** — Carbon black, graphite, or metal fillers can lower ρ_s by 6–10 orders of magnitude (used to make antistatic plastics). 6. **UV aging / oxidation** — Creates polar carbonyl groups on the surface, reducing ρ_s over time. 7. **Electrode geometry & contact pressure** — Affects contact resistance and effective area. **(2 marks) Humidity Control Mechanism:** ASTM D257 mandates **conditioning at 23 ± 1 °C and 50 ± 2% relative humidity for at least 24 hours** before testing. The test is conducted in an **environmental chamber** that maintains these conditions. This ensures reproducibility, since humidity can cause ρ_s to vary by 1–3 orders of magnitude. **(1 mark) Mitigation in Practice:** - Clean surfaces with isopropanol before electrode application. - Use of **guard electrodes** to remove stray leakage. - For critical applications, test at **multiple humidities** to plot ρ_s vs. RH curves. **(1 mark) Engineering Implication:** Outdoor polymer insulators (silicone rubber, EPDM) are designed with hydrophobic surfaces and additives (ATH fillers) to minimize humidity-induced ρ_s drop and prevent **surface tracking and flashover**. --- **(b)** *Compare surface and volume resistivity. A polymer has ρ_v = 10¹⁶ Ω·cm and ρ_s = 10¹⁴ Ω. If a 2 mm thick plaque of area 10 cm² is used as a dielectric in a capacitor, calculate the total DC resistance (a) through the bulk and (b) along the surface (assume electrode separation D = 2 cm along surface). Comment on which leakage path dominates.* **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **Step 1 (1 mark):** Recall fundamental formulas. - Volume resistance: $R_v = \rho_v \cdot \dfrac{t}{A}$ - Surface resistance: $R_s = \rho_s \cdot \dfrac{D}{L}$ (where $L$ = electrode length = $A/t$ for a square surface — here we take $L$ = 5 cm and $D$ = 2 cm for direct calculation) **Step 2 (2 marks):** Calculate volume (bulk) resistance. $$R_v = \rho_v \cdot \dfrac{t}{A} = 10^{16} \, \Omega \cdot \text{cm} \cdot \dfrac{0.2 \text{ cm}}{10 \text{ cm}^2} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{15}}{10} = 2.0 \times 10^{14} \, \Omega$$ **Step 3 (2 marks):** Calculate surface resistance (for electrodes L = 5 cm, D = 2 cm along the surface). $$R_s = \rho_s \cdot \dfrac{D}{L} = 10^{14} \, \Omega \cdot \dfrac{2 \text{ cm}}{5 \text{ cm}} = 4.0 \times 10^{13} \, \Omega$$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Compare the two paths. $$\dfrac{R_s}{R_v} = \dfrac{4.0 \times 10^{13}}{2.0 \times 10^{14}} = 0.20$$ So $R_s < R_v$ — the **surface path offers less resistance** and therefore carries more leakage current. **Step 5 (1 mark):** Conclude. > [!NOTE] > The **surface leakage path dominates** in this case. This is a common scenario in humid or contaminated environments, and is why polymer insulators are designed with **creepage distances** (longer surface paths) and **hydrophobic surfaces** to suppress surface conduction. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Unit Confusion (–1 to –2 marks):** Writing ρ_s in Ω·cm or ρ_v in Ω. *Memorize: surface = Ω; volume = Ω·cm.* > 2. **Forgetting the Geometry Factor (–2 marks):** Reporting $R_s = V/I$ as the final answer instead of multiplying by $L/D$ or $2\pi/\ln(r_1/r_2)$. > 3. **Skipping the Guard Electrode Explanation (–1 mark):** Always explain its role in isolating surface current from bulk current. > 4. **Ignoring Conditioning Conditions (–1 mark):** Failing to mention 23 °C / 50% RH conditioning per ASTM D257. > 5. **Mixing up SI and Practical Units:** Write **Ω (or Ω/□)** explicitly to avoid ambiguity. > 6. **No Sketch (–1 mark):** A neat concentric ring or parallel-strip diagram is *expected* in a 7-mark sub-question. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Surface Resistivity** - ✅ **Definition:** Resistance to current leakage *along the surface* of a polymer. - ✅ **Symbol:** ρ_s (rho sub s) - ✅ **Units:** Ω (ohms) or Ω/□ (ohms per square) - ✅ **Standard:** ASTM D257 (DC Resistance of Insulating Materials) - ✅ **Core Formula:** $\rho_s = \dfrac{V}{I} \cdot \dfrac{L}{D}$ (parallel strips) or $\rho_s = \dfrac{V}{I} \cdot \dfrac{2\pi}{\ln(r_1/r_2)}$ (concentric ring) - ✅ **Typical Voltage:** 500 V DC; **Time:** 60 s electrification - ✅ **Environment:** 23 °C, 50% RH - ✅ **Guard Electrode:** Diverts bulk current → ensures pure surface measurement - ✅ **Surface vs Volume:** Surface = same-face electrodes (Ω); Volume = opposite-face electrodes (Ω·cm) - ✅ **Insulator range:** ρ_s > 10¹² Ω; **Antistatic range:** 10⁶ – 10⁹ Ω; **Conductive range:** < 10⁵ Ω - ✅ **Key Influencing Factors:** Humidity, temperature, surface contamination, filler type, polymer polarity, UV aging - ✅ **"Per Square" Property:** ρ_s is numerically independent of square size — a unique property of sheet materials - ✅ **Applications:** Cable insulation, antistatic packaging, ESD-safe enclosures, biomedical devices, aerospace composites - ✅ **Common KTU Pitfall:** Do **NOT** confuse with volume resistivity units.

topics.title: Breakdown Voltage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT602 - POLYMER TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Breakdown Voltage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Breakdown Voltage (BDV)**, also known as **dielectric withstand voltage** or **puncture voltage**, is defined as the **minimum value of voltage** that causes a portion of an electrical insulating (dielectric) polymer material to lose its insulating properties and become electrically conductive. At this critical voltage threshold, the polymer transitions from an insulating state (high electrical resistance) to a conductive state (low resistance), resulting in a sudden, often catastrophic, surge of current through the material.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PCPOT602)**, the breakdown voltage is quantified through standardized test methods (predominantly **ASTM D149** and **IEC 60243**), which specify the procedure for applying an alternating or direct voltage across a polymer specimen of defined geometry until dielectric failure occurs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology for KTU Examinations**
> - **Dielectric Strength (E)**: The breakdown voltage expressed per unit thickness, typically in **kV/mm** or **MV/m**.
> - **Dielectric Breakdown**: The complete failure of an insulating material under electrical stress.
> - **Puncture Voltage**: Synonym for breakdown voltage, specifically used in solid polymer testing.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Simple Analogy — The Dam Wall**
> Imagine a concrete dam holding back a massive reservoir of water. The dam is your **polymer insulator**, and the water pressure is the **applied electrical voltage**. As long as the water pressure stays below the dam's design limit, the dam holds firm (the polymer remains insulating). However, the moment the water pressure exceeds that critical limit, the dam cracks and water gushes through uncontrollably. The pressure value at which the dam cracks is the **breakdown voltage**. Similarly, beyond the BDV, electrons "punch through" the polymer, causing a sudden and irreversible electrical failure.

In simpler terms:
- The polymer is like a **wall of soldiers** standing in formation, blocking the enemy's (electrons') advance.
- As voltage increases, more enemy soldiers are pushed forward.
- At **breakdown voltage**, the wall collapses — every soldier is overwhelmed at once.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standardized Metrics in BDV Testing**
> - **Standard Test Frequency**: **50 Hz or 60 Hz** (commercial power frequency)
> - **Standard Units of Dielectric Strength**: **kV/mm**, **V/mil**, or **MV/m**
> - **Standard Rate of Voltage Rise**: **500 V/s** to **2000 V/s** (short-time method) or step-by-step (slow-rate method)
> - **Reference Standard Documents**: **ASTM D149**, **IEC 60243-1**, **IS 2584**

**Typical Dielectric Strength Values for Common Polymers (Industry Reference):**

| Polymer | Approximate Dielectric Strength (kV/mm) |
|---|---:|
| Polyethylene (PE) | 18 – 28 |
| Polypropylene (PP) | 20 – 30 |
| Polystyrene (PS) | 19 – 27 |
| PVC (rigid) | 14 – 20 |
| PTFE (Teflon) | 60 – 100 |
| Epoxy Resin | 16 – 22 |
| Polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) | 18 – 22 |
| Nylon 6,6 | 12 – 16 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanism of Dielectric Breakdown in Polymers

The breakdown phenomenon in polymeric dielectrics is not a single event but a **cascading physical-chemical process**. Understanding it requires examining the four primary mechanisms:

#### A. Intrinsic (Electronic) Breakdown
- **Cause**: Direct field-induced ionization of molecules or electrons.
- **Time Scale**: Extremely fast — occurs in **microseconds to nanoseconds**.
- **Conditions**: Pure, defect-free polymer at low temperatures; high electric field (~$10^8$ V/m).
- **Mechanism**: Electrons in the valence band gain sufficient kinetic energy from the field to collide with lattice atoms, knocking out more electrons in an **electron avalanche** (Townsend-type mechanism).
- **KTU Significance**: Represents the theoretical upper limit of dielectric strength.

#### B. Thermal Breakdown
- **Cause**: Internal heat generation exceeds heat dissipation.
- **Time Scale**: Seconds to minutes.
- **Mechanism**: Dielectric losses ($I^2R$ and dipole relaxation losses) heat the polymer. As temperature rises, conductivity increases exponentially (Arrhenius-type), causing runaway heating until the polymer melts, chars, or vaporizes.
- **Governing Relation**: Heat generated $Q_g = V^2 \omega C \tan\delta$, Heat dissipated $Q_d = k \cdot \Delta T$ (where $k$ is thermal conductivity, $\Delta T$ is temperature rise).
- **Breakdown Condition**: $Q_g \geq Q_d$.

#### C. Electrochemical (Tracking/Erosion) Breakdown
- **Cause**: Long-term exposure to moisture, contaminants, and electrical stress.
- **Mechanism**: Surface tracking — formation of **carbonaceous conducting paths** (dry band arcing) on the polymer surface due to partial discharges, combined with chemical degradation.
- **Common Test**: **Inclined Plane Tracking (IPT) Test** per ASTM D2303.

#### D. Discharge (Partial Discharge) Breakdown
- **Cause**: Presence of **voids, bubbles, or delaminations** inside the polymer.
- **Mechanism**: Gas in voids ionizes at lower voltages than the solid polymer, creating **partial discharges (corona)**. These discharges erode the polymer, gradually enlarging the void until complete failure.
- **Detection**: ASTM D1868 (Destructive) and IEC 60270 (Non-destructive partial discharge measurement).

### 2.2 Key Influencing Factors (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Factors that DECREASE Breakdown Voltage** (commonly asked in KTU exams)
> 1. **Increased specimen thickness** (statistical/Weibull effect at higher volumes of material exposed).
> 2. **Increased temperature** (lowers thermal stability).
> 3. **Increased humidity** (water absorption on surface).
> 4. **Presence of voids, contaminants, or impurities**.
> 5. **Higher test frequency** (more heating per unit time in AC tests).
> 6. **Faster rate of voltage application** (less time for partial discharge healing).
> 7. **Larger electrode area** (more probability of defects).

### 2.3 Electrode Configurations (ASTM D149 Standard)

The choice of electrode geometry strongly influences the measured BDV. The most common configurations are:

1. **Plane-Plane Electrodes**: Two flat circular metal cylinders, both faces flat and parallel. Used for sheets and films.
2. **Sphere-Plane Electrodes**: One sphere pressing on one face, a flat plate on the other. Generates a more uniform field.
3. **Needle-Plane Electrodes**: A sharp needle on one side, plane on the other. Used to simulate **point defects** and study puncture behavior.
4. **Foil Wrapping**: Aluminum or tin foil wrapped around flexible specimens; prevents surface flashover.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Variables & Units** |
|---|---|---|
| $E = \dfrac{V_{bd}}{t}$ | Dielectric Strength (most important) | $E$ = dielectric strength (kV/mm), $V_{bd}$ = breakdown voltage (kV), $t$ = thickness (mm) |
| $Q_g = V^2 \cdot \omega \cdot C \cdot \tan\delta$ | Heat generated in dielectric | $V$ = applied voltage, $\omega = 2\pi f$, $C$ = capacitance, $\tan\delta$ = loss tangent |
| $Q_d = k \cdot A \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat dissipated by conduction | $k$ = thermal conductivity, $A$ = area, $\Delta T$ = temperature rise |
| $P(V) = 1 - e^{-\left(\dfrac{V}{V_0}\right)^\beta}$ | Weibull cumulative failure probability | $V$ = applied voltage, $V_0$ = scale parameter, $\beta$ = shape parameter |
| $\eta = \dfrac{V_2}{V_1} \cdot 100\%$ | Statistical efficiency between samples | $V_1, V_2$ = BDV of individual specimens |
| $E_{rms} = \dfrac{E_{peak}}{\sqrt{2}}$ | RMS field from peak measurement | Used in AC breakdown analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of BDV Testing**
> - **High-Voltage Cable Insulation** (XLPE cables for power transmission).
> - **Capacitor Film Selection** (BOPP, PET films).
> - **PCB Substrate Evaluation** (FR-4 epoxy).
> - **Transformer Oil & Solid Insulation** quality control.
> - **Safety Certification** of consumer electronic enclosures.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Dielectric Strength from Breakdown Voltage

The fundamental relationship linking breakdown voltage and dielectric strength is derived as follows:

**Step 1 — Define the variables.** Let a polymer specimen of uniform thickness $t$ (in millimetres) be subjected to a gradually increasing AC voltage. The electric field $E$ inside the dielectric is, by Gauss's law for a parallel-plate configuration, uniform and given by:

$$E = \dfrac{V}{t}$$

where $V$ is the instantaneous applied voltage (in kV) and $t$ is the thickness (in mm).

**Step 2 — Identify the critical point.** As $V$ is increased monotonically, a value $V = V_{bd}$ is reached at which the material transitions to conduction. At this instant, the field is the **dielectric strength** $E_{bd}$:

$$E_{bd} = \dfrac{V_{bd}}{t}$$

**Step 3 — Restate with explicit units.** The standard engineering units for this equation in polymer testing are:

- $V_{bd}$ in **kV** (kilovolts)
- $t$ in **mm** (millimetres)
- Therefore, $E_{bd}$ in **kV/mm**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Example (Worked Out for KTU Practice)**
> A polyethylene (PE) insulation sheet of thickness $0.5\ \text{mm}$ fails at an applied voltage of $12\ \text{kV}$. Calculate the dielectric strength.
>
> **Solution:**
> $$E_{bd} = \dfrac{V_{bd}}{t} = \dfrac{12\ \text{kV}}{0.5\ \text{mm}} = 24\ \text{kV/mm}$$
>
> The dielectric strength of this PE sample is **24 kV/mm**, which is consistent with the typical range for LDPE (18 – 28 kV/mm). ✓

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Thermal Breakdown Criterion

For thermal breakdown, the critical condition is that the rate of **heat generated** within the polymer equals or exceeds the rate of **heat dissipated** to the surroundings.

**Step 1 — Heat generated per unit volume.**

The power loss per unit volume in a dielectric under AC stress is:

$$p = \omega \cdot \varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_r \cdot E^2 \cdot \tan\delta$$

where:
- $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the angular frequency
- $\varepsilon_0$ is the permittivity of free space ($8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m)
- $\varepsilon_r$ is the relative permittivity of the polymer
- $E$ is the applied electric field
- $\tan\delta$ is the loss tangent (dissipation factor)

**Step 2 — Heat generated per unit time (for a volume of area $A$ and thickness $t$):**

$$Q_g = p \cdot A \cdot t = \omega \cdot \varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_r \cdot E^2 \cdot \tan\delta \cdot A \cdot t$$

**Step 3 — Heat dissipated by conduction through the polymer and to the electrodes/surroundings.** Using Fourier's law of heat conduction:

$$Q_d = \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{t}$$

where $k$ is the thermal conductivity of the polymer and $\Delta T = T - T_0$ is the temperature rise.

**Step 4 — Thermal equilibrium condition.** At the brink of thermal breakdown, $Q_g = Q_d$:

$$\omega \cdot \varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_r \cdot E^2 \cdot \tan\delta \cdot A \cdot t = \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{t}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the breakdown field.** Cancelling $A$ from both sides and rearranging:

$$E_{th}^2 = \dfrac{k \cdot \Delta T}{\omega \cdot \varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_r \cdot t^2 \cdot \tan\delta}$$

**Step 6 — Expressing the thermal breakdown voltage:**

$$V_{th} = E_{th} \cdot t = \sqrt{\dfrac{k \cdot \Delta T}{\omega \cdot \varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_r \cdot \tan\delta}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why **thicker specimens** can show **lower apparent dielectric strength** in thermal breakdown — the heat has a longer path to escape, so runaway heating occurs at a lower voltage per unit thickness.

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Weibull Statistical Analysis of BDV Data

Because polymer breakdown is stochastic (defects are randomly distributed), the data is statistically analyzed using the **two-parameter Weibull distribution**.

**Step 1 — Cumulative probability of failure.** The probability that a specimen has failed at or below voltage $V$ is:

$$P(V) = 1 - \exp\!\left[-\left(\dfrac{V}{V_0}\right)^\beta\right]$$

where:
- $V_0$ is the **scale parameter** (the voltage at which 63.2% of specimens have failed).
- $\beta$ is the **shape parameter** (higher $\beta$ = narrower data spread = better quality).

**Step 2 — Linearize the Weibull equation.** Take the double natural logarithm:

$$\ln\!\left[-\ln(1-P)\right] = \beta \cdot \ln(V) - \beta \cdot \ln(V_0)$$

**Step 3 — Plot interpretation.** If you plot $\ln[-\ln(1-P)]$ on the y-axis versus $\ln(V)$ on the x-axis, you should get a **straight line** with:
- **Slope** = $\beta$
- **y-intercept** = $-\beta \ln(V_0)$

This linearization is the **standard procedure** for KTU laboratory data analysis and quality reporting.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Breakdown Mechanisms

| **Parameter** | **Intrinsic** | **Thermal** | **Electrochemical** | **Discharge** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Time to failure | $\mu s$ | $s$ to $min$ | $h$ to $years$ | $min$ to $h$ |
| Temperature dependence | Weak | Strong (Arrhenius) | Moderate | Moderate |
| Frequency dependence | None | Strong | Weak | Strong |
| Specimen purity | Pure polymers | Industrial grade | Industrial grade | Defective |
| Voltage dependence | Field only | $V_{th} \propto \sqrt{1/f}$ | Long-term stress | Local voids |
| Dominant in | Thin films, DC | Thick insulations, AC | Outdoor insulation | Laminated composites |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: BDV Test Cell Architecture (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["HV TRANSFORMER"]:::power
    nodeB["VOLTAGE REGULATOR 0 TO 50 KV"]:::power
    nodeC["CURRENT LIMITING RESISTOR"]:::power
    nodeD["SPECIMEN HOLDER WITH ELECTRODES"]:::test
    nodeE["POLYMER SPECIMEN"]:::test
    nodeF["FAILURE DETECTION CIRCUIT"]:::sensor
    nodeG["DIGITAL VOLTMETER"]:::meter
    nodeH["TRIP RELAY FOR CIRCUIT BREAKER"]:::control
    nodeI["DATA LOGGER PC INTERFACE"]:::logger
    nodeJ["SAFETY INTERLOCK GROUNDING SWITCH"]:::control

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeD --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeF --> nodeI
    nodeJ -.-> nodeD
    nodeJ -.-> nodeB

    classDef power fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef test fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sensor fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef meter fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef logger fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Cascading Stages of Dielectric Failure in Polymers

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1["STAGE 1: APPLIED VOLTAGE INCREASES"]:::s1
    s2["STAGE 2: POLARIZATION AND DIPOLAR ORIENTATION"]:::s2
    s3["STAGE 3: PARTIAL DISCHARGE INITIATION IN VOIDS"]:::s3
    s4["STAGE 4: LOCAL HEATING AND TREEING CHANNEL FORMATION"]:::s4
    s5["STAGE 5: ELECTRICAL TREE PROPAGATION"]:::s5
    s6["STAGE 6: COMPLETE DIELECTRIC BREAKDOWN ARCING"]:::s6
    s7["FINAL STATE: PERMANENT PUNCTURE OR CARBON TRACK"]:::s7

    s1 --> s2
    s2 --> s3
    s3 --> s4
    s4 --> s5
    s5 --> s6
    s6 --> s7

    classDef s1 fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef s2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef s3 fill:#FFDAB9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef s4 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef s5 fill:#FF7F50,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef s6 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef s7 fill:#DC143C,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#FFF
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: ASTM D149 Test Procedure Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    p1["PREPARE SPECIMEN AS PER ASTM D149"]:::proc
    p2["MEASURE THICKNESS AT 5 POINTS"]:::proc
    p3["CONDITION SPECIMEN AT 23 DEG C 50 PERCENT RH"]:::proc
    p4["PLACE BETWEEN ELECTRODES IN TEST CELL"]:::proc
    p5["APPLY VOLTAGE AT 500 V PER SECOND"]:::proc
    p6["MONITOR CURRENT AND VOLTAGE CONTINUOUSLY"]:::sensor
    p7["RECORD VOLTAGE AT ONSET OF FAILURE"]:::sensor
    p8["REPEAT FOR 5 SPECIMENS MINIMUM"]:::proc
    p9["CALCULATE DIELECTRIC STRENGTH EBD"]:::calc
    p10["PERFORM WEIBULL STATISTICAL ANALYSIS"]:::calc
    p11["REPORT VBD AND EBD WITH UNCERTAINTY"]:::calc

    p1 --> p2
    p2 --> p3
    p3 --> p4
    p4 --> p5
    p5 --> p6
    p6 --> p7
    p7 --> p8
    p8 --> p9
    p9 --> p10
    p10 --> p11

    classDef proc fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef sensor fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef calc fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: BDV vs Thickness Behaviour (Conceptual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a["INCREASING THICKNESS"]:::input
    b["MORE DEFECT SITES EXPOSED"]:::effect
    c["LONGER HEAT DISSIPATION PATH"]:::effect
    d["HIGHER ABSOLUTE VBD IN KV"]:::result
    e["LOWER APPARENT EBD IN KV PER MM"]:::result
    f["WEIBULL SHAPE BETA DECREASES"]:::result
    g["WIDER DATA SCATTER"]:::result

    a --> b
    a --> c
    b --> d
    c --> d
    c --> e
    b --> f
    f --> g

    classDef input fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef effect fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#F08080,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### **PART A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)**

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define dielectric breakdown voltage. Mention the standard test method used for its determination.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition (2 Marks):** Dielectric breakdown voltage is the minimum voltage applied across an insulating material at which the material loses its insulating properties and allows a significant current to flow through it, resulting in a sudden loss of dielectric strength.
- **Standard Test Method (1 Mark):** The most widely accepted standard is **ASTM D149** ("Standard Test Method for Dielectric Breakdown Voltage and Dielectric Strength of Solid Electrical Insulating Materials at Commercial Power Frequencies"). The equivalent international standard is **IEC 60243-1**.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any three factors that affect the breakdown voltage of a polymer.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Thickness of the specimen** — thicker specimens generally show lower apparent dielectric strength due to greater probability of defect presence and longer heat dissipation paths.
2. **Temperature** — higher temperature decreases BDV because of increased conductivity and loss tangent.
3. **Humidity / Moisture content** — water absorption on the surface or within the polymer reduces the BDV significantly.
4. (Bonus acceptable answer) **Rate of voltage application, electrode geometry, frequency, presence of voids.**

---

### **PART B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — With Internal Choice**

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Module 3 Model Paper]`

**(a)** Discuss the different **mechanisms of dielectric breakdown** in polymers with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A polymer insulation specimen of thickness **0.8 mm** fails at a voltage of **18 kV** under standard test conditions.
   - (i) Calculate the **dielectric strength**. **(3 Marks)**
   - (ii) If two such specimens are stacked, what is the **expected breakdown voltage** assuming independent and identical behaviour? **(2 Marks)**
   - (iii) Mention **two ASTM/IEC standards** relevant to this test. **(2 Marks)**

---

**Model Answer for Question A:**
