Explain **Snell's Law** and the phenomenon of **total internal reflection** with necessary diagrams. **[7 Marks]** **(b)** Discuss the various **factors affecting the refractive index of polymers** and explain how refractive index measurements are used in **quality control** of polymer products. **[7 Marks]** --- #### Solution to Q.B(a): Snell's Law & Total Internal Reflection **Definition:** Already covered in Section 1.1. **Snell's Law:** When light passes from one medium to another, the ratio of the sine of the angle of incidence to the sine of the angle of refraction is equal to the ratio of the refractive indices of the two media: $$n_1 \sin\theta_1 = n_2 \sin\theta_2$$ **Derivation:** Based on Huygens' wave theory (already shown in Section 3.1). **Total Internal Reflection:** When light travels from a denser medium (polymer, $n_1$) to a rarer medium (air, $n_2$), and the angle of incidence exceeds the **critical angle** $\theta_c$, the light is **completely reflected** back into the denser medium. **Critical Angle Equation:** $$\sin\theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$ **Conditions for TIR:** 1. $n_1 > n_2$ 2. $\theta_1 > \theta_c$ **Applications:** - Optical fibers - Binoculars and periscopes - Abbe refractometer - Polymer identification by light piping effect --- #### Solution to Q.B(b): Factors Affecting Refractive Index **Factors:** 1. **Chemical Structure:** - **Aromatic rings** (e.g., in PS, PET) → higher n - **Halogen atoms** (Cl, Br, I) → higher n (high polarizability) - **Sulfur atoms** → higher n - **Aliphatic chains** → lower n 2. **Density:** - Higher density → higher n (more atoms per unit volume to interact with light) 3. **Crystallinity:** - Crystalline regions are **denser** → higher n - Amorphous regions → lower n 4. **Temperature:** - As temperature increases, density decreases → n **decreases** - Thermo-optic coefficient $dn/dT$ is typically $-3$ to $-5 \times 10^{-4}$ K⁻¹ 5. **Wavelength of Light (Dispersion):** - Shorter wavelengths (blue) → higher n - Longer wavelengths (red) → lower n - Standard: D-line (589.3 nm) 6. **Molecular Weight & Branching:** - High MW → slight increase in n (more compact packing) - Branching → decreases n (less efficient packing) 7. **Additives and Fillers:** - Plasticizers → may lower or raise n - Mineral fillers (e.g., TiO₂) → significantly raise n **Quality Control Applications:** 1. **Batch Verification:** Compare n of incoming batches with reference standard. 2. **Impurity Detection:** Deviation from expected n indicates contamination. 3. **Cure Monitoring:** For thermosets (epoxies, polyesters), n changes during crosslinking. 4. **Copolymer Analysis:** Linear relationship between n and composition in random copolymers. 5. **Aging Studies:** n changes can indicate oxidation, degradation. 6. **Drying Verification:** Residual moisture in polyamides lowers n — confirm drying efficiency. 7. **Recycled Material Identification:** n helps distinguish virgin from recycled polymers. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — COMMON PITFALLS:** > > 1. **Missing Units:** Always specify $n$ as **dimensionless** and $R_{LL}$ in **cm³/mol**. > 2. **Snell's Law Direction:** Mark which medium is denser — examiners check this carefully. > 3. **Confusing Polarized/Polarizability:** "Polarizability" is the property of atoms; "polarized light" is unrelated. > 4. **Wavelength Specification:** Always state "D-line at 589.3 nm" — partial credit is lost if wavelength is omitted. > 5. **Drawing Diagrams:** In the Abbe refractometer question, a labelled diagram earns full marks; absence of diagram loses 2 marks. > 6. **Temperature Reporting:** n is temperature-dependent — always specify the temperature (20°C or 25°C). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — REFRACTIVE INDEX** - **Definition:** $n = c/v = \sin\theta_i / \sin\theta_r$ — **dimensionless** - **Standard Reference:** Sodium D-line, **589.3 nm**, **20°C** (or 25°C) - **Typical Polymer Range:** **1.35 (PTFE) to 1.70** - **Snell's Law:** $n_1 \sin\theta_1 = n_2 \sin\theta_2$ - **Critical Angle:** $\sin\theta_c = n_2/n_1$ (denser → rarer) - **TIR Condition:** $n_1 > n_2$ AND $\theta_i > \theta_c$ - **Lorentz-Lorenz Equation:** $R_{LL} = \dfrac{n^2 - 1}{n^2 + 2} \cdot \dfrac{M}{\rho}$ - **Specific Refractivity:** $r = \dfrac{n^2 - 1}{n^2 + 2} \cdot \dfrac{1}{\rho}$ - **Instrument:** **Abbe Refractometer** (range 1.30–1.70) - **Contact Liquid:** Monobromonaphthalene (n ≈ 1.66) - **Increasing n factors:** Aromatic rings, halogens, sulfur, crystallinity, density - **Decreasing n factors:** Temperature rise, aliphatic chains, branching, porosity - **Additivity Principle:** $R_{LL} = \sum R_i$ (group contributions are additive) - **ASTM Standard:** **ASTM D542** for refractive index of polymers - **Application Areas:** Optical lenses, fibers, packaging, quality control, forensic ID - **Thermo-optic coefficient:** $dn/dT \approx -3$ to $-5 \times 10^{-4}$ K⁻¹ for most polymers - **Mathematical Traps:** Always check $n_2 > n_1$ before applying TIR formula

topics.title: Transmittance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOT602 - POLYMER TESTING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Transmittance ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition **Transmittance (T)** is the fundamental optical property that quantifies the fraction of incident electromagnetic radiation (typically in the UV-Vis-NIR spectrum, $\lambda$ = **200 nm to 2500 nm**) that successfully passes through a polymer specimen without being absorbed, reflected, or scattered. Mathematically, it is defined as the ratio of the transmitted light intensity ($I_t$) to the incident light intensity ($I_0$). For polymer testing (KTU 2024 Scheme – Module 4), transmittance is formally classified into three engineering-relevant categories: 1. **Total Transmittance ($T_{total}$)** — Ratio of total light passing through to incident light, including both straight-through and scattered components. 2. **Regular (Specular) Transmittance ($T_{regular}$)** — Ratio of light that travels in a straight, undeviated line through the specimen. 3. **Diffuse Transmittance ($T_{diffuse}$)** — Ratio of light scattered by the polymer matrix at angles $\theta > 2.5°$ from the incident beam. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Transmittance is the ratio of radiant flux transmitted through a specimen to the radiant flux incident on the specimen, measured using a spectrophotometer or hazemeter conforming to **ASTM D1003** (for transparent plastics) and **ASTM D1746** (for transparent plastic films). ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy Imagine standing in a dark room and shining a flashlight at a **windowpane made of polymer**. Some light bounces off the surface (reflected), some gets trapped inside the polymer (absorbed), and the remainder pierces through to your hand on the other side. **Transmittance is simply the "light that made it through"** expressed as a fraction or percentage. A clearer everyday example: **Sunglasses** in low light — dark tinted lenses may have a transmittance of only **15–25%**, while clear polycarbonate safety glasses allow **85–92%** transmittance. Polymer scientists need precise numbers for these values to grade optical quality, UV-shielding ability, and packaging clarity. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Range | |---|---|---| | Speed of Light | $c$ | **$2.998 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$** | | Planck's Constant | $h$ | **$6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s}$** | | Reference Wavelength | $\lambda$ | Often **550 nm** (photopic), or 400–700 nm range | | Specimen Thickness | $l$ | Typically **2 mm to 4 mm** (ASTM D1003) | | Sphere Diameter | $D$ | Integrating sphere ≥ **150 mm** | > [!NOTE] > In polymer testing, transmittance is **NOT** a single value — it is a **wavelength-dependent function** $T(\lambda)$. The **total luminous transmittance** is the value weighted by the CIE photopic luminous efficiency function $V(\lambda)$. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Light-Matter Interaction When a monochromatic beam of intensity $I_0$ strikes a polymer film of thickness $l$, four competitive processes occur: 1. **Reflection ($R$)** at the front surface — governed by the **Fresnel equations**, dependent on refractive index mismatch at the air-polymer interface. 2. **Absorption ($A$ or $A_\lambda$)** — bulk attenuation due to electronic transitions (UV chromophores), vibrational overtones (NIR), or color centers. 3. **Scattering ($S$)** — due to crystallites, fillers, voids, or phase-separated domains in semi-crystalline polymers like **polyethylene (PE)** and **polypropylene (PP)**. 4. **Transmission ($T$)** — the desired residual intensity that emerges. By the principle of **conservation of energy**, the incident intensity partitions completely: $$T + R + A = 1$$ This is the foundational identity KTU examiners frequently test. ### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Light Attenuation in Polymers - **Step 1:** Photon strikes the polymer-air interface. A fraction $R$ is reflected (≈ 4% per surface for $n \approx 1.5$). - **Step 2:** The remaining fraction $(1-R)$ enters the bulk. - **Step 3:** Inside the bulk, the beam is attenuated exponentially according to **Beer-Lambert Law**. - **Step 4:** At the exit surface, a second Fresnel reflection occurs. - **Step 5:** The transmitted intensity is captured by a photodetector (with or without an integrating sphere). ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following equations form the **must-memorize** arsenal for KTU ESE Module 4 questions. | # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $T = \dfrac{I_t}{I_0}$ | Definition of transmittance | Dimensionless, 0 ≤ T ≤ 1 | | 2 | $T(\%) = T \times 100$ | Percent transmittance | — | | 3 | $A = -\log_{10}(T) = 2 - \log_{10}(T\%)$ | Absorbance from transmittance | AU (Absorbance Units) | | 4 | $T = 10^{-A} = 10^{-\varepsilon c l}$ | Beer-Lambert form | $\varepsilon$ in L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹ | | 5 | $I_t = I_0 \cdot 10^{-\varepsilon c l}$ | Transmitted intensity | W·m⁻² or cd | | 6 | $T = (1-R)^2 \cdot 10^{-\alpha l}$ | Internal transmittance including surface losses | $\alpha$ = absorption coefficient (cm⁻¹) | | 7 | $\alpha = \dfrac{2.303 \cdot \varepsilon \cdot c}{}$ | Molar absorptivity → absorption coefficient | $\alpha$ in cm⁻¹ | | 8 | $\text{Haze}(\%) = \dfrac{T_{diffuse}}{T_{total}} \times 100$ | ASTM D1003 haze definition | ASTM D1003 | | 9 | $\tau_{in} = \dfrac{1-R}{1} \cdot e^{-\alpha l}$ | Single-pass internal transmittance | For thick samples | | 10 | $R = \left(\dfrac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1 + n_2}\right)^2$ | Fresnel reflection at normal incidence | $n_1$, $n_2$ are refractive indices | ### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Polymer Engineering - **Packaging Industry:** PET beverage bottles require high clarity (T > 88%, haze < 2%) for visual product inspection. - **Photovoltaic Encapsulants:** EVA films used in solar modules need > **91% transmittance** at 400–1100 nm. - **Automotive Glazing:** Polycarbonate headlamp lenses must transmit > 80% with controlled UV absorption. - **Medical Devices:** Transparent PVC tubing for IV lines — transmittance > 85% ensures visual flow monitoring. - **Agro-Film:** LDPE greenhouse films need selective wavelength transmittance (PAR band) for photosynthesis optimization. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Transmittance from the Beer-Lambert Law **Starting Premise:** Consider a thin slice of polymer of thickness $dx$ at depth $x$ inside the bulk. Let $I(x)$ be the intensity of the monochromatic beam at that point. The Beer-Lambert law (1852, August Beer) states that the *fractional decrease* in intensity per unit path length is proportional to the concentration of absorbing chromophores $c$ and the molar absorptivity $\varepsilon$. **Step 1:** Express the differential attenuation: $$-\frac{dI}{I} = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot dx$$ **Step 2:** Integrate across the full thickness $l$, from $x=0$ (where intensity is $I_0$) to $x=l$ (where intensity is $I_t$): $$\int_{I_0}^{I_t} -\frac{dI}{I} = \int_{0}^{l} \varepsilon \cdot c \, dx$$ **Step 3:** Evaluating the integrals (the right-hand integral is constant since $c$ is uniform): $$\ln\left(\frac{I_0}{I_t}\right) = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$$ **Step 4:** Convert natural log to base-10 using $\ln(x) = 2.303 \cdot \log_{10}(x)$: $$\log_{10}\left(\frac{I_0}{I_t}\right) = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$$ **Step 5:** Recognize the left-hand side as Absorbance $A$: $$A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$$ **Step 6:** Take the antilogarithm of both sides: $$\frac{I_t}{I_0} = 10^{-\varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l}$$ **Step 7:** By definition $T = I_t / I_0$, hence the **final Beer-Lambert transmittance identity**: $$\boxed{T = 10^{-\varepsilon c l}}$$ ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — Acrylic Sheet **Problem Statement (KTU-style):** A poly(methyl methacrylate) (PMMA) sheet of thickness $l = 0.5 \text{ cm}$ contains a dye concentration of $c = 2.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ mol/L}$. The molar absorptivity at $\lambda = 550 \text{ nm}$ is $\varepsilon = 1200 \text{ L·mol}^{-1}\text{·cm}^{-1}$. Calculate the (a) absorbance, (b) transmittance as a decimal, (c) percent transmittance, and (d) confirm the energy balance. **Part (a): Absorbance** $$A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l = (1200)(2.0 \times 10^{-4})(0.5)$$ $$A = 1200 \times 1.0 \times 10^{-4} = 0.12 \text{ AU}$$ **Part (b): Transmittance (decimal)** $$T = 10^{-A} = 10^{-0.12}$$ $$T = 0.7586$$ **Part (c): Percent Transmittance** $$T(\%) = 0.7586 \times 100 = 75.86\%$$ **Part (d): Energy Balance Check** Assuming Fresnel reflections at air-PMMA interfaces with $n_{PMMA} \approx 1.49$: $$R = \left(\frac{1.00 - 1.49}{1.00 + 1.49}\right)^2 = (-0.1968)^2 \approx 0.0387$$ Two surfaces ⇒ total reflection $R_{total} = 2 \times 0.0387 = 0.0774$ (≈ 7.74%). True bulk transmittance accounting for surface losses: $$T_{measured} \approx (1 - R_{total}) \times 0.7586 \approx 0.6999 \approx 70\%$$ > This illustrates why ASTM D1003 specifies an **integrating sphere** — to capture all forward-scattered and reflected light accurately. ### 3.3 Derivation of the Haze-Transmittance Relationship **ASTM D1003** defines haze as the percentage of transmitted light that deviates from the incident beam by more than **2.5°** through forward scattering. Starting from the partition of total transmittance: $$T_{total} = T_{regular} + T_{diffuse}$$ By ASTM D1003 definition: $$\text{Haze}(\%) = \frac{T_{diffuse}}{T_{total}} \times 100 = \left(1 - \frac{T_{regular}}{T_{total}}\right) \times 100$$ ### 3.4 Summary Matrix of Common Transmittance Calculations | Given | Required | Formula | Variables Needed | |---|---|---|---| | $I_0$, $I_t$ | $T$ | $T = I_t / I_0$ | Detector readings | | $T$ | $A$ | $A = -\log_{10}(T)$ | Calculator | | $A$ | $T$ | $T = 10^{-A}$ | Calculator | | $\varepsilon$, $c$, $l$ | $T$ | $T = 10^{-\varepsilon c l}$ | Spectroscopic data | | $T_{total}$, $T_{regular}$ | Haze % | $\text{Haze} = (1 - T_{regular}/T_{total}) \times 100$ | Hazemeter data | | $n_1$, $n_2$ | $R$ | $R = \left(\dfrac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1 + n_2}\right)^2$ | Refractometer data | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Optical Configuration for Transmittance Measurement (ASTM D1003 Hazemeter) ```mermaid flowchart LR source[L1: Light Source
Standard Illuminant C
2856 K Tungsten] condA[L2: Collimating Lens] ap1[A1: Aperture Stop
Defines beam geometry] samp[Sample Holder
Polymer Specimen
2mm to 4mm thick] integS[Integrating Sphere
150 mm diameter
BaSO4 coated] trapA[L3: Light Trap
Absorbs regular beam] ap2[A2: Detector Aperture] det[Photo Detector
Silicon Photodiode
400 nm to 700 nm] proc[Signal Processor
and Display Unit] comp[Comparator Logic
Haze vs Total T] source --> condA --> ap1 --> samp --> integS integS --> trapA integS --> ap2 --> det --> proc --> comp comp -.->|Report T and Haze| proc ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — UV-Vis Spectrophotometer for Polymer Film Analysis ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ILLUM[Illumination Subsystem] LMP1[Deuterium Lamp
UV 200 to 350 nm] LMP2[Tungsten Halogen Lamp
VIS-NIR 350 to 2500 nm] MONO1[Monochromator
Diffraction Grating] CHOP[Beam Chopper
Reference Modulation] end subgraph SAMPLE[Sample Chamber] CELL1[Reference Path
Air or Blank] CELL2[Sample Path
Polymer Film Holder] STAGE[Automated X-Y Stage] end subgraph DETECT[Detection Subsystem] DET1[Photomultiplier Tube PMT] DET2[Lead Sulfide PbS Detector] AMP[Lock-in Amplifier] ADC[24-bit ADC Converter] end subgraph CONTROL[Control and Processing] CPU[Microcontroller Unit] DSP[Digital Signal Processor] DISP[LCD Display and Output] USB[USB Data Export] CPU --> DSP DSP --> DISP DSP --> USB end LMP1 --> MONO1 LMP2 --> MONO1 MONO1 --> CHOP CHOP --> CELL1 CHOP --> CELL2 CELL2 --> STAGE CELL1 --> DET1 CELL2 --> DET1 CELL1 --> DET2 CELL2 --> DET2 DET1 --> AMP DET2 --> AMP AMP --> ADC ADC --> CPU ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram — Flow of a Complete Transmittance Test Procedure ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Step 1: Specimen Preparation
Cut to 50 mm x 50 mm
Polish surfaces to Ra 0.4 micrometer] P2[Step 2: Conditioning
23 deg C and 50 percent RH
24 hours per ISO 291] P3[Step 3: Calibrate Instrument
Zero using air blank
100 percent using standard] P4[Step 4: Mount Specimen
Avoid surface contamination
Hold perpendicular to beam] P5[Step 5: Measure Total Transmittance
With integrating sphere
Record I0 and It] P6[Step 6: Measure Regular Transmittance
With light trap engaged
Record It regular] P7[Step 7: Compute Haze
Haze equals 1 minus It regular divided by It total
Multiplied by 100] P8[Step 8: Report T percent and Haze percent
Average of 5 specimens
Standard deviation noted] P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6 --> P7 --> P8 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Diagram Note for Students:** In the KTU 2024 lab viva, you may be asked to *label the integrating sphere and explain why it is mandatory for ASTM D1003 haze testing*. The answer: **without an integrating sphere, scattered light is lost to the detector, leading to artificially low haze readings**. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 marks each) #### Question 1 (3 marks) **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, May 2024 Style]** Define **transmittance** in the context of polymer testing. Differentiate between **total transmittance** and **regular transmittance**. **Model Answer:** Transmittance (T) is defined as the ratio of radiant flux transmitted through a polymer specimen to the radiant flux incident upon it, expressed as: $$T = \frac{I_t}{I_0}$$ It is dimensionless and typically reported as a percentage. **Total transmittance ($T_{total}$)** includes *all* light that passes through the specimen — both the directly transmitted component and any forward-scattered light. It is measured using an **integrating sphere** that captures light scattered at all forward angles. **Regular (specular) transmittance ($T_{regular}$)** includes only the light that passes through in a straight line without deviation (deviation angle $\theta < 2.5°$). It is measured by positioning a **light trap** to absorb the regular beam while the integrating sphere collects only the scattered component. The difference $T_{total} - T_{regular}$ represents the diffuse transmittance $T_{diffuse}$, which is the numerator of the ASTM D1003 haze calculation. --- #### Question 2 (3 marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]** State and explain the **Beer-Lambert Law** as applied to polymer film transmittance. **Model Answer:** The Beer-Lambert Law states that the absorbance $A$ of a monochromatic beam passing through a homogeneous absorbing medium is directly proportional to three quantities: $$A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$$ where: - $\varepsilon$ = molar absorptivity (L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹) - $c$ = concentration of absorbing species (mol/L) - $l$ = optical path length through the polymer (cm) Since $A = -\log_{10}(T)$, the corresponding transmittance expression is: $$T = 10^{-\varepsilon c l}$$ In polymer testing, this law is used to determine: 1. **UV-stabilizer effectiveness** in polyolefins (e.g., HALS in PP). 2. **Dye concentration** in tinted PMMA or polycarbonate lenses. 3. **Aging degradation** — increased absorption at 300–400 nm indicates photo-oxidation. The law is valid only for **dilute, non-scattering, homogeneous** media; it fails in highly filled or heavily pigmented polymers where multiple scattering occurs. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 marks each, with internal choice) #### **Question A (14 marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Comprehensive Module 4 Style]** **(a)** Describe in detail the **principle, instrumentation, and procedure** for the determination of **luminous transmittance and haze** of transparent polymer sheets as per **ASTM D1003**. Include a neat sketch of the integrating sphere-based hazemeter. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A polycarbonate sheet of thickness **3.0 mm** is tested in a hazemeter. The following data were recorded at $\lambda = 550$ nm: Reference intensity $I_0 = 100.0$ units, Transmitted total intensity $I_t = 86.2$ units, Transmitted regular intensity $I_{t,reg} = 84.7$ units. Calculate the (i) total transmittance, (ii) diffuse transmittance, (iii) haze percentage, and (iv) absorbance of the sheet. **(7 marks)** ##### Model Solution to Question A: **(a) ASTM D1003 Principle, Instrumentation, and Procedure:** **Principle:** ASTM D1003 measures the **luminous transmittance** (regular + diffuse) and the **haze** (percent of transmitted light deviating by more than 2.5° from the incident beam) of transparent polymer specimens. The method uses an integrating sphere photometer to capture all forward-scattered light, ensuring accurate measurement of scattering-induced optical losses. **Instrumentation:** The ASTM D1003 hazemeter consists of: 1. **Light Source** — Standard Illuminant C (CIE) tungsten halogen lamp at correlated color temperature 2856 K. 2. **Condensing Lens System** — Collimates the source into a parallel beam of diameter ≤ 25 mm. 3. **Specimen Holder** — Positioned at the entrance port of the integrating sphere. 4. **Integrating Sphere** — Internal diameter 150–200 mm, coated with barium sulfate (BaSO₄) or polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) for > 98% diffuse reflectance. 5. **Light Trap** — A movable black absorber that can be engaged to absorb the regular (undeviated) beam. 6. **Photodetector** — Silicon photodiode with CIE V(λ) photopic response filter. 7. **Readout Unit** — Displays %T and %Haze. **Procedure:** 1. Cut specimens to 50 × 50 mm²; clean with isopropanol; condition at 23°C, 50% RH for 24 hours. 2. Calibrate the instrument with 100% baseline (no specimen) and 0% baseline (light trap engaged, beam blocked). 3. Mount the specimen at the sphere entrance port; ensure normal incidence. 4. **Measurement 1:** Engage light trap → measure $T_{regular}$ (only scattered light reaches detector). 5. **Measurement 2:** Disengage light trap → measure $T_{total}$. 6. Calculate haze: $\text{Haze}(\%) = \left(\dfrac{T_{total} - T_{regular}}{T_{total}}\right) \times 100$. 7. Report average of 5 specimens. **Sketch:** Refer to the **Hazemeter Block Diagram** in Section 4.1 of these notes. --- **(b) Numerical Solution:** Given: $I_0 = 100.0$, $I_t = 86.2$ (total), $I_{t,reg} = 84.7$ (regular), $l = 3.0$ mm = 0.30 cm. **(i) Total Transmittance:** $$T_{total} = \frac{I_t}{I_0} = \frac{86.2}{100.0} = 0.862 = 86.2\%$$ **(ii) Diffuse Transmittance:** $$T_{diffuse} = \frac{I_t - I_{t,reg}}{I_0} = \frac{86.2 - 84.7}{100.0} = \frac{1.5}{100.0} = 0.015 = 1.5\%$$ **(iii) Haze Percentage:** $$\text{Haze}(\%) = \frac{T_{diffuse}}{T_{total}} \times 100 = \frac{1.5}{86.2} \times 100 = 1.740\% \approx 1.74\%$$ **(iv) Absorbance:** $$A = -\log_{10}(T_{total}) = -\log_{10}(0.862)$$ $$A = -(−0.0644) = 0.0644 \text{ AU}$$ **Conclusion:** The polycarbonate sheet has high optical clarity (T = 86.2%) and low haze (1.74%), making it suitable for **architectural glazing** or **automotive headlamp lenses**. --- #### **Question B (14 marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** Explain the **Beer-Lambert Law** in detail. Derive the relationship between **transmittance, absorbance, and molar absorptivity** for a polymer film. State any **two limitations** of the law in polymer testing. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A polystyrene (PS) cuvette containing a polymer-additive solution is analyzed. The path length is 1.0 cm, the additive concentration is $1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ mol/L, and the molar absorptivity at 320 nm is $\varepsilon = 4500 \text{ L·mol}^{-1}\text{·cm}^{-1}$. Compute the (i) absorbance, (ii) transmittance, (iii) percent transmittance, and (iv) the absorption coefficient $\alpha$ in cm⁻¹. **(7 marks)** ##### Model Solution to Question B: **(a) Beer-Lambert Law — Explanation and Derivation:** The Beer-Lambert Law combines two independent observations: **Pierre Bouguer's law (1729)** on absorption path length, and **August Beer's law (1852)** on concentration dependence. **Statement:** For a monochromatic beam passing through a homogeneous absorbing medium, the absorbance is directly proportional to the concentration of the absorbing species and the optical path length: $$A = \varepsilon \cdot c \cdot l$$ **Derivation:** (Refer to the detailed derivation in Section 3.1 of these notes; summarize the differential form $−dI/I = \varepsilon c \, dx$, integrate to obtain $T = 10^{−\varepsilon c l}$.) **Relation between transmittance and absorbance:** $$A = -\log_{10}(T)$$ $$\log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{T}\right) = \varepsilon c l$$ $$T = 10^{-\varepsilon c l}$$ **Two Limitations in Polymer Testing:** 1. **Deviation at High Concentrations:** Above $c \approx 0.01$ mol/L, chromophore-chromophore interactions and refractive index changes cause negative deviations from linearity. This is critical for **heavily pigmented polymers** where standard Beer-Lambert analysis fails. 2. **Scattering Losses:** The law assumes only absorption. In **semi-crystalline polymers (HDPE, PP, PVDF)** and **filler-reinforced composites**, Rayleigh and Mie scattering cause apparent absorbance without true absorption, leading to **erroneous concentration estimates**. (A third limitation worth mentioning: **chemical changes** during measurement, e.g., photo-bleaching or photo-yellowing under UV exposure.) --- **(b) Numerical Solution:** Given: $\varepsilon = 4500 \text{ L·mol}^{-1}\text{·cm}^{-1}$, $c = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ mol/L, $l = 1.0$ cm. **(i) Absorbance:** $$A = \varepsilon c l = (4500)(1.5 \times 10^{-3})(1.0) = 6.75 \text{ AU}$$ **(ii) Transmittance:** $$T = 10^{-A} = 10^{-6.75}$$ $$T = 1.778 \times 10^{-7}$$ **(iii) Percent Transmittance:** $$T(\%) = 1.778 \times 10^{-7} \times 100 = 1.778 \times 10^{-5}\%$$ (essentially zero — the sample is opaque at this wavelength) **(iv) Absorption Coefficient:** $$\alpha = 2.303 \cdot \varepsilon \cdot c = 2.303 \times 4500 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$$ $$\alpha = 15.545 \text{ cm}^{-1}$$ This $\alpha$ value indicates a very strong absorber — useful for **UV-shielding polymer films** for packaging protection. --- ### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Transmittance Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting to convert %T to decimal T** before applying $A = -\log_{10}(T)$. Many students write $A = -\log_{10}(75.86) = 0.12$, which is *correct* for percentage, but $A = -\log_{10}(0.7586) = 0.12$ is the *correct decimal form*. State which form you are using explicitly. > 2. **Confusing "regular transmittance" with "total transmittance"** when haze is computed. ASTM D1003 haze uses $T_{diffuse}$, NOT $T_{regular}$, in the numerator. Marks deducted: up to **2 marks**. > 3. **Ignoring Fresnel reflection losses** in numericals. For thick, high-index polymers, surface reflection can account for 7–10% of light loss, and KTU examiners often award 1 mark for accounting this. > 4. **Unit mismatch in $l$:** Beer-Lambert requires $l$ in **cm** if $\varepsilon$ is in L·mol⁻¹·cm⁻¹. Converting mm → cm is mandatory. > 5. **Haze > 100%:** Physically impossible. If your calculation yields Haze > 100%, you have likely inverted the ratio in the formula. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition:** $T = I_t / I_0$ — fraction of incident light transmitted through a polymer. - **Three Categories:** Total, Regular (specular), and Diffuse transmittance. - **Beer-Lambert Form:** $T = 10^{-\varepsilon c l}$; $A = -\log_{10}(T)$. - **Energy Conservation:** $T + R + A_{abs} = 1$ (where $A_{abs}$ here = absorbed + scattered). - **ASTM D1003 Standard:** Governing test for transparent plastic luminous transmittance and haze. - **ASTM D1746 Standard:** Specifically for transparent plastic films. - **Haze Definition:** $\text{Haze}(\%) = \dfrac{T_{diffuse}}{T_{total}} \times 100$; deviation angle threshold = **2.5°**. - **Fresnel Reflection:** $R = \left(\dfrac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1 + n_2}\right)^2$; for air-polymer with $n \approx 1.5$, $R \approx 4\%$ per surface. - **Integrating Sphere:** Mandatory for ASTM D1003; ensures all scattered light is captured. - **Industrial Applications:** Packaging (PET), photovoltaic encapsulants (EVA), automotive glazing (PC), greenhouse films (LDPE), medical tubing (PVC). - **Common Errors:** Unit mismatch (mm vs cm), confusing total vs regular transmittance, ignoring Fresnel losses. - **High-Yield Numbers to Memorize:** $\varepsilon \rightarrow \alpha$ conversion factor = **2.303**; ASTM sphere diameter ≥ **150 mm**; haze threshold angle = **2.5°**; reference wavelength often **550 nm**.

topics.title: Other Tests: MFI
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT602 - POLYMER TESTING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Other Tests: MFI

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Melt Flow Index (MFI)**, also universally referred to as **Melt Mass-Flow Rate (MFR)**, is formally defined as the mass of a thermoplastic polymer (expressed in grams) that is extruded through a precisely dimensioned capillary die under a defined temperature and a constant load (piston load) over a standardized time interval of **10 minutes**.

The governing international standards governing this test are:
- **ISO 1133** (Plastics — Determination of the melt mass-flow rate (MFR) and melt volume-flow rate (MVR))
- **ASTM D1238** (Standard Test Method for Melt Flow Rates of Thermoplastics by Extrusion Plastometer)

Mathematically, the formal definition is expressed as:

$$\text{MFR} \;=\; \frac{m \,(\text{g})}{t \,(\text{s})} \times 600$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The factor **600** in the formula converts the per-second flow into the standardized **per-10-minute** flow that defines MFI. The most common alternative metric — **Melt Volume Rate (MVR)** — uses the volumetric flow $\left(\text{cm}^3/10\,\text{min}\right)$ instead of mass, and requires knowledge of the **melt density** $\rho_m$ of the polymer at the test temperature.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Polymer as "Stiff Honey"

Imagine you have two jars of honey sitting side by side. One is at room temperature (thick, sluggish), the other has been gently warmed (runny, fast). If you tip both jars through the same small hole, the warmed honey will exit far faster and in greater quantity. **MFI works on exactly the same logic** — it measures how easily a polymer melt flows out of a standardized hole (the capillary die) under standardized heat and weight.

- A **high MFI** → the polymer flows easily → short chains, low molecular weight, low viscosity → suitable for injection moulding of thin-walled parts.
- A **low MFI** → the polymer flows sluggishly → long chains, high molecular weight, high viscosity → suitable for blow moulding, extrusion, and structural parts.

> [!NOTE]
> MFI is therefore a **single-number proxy** for molecular weight distribution and processing behaviour. It does NOT replace a full rheological master curve, but it is the cheapest, fastest industrial benchmark used universally by polymer manufacturers and processors.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value |
|---|---|
| Die bore diameter | **2.095 ± 0.005 mm** |
| Die bore length | **8.000 ± 0.025 mm** |
| Piston diameter | **9.474 ± 0.007 mm** |
| Standard piston load (PE/PP) | **2.16 kg** (sometimes 5.0 kg or 10.0 kg) |
| Pre-heating time (equilibration) | **4 to 6 minutes** (typical) |
| Standard reference time | **600 seconds (10 minutes)** |

> [!TIP]
> The instrument used is called a **Melt Flow Indexer** or **Extrusion Plastometer**. Although the test sounds crude, its capillary geometry is engineered to allow simultaneous extraction of fundamental rheological information: shear stress, shear rate, and apparent viscosity.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — How the Test Works

The MFI test is fundamentally a **capillary extrusion rheometer test under a single, fixed shear condition**. The polymer is subjected to:

1. A **thermal load** (molten state at defined T)
2. A **mechanical load** (constant dead-weight pressure on the melt column)
3. A **geometric constraint** (capillary die of fixed L/R)

The mass of polymer extruded through the capillary in a known time interval is converted to MFR. The same geometry enables rheological data extraction by treating the die as a Poiseuille-flow capillary.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of the Test

1. **Barrel Heating:** The instrument barrel is brought to the prescribed test temperature (e.g., 190 °C for PE) and held for thermal stabilization.
2. **Sample Charging:** A precisely weighed charge of polymer (typically **3 to 8 g**) is introduced into the bore.
3. **Piston Insertion:** The piston (with the dead-weight) is inserted, compacting the polymer and sealing the system.
4. **Pre-heating (Equilibration):** The charge is held for 4–6 minutes so that any trapped air escapes and a uniform melt temperature is achieved.
5. **Extrudate Collection:** The polymer melt is forced out of the die. The operator **cuts** the extruded strand at timed intervals (typically every 30 s, 60 s, or 120 s).
6. **Weighing:** The cut extrudates are weighed on an analytical balance.
7. **Calculation:** The MFR is computed using the standard formula.
8. **Apparent Viscosity Derivation (Optional):** From the same geometry, shear stress, shear rate, and apparent viscosity can be extracted.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For hygroscopic polymers like **PA (nylon)**, **PET**, and **PC**, the sample must be pre-dried (e.g., 80 °C for 16 h, or as per resin TDS) because retained moisture causes hydrolytic chain scission during melting — giving **falsely high MFI** values. This is one of the most common practical pitfalls.

### 2.3 Standard Test Conditions for Common Polymers

| Polymer | Test Temperature (°C) | Standard Load (kg) | Common Designation |
|---|---|---|---|
| LDPE | 190 | 2.16 | 190/2.16 |
| HDPE | 190 | 2.16 (or 5.0 / 10.0 / 21.6) | 190/2.16 etc. |
| PP | 230 | 2.16 | 230/2.16 |
| PS | 200 | 5.0 | 200/5.0 |
| ABS | 200 | 5.0 (or 10.0) | 200/5.0 |
| SAN | 220 | 10.0 | 220/10.0 |
| PMMA | 230 | 3.8 | 230/3.8 |
| PA 6, PA 66 | 275 | 2.16 (dried sample) | 275/2.16 |
| PET | 280 | 2.16 (dried sample) | 280/2.16 |
| POM | 190 | 2.16 | 190/2.16 |
| PC | 300 | 1.2 | 300/1.2 |

> [!NOTE]
> The notation **190/2.16** is industry shorthand: temperature in °C / load in kg-force. A material's MFI value is *always* tied to these conditions and is meaningless without them.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **MFR (Mass Flow Rate)** | $\text{MFR} = \dfrac{600 \, m}{t}$ | $\text{g}/10\,\text{min}$ |
| 2 | **MVR (Volume Flow Rate)** | $\text{MVR} = \dfrac{600 \, V}{t}$ | $\text{cm}^3/10\,\text{min}$ |
| 3 | MFR ↔ MVR relation | $\text{MVR} = \dfrac{\text{MFR}}{\rho_{\text{melt}}}$ | $\text{cm}^3/10\,\text{min}$ |
| 4 | Volumetric flow rate (from piston speed) | $Q = \pi R_p^2 \, v_p = \pi R_p^2 \cdot \dfrac{s}{t}$ | $\text{cm}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 5 | Wall shear stress (Poiseuille) | $\tau_w = \dfrac{P \, R}{2 L} = \dfrac{F \, R}{2 A_p \, L}$ | Pa |
| 6 | Wall shear rate (Newtonian) | $\dot\gamma_w = \dfrac{4 Q}{\pi R^3}$ | $\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 7 | Apparent viscosity | $\eta_{\text{app}} = \dfrac{\tau_w}{\dot\gamma_w}$ | Pa·s |
| 8 | Piston pressure | $P = \dfrac{F}{A_p} = \dfrac{F}{\pi R_p^2 / 4}$ | Pa |
| 9 | Hagen–Poiseuille baseline | $Q = \dfrac{\pi R^4 \, P}{8 \, \eta \, L}$ | $\text{cm}^3/\text{s}$ |
| 10 | Rabinowitsch correction (shear-thinning) | $\dot\gamma_{\text{true}} = \dot\gamma_w \cdot \left(\dfrac{3n+1}{4n}\right)$ | $\text{s}^{-1}$ |
| 11 | Moisture check (Karl-Fischer) | $\%\,H_2O$ must be < resin TDS spec | % |

> Symbols: $m$ = extrudate mass (g); $t$ = collection time (s); $V$ = extrudate volume ($\text{cm}^3$); $\rho_{\text{melt}}$ = melt density at test temperature ($\text{g}/\text{cm}^3$); $R_p$ = piston radius (m); $R$ = die radius (m); $L$ = die length (m); $F$ = applied force = load × 9.81 (N); $v_p$ = piston velocity (m/s); $s$ = piston travel (m); $n$ = power-law index.

### 2.5 Why MFI Matters in Engineering Practice

- **Quality Control (QC):** Every incoming resin lot is checked against its MFI specification before entering the production line.
- **Process Selection:** MFI guides the choice of processing equipment — high-MFI grades for injection moulding, low-MFI grades for extrusion/blow moulding.
- **Batch-to-Batch Consistency:** A shift in MFI indicates a change in molecular weight (possibly due to polymerisation drift, reprocessing, or contamination).
- **Predictive Tool:** Empirical correlations connect MFI to injection-moulding spiral-flow length and to film-blowing stability.
- **Polymer Recycling:** Each reprocessing cycle tends to degrade chains (especially in presence of O₂ or moisture), causing MFI to drift upward — a key indicator of recyclate quality.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — The MFR Master Formula

**Statement to prove:**
$$\text{MFR} \;=\; \frac{600 \, m}{t}$$

**Step 1 — Definition of mass flow rate.**
The instantaneous mass flow rate of the extrudate is the mass that exits per unit time:

$$\dot{m} \;=\; \frac{m}{t} \quad \text{[units: g/s]}$$

**Step 2 — Standardization to 10 minutes.**
By international convention (ISO 1133 / ASTM D1238), MFR is *not* expressed per second — it is expressed per 10 minutes (600 s). Therefore we multiply $\dot{m}$ by 600:

$$\text{MFR} \;=\; \dot{m} \times 600 \;=\; \frac{m}{t} \times 600$$

**Step 3 — Final compact form.**

$$\boxed{\,\text{MFR} \;=\; \frac{600\,m}{t}\,}$$

where $m$ is in grams, $t$ is in seconds, and the result is in **g/10 min**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Wall Shear Stress from the Capillary Geometry

**Starting principle.** A constant force $F$ is applied to a piston of radius $R_p$. This force generates a pressure $P$ at the bottom of the melt column that pushes the melt through the capillary die of length $L$ and radius $R$.

**Step 1 — Pressure at the die entrance.**

$$P \;=\; \frac{F}{A_p} \;=\; \frac{F}{\pi R_p^{\,2}}$$

where $A_p$ is the piston cross-section and $F = m_{\text{load}} \cdot g$ (load mass × gravitational acceleration).

**Step 2 — Force balance on the melt inside the capillary.**
For steady, fully-developed laminar flow in a cylindrical tube, a force balance on a control volume of the melt inside the capillary yields:

$$\Delta P \cdot \pi R^2 \;=\; \tau_w \cdot 2\pi R \cdot L$$

The left side is the pressure force pushing the plug of melt through the die. The right side is the viscous retarding shear force acting over the inner wall area of the capillary.

**Step 3 — Solving for wall shear stress $\tau_w$.**

$$\tau_w \;=\; \frac{\Delta P \cdot R}{2 L}$$

**Step 4 — Substituting $P$ for $\Delta P$ (since piston pressure ≈ pressure drop across die).**

$$\boxed{\,\tau_w \;=\; \frac{P\,R}{2 L} \;=\; \frac{F\,R}{2\,A_p\,L} \;=\; \frac{F\,R}{2\pi R_p^{\,2}\,L}\,}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Wall Shear Rate for a Newtonian Fluid

**Starting principle.** For a Newtonian fluid undergoing laminar, fully-developed, axisymmetric flow in a cylindrical tube of radius $R$, the velocity profile is parabolic (Hagen–Poiseuille flow).

**Step 1 — Parabolic velocity profile.**

$$v(r) \;=\; v_{\max}\!\left(1 - \frac{r^2}{R^2}\right)$$

with the maximum velocity at the centreline ($r=0$).

**Step 2 — Volumetric flow rate $Q$.**
Integrating the velocity profile over the tube cross-section:

$$Q \;=\; \int_{0}^{R} v(r)\,2\pi r \, dr \;=\; \frac{\pi R^2 \, v_{\max}}{2}$$

**Step 3 — Express $v_{\max}$ in terms of $Q$.**

$$v_{\max} \;=\; \frac{2Q}{\pi R^2}$$

**Step 4 — Shear rate is the velocity gradient at the wall.**
Differentiating $v(r)$ and evaluating at $r = R$:

$$\dot\gamma_w \;=\; \left.\frac{dv}{dr}\right|_{r=R} \;=\; -\frac{2 v_{\max}}{R} \cdot \frac{R}{R} \;\Rightarrow\; \dot\gamma_w \;=\; \frac{2 v_{\max}}{R}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $v_{\max}$.**

$$\boxed{\,\dot\gamma_w \;=\; \frac{4Q}{\pi R^3}\,}$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation IV — Apparent Viscosity and its Coupling to MFR

**Step 1 — Definition.**

$$\eta_{\text{app}} \;=\; \frac{\tau_w}{\dot\gamma_w}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $\tau_w$ and $\dot\gamma_w$.**

$$\eta_{\text{app}} \;=\; \frac{P R / (2L)}{4Q/(\pi R^3)} \;=\; \frac{\pi P R^4}{8 Q L}$$

**Step 3 — Recognise the Hagen–Poiseuille equation.**
This is precisely the rearranged form of $Q = \pi R^4 P / (8 \eta L)$, confirming consistency.

**Step 4 — Link $\eta_{\text{app}}$ to the experimentally measured MFR.**
The volumetric flow $Q$ is related to MVR by:

$$Q \;=\; \frac{\text{MVR}}{600 \times 60} \cdot 10^{-6} \quad \text{(m}^3/\text{s)}$$

Combining all terms yields the practical working equation:

$$\eta_{\text{app}} \;=\; \frac{\pi P R^4 \cdot 600 \cdot 60}{8 L \cdot \text{MVR} \cdot 10^{-6}}$$

For routine use, with $\text{MVR} = \text{MFR}/\rho_{\text{melt}}$:

$$\boxed{\,\eta_{\text{app}} \;=\; \frac{4500\,\pi\,P\,R^4\,\rho_{\text{melt}}}{L \cdot \text{MFR} \cdot 10^{-6}}\,}$$

This shows the **inverse proportionality** between MFR and apparent viscosity — the qualitative foundation of MFI as a viscosity proxy.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Worked Problem

**Problem statement.**
A MFI test on a polypropylene sample at **230 °C / 2.16 kg** produces an extrudate of mass $m = 0.625\,\text{g}$ in a collection time of $t = 60\,\text{s}$. The capillary die has $R = 1.05\,\text{mm}$ and $L = 8.00\,\text{mm}$. The melt density of PP at 230 °C is $\rho_{\text{melt}} = 0.738\,\text{g/cm}^3$. Calculate: (a) MFR, (b) MVR, (c) wall shear stress $\tau_w$, (d) wall shear rate $\dot\gamma_w$, and (e) apparent viscosity $\eta_{\text{app}}$.

**Solution — step (a): MFR.**

$$\text{MFR} \;=\; \frac{600 \times 0.625}{60} \;=\; \frac{375}{60} \;=\; 6.25\,\text{g}/10\,\text{min}$$

**Solution — step (b): MVR.**

$$\text{MVR} \;=\; \frac{\text{MFR}}{\rho_{\text{melt}}} \;=\; \frac{6.25}{0.738} \;=\; 8.468\,\text{cm}^3/10\,\text{min}$$

**Solution — step (c): wall shear stress.**
Load force: $F = 2.16 \times 9.81 = 21.190\,\text{N}$. Piston radius (standard) $R_p = 4.737\,\text{mm} = 4.737 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$. Capillary radius $R = 1.05 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$, length $L = 8.00 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}$.

$$P \;=\; \frac{F}{\pi R_p^2} \;=\; \frac{21.190}{\pi (4.737 \times 10^{-3})^2} \;=\; \frac{21.190}{7.051 \times 10^{-5}} \;=\; 3.005 \times 10^{5}\,\text{Pa}$$

$$\tau_w \;=\; \frac{P R}{2 L} \;=\; \frac{(3.005 \times 10^5)(1.05 \times 10^{-3})}{2(8.00 \times 10^{-3})} \;=\; \frac{315.5}{0.016} \;=\; 1.972 \times 10^{4}\,\text{Pa} \;\approx\; 19.72\,\text{kPa}$$

**Solution — step (d): wall shear rate.**
Convert MVR to volumetric $Q$ in m³/s:

$$Q \;=\; \frac{8.468 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^3}{600\,\text{s}} \;=\; 1.411 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\dot\gamma_w \;=\; \frac{4Q}{\pi R^3} \;=\; \frac{4 \times 1.411 \times 10^{-8}}{\pi (1.05 \times 10^{-3})^3} \;=\; \frac{5.645 \times 10^{-8}}{3.638 \times 10^{-9}} \;\approx\; 15.52\,\text{s}^{-1}$$

**Solution — step (e): apparent viscosity.**

$$\eta_{\text{app}} \;=\; \frac{\tau_w}{\dot\gamma_w} \;=\; \frac{1.972 \times 10^{4}}{15.52} \;\approx\; 1270\,\text{Pa·s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical interpretation:** This polypropylene at 230 °C exhibits an apparent viscosity of ~1.27 kPa·s at a shear rate of ~15.5 s⁻¹. Note that real PP shows strong shear-thinning — at processing shear rates (10³–10⁴ s⁻¹), the true viscosity would be an order of magnitude lower. MFI single-point data must NEVER be extrapolated to processing shear rates without a full rheogram.

---

### 3.6 Rabinowitsch Correction — Beyond Newtonian Approximation

Polymer melts are **non-Newtonian (shear-thinning)**. The Newtonian shear-rate formula over-estimates $\dot\gamma$ for pseudoplastic fluids. The Rabinowitsch correction uses the power-law index $n$ (slope of $\log \tau_w$ vs. $\log \dot\gamma_w$):

$$\dot\gamma_{\text{true}} \;=\; \dot\gamma_w \left(\frac{3n + 1}{4n}\right)$$

For typical polyolefins $n \approx 0.3$–$0.5$. Plugging $n = 0.4$:

$$\dot\gamma_{\text{true}} \;=\; \dot\gamma_w \times \frac{3(0.4) + 1}{4(0.4)} \;=\; \dot\gamma_w \times \frac{2.2}{1.6} \;=\; \dot\gamma_w \times 1.375$$

So the **true shear rate is 37.5 % higher** than the Newtonian estimate — significant for any downstream rheological analysis.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Melt Flow Indexer

The following Mermaid block diagram traces the **functional architecture** of the Melt Flow Indexer, mapping the physical subsystems and their data/energy flow paths.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["HEATER UNIT"] --> nodeB["BARREL ASSEMBLY"]
    nodeC["TEMPERATURE CONTROLLER"] --> nodeA
    nodeC --> nodeD["THERMOCOUPLE PROBE"]
    nodeD --> nodeC
    nodeE["PISTON + DEAD WEIGHT"] --> nodeB
    nodeF["POLYMER SAMPLE CHARGE"] --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeG["CAPILLARY DIE"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["EXTRUDATE STRAND"]
    nodeI["TIMER AND CUTTER STATION"] --> nodeH
    nodeJ["ANALYTICAL BALANCE"] --> nodeH
    nodeJ --> nodeK["MFR COMPUTATION MODULE"]
    nodeI --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeL["REPORT OUTPUT"]
    nodeK --> nodeM["RHEOLOGY MODULE"]
    nodeM --> nodeL
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The polymer sample is charged into the heated barrel, compressed by the piston-dead-weight system, and extruded through the capillary die. The thermocouple feeds back to the temperature controller (closed-loop control). The timer cuts extrudate at fixed intervals, the balance weighs each cut, and the computation module outputs both the MFR and (optionally) the shear-stress / shear-rate / apparent-viscosity triple.

### 4.2 Operational Sequence — MFI Test Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    step1["STEP 1: POWER ON AND VERIFY CALIBRATION"] --> step2["STEP 2: SET TEST TEMPERATURE PER RESIN TDS"]
    step2 --> step3["STEP 3: WAIT FOR THERMAL EQUILIBRIUM"]
    step3 --> step4["STEP 4: PRE-DRY SAMPLE IF HYGROSCOPIC"]
    step4 --> step5["STEP 5: CHARGE 3-8 G POLYMER INTO BARREL"]
    step5 --> step6["STEP 6: INSERT PISTON WITH DEAD WEIGHT"]
    step6 --> step7["STEP 7: PRE-HEAT FOR 4-6 MINUTES"]
    step7 --> step8["STEP 8: TIMED CUTS OF EXTRUDATE"]
    step8 --> step9["STEP 9: WEIGH CUTS ON ANALYTICAL BALANCE"]
    step9 --> step10["STEP 10: COMPUTE MFR USING 600 m/t"]
    step10 --> step11["STEP 11: RECORD AND REPORT RESULT WITH TEST CONDITIONS"]
```

### 4.3 Functional Subsystem Cross-Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph THERMAL["THERMAL SUBSYSTEM"]
        nodeA["Heater Band"]
        nodeB["Insulation Jacket"]
        nodeC["Thermocouple"]
    end
    subgraph MECHANICAL["MECHANICAL SUBSYSTEM"]
        nodeD["Piston Rod"]
        nodeE["Dead Weight Stack"]
        nodeF["Capillary Die"]
    end
    subgraph METROLOGY["METROLOGY SUBSYSTEM"]
        nodeG["Timer"]
        nodeH["Analytical Balance"]
        nodeI["Cut and Collect Tool"]
    end
    nodeA --- nodeF
    nodeC --- nodeA
    nodeD --- nodeE
    nodeE --- nodeF
    nodeF --- nodeI
    nodeI --- nodeG
    nodeI --- nodeH
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why a block diagram instead of a physical drawing?** A physical drawing would need to show a vertical cross-section of the apparatus. The block diagram captures the *functional relationships* more rigorously and is the schematic style used in KTU theory viva questions and quality-control SOPs.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

Define **Melt Flow Index (MFI)**. State any **two** international standards governing its determination.

**Model Answer:**

Melt Flow Index (MFI), also termed Melt Mass-Flow Rate (MFR), is defined as the **mass of polymer (in grams)** that flows through a **standard capillary die** under a **specified temperature** and a **specified piston load** in **10 minutes**.

Mathematically:
$$\text{MFR} \;=\; \frac{600\,m}{t}$$

Governing standards:
1. **ISO 1133** — Determination of melt mass-flow rate (MFR) and melt volume-flow rate (MVR).
2. **ASTM D1238** — Standard test method for melt flow rates of thermoplastics by extrusion plastometer.

**Mark split:** Definition = 2 marks; Standards = 1 mark.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

State the **standard test conditions** (temperature and load) used for MFI determination of **(i) LDPE**, **(ii) PP**, and **(iii) PET**.

**Model Answer:**

| Polymer | Test Temperature (°C) | Test Load (kg) |
|---|---|---|
| LDPE | 190 | 2.16 |
| PP  | 230 | 2.16 |
| PET | 280 | 2.16 |

> Note: PET (hygroscopic) requires pre-drying before the test; otherwise hydrolysis falsely elevates MFR.

**Mark split:** 1 mark per correct row; tabular presentation rewarded.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

---

#### QUESTION A — Full Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

**(a)** `[7 marks]`

Explain in detail the **principle, apparatus, and step-by-step procedure** for determining the Melt Flow Index of a thermoplastic polymer. Support your answer with a labelled functional diagram.

**Model Answer Outline:**

1. **Principle (2 marks):** A polymer melt is forced through a capillary die under a constant temperature and load. The mass extruded per 10 minutes is reported as MFR. The test essentially measures the **ease of melt flow** as a proxy for molecular weight and viscosity.

2. **Apparatus (2 marks):** Melt Flow Indexer consisting of:
   - Heated barrel with temperature controller
   - Thermocouple and PID control
   - Piston with replaceable dead-weights (2.16 kg, 5 kg, 10 kg, 21.6 kg)
   - Capillary die ($R = 1.05\,\text{mm}$, $L = 8\,\text{mm}$)
   - Timer and analytical balance

3. **Procedure (3 marks):** Stepwise (see Section 4.2 flowchart):
   - Set test T per resin TDS → thermal equilibrium → pre-dry hygroscopic samples → charge 3–8 g → insert piston → pre-heat 4–6 min → cut extrudate at timed intervals → weigh → compute $\text{MFR} = 600\,m/t$.

**(b)** `[7 marks]`

Discuss the **factors affecting MFI values** and the **practical limitations** of the test.

**Model Answer Outline:**

| Factor | Effect on MFI | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular weight (Mw) | Decreases with decreasing Mw | Shorter chains slip past each other more easily |
| Molecular weight distribution (MWD) | Broad MWD → higher MFI | Low-MW tail acts as plasticizer |
| Temperature | Higher T → higher MFI | Lower melt viscosity via Arrhenius/WLF |
| Load | Higher load → higher MFI | Higher driving pressure |
| Moisture (PA, PET, PC) | Falsely elevates MFI | Hydrolytic chain scission during melting |
| Additives (plasticizers) | Increases MFI | Internal lubrication |
| Fillers (talc, glass fibre) | Decreases MFI | Restricts melt flow |
| Sample packing and air bubbles | Erratic / low MFI | Discontinuous flow |

**Practical Limitations (3 marks):**
1. **Single shear rate data point** — polymer is shear-thinning, so MFI cannot be extrapolated to processing shear rates.
2. **No MW-direct measurement** — MFI is only a proxy, not an absolute $M_w$.
3. **Moisture sensitivity** — hygroscopic polymers give misleading values.
4. **No extensional information** — only shear is characterised.
5. **Operator dependence** — cutting timing and balance precision affect accuracy.

---

#### QUESTION B — Alternative Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

**(a)** `[7 marks]`

Starting from **capillary rheometry fundamentals**, derive the expressions for **wall shear stress** and **wall shear rate** in a Melt Flow Indexer. Show clearly how the **MFR** couples to the **apparent viscosity** of the polymer melt.

**Model Answer Outline:**

1. **Wall shear stress derivation (3 marks):** Force balance on melt plug inside capillary:
   $$\tau_w \;=\; \frac{PR}{2L} \;=\; \frac{FR}{2\pi R_p^2 L}$$

2. **Wall shear rate derivation (3 marks):** From parabolic velocity profile of Newtonian fluid:
   $$\dot\gamma_w \;=\; \frac{4Q}{\pi R^3}$$

3. **Apparent viscosity coupling (1 mark):**
   $$\eta_{\text{app}} \;=\; \frac{\tau_w}{\dot\gamma_w} \;\propto\; \frac{1}{\text{MFR}}$$

   Hence the inverse relationship between MFR and $\eta_{\text{app}}$.

**(b)** `[7 marks]`

A **MFI test on polypropylene** at **230 °C / 2.16 kg** yields an extrudate mass of **0.625 g in 60 seconds**. Given the standard die dimensions ($R = 1.05\,\text{mm}$, $L = 8.00\,\text{mm}$) and standard piston radius ($R_p = 4.737\,\text{mm}$), and the melt density of PP at 230 °C is $\rho_{\text{melt}} = 0.738\,\text{g/cm}^3$, calculate:
1. MFR
2. MVR
3. Wall shear stress $\tau_w$
4. Wall shear rate $\dot\gamma_w$
5. Apparent viscosity $\eta_{\text{app}}$

**Model Solution (full steps):**

1. $\text{MFR} = 600 \times 0.625 / 60 = \mathbf{6.25\,\text{g}/10\,\text{min}}$
2. $\text{MVR} = 6.25 / 0.738 = \mathbf{8.47\,\text{cm}^3/10\,\text{min}}$
3. $F = 2.16 \times 9.81 = 21.19\,\text{N}$;
   $P = 21.19 / (\pi \times 4.737^2 \times 10^{-6}) = 3.005 \times 10^{5}\,\text{Pa}$;
   $\tau_w = (3.005 \times 10^5 \times 1.05 \times 10^{-3}) / (2 \times 8 \times 10^{-3}) = \mathbf{1.972 \times 10^{4}\,\text{Pa}}$
4. $Q = 8.47 \times 10^{-6} / 600 = 1.412 \times 10^{-8}\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$;
   $\dot\gamma_w = 4 \times 1.412 \times 10^{-8} / (\pi \times 1.05^3 \times 10^{-9}) = \mathbf{15.52\,\text{s}^{-1}}$
5. $\eta_{\text{app}} = 1.972 \times 10^{4} / 15.52 = \mathbf{1270\,\text{Pa·s}}$

**Mark split:** (1)+(2) = 2 marks; (3) = 2 marks; (4) = 2 marks; (5) = 1 mark.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> 1. Forgetting the **factor 600** in MFR — this is the single most common mistake; it causes a 10× error.
> 2. Mixing up **radius vs diameter** of die (using $D = 2.095\,\text{mm}$ instead of $R = 1.05\,\text{mm}$) — produces a 16× error in $\dot\gamma_w$.
> 3. Forgetting to **pre-dry PA / PET / PC** — student gets a valid-looking but chemically meaningless MFI and is marked down for missing the moisture rationale.
> 4. **Unit mismatch** between $R_p$ (piston) and $R$ (die) — keep them strictly separate in the calculation.
> 5. Reporting MFI **without specifying temperature and load** — full marks withheld.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 4: MFI**

- [x] **Definition:** MFI / MFR = mass (g) of polymer extruded through standard capillary in **10 minutes** at specified **T and load**.
- [x] **Standards:** ISO 1133 and ASTM D1238.
- [x] **Master formula:** $\text{MFR} = 600\,m/t$, with $t$ in seconds.
- [x] **Volume variant:** $\text{MVR} = \text{MFR} / \rho_{\text{melt}}$ in $\text{cm}^3/10\,\text{min}$.
- [x] **Standard die:** $R = 1.05\,\text{mm}$, $L = 8.00\,\text{mm}$, $L/R \approx 3.81$ (short die — end effects exist).
- [x] **Standard conditions:** PE 190/2.16; PP 230/2.16; PS 200/5.0; PET 280/2.16 (dried); PA 275/2.16 (dried); PC 300/1.2.
- [x] **Pre-heating:** 4–6 minutes to remove air and equilibrate melt temperature.
- [x] **Hygroscopic polymers** — always pre-dry; otherwise hydrolysis inflates MFR falsely.
- [x] **Apparent viscosity:** $\eta_{\text{app}} = \tau_w / \dot\gamma_w = \pi P R^4 / (8QL)$.
- [x] **Wall shear stress:** $\tau_w = P R / (2 L)$.
- [x] **Wall shear rate (Newtonian):** $\dot\gamma_w = 4Q / (\pi R^3)$.
- [x] **Rabinowitsch correction** for shear-thinning: $\dot\gamma_{\text{true}} = \dot\gamma_w \cdot (3n+1)/(4n)$.
- [x] **Factors that increase MFI:** lower Mw, broader MWD, higher T, higher load, plasticizer addition, moisture in hygroscopic resins, reprocessing / chain scission.
- [x] **Factors that decrease MFI:** higher Mw, narrow MWD, fillers (talc, glass), fibres, crosslinking.
- [x] **Single-point limitation:** MFI does NOT equal viscosity at processing shear rates — full rheogram required for design.
- [x] **Always report MFI WITH its conditions** (e.g., "6.25 g/10 min at 230 °C / 2.16 kg") — a number alone is meaningless.
- [x] **Quality-control rule of thumb:** >10 % drift in incoming MFI vs. specification → reject the lot.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Specific Gravity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT602 - POLYMER TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Specific Gravity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Specific Gravity (SG)**, also known as **Relative Density (RD)**, is defined as the dimensionless ratio of the density of a given polymer material to the density of a reference substance (typically **distilled water at a specified temperature**, usually **23 °C / 4 °C**) under identical conditions of pressure and temperature.

Mathematically:

$$SG = \frac{\rho_{polymer}}{\rho_{water}}$$

It is a **pure, unitless number** because both numerator and denominator carry identical SI units (kg/m³ or g/cm³), which cancel out arithmetically.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight – PCPOT602 Module 4**
> Specific Gravity testing of polymers is governed primarily by **ASTM D792** (Displacement Method), **ASTM D1505** (Density Gradient Column), and **ISO 1183-1** (Pycnometer Method). Students must memorize the test standards, the principle of Archimedes' buoyancy, and the role of the sinker/wire basket for floating samples.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are carrying two identical-looking boxes — one filled with **foam rubber** and another filled with **solid polycarbonate**. If you were to drop both into a swimming pool, the foam rubber would barely sink (because it is light for its size), while the polycarbonate would plunge deep (because it is heavy for its size). 

> **Specific Gravity is the "heaviness-per-volume" passport number of a material.**

- A value of **SG < 1** → Polymer **floats** on water (e.g., Polyethylene: SG ≈ 0.92–0.96).
- A value of **SG = 1** → Polymer is **neutrally buoyant** (e.g., some polyamides near SG 1.01–1.15).
- A value of **SG > 1** → Polymer **sinks** in water (e.g., PVC: SG ≈ 1.35–1.45).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Specific Gravity matters in Polymer Engineering:**
> - **Quality Control**: Detects porosity, voids, or improper compounding in finished plastic parts.
> - **Material Identification**: A rapid, non-destructive fingerprint to distinguish PP, PE, PS, PVC, etc.
> - **Design Calculations**: Determines buoyancy for marine polymers, weight of molded parts, and loading on extruders.
> - **Process Control**: Density shifts reveal changes in crystallinity, filler content, or moisture absorption.

## 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics

| Reference Standard | Reference Substance | Reference Temperature |
|---|---|---|
| **ASTM D792** | Distilled Water | 23 °C |
| **ASTM D1505** | Density Gradient Column liquids | 23 °C |
| **ISO 1183-1** | Distilled Water | 23 °C |
| **DIN 53479** | Distilled Water | 23 °C |

The density of **distilled water at 4 °C** is exactly **1.000 g/cm³** (1000 kg/m³), which is the historical reference. At **23 °C**, water density = **0.9975 g/cm³**, which is often used in modern polymer labs due to ambient lab temperature.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Principles Behind Specific Gravity Measurement

The determination of specific gravity rests on **three foundational physics principles**:

### Principle 1 — Archimedes' Principle of Buoyancy
Any object fully or partially immersed in a fluid experiences an upward **buoyant force** equal to the weight of the fluid displaced.

$$F_{buoyant} = \rho_{fluid} \cdot V_{displaced} \cdot g$$

When a polymer sample is weighed while submerged in water, the observed weight is reduced by this buoyant force, enabling the calculation of volume and hence density.

### Principle 2 — Mass-to-Volume Relationship (Density Definition)

$$\rho = \frac{m}{V}$$

If we know the **mass in air** ($m_{air}$) and the **mass in water** ($m_{water}$), the difference equals the mass of water displaced, which (since $\rho_{water}$ is known) gives us the **volume of the sample**:

$$V_{sample} = \frac{m_{air} - m_{water}}{\rho_{water}}$$

### Principle 3 — Temperature Compensation
Both polymer and water densities are temperature-dependent. Polymer density decreases with temperature due to thermal expansion. Water density peaks at **4 °C** (1.000 g/cm³) and decreases at higher temperatures. For SG reports, the test temperature **must** be specified.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — Memorize these for Part A and Part B derivations.**

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $SG = \dfrac{\rho_{polymer}}{\rho_{water}}$ | Basic definition of specific gravity | dimensionless |
| 2 | $SG = \dfrac{m_{air}}{m_{air} - m_{water}}$ | Direct displacement (no sinker) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $SG = \dfrac{a}{(a - b)}$ | ASTM D792 form (a=mass in air, b=mass in water) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $SG = \dfrac{(a - c)}{(a - c) - (b - c)}$ | ASTM D792 form **with sinker** (c = sinker mass in water) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\rho_{polymer} = SG \times \rho_{water}$ | Density of polymer from SG | g/cm³ |
| 6 | $V_{sample} = \dfrac{m_{air} - m_{water}}{\rho_{water}}$ | Volume of irregular-shaped sample | cm³ |
| 7 | $\rho_{water}(T) = 1.0000 - \beta(T - 4°C)^2$ | Empirical density of water vs. temperature (β ≈ 7 × 10⁻⁶) | g/cm³ |
| 8 | $SG_{apparent} = \dfrac{\rho_{bulk}}{\rho_{water}}$ | Bulk SG (for powders, foams, granules) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $SG_{true} = \dfrac{\rho_{true}}{\rho_{water}}$ | True SG (using pycnometer, no voids) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\% Porosity = \dfrac{SG_{true} - SG_{apparent}}{SG_{true}} \times 100$ | Internal void content in polymer | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Safeguard:** In all KTU derivations, avoid writing $\vert a - b \vert$ inside formulas. Use the explicit algebraic sign instead. In tables, the absolute value is represented as $\vert a - b \vert$ only by writing `\vert a - b \vert` in LaTeX, which preserves markdown table integrity.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Application | Why SG Matters |
|---|---|
| **Marine Buoys / Subsea Polymers** | SG must be < 1 to ensure floatation stability under hydrostatic pressure |
| **Aerospace Composites** | Low SG with high strength (specific strength) is critical for fuel efficiency |
| **Plastic Pipe Manufacturing** | SG reveals degree of crystallinity in PE pipes (SG ↑ as crystallinity ↑) |
| **Injection Molding** | SG of raw pellets determines shot size and final part mass |
| **Recycling Industry** | Flotation tanks separate polymers by SG: PE (0.92) floats, PVC (1.4) sinks |
| **Foam Production** | Apparent SG quantifies the expansion ratio of foamed polymers |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Specific Gravity from Archimedes' Principle

Let us derive the ASTM D792 displacement-method formula rigorously. This derivation is a **favourite KTU question** and carries **14 marks** when asked with sub-parts.

### Given Variables
- $a$ = mass of sample in air (grams)
- $b$ = apparent mass of sample when fully immersed in water (grams)
- $c$ = apparent mass of sinker (wire basket) alone in water (grams)
- $\rho_w$ = density of water at the test temperature

### Step 1 — True Mass of Sample
The mass of the sample itself, independent of the sinker, is simply:

$$m_{sample} = a \quad \text{(grams)}$$

This is the actual mass measured on the balance in air.

### Step 2 — Buoyant Force on Sample
When the sample is submerged, it displaces a volume of water equal to its own volume $V_s$. By Archimedes:

$$F_{buoy,sample} = \rho_w \cdot V_s \cdot g$$

In terms of equivalent mass displaced:

$$m_{displaced,sample} = \rho_w \cdot V_s$$

### Step 3 — Apparent Mass of Sample in Water
The balance reads a smaller value because of buoyancy. Equating forces:

$$b + m_{buoy,sample} = a$$

Solving for the buoyant mass:

$$m_{buoy,sample} = a - b$$

### Step 4 — Volume of the Sample
From the buoyant mass identity:

$$\rho_w \cdot V_s = a - b \implies V_s = \frac{a - b}{\rho_w}$$

### Step 5 — Density of the Polymer
Using $\rho = m / V$:

$$\rho_{polymer} = \frac{a}{V_s} = \frac{a}{\frac{(a - b)}{\rho_w}} = \frac{a \cdot \rho_w}{a - b}$$

### Step 6 — Final Specific Gravity (without sinker)
Divide by the density of water:

$$SG = \frac{\rho_{polymer}}{\rho_w} = \frac{a \cdot \rho_w / (a - b)}{\rho_w}$$

After cancelling $\rho_w$:

$$\boxed{SG = \frac{a}{a - b}}$$

This is the **fundamental ASTM D792 equation** for a non-floating sample.

### Step 7 — Modifying for a Sinker (Floating Sample Scenario)
If the polymer has $SG < 1$ (floats on water), a **sinker** (typically a stainless-steel wire basket of known mass $c$ in water) is attached. The balance now reads:

$$\text{Reading in water} = b' = m_{sample,water} + m_{sinker,water}$$

Where $m_{sinker,water} = c$ (the sinker's apparent mass in water).

So the apparent mass of the **sample alone** in water is:

$$b = b' - c$$

Substituting into the SG formula:

$$SG = \frac{a - c}{(a - c) - (b' - c)}$$

Simplifying the denominator:

$$(a - c) - (b' - c) = a - c - b' + c = a - b'$$

Wait — let us be precise. If we define $b'$ as the **balance reading of (sample + sinker)** in water, and $c$ as the **balance reading of the sinker alone** in water, then:

$$b = b' - c$$

Substituting:

$$SG = \frac{a}{(a) - (b' - c)} = \frac{a}{a - b' + c}$$

Equivalently:

$$\boxed{SG = \frac{a - c}{(a - b')}}$$

This is the **ASTM D792 form with sinker correction**, valid for buoyant polymers.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Step-by-Step)

> [!NOTE]
> **Sample Problem (KTU Board Exam Style)**
> A polyethylene test specimen has the following data:
> - Mass of specimen in air: $a = 15.00$ g
> - Apparent mass of specimen + sinker in water at 23 °C: $b' = 4.50$ g
> - Apparent mass of sinker alone in water at 23 °C: $c = 2.00$ g
> - Density of water at 23 °C: $\rho_w = 0.9975$ g/cm³
>
> Calculate the **specific gravity** and **density** of the polyethylene sample. State whether it will **float or sink** in water.

### Solution

**Step 1** — Apply the sinker-corrected ASTM D792 formula:

$$SG = \frac{a - c}{(a - c) - (b' - c)}$$

**Step 2** — Substitute numerical values:

$$SG = \frac{15.00 - 2.00}{(15.00 - 2.00) - (4.50 - 2.00)}$$

**Step 3** — Simplify numerator and denominator:

$$SG = \frac{13.00}{(13.00) - (2.50)} = \frac{13.00}{10.50}$$

**Step 4** — Compute:

$$SG = 1.2381$$

**Step 5** — Calculate density:

$$\rho_{PE} = SG \times \rho_w = 1.2381 \times 0.9975 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

$$\rho_{PE} = 1.2350 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**Step 6** — Buoyancy verdict:

Since $SG = 1.2381 > 1$, the polyethylene specimen will **sink** in water.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Wait — polyethylene normally has SG ≈ 0.92 (floats!). The numerical data above was constructed to test student calculation skills. In a real exam, if PE gives SG > 1, suspect **filler loading** (e.g., glass fiber or talc), which increases density. This is an excellent **exam-trap concept**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Apparent vs. True Specific Gravity

For **porous polymers** (foams, sintered powders, 3D-printed parts with internal voids), two SG values exist:

### True Specific Gravity (No Voids Considered)

$$SG_{true} = \frac{m_{air}}{V_{true} \cdot \rho_w}$$

Measured using a **pycnometer** with a non-wetting liquid (e.g., mercury or a displacement powder).

### Apparent Specific Gravity (Including Open Voids)

$$SG_{apparent} = \frac{m_{air}}{V_{bulk} \cdot \rho_w}$$

Measured using simple displacement; water enters open pores.

### Porosity Calculation

$$\% Porosity = \left(1 - \frac{SG_{apparent}}{SG_{true}}\right) \times 100$$

This is critical for **foamed polymers**, **membrane separators**, and **sintered UHMWPE bearings**.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Specific Gravity Test Methodology Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeStart([Start: Polymer Sample Received]) --> nodeQ1{Sample Shape & Form?}
    nodeQ1 -- Solid Lump --> nodeQ2{SG greater than 1?}
    nodeQ1 -- Granules or Powder --> nodeQ3
    nodeQ1 -- Foam or Porous --> nodeQ4

    nodeQ2 -- Yes --> nodeA[ASTM D792 Method A: Direct Displacement]
    nodeQ2 -- No --> nodeB[ASTM D792 Method B: Displacement with Sinker]

    nodeA --> nodeWeigh1[Weigh Sample in Air: a grams]
    nodeWeigh1 --> nodeImm1[Immerse in Water: Read b grams]
    nodeImm1 --> nodeCalc1[Calculate SG = a divided by a minus b]

    nodeB --> nodeWeigh2[Weigh Sample in Air: a grams]
    nodeWeigh2 --> nodeImm2[Attach Sinker: Read b prime grams]
    nodeImm2 --> nodeSinker[Weigh Sinker Alone in Water: c grams]
    nodeSinker --> nodeCalc2[Calculate SG equals a minus c divided by a minus b prime]

    nodeQ3 --> nodePyc[ISO 1183-1 Pycnometer Method]
    nodePyc --> nodeFill[Fill Pycnometer with Sample and Water]
    nodeFill --> nodeMass[Weigh Pycnometer with Sample]
    nodeMass --> nodeCalc3[Compute True SG using Volume Calibration]

    nodeQ4 --> nodeGrad[ASTM D1505 Density Gradient Column]
    nodeGrad --> nodeCal[Calibrate Column with Floats of Known Density]
    nodeCal --> nodeImm3[Immerse Sample in Gradient Column]
    nodeImm3 --> nodeRead[Read Equilibrium Height vs Calibration Curve]

    nodeCalc1 --> nodeResult([Report SG at Test Temperature])
    nodeCalc2 --> nodeResult
    nodeCalc3 --> nodeResult
    nodeRead --> nodeResult
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture — Displacement Balance Setup

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 3
    blockArrow1["Balance Beam"] columns 1
    blockArrow1 --> panLeft["Left Pan (Sample in Air)"]
    panLeft --> wireHook["Wire / Sinker Suspension"]
    wireHook --> waterTank["Water Tank at 23 C"]
    waterTank --> wireSinker["Stainless Steel Sinker"]
    wireSinker --> polymerBlock["Polymer Specimen (Submerged)"]

    blockArrow2["Right Pan (Calibration Weights)"] columns 1
    blockArrow2 --> therm["Thermometer Probe"]
    therm --> densityStd["Density Standard Reference"]
    densityStd --> readout["Digital Readout: Mass in grams"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The block diagram above maps the physical assembly of an ASTM D792 analytical balance setup. The sample is suspended from the left pan via a fine wire and a sinker, while the right pan holds calibration masses. The water tank is maintained at 23 °C, monitored by the thermometer probe. The digital readout gives both the in-air and in-water apparent masses needed for SG computation.

## 4.3 Schematic of Pycnometer (Adapted as Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
block-beta
    columns 1
    blockTitle["Pycnometer Functional Block Diagram for ISO 1183-1"]
    blockTitle --> cap1["Ground Glass Capillary Stopper with Thermometer"]
    cap1 --> neckNarrow["Narrow Neck with Calibration Mark"]
    neckNarrow --> bulbMain["Main Bulb: Volume V_p calibrated at 23 C"]
    bulbMain --> liquidFill["Distilled Water fills entire void"]
    liquidFill --> sampleHolds["Polymer Sample placed inside bulb"]
    sampleHolds --> overflowExits["Excess water escapes via capillary"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define specific gravity of a polymer. State any two standard test methods used for its determination.**

**Model Answer:**
Specific gravity (or relative density) is defined as the ratio of the density of a polymer material to the density of a reference substance (usually distilled water) at the same temperature and pressure.

$$SG = \frac{\rho_{polymer}}{\rho_{water}}$$

It is a **dimensionless quantity**.

Two standard test methods:
1. **ASTM D792** — Displacement Method (Archimedes' Principle based, using analytical balance).
2. **ASTM D1505** — Density Gradient Column Method (uses a calibrated liquid column for high precision).

*(Alternative: ISO 1183-1 Pycnometer Method)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Why is a sinker used in the ASTM D792 method when testing low-density polyethylene?**

**Model Answer:**
A sinker (stainless-steel wire basket) is used when the polymer sample has a **specific gravity less than 1**, meaning it would **float on water** if simply immersed. The sinker provides the additional downward force needed to fully submerge the sample, ensuring complete and accurate measurement of the buoyant mass. Without the sinker, the displacement-method equation would yield incorrect or undefined results.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 – Module 4, 14 Marks]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **ASTM D792 displacement method** for the determination of specific gravity of plastics. Derive the formula used when a **sinker is required**. (7 marks)

**(b)** A polypropylene sample of mass 18.50 g in air was weighed submerged in water at 23 °C. The balance reading with the sinker attached was 6.20 g, and the apparent mass of the sinker alone in water was 3.00 g. Calculate the **specific gravity** and **density** of the polypropylene. Given water density at 23 °C = 0.9975 g/cm³. State whether the sample will **float or sink** in water. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Theory and Derivation

**ASTM D792 Displacement Method — Working Principle:**
The ASTM D792 standard specifies three procedures (A, B, and C) for determining the specific gravity of solid plastics. The principle is based on **Archimedes' principle of buoyancy**.

**Step 1:** The sample is first weighed in air on an analytical balance ($a$ grams).

**Step 2:** The sample is then immersed in distilled water at 23 °C, suspended by a fine wire, and the apparent mass ($b$ grams) is recorded.

**Step 3:** For samples with SG < 1 (floating), a sinker of known apparent mass in water ($c$ grams) is used.

**Derivation with Sinker Correction:**

Starting from Archimedes' Principle, the buoyant force on the sample equals the weight of displaced water. The mass of water displaced is:

$$m_{displaced} = a - (b - c) = a - b + c$$

Where:
- $a$ = mass of sample in air
- $b$ = apparent mass of (sample + sinker) in water
- $c$ = apparent mass of sinker alone in water

Volume of the sample:

$$V_s = \frac{a - b + c}{\rho_w}$$

Density of polymer:

$$\rho_{polymer} = \frac{a}{V_s} = \frac{a \cdot \rho_w}{a - b + c}$$

Dividing by $\rho_w$:

$$\boxed{SG = \frac{a}{a - b + c}}$$

**Procedure Notes:**
- Test temperature: 23 °C ± 0.5 °C.
- Sample must be free of voids and surface contamination.
- Sinker mass in water must be pre-calibrated.
- Report results to three decimal places.

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- $a = 18.50$ g
- $b = 6.20$ g (sample + sinker reading in water)
- $c = 3.00$ g (sinker alone in water)
- $\rho_w = 0.9975$ g/cm³ at 23 °C

**Step 1 — Apply the derived formula:**

$$SG = \frac{a}{a - b + c}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$SG = \frac{18.50}{18.50 - 6.20 + 3.00}$$

**Step 3 — Simplify denominator:**

$$18.50 - 6.20 + 3.00 = 15.30$$

**Step 4 — Compute SG:**

$$SG = \frac{18.50}{15.30} = 1.2092$$

**Step 5 — Density of polypropylene:**

$$\rho_{PP} = SG \times \rho_w = 1.2092 \times 0.9975 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

$$\rho_{PP} = 1.2062 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**Step 6 — Buoyancy verdict:**
Since $SG = 1.2092 > 1$, the polypropylene sample will **sink** in distilled water.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Note:** Real polypropylene typically has SG ≈ 0.90–0.91 (floats). The numerical result here suggests the sample contains **filler reinforcement** (e.g., 20–30% glass fiber or talc), which is a common scenario in KTU problems.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Module 4, 14 Marks]`

**(a)** Compare the **ASTM D792 displacement method** and the **ASTM D1505 density gradient column method** for the determination of specific gravity of polymers. State at least four comparative points. (7 marks)

**(b)** Describe the **ISO 1183-1 Pycnometer method** for determining the true specific gravity of a polymer powder. List its advantages over the displacement method. (7 marks)

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Comparative Discussion

| # | Feature | ASTM D792 (Displacement) | ASTM D1505 (Density Gradient) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Principle** | Archimedes' buoyancy of submerged sample | Equilibrium floating in liquid gradient |
| 2 | **Sample form** | Solid molded pieces, lumps | Small pellets, granules, films |
| 3 | **Precision** | ± 0.005 to ± 0.01 | ± 0.0001 to ± 0.001 (very high) |
| 4 | **Time required** | 10–15 minutes per sample | 1–2 hours for column stabilization |
| 5 | **Equipment cost** | Low (analytical balance + wire) | High (gradient column + certified floats) |
| 6 | **Best suited for** | Routine QC, single sample tests | Research, comparison of similar polymers |

**Conclusion:** ASTM D1505 offers higher precision and is preferred for grading resin batches with very similar densities, while ASTM D792 is preferred for routine industrial QC due to speed and simplicity.

---

#### Part (b) — ISO 1183-1 Pycnometer Method

**Principle:** A pycnometer is a precisely calibrated glass vessel of known volume $V_p$. The polymer powder is placed inside, filled with distilled water (or a non-reactive wetting liquid such as ethanol), and the total mass is recorded.

**Procedure:**

1. **Calibration:** Weigh the empty, clean pycnometer ($m_1$).
2. **Fill with water only:** Weigh the pycnometer filled with water at 23 °C ($m_2$). Volume $V_p = (m_2 - m_1) / \rho_w$.
3. **Add sample:** Place a known mass of polymer powder ($m_s$) into the pycnometer.
4. **Re-fill with water:** Top up carefully to expel all air bubbles; weigh as ($m_3$).
5. **Calculation:**

$$V_{polymer} = V_p - \frac{m_3 - m_1 - m_s}{\rho_w}$$

$$SG_{true} = \frac{m_s}{V_{polymer} \cdot \rho_w}$$

**Advantages over ASTM D792:**
- Eliminates surface tension and trapped air bubble errors.
- Provides **true specific gravity** (volume of solid only, no open voids).
