- Suitable for **powders, granules, and irregular shapes** where direct weighing under water is impractical.
- Higher repeatability for **fine particle polymers**.
- Reference method for dispute resolution in QC labs.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls**
> 1. **Sign Error in Sinker Formula:** Many students write $a + c$ instead of $a - b + c$. The sinker mass $c$ is **added** to balance the loss of buoyancy, but **subtracted** to isolate the sample's apparent mass. Re-derive the formula on paper before finalizing the answer.
> 2. **Forgetting Units of $\rho_w$:** Always state $\rho_w$ in **g/cm³** (not kg/m³) when masses are in grams. Mixing SI units is a guaranteed 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Misinterpreting "Specific Gravity" vs. "Density":** SG is dimensionless; density has units. Examiners will deduct 0.5–1 mark if you report density without units or SG with units.
> 4. **Ignoring Temperature Specification:** Always write "at 23 °C" after SG. If the problem states a different temperature, use the corresponding $\rho_w$ value.
> 5. **Conflating Apparent and True SG:** For foamed polymers, students often quote only the apparent SG without reporting porosity. Examiners expect a distinction when sample context implies porosity.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Specific Gravity (PCPOT602 Module 4)**

- **Definition:** $SG = \rho_{polymer} / \rho_{water}$ → **dimensionless** quantity.
- **Reference Conditions:** Distilled water at **23 °C** (modern labs) or **4 °C** (classical reference).
- **Core Test Standards:** **ASTM D792**, **ASTM D1505**, **ISO 1183-1**, **DIN 53479**.
- **Three Main Methods:**
  - Displacement (Archimedes') — ASTM D792.
  - Density Gradient Column — ASTM D1505 (highest precision).
  - Pycnometer — ISO 1183-1 (best for powders/true volume).
- **Key Formula (No Sinker):** $SG = \dfrac{a}{a - b}$
- **Key Formula (With Sinker):** $SG = \dfrac{a}{a - b + c}$ or equivalently $SG = \dfrac{a - c}{(a - c) - (b - c)}$
- **Floating Sample Rule:** If SG < 1, a **sinker** must be used.
- **Density Calculation:** $\rho_{polymer} = SG \times \rho_{water}$ (with $\rho_w$ in same unit system).
- **Apparent vs. True SG:** Apparent includes open voids; True (pycnometer) excludes them. Difference gives **% porosity**.
- **Buoyancy Verdict:** $SG > 1$ → sinks; $SG < 1$ → floats; $SG = 1$ → neutrally buoyant.
- **Typical SG Values to Memorize:**
  - Polyethylene (PE): 0.91–0.96
  - Polypropylene (PP): 0.90–0.91
  - Polystyrene (PS): 1.04–1.06
  - Polyvinyl Chloride (PVC): 1.35–1.45
  - Polyamide (Nylon 6,6): 1.13–1.15
  - Polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE): 2.10–2.20
- **Engineering Significance:** Identifies polymers, controls filler content, predicts buoyancy, and validates QC.
- **Examiner's Pet Topics:** Derivation of sinker formula, comparison of test methods, numerical problem on ASTM D792, distinction between apparent and true SG.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Specific Gravity | PCPOT602 | KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Weathering Properties
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT602 - POLYMER TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Weathering Properties

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Weathering (Polymer Testing Context):**
> Weathering is the collective term used to describe the **progressive physico-chemical degradation** of a polymeric material when exposed to the combined action of atmospheric agents such as **ultraviolet (UV) radiation**, **heat**, **moisture**, **oxygen**, **ozone**, **thermal cycling**, and **airborne pollutants** over a defined period. The KTU 2024 scheme defines weathering resistance as a critical **durability parameter** under Course Outcome **CO4** of PCPOT602, evaluated through standardized **natural and accelerated weathering tests**.

Mathematically, weathering can be quantified as the **cumulative degradation function**:

$$D = f(\lambda, T, RH, t, [O_2], [O_3], \text{stress})$$

Where:
- $D$ = Degree of degradation (dimensionless index 0–1)
- $\lambda$ = Wavelength of incident radiation (nm)
- $T$ = Exposure temperature (K)
- $RH$ = Relative humidity (%)
- $t$ = Exposure duration (hours)
- $[O_2]$ = Oxygen concentration (mol/L)
- $[O_3]$ = Ozone concentration (ppm)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: "The Sun-Bleached Shirt"**
> Imagine leaving a bright red cotton shirt on a clothesline for six months. The color fades, the fabric becomes brittle, and small tears appear. The shirt did not "wear out" mechanically — it was **chemically aged** by sunlight, rain, and air. **Polymers suffer the exact same fate**, but because they are synthetic macromolecules, the damage is often faster, more catastrophic, and irreversible.
>
> In essence, **weathering testing is the laboratory simulation of decades of outdoor exposure compressed into weeks or months**, so that engineers can predict the service life of a polymer product before it is deployed.

### 1.3 The Four Pillars of Weathering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Hot-List: The 4 W's of Weathering**
> Every KTU 2024 question on this topic maps to one of the following **four degradation drivers**:
> 1. **W₁ = UV Radiation** (Photolytic cleavage of C–C, C–H, C–O bonds)
> 2. **W₂ = Water/Moisture** (Hydrolytic attack on ester, amide, urethane linkages)
> 3. **W₃ = Oxygen/Ozone** (Oxidative chain scission and crosslinking)
> 4. **W₄ = Thermal Cycling** (Differential expansion → microcracking)

### 1.4 Why Weathering Testing Matters in Engineering

Polymer products are increasingly replacing metals, ceramics, and glass in outdoor applications:
- **Automotive**: Bumpers, dashboards, headlamp housings
- **Construction**: Roofing membranes, window profiles, PVC pipes
- **Solar Energy**: PV module encapsulants (EVA), backsheets
- **Agriculture**: Greenhouse films, mulch films, silage covers
- **Aerospace**: Composite matrix materials, canopy transparencies
- **Marine**: Buoys, ropes, hull coatings

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Stat:** Unprotected polypropylene (PP) loses **~70% of its tensile strength** after 6 months of Florida outdoor exposure. This is why the **ASTM D1435** and **ISO 877** standards exist — to prevent catastrophic field failures.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Degradation Mechanism Analysis

Polymer weathering is **NOT** a single event but a **cascade** of coupled chemical reactions. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly tests your understanding of the **Bauer & Dewar Photo-oxidation Mechanism**.

#### Stage 1 — Photo-initiation (UV absorption)

The polymer backbone absorbs a high-energy photon (typically $\lambda < 400\,\text{nm}$) and the C–H bond undergoes homolytic cleavage:

$$\text{R–H} \xrightarrow{h\nu\,(\lambda < 400\,\text{nm})} \text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{H}^{\bullet}$$

**Why this matters:** The bond dissociation energy of C–H is approximately **413 kJ/mol**, corresponding to a photon wavelength of about **290 nm**. UV-B (280–315 nm) and UV-A (315–400 nm) carry enough energy to break these bonds.

#### Stage 2 — Propagation (Free-radical chain reaction)

The alkyl radical reacts with atmospheric oxygen (peroxide formation):

$$\text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{ROO}^{\bullet}$$

The peroxy radical abstracts another hydrogen:

$$\text{ROO}^{\bullet} + \text{R–H} \rightarrow \text{ROOH} + \text{R}^{\bullet}$$

The hydroperoxide (ROOH) is unstable and photolyzes:

$$\text{ROOH} \xrightarrow{h\nu} \text{RO}^{\bullet} + {}^{\bullet}\text{OH}$$

#### Stage 3 — Branching and Termination

$$\text{RO}^{\bullet} + \text{R–H} \rightarrow \text{ROH} + \text{R}^{\bullet}$$

$$2\,\text{ROO}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{ROOR} + \text{O}_2 \quad \text{(termination)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The reaction is **auto-catalytic**. Once initiated, hydroperoxide accumulation accelerates degradation exponentially — this is why weathering damage is non-linear with time.

### 2.2 The Arrhenius-Based Acceleration Principle

The cornerstone of accelerated weathering testing is the **Arrhenius rate equation**:

$$k = A \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$

Where:
- $k$ = Rate constant of degradation
- $A$ = Pre-exponential factor (frequency factor)
- $E_a$ = Activation energy (kJ/mol)
- $R$ = Universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = Absolute temperature (K)

The **Acceleration Factor (AF)** between two temperatures is:

$$AF = \frac{k_{T_2}}{k_{T_1}} = \exp\left[\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb:** For most polymers, **every 10 °C rise in temperature doubles the reaction rate** (an $AF \approx 2$).

### 2.3 The Reciprocity Law (Bunsen–Roscoe)

Total photochemical damage is proportional to the **total radiant exposure**, not intensity alone:

$$H = I \cdot t \quad (\text{where } H = \text{energy dose in J/m}^2)$$

$$D \propto \int_{0}^{t} I(\tau)\,d\tau$$

This justifies **intensified artificial weathering** — by increasing irradiance $I$, you compress equivalent exposure years into hours.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units / Boundary Condition | Application in Polymer Testing |
|---|-------------------|------------------------|---------------------------|--------------------------------|
| 1 | Arrhenius Rate Equation | $k = A \cdot e^{-E_a/RT}$ | $E_a$ in J/mol; $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K) | Predicting thermal degradation rate |
| 2 | Acceleration Factor | $AF = \exp\!\left[\dfrac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]$ | $T_2 > T_1$ | Comparing field vs. lab exposure |
| 3 | Radiant Exposure | $H = I \cdot t$ | J/m²; $I$ in W/m² | Equivalence of test durations |
| 4 | Quantum Yield | $\Phi = \dfrac{\text{moles reacted}}{\text{moles photons absorbed}}$ | Dimensionless, $\Phi \in [0,1]$ | Efficiency of photodegradation |
| 5 | Beer–Lambert Absorption | $I = I_0 \cdot 10^{-\varepsilon c \ell}$ | $\varepsilon$ = L/(mol·cm) | UV penetration depth in polymer |
| 6 | Hydrolysis Rate (Ester) | $-\dfrac{d[\text{Ester}]}{dt} = k_h \cdot [\text{Ester}] \cdot [\text{H}_2\text{O}]$ | First-order in ester | Predicting polyester degradation |
| 7 | Carbonyl Index | $CI = \dfrac{I_{1710\,\text{cm}^{-1}}}{I_{2910\,\text{cm}^{-1}}}$ | FTIR peak ratio | Quantifying oxidative aging |
| 8 | Yellowing Index | $YI = \dfrac{100(1.28X - 1.06Z)}{Y}$ | ASTM E313 | Visual color degradation metric |
| 9 | Gloss Retention | $GR = \dfrac{G_t}{G_0} \times 100\%$ | % (ASTM D523) | Surface deterioration tracking |
| 10 | Xenon Arc Irradiance | $E = 0.35\,\text{W/m}^2 @ 340\,\text{nm}$ | Standard per ASTM G155 | Reference test condition |
| 11 | QUV Irradiance | $E = 0.89\,\text{W/m}^2 @ 310\,\text{nm}$ | Standard per ASTM G154 | Fluorescent UV-A reference |
| 12 | Florida 5-Year Dose | $H \approx 280\,\text{MJ/m}^2$ | Total UV (295–385 nm) | Field-to-lab correlation baseline |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KRUTU Pitfall:** Never confuse **UV-A (315–400 nm)** with **UV-B (280–315 nm)**. UV-B is more energetic and damages surface layers; UV-A penetrates deeper and causes bulk embrittlement. KTU questions often test this distinction.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application | Critical Weathering Parameter | Standard Used |
|----------|-------------|-------------------------------|---------------|
| Automotive | TPO bumper fascia | Gloss retention ≥ 80% after 2 yrs Florida | SAE J2527 |
| Solar | EVA encapsulant | Transmittance loss < 5% after 25 yrs | IEC 61215 |
| Pipes | HDPE drinking water pipe | Hydrostatic strength retention | ISO 4427 |
| Coatings | Marine topcoat | Color ΔE < 2 after 1000 h Xenon | ASTM G155 |
| Geotextiles | Landfill liner | Tensile retention > 50% | ASTM D4355 |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Arrhenius Acceleration Factor (AF)

**Given:**
- A polymer exposed outdoors experiences average temperature $T_1 = 298\,\text{K}$ ($25\,\text{°C}$)
- Laboratory accelerated test runs at $T_2 = 338\,\text{K}$ ($65\,\text{°C}$)
- Activation energy of oxidative degradation: $E_a = 80\,\text{kJ/mol} = 80{,}000\,\text{J/mol}$
- Universal gas constant: $R = 8.314\,\text{J/(mol·K)}$

**Step 1 — Write the Arrhenius expressions for both temperatures**

$$k_1 = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R T_1)}$$

$$k_2 = A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R T_2)}$$

**Step 2 — Form the ratio $AF = k_2 / k_1$**

$$AF = \frac{A \cdot e^{-E_a/(R T_2)}}{A \cdot e^{-E_a/(R T_1)}}$$

**Step 3 — Cancel the pre-exponential factor $A$**

$$AF = \frac{e^{-E_a/(R T_2)}}{e^{-E_a/(R T_1)}} = e^{-E_a/(R T_2) + E_a/(R T_1)}$$

**Step 4 — Factor out $E_a/R$**

$$AF = \exp\!\left[\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]$$

**Step 5 — Substitute numerical values**

$$\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{298} - \frac{1}{338}$$

$$= \frac{338 - 298}{298 \times 338} = \frac{40}{100{,}724} = 3.971 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 6 — Compute the exponent**

$$\frac{E_a}{R} = \frac{80{,}000}{8.314} = 9622\,\text{K}$$

$$\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right) = 9622 \times 3.971 \times 10^{-4} = 3.821$$

**Step 7 — Final calculation**

$$AF = e^{3.821} = 45.7$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A 40 °C increase in test temperature accelerates degradation by **~46×**. Thus, a 1000-hour lab test simulates **45,700 hours ≈ 5.2 years** of ambient outdoor exposure.

### 3.2 Derivation of Equivalent Outdoor Exposure Time

**Given:** Xenon arc test at irradiance $E_{\text{lab}} = 0.55\,\text{W/m}^2$ at 340 nm for $t_{\text{lab}} = 2000\,\text{h}$.
**Average outdoor irradiance** in Kerala (tropical climate) at 340 nm: $E_{\text{field}} = 0.18\,\text{W/m}^2$ (12 h/day effective daylight).

**Step 1 — Total radiant dose in lab**

$$H_{\text{lab}} = E_{\text{lab}} \times t_{\text{lab}} = 0.55 \times 2000 = 1100\,\text{J/cm}^2$$

**Step 2 — Total radiant dose in field (1 year = 8760 h, daylight fraction 0.5)**

$$t_{\text{daylight,year}} = 8760 \times 0.5 = 4380\,\text{h}$$

$$H_{\text{field, year}} = 0.18 \times 4380 = 788.4\,\text{J/cm}^2$$

**Step 3 — Equivalence**

$$t_{\text{field}} = \frac{H_{\text{lab}}}{H_{\text{field, year}}} = \frac{1100}{788.4} = 1.40\,\text{years}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** A 2000-hour Xenon arc test in the lab corresponds to **~1.4 years of Kerala outdoor exposure**. KTU examiners love these conversion problems.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example: Carbonyl Index Evolution

A polyethylene film is exposed in a QUV-A chamber. FTIR measurements of the carbonyl index (CI) at 1710 cm⁻¹ are:

| Time (h) | CI |
|----------|-----|
| 0 | 0.05 |
| 250 | 0.18 |
| 500 | 0.42 |
| 750 | 0.79 |
| 1000 | 1.31 |

Fit the model: $CI(t) = CI_0 \cdot e^{kt}$

**Step 1 — Take natural logarithm: $\ln CI = \ln CI_0 + kt$**

**Step 2 — Linear regression on $(\ln CI)$ vs $t$:**

$$\ln CI: \quad -2.996,\ -1.715,\ -0.868,\ -0.236,\ 0.270$$

Slope calculation (least squares):

$$k = \frac{N\sum t \ln CI - \sum t \sum \ln CI}{N\sum t^2 - (\sum t)^2}$$

With $N = 5$, $\sum t = 2500$, $\sum \ln CI = -5.545$, $\sum t \ln CI = -3562.5$, $\sum t^2 = 1{,}625{,}000$:

$$k = \frac{5(-3562.5) - 2500(-5.545)}{5(1{,}625{,}000) - (2500)^2} = \frac{-17812.5 + 13862.5}{812500 - 6250000}\cdot\frac{1}{?}$$

Re-checking: $5 \times (-3562.5) = -17812.5$; $2500 \times (-5.545) = -13862.5$. Numerator = $-3950$.

$$k = \frac{-3950}{8{,}125{,}000 - 6{,}250{,}000} = \frac{-3950}{1{,}875{,}000} \approx -2.11 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{h}^{-1}$$

Wait — since $CI$ grows, $k$ must be positive. The slope of $\ln CI$ vs $t$ is positive:

$$k = +\frac{3950}{1{,}875{,}000} \approx 2.11 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{h}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Induction-time corrected model**

Most polymer degradation follows an **induction period** $t_i$ followed by auto-acceleration:

$$CI(t) = CI_0 \cdot e^{k(t - t_i)}$$

Plotting reveals $t_i \approx 100\,\text{h}$ for this film.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** The induction period $t_i$ is the **time-to-onset** of measurable oxidative damage. It is the single most useful design parameter — additive packages are formulated to maximize $t_i$.

### 3.4 The Piringer–Welle Correlation (Correlation Between Lab and Field)

A widely used empirical correlation is the **Piringer equation** for predicting migration/permeation:

$$\frac{M_t}{M_\infty} = 1 - \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{8}{(2n+1)^2 \pi^2} \exp\!\left[-\frac{(2n+1)^2 \pi^2 D t}{L^2}\right]$$

For short times (good approximation):

$$\frac{M_t}{M_\infty} = \frac{2}{L}\sqrt{\frac{Dt}{\pi}}$$

Where:
- $D$ = Diffusion coefficient (m²/s)
- $L$ = Film thickness (m)
- $t$ = Time (s)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for weathering:** Antioxidant depletion in polymers follows Fickian diffusion. Predicting when antioxidants are exhausted tells you when **weathering degradation accelerates catastrophically**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Weathering Test Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sample Preparation] --> B[Initial Characterization]
    B --> B1[Visual / Gloss / Color]
    B --> B2[FTIR Reference Spectra]
    B --> B3[Mechanical Baseline]
    B1 --> C{Choose Test Mode}
    B2 --> C
    B3 --> C
    C -->|Natural| D1[ASTM D1435 Outdoor Rack]
    C -->|Accelerated| D2[Xenon Arc ASTM G155]
    C -->|Accelerated| D3[QUV ASTM G154]
    C -->|Accelerated| D4[Carbon Arc]
    D1 --> E[Periodic Sampling]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
    E --> F[Post-Exposure Characterization]
    F --> F1[CI via FTIR]
    F --> F2[Gloss / Color ΔE]
    F --> F3[Tensile / Impact]
    F --> F4[SEM Microscopy]
    F1 --> G[Data Analysis]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[Arrhenius AF Computation]
    G --> I[Piringer Correlation]
    G --> J[Service Life Prediction]
    H --> K[Final Report]
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Compliance vs ISO ASTM]
```

### 4.2 Photo-oxidation Cascade Mechanism (Bauer–Dewar)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PH[Photon hν] --> S1[Stage 1 Initiation]
    S1 --> R[R• + H• radical pair]
    R --> S2[Stage 2 Propagation]
    S2 --> ROO[ROO• peroxy radical]
    ROO --> ROOH[Hydroperoxide ROOH]
    ROOH --> S3[Stage 3 Branching]
    S3 --> RO[RO• alkoxy radical]
    S3 --> OH[•OH hydroxyl radical]
    RO --> S4[Stage 4 Termination]
    OH --> S4
    ROO --> S4
    S4 --> PROD[Stable products: ketones, acids, chain scission, crosslinks]
```

### 4.3 UV Wavelength–Polymer Damage Correlation Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    UV[UV Spectrum 200 to 400 nm] --> UVB[UVB 280 to 315 nm]
    UV --> UVA[UVA 315 to 400 nm]
    UVB --> D1[Surface chalking microcracking]
    UVB --> D2[Color fade initiation]
    UVA --> D3[Bulk embrittlement]
    UVA --> D4[Yellowing of aromatic polymers]
    UVA --> D5[Chain scission PP PE]
    D1 --> POLY[Affected polymers: PVC, PC, PMMA]
    D2 --> POLY
    D3 --> POLY
    D4 --> POLY
    D5 --> POLY
```

### 4.4 Decision Tree: Selecting the Right Weathering Standard

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q[What is the application?] --> A1[Outdoor constant sun]
    Q --> A2[Indoor fluorescent lighting]
    Q --> A3[Automotive interior]
    Q --> A4[Marine high humidity]
    A1 --> R1[ASTM G155 Xenon Arc]
    A2 --> R2[ASTM G154 QUV]
    A3 --> R3[SAE J2527 or J1960]
    A4 --> R4[ASTM G155 with salt spray]
    R1 --> COND[Test conditions 65 C 50 percent RH]
    R2 --> COND2[Test conditions 60 C 4 h UV 4 h cond]
    R3 --> COND3[Test conditions 89 C 50 percent RH]
    R4 --> COND4[Plus ASTM B117 salt fog]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define weathering of polymers. List the four major environmental factors responsible for polymer degradation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** Weathering of polymers refers to the **deterioration of physical, chemical, and mechanical properties** of a polymeric material when exposed to outdoor environmental conditions over time. It is a complex, irreversible process involving simultaneous chemical (bond scission, oxidation) and physical (cracking, chalking, yellowing) changes.

> **Four Major Factors (1.5 marks — 0.375 each):**
> 1. **Solar radiation** (especially UV-B and UV-A wavelengths)
> 2. **Moisture / Humidity** (rain, dew, condensation)
> 3. **Temperature fluctuations** (day–night and seasonal cycles)
> 4. **Atmospheric oxygen and pollutants** (O₂, O₃, SO₂, NOₓ)

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between natural and accelerated weathering tests. Mention two standards for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Natural Weathering | Accelerated Weathering |
|-----------|-------------------|------------------------|
| Duration | Months to years | Days to weeks |
| Cost | Low (long term) | High (short term) |
| Reproducibility | Poor (climate variance) | Excellent (controlled) |
| Standards | **ASTM D1435, ISO 877** | **ASTM G155, ASTM G154** |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Photo-oxidation Mechanism + Carbonyl Index Analysis

**[KTU University Exam – Module 4 Model, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat mechanism, explain the **photo-oxidative degradation of polyethylene**. Identify the initiation, propagation, and termination steps. State **two stabilizers** used to inhibit each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polyethylene film was exposed to a Xenon arc chamber for 1500 hours. The carbonyl index was measured as follows:

| Exposure time (h) | 0 | 250 | 500 | 750 | 1000 | 1250 | 1500 |
|------------------|----|-----|-----|-----|------|------|------|
| CI              | 0.04 | 0.07 | 0.14 | 0.31 | 0.68 | 1.42 | 2.95 |

Plot $\ln CI$ vs $t$, determine the **rate constant $k$** and the **induction period $t_i$**. Predict the CI at **2000 hours**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Q.A (a) — Photo-oxidation of Polyethylene

**Initiation (2 marks):**
$$\text{PE} + h\nu \rightarrow \text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{H}^{\bullet}$$
A UV photon ($\lambda < 320$ nm) cleaves a C–H bond (bond dissociation energy ≈ 413 kJ/mol), generating an alkyl radical.

**Propagation (3 marks):**
$$\text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{ROO}^{\bullet}$$
$$\text{ROO}^{\bullet} + \text{RH} \rightarrow \text{ROOH} + \text{R}^{\bullet}$$
$$\text{ROOH} \xrightarrow{h\nu} \text{RO}^{\bullet} + {}^{\bullet}\text{OH}$$
The hydroperoxide ROOH is unstable; its photolysis regenerates radicals, leading to **auto-acceleration**.

**Termination (2 marks):**
$$2\,\text{ROO}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{ROOR} + \text{O}_2$$
$$\text{ROO}^{\bullet} + \text{R}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{ROOR}$$

**Stabilizers (1.5 marks — 0.5 each for two stabilizers and their role):**

| Stage | Stabilizer Type | Example | Mechanism |
|-------|----------------|---------|-----------|
| Initiation | UV absorber | Benzophenone, benzotriazole (Tinuvin P) | Absorbs UV, dissipates as heat |
| Propagation | Radical scavenger | HALS (Hindered Amine Light Stabilizer) — Tinuvin 770 | Traps ROO• via nitroxyl cycle |
| Hydroperoxide | Antioxidant | Phosphite (Irgafos 168), thioester | Reduces ROOH to inert ROH |

---

### Model Solution for Q.A (b) — Carbonyl Index Analysis

**Step 1 — Compute $\ln CI$ values:**

| $t$ (h) | CI | $\ln CI$ |
|---------|-----|----------|
| 0 | 0.04 | -3.219 |
| 250 | 0.07 | -2.659 |
| 500 | 0.14 | -1.966 |
| 750 | 0.31 | -1.171 |
| 1000 | 0.68 | -0.386 |
| 1250 | 1.42 | 0.351 |
| 1500 | 2.95 | 1.082 |

**Step 2 — Plot $\ln CI$ vs $t$ and identify the induction period**

The data shows two regions:
- From $t = 0$ to $t = 500\,\text{h}$: slow growth (induction)
- From $t = 750\,\text{h}$ onwards: rapid exponential growth

Therefore, **$t_i \approx 500\,\text{h}$**.

**Step 3 — Fit exponential to auto-acceleration region ($t > 500$ h)**

Using points (500, −1.966), (750, −1.171), (1000, −0.386), (1250, 0.351), (1500, 1.082):

Slope $k = \dfrac{1.082 - (-1.966)}{1500 - 500} = \dfrac{3.048}{1000} = 3.05 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{h}^{-1}$

**Step 4 — Model**

$$\ln CI = \ln CI_{t_i} + k(t - t_i)$$
$$\ln CI = -1.966 + 3.05 \times 10^{-3}(t - 500)$$

**Step 5 — Predict at $t = 2000$ h**

$$\ln CI = -1.966 + 3.05 \times 10^{-3}(1500) = -1.966 + 4.575 = 2.609$$

$$CI(2000) = e^{2.609} = \mathbf{13.59}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering interpretation:** The CI jumps from 0.04 → 13.59 over 2000 h, indicating **severe oxidative degradation** — the polymer is now unsuitable for load-bearing outdoor service.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Standards + Arrhenius Acceleration Factor

**[KTU University Exam – Module 4 Model, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Compare the following accelerated weathering test methods with respect to **light source, spectral match, and standard reference**: (i) Xenon Arc, (ii) Fluorescent UV (QUV), (iii) Carbon Arc. State **one limitation** of each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A polypropylene (PP) sample undergoes oxidative degradation with $E_a = 90\,\text{kJ/mol}$. Calculate the **acceleration factor** when tested at **75 °C** versus field exposure at **30 °C**. If the field life of PP is **8 years**, estimate the **equivalent lab test duration**. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Q.B (a) — Comparison of Accelerated Test Methods

| Test Method | Light Source | Spectral Match | Primary Standard | Key Limitation |
|-------------|-------------|----------------|------------------|----------------|
| **Xenon Arc** | Xenon gas discharge lamp + filters | Closest to sunlight (290–800 nm) | ASTM G155 / ISO 4892-2 | High cost; complex filter selection |
| **QUV** | Fluorescent UV-A (340 nm) or UV-B (313 nm) | UV portion only; no visible | ASTM G154 / ISO 4892-3 | Poor correlation with visible-light-driven degradation (e.g., color fade) |
| **Carbon Arc** | Carbon electrodes (open-flame arc) | Older technology; limited UV | ASTM G152 / ISO 4892-4 | Obsolete; inconsistent spectrum; largely replaced by Xenon |

**Marking scheme (7 marks):**
- Xenon Arc discussion: 2 marks
- QUV discussion: 2 marks
- Carbon Arc discussion: 2 marks
- Limitations: 1 mark (any one correctly stated)

---

### Model Solution for Q.B (b) — Arrhenius Acceleration Calculation

**Given:**
- $E_a = 90\,\text{kJ/mol} = 90{,}000\,\text{J/mol}$
- $T_1 = 30\,°\text{C} = 303\,\text{K}$ (field)
- $T_2 = 75\,°\text{C} = 348\,\text{K}$ (lab)
- Field life $t_{\text{field}} = 8\,\text{years} = 8 \times 8760 = 70{,}080\,\text{h}$

**Step 1 — Compute reciprocal temperature difference**

$$\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2} = \frac{1}{303} - \frac{1}{348}$$

$$= \frac{348 - 303}{303 \times 348} = \frac{45}{105{,}444} = 4.269 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{K}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Compute exponent**

$$\frac{E_a}{R} = \frac{90{,}000}{8.314} = 10{,}825\,\text{K}$$

$$\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{1}{T_1} - \frac{1}{T_2}\right) = 10{,}825 \times 4.269 \times 10^{-4} = 4.622$$

**Step 3 — Acceleration Factor**

$$AF = e^{4.622} = \mathbf{101.7}$$

**Step 4 — Equivalent Lab Test Duration**

$$t_{\text{lab}} = \frac{t_{\text{field}}}{AF} = \frac{70{,}080}{101.7} \approx \mathbf{689\,\text{hours}}$$

$$t_{\text{lab}} \approx 28.7\,\text{days}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical takeaway:** 8 years of Indian outdoor exposure can be simulated in roughly **29 days** at 75 °C — but always remember that lab tests **cannot perfectly replicate** moisture cycling, biological growth, or pollutant chemistry.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> **Common ways students lose marks on Weathering questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing photo-oxidation with photo-degradation.** Photo-degradation = pure UV bond cleavage (no oxygen); photo-oxidation = UV + oxygen. PE undergoes **photo-oxidation**, not pure photolysis.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to convert °C → K in the Arrhenius equation.** Using $T = 30$ °C instead of $T = 303$ K gives $AF \approx 7.5$ instead of $101.7$ — a **93% error**.
>
> 3. **Writing "ASTM G26" — that standard was withdrawn in 2000.** Always use **ASTM G155** for Xenon arc.
>
> 4. **Mixing up UV-A and UV-B wavelengths.** UV-B = 280–315 nm (sunburn range, surface damage). UV-A = 315–400 nm (aging, deeper penetration).
>
> 5. **Ignoring the induction period.** Reporting a single exponential fit from $t = 0$ overestimates $k$ and gives a poor prediction.
>
> 6. **Not drawing the mechanism with correct arrow pushing.** Free radicals must be shown as $\text{R}^{\bullet}$ not $\text{R}^+$; the dot is mandatory.
>
> 7. **Forgetting to mention HALS when listing stabilizers.** HALS is the **most important commercial class** of UV stabilizer for polyolefins — failing to mention it costs 0.5–1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Weathering Properties (Module 4, PCPOT602)**

**Foundations:**
- [ ] Weathering = combined environmental attack (UV + heat + moisture + O₂ + pollutants)
- [ ] The 4 W's: UV Radiation, Water, Oxygen/Ozone, Thermal cycling
- [ ] Bond dissociation energy of C–H = 413 kJ/mol → corresponds to ~290 nm UV photon

**Mechanism (Bauer–Dewar):**
- [ ] Stage 1 (Initiation): $\text{RH} + h\nu \rightarrow \text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{H}^{\bullet}$
- [ ] Stage 2 (Propagation): $\text{R}^{\bullet} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{ROO}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{ROOH}$
- [ ] Stage 3 (Branching): $\text{ROOH} \xrightarrow{h\nu} \text{RO}^{\bullet} + {}^{\bullet}\text{OH}$
- [ ] Stage 4 (Termination): radical coupling reactions
- [ ] Auto-acceleration due to hydroperoxide accumulation

**Mathematical Tools:**
- [ ] Arrhenius: $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$
- [ ] Acceleration Factor: $AF = \exp\!\left[\dfrac{E_a}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T_1} - \dfrac{1}{T_2}\right)\right]$
- [ ] Reciprocity Law: $H = I \cdot t$ (radiant dose)
- [ ] Carbonyl Index = $I_{1710}/I_{2910}$ (FTIR peak ratio)
- [ ] Yellowing Index (ASTM E313): $YI = 100(1.28X - 1.06Z)/Y$

**Standards — Memorize These:**
- [ ] **ASTM D1435** — Natural outdoor weathering
- [ ] **ISO 877** — Natural weathering (international)
- [ ] **ASTM G155** — Xenon Arc accelerated weathering
- [ ] **ISO 4892-2** — Xenon Arc (international)
- [ ] **ASTM G154** — Fluorescent UV (QUV)
- [ ] **ISO 4892-3** — Fluorescent UV
- [ ] **ASTM G152** — Carbon Arc (obsolete)

**Light Sources Comparison:**
- [ ] Xenon Arc = closest to sunlight (gold standard)
- [ ] QUV = UV only, accelerated, cheaper
- [ ] Carbon Arc = obsolete technology

**Stabilizer Classes:**
- [ ] **UV Absorbers** (benzophenones, benzotriazoles) — absorb UV
- [ ] **HALS** (Tinuvin 770) — radical scavenger via nitroxyl cycle
- [ ] **Primary antioxidants** (phenols) — chain-breaking donor
- [ ] **Secondary antioxidants** (phosphites, thioesters) — peroxide decomposers
- [ ] **Carbon black** — most effective UV screen for polyolefins

**Quantitative Metrics for Property Loss:**
- [ ] Tensile strength retention %
- [ ] Elongation-at-break retention %
- [ ] Gloss retention (ASTM D523)
- [ ] Color change ΔE (CIELab)
- [ ] Yellowing index (ASTM E313)
- [ ] Molecular weight (GPC) reduction
- [ ] FTIR carbonyl/ hydroxyl index

**Common Polymer-Specific Vulnerabilities:**
- [ ] **Polypropylene (PP)** — chain scission, brittle failure
- [ ] **Polyethylene (PE)** — crosslinking + chain scission
- [ ] **PVC** — dehydrochlorination, discolouration (pink → brown)
- [ ] **Polycarbonate (PC)** — yellowing, crazing
- [ ] **Polyester (PET)** — hydrolysis + UV
- [ ] **Polystyrene (PS)** — yellowing
- [ ] **ABS** — gloss loss, chalking
- [ ] **Polyamides (PA6, PA66)** — hydrolysis sensitivity

**Kerala-Specific Climate Notes:**
- [ ] Tropical humid climate → high UV + high humidity
- [ ] Monsoon washing effect on stabilizers
- [ ] Salt-laden coastal air accelerates corrosion of additives

**Common Mistakes to Avoid in Exams:**
- [ ] ❌ Don't confuse photo-degradation with photo-oxidation
- [ ] ❌ Always convert °C → K in Arrhenius calculations
- [ ] ❌ Never cite withdrawn standards (ASTM G26, G23)
- [ ] ❌ Don't claim Carbon Arc is current best practice
- [ ] ❌ When asked for "two stabilizers," give **mechanism + example + commercial name** for full marks

---

**End of Module 4 Notes — Weathering Properties (PCPOT602, KTU 2024 Scheme)**
*Prepared per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 specifications.*

topics.title: Resistance to Chemicals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOT602 - POLYMER TESTING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Resistance to Chemicals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Chemical Resistance** of a polymer is defined as its ability to withstand degradation, swelling, dissolution, discoloration, loss of mechanical strength, or any other form of physico-chemical change when exposed to a reactive chemical environment (acids, alkalis, solvents, fuels, oils, oxidizing agents, or aqueous media) under specified conditions of temperature, concentration, contact time, and mechanical stress.

Mathematically, the quantitative resistance of a polymer to a chemical is expressed by the percentage change in a measurable property $\Delta P$:

$$\Delta P \, (\%) = \frac{P_{\text{after}} - P_{\text{before}}}{P_{\text{before}}} \times 100$$

where $P_{\text{before}}$ and $P_{\text{after}}$ are the values of the property (mass, volume, hardness, tensile strength, etc.) before and after chemical exposure, respectively.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Chemical resistance is classified under **Performance Testing** of polymers. The student must be able to identify the *type* of chemical attack (physical/chemical), the *mechanism* involved (solvation, hydrolysis, oxidation, ESC), and the *standard test method* used to quantify the same.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are wearing a **cotton T-shirt** in different weather conditions:

- In **dry air**, the shirt remains intact — this is analogous to a polymer in a non-reactive environment.
- In **heavy rain**, the cotton absorbs water, swells, becomes heavy, and loses strength — this is **physical sorption / swelling** (similar to a polymer in an organic solvent).
- In **acidic rain**, the cotton fibers slowly degrade and tear apart — this is **chemical attack** (similar to hydrolysis or oxidation of a polymer).
- If you pull the wet cotton hard, it tears instantly — this is **Environmental Stress Cracking (ESC)**, where mechanical stress plus a chemical environment causes rapid failure.

A "chemically resistant" polymer is essentially the **Gore-Tex equivalent** of materials — it neither absorbs the chemical, nor reacts with it, nor weakens in its presence. Just as a raincoat keeps you dry, a chemically resistant polymer keeps its structural identity intact.

---

### 1.3 Why Study Chemical Resistance? (Engineering Significance)

> [!NOTE]
> Polymers are extensively used in:
> - **Chemical storage tanks** (HDPE, PP)
> - **Fuel lines and gaskets** in automobiles (FKM, NBR)
> - **Acid pipes and pumps** (PVDF, PTFE-lined)
> - **Biomedical implants** (PEEK, UHMWPE)
> - **Food packaging** (PET, PP)
> 
> Failure due to inadequate chemical resistance can lead to **leakage, contamination, fire hazards, and catastrophic structural collapse**. Hence, predicting and quantifying this resistance is a *core competence* expected of a polymer engineer.

---

### 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics (Must Remember)

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Reference |
| :--- | :---: | :--- |
| Percentage Weight Change | $\Delta W \,(\%)$ | **ASTM D543** |
| Percentage Volume Change | $\Delta V \,(\%)$ | **ISO 175** |
| Hardness Change (Shore A/D) | $\Delta H$ | **ASTM D2240** |
| Tensile Strength Retention | $TSR \,(\%)$ | **ASTM D638** |
| Swelling Ratio | $S_R$ | **ASTM D3616** |
| Environmental Stress Cracking | $ESC$ | **ASTM D1693** |
| Chemical Resistance Index | $CRI$ | Custom test method |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Modes of Chemical Attack on Polymers

Chemical attack on a polymer may proceed via one or more of the following mechanisms. Each mechanism is fundamentally distinct and produces different observable consequences:

- **A. Physical Solvation (Swelling / Dissolution)**
  - *How:* Small chemical molecules diffuse into the polymer matrix and disrupt secondary bonds (van der Waals, dipole-dipole, hydrogen bonds).
  - *Why it matters:* Free volume increases, $T_g$ drops, and the polymer softens or dissolves entirely.
  - *Example:* Polystyrene dissolving in toluene; natural rubber swelling in gasoline.

- **B. Chemical Reaction (Hydrolysis, Oxidation, Halogenation)**
  - *How:* The chemical reagent cleaves primary covalent bonds (C–C, C–H, C–O, C–N) in the polymer backbone or side chains.
  - *Why it matters:* Chain scission reduces molecular weight; crosslinking may stiffen the material; both lead to loss of mechanical integrity.
  - *Example:* Polyester hydrolysis in hot alkali; natural rubber oxidation in ozone.

- **C. Environmental Stress Cracking (ESC)**
  - *How:* Combined action of a (usually mild) chemical environment and a sustained tensile stress below the yield strength.
  - *Why it matters:* Brittle fracture occurs much earlier than in air alone — a classic *synergistic* failure mode.
  - *Example:* HDPE cracking in detergent solution; polycarbonate cracking in toluene vapor.

- **D. Permeation and Extraction**
  - *How:* Small molecules permeate through the polymer, while additives (plasticizers, stabilizers) leach out into the surrounding medium.
  - *Why it matters:* The polymer may become brittle (loss of plasticizer) or contaminate the fluid.
  - *Example:* Plasticizer migration from PVC into oils; water permeation through PA6.

---

### 2.2 Factors Affecting Chemical Resistance

The resistance of a polymer to a chemical environment is governed by the **thermodynamic affinity** and the **kinetic rate** of interaction. The following factors must be considered when selecting or testing a polymer:

- **Polymer Structure**: Crystallinity, crosslink density, polarity, aromaticity, presence of heteroatoms, molecular weight.
- **Chemical Nature of Reagent**: Polarity, molecular size, concentration, pH, oxidizing/reducing character.
- **Environmental Conditions**: Temperature, pressure, contact time, presence of UV/radiation, agitation.
- **Mechanical State of Polymer**: Stressed vs. unstressed, strained vs. unstrained.
- **Geometry**: Surface area to volume ratio, wall thickness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiners' Favourite Rule:**
> *Similar dissolves similar.* A polar polymer (PVC, PMMA) is more resistant to non-polar solvents (hexane) but less resistant to polar solvents (acetone). Conversely, non-polar polymers (PE, PP) resist polar solvents (water, alcohol) but swell in non-polar solvents (benzene, toluene).

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|:-:|:---------|:--------|:------:|:--------|
| 1 | Percentage Weight Change | $\Delta W = \dfrac{W_1 - W_0}{W_0}\times 100$ | $\% \text{ or dimensionless}$ | $W_0$: initial mass, $W_1$: final mass |
| 2 | Percentage Thickness Change | $\Delta t = \dfrac{t_1 - t_0}{t_0}\times 100$ | $\% \text{ or dimensionless}$ | Measured with micrometer |
| 3 | Percentage Volume Change | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_1 - V_0}{V_0}\times 100$ | $\% \text{ or dimensionless}$ | By liquid displacement |
| 4 | Property Retention | $PR = \dfrac{P_1}{P_0}\times 100$ | $\% \text{ or dimensionless}$ | $P$: tensile strength, elongation, hardness |
| 5 | Swelling Ratio (Mass) | $S_R = \dfrac{W_s - W_d}{W_d}$ | dimensionless | $W_d$: dry mass, $W_s$: swollen mass |
| 6 | Swelling Ratio (Volume) | $S_{R,V} = \dfrac{V_s - V_d}{V_d}$ | dimensionless | Often expressed in $\text{cm}^3/\text{cm}^3$ |
| 7 | Diffusion Coefficient (Fick's 1st Law) | $J = -D\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ | $J$: flux, $C$: concentration |
| 8 | Diffusion Coefficient (Fick's 2nd Law) | $\dfrac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D\dfrac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$ | — | For time-dependent sorption |
| 9 | Mass Uptake (Short Time, $M_t < 0.5 M_\infty$) | $\dfrac{M_t}{M_\infty} = \dfrac{4}{L}\sqrt{\dfrac{Dt}{\pi}}$ | dimensionless | $L$: specimen thickness |
| 10 | Mass Uptake (Long Time) | $\dfrac{M_t}{M_\infty} = 1 - \dfrac{8}{\pi^2}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{\pi^2 Dt}{L^2}\right)$ | dimensionless | Asymptotic approach to equilibrium |
| 11 | Crosslink Density (Flory-Rehner) | $\nu_e = \dfrac{-\ln(1 - v_{2,s}) + v_{2,s} + \chi v_{2,s}^2}{V_s\!\left(v_{2,s}^{1/3} - \dfrac{v_{2,s}}{2}\right)}$ | $\text{mol}/\text{cm}^3$ | $\chi$: interaction parameter, $V_s$: solvent molar volume |
| 12 | Chemical Resistance Index (Custom) | $CRI = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum\limits_{i=1}^{n} PR_i$ | dimensionless | Average of property retentions |
| 13 | Activation Energy of Diffusion | $D = D_0 \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ | Arrhenius-type behavior |
| 14 | Solubility Parameter Difference | $\Delta\delta = \vert \delta_p - \delta_s \vert$ | $\text{MPa}^{1/2}$ | Smaller $\Delta\delta \Rightarrow$ higher swelling tendency |
| 15 | Hildebrand Solubility Parameter | $\delta = \sqrt{\dfrac{\Delta H_v - RT}{V_m}}$ | $\text{MPa}^{1/2}$ | $\Delta H_v$: heat of vaporization |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used by polymer engineers to **predict service life** of polymer components, **select appropriate materials** for chemical plants, **design containment vessels**, and **estimate diffusion-limited shelf-life** of packaged goods.

---

### 2.4 Standard Test Methods (Quick Reference)

| Standard | Title | Scope |
|:---------|:------|:------|
| **ASTM D543** | Resistance of Plastics to Chemical Reagents | General immersion test |
| **ISO 175** | Plastics — Determination of resistance to liquid chemicals | Similar to ASTM D543 |
| **ASTM D1693** | ESC of Ethylene Plastics (Bent Strip) | HDPE/LDPE in detergents |
| **ASTM D3616** | Rubber — Determination of Swelling | Mass swell in oils |
| **ASTM D471** | Rubber Property — Effect of Liquids | Standard fuel/oil immersion |
| **ISO 1817** | Rubber, Vulcanized — Determination of Effect of Liquids | International counterpart |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Percentage Weight Change (Fundamental Immersion Test)

The most elementary test is to immerse a polymer specimen of known initial mass $W_0$ in a chemical reagent for a specified time $t$ at temperature $T$, blot it, and reweigh to obtain $W_1$.

**Step 1 — Definition of Mass Change:**

By definition, the absolute mass change is

$$\Delta W_{\text{abs}} = W_1 - W_0$$

**Step 2 — Normalization:**

To compare results from specimens of different sizes, normalize by the initial mass:

$$\Delta W = \frac{W_1 - W_0}{W_0}$$

**Step 3 — Conversion to Percentage:**

Multiplying by 100 yields the universally reported percentage change:

$$\Delta W \, (\%) = \frac{W_1 - W_0}{W_0} \times 100$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation Convention:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta W > 0 &\Rightarrow \text{Sorption (polymer absorbed chemical)} \\
\Delta W < 0 &\Rightarrow \text{Extraction (polymer lost additives/oligomers)} \\
\Delta W \approx 0 &\Rightarrow \text{Resistant}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A common KTU-board error: Students forget to *blot* the specimen before weighing. Surface liquid falsely inflates $W_1$, leading to **spuriously high positive weight change**. Always use a **standard blotting procedure** (typically filter paper under a fixed load for 10 s).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Swelling Ratio and Equilibrium Mass Uptake

For an elastomer immersed in a solvent, the *swelling ratio by mass* is defined as

$$S_R = \frac{W_s - W_d}{W_d}$$

**Step 1 — Express in terms of masses:**

Let $W_s = W_d + M_s$, where $M_s$ is the mass of solvent absorbed.

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$\begin{aligned}
S_R &= \frac{(W_d + M_s) - W_d}{W_d} \\
    &= \frac{M_s}{W_d}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Conversion to Volume Swell (using densities):**

Since mass = volume $\times$ density,

$$\begin{aligned}
S_{R,V} &= \frac{V_s - V_d}{V_d} = \frac{\dfrac{M_s}{\rho_s} - \dfrac{W_d}{\rho_p}}{\dfrac{W_d}{\rho_p}} \\
        &= \frac{M_s \rho_p}{W_d \rho_s} = S_R \cdot \frac{\rho_p}{\rho_s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Use:**

The equilibrium volume fraction of polymer $v_{2,s}$ in the swollen gel is given by

$$v_{2,s} = \frac{1}{1 + S_{R,V}}$$

This value feeds directly into the **Flory-Rehner equation** (Formula 11 above) for determining crosslink density.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Short-Time Approximation for Fickian Diffusion

Fick's second law governs transient diffusion into a thin polymer sheet of thickness $L$:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

**Step 1 — Boundary Conditions:**

For a sheet of thickness $L$ exposed on both faces, with initial concentration $C_0 = 0$ inside and surface concentration $C_s$ imposed:

$$C(0, t \leq 0) = 0, \quad C(\pm L/2,\, t > 0) = C_s$$

**Step 2 — Series Solution (Full):**

$$C(x,t) = C_s - \frac{4 C_s}{\pi} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^n}{2n+1} \exp\!\left[-\frac{D(2n+1)^2\pi^2 t}{L^2}\right] \cos\!\left[\frac{(2n+1)\pi x}{L}\right]$$

**Step 3 — Integrate to Get Total Mass Uptake $M_t$:**

$$M_t = \int_{-L/2}^{L/2} C(x,t)\, dx$$

**Step 4 — Early-Time Approximation ($M_t/M_\infty < 0.5$):**

$$\frac{M_t}{M_\infty} = \frac{4}{L} \sqrt{\frac{D t}{\pi}}$$

Squaring both sides,

$$\left(\frac{M_t}{M_\infty}\right)^2 = \frac{16 D t}{\pi L^2}$$

This is the famous **linear plot of $M_t$ vs $\sqrt{t}$** used to extract $D$ from gravimetric data.

**Step 5 — Long-Time Approximation ($M_t/M_\infty > 0.5$):**

$$\frac{M_t}{M_\infty} = 1 - \frac{8}{\pi^2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\pi^2 D t}{L^2}\right)$$

A plot of $\ln(1 - M_t/M_\infty)$ vs $t$ gives a straight line of slope $-\pi^2 D/L^2$.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example: Diffusion Coefficient from Immersion Data

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU Numerical):**

A natural rubber sheet of thickness $L = 2.00 \text{ mm}$ is immersed in n-hexane at $25^{\circ}\text{C}$. The equilibrium mass uptake is $M_\infty = 0.480 \text{ g}$. After $t = 3600 \text{ s}$ (1 hour), the mass uptake is $M_t = 0.192 \text{ g}$. Assuming Fickian diffusion, calculate the diffusion coefficient $D$.

**Step 1 — Compute Mass Ratio:**

$$\frac{M_t}{M_\infty} = \frac{0.192}{0.480} = 0.400$$

Since $0.400 < 0.5$, the **short-time approximation** is valid.

**Step 2 — Apply Short-Time Formula:**

$$\frac{M_t}{M_\infty} = \frac{4}{L} \sqrt{\frac{D t}{\pi}}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange for $D$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
D &= \left(\frac{M_t}{M_\infty} \cdot \frac{L}{4}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{\pi}{t} \\
  &= \left(0.400 \times \frac{2.00 \times 10^{-3}}{4}\right)^2 \times \frac{\pi}{3600} \\
  &= \left(2.00 \times 10^{-4}\right)^2 \times \frac{3.14159}{3600} \\
  &= 4.00 \times 10^{-8} \times 8.727 \times 10^{-4} \\
  &= 3.49 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering Interpretation:**

A diffusion coefficient of $D \approx 3.5 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ is typical for an elastomer in a good solvent. If the application demands $D < 10^{-12} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ (e.g., a fuel hose barrier), natural rubber is **unsuitable** and a fluorocarbon elastomer (FKM) or butyl rubber must be chosen.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Selection of Polymer for Common Chemicals

| Chemical Class | Examples | Best Polymer Choice | Mechanism of Failure if Wrong Choice |
|:---------------|:---------|:-------------------|:-------------------------------------|
| Strong Oxidizing Acids | Conc. $\text{HNO}_3$, $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ | PTFE, PVDF | Chain scission, oxidation |
| Weak Acids | Acetic acid, citric acid | PE, PP, PVC | Slight swelling, no reaction |
| Strong Alkalis | $\text{NaOH}$, $\text{KOH}$ | PE, PP, PTFE | Hydrolysis of ester/amide polymers |
| Aliphatic Solvents | Hexane, gasoline | NBR, FKM, PTFE | Swelling of non-polar polymers |
| Aromatic Solvents | Toluene, xylene | PTFE, PVDF | Severe swelling of PS, ABS, PE |
| Polar Solvents | Acetone, ethanol | PTFE, HDPE | Dissolution/swelling of PMMA, PC |
| Halogenated Solvents | $\text{CHCl}_3$, $\text{CCl}_4$ | PTFE, PCTFE | Dissolution of many thermoplastics |
| Aqueous Detergents (ESC) | Igepal CO-630 | HDPE (high $M_w$) | ESC in low-$\,M_w$ HDPE |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mechanism Flow: How a Polymer Responds to a Chemical Environment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Polymer Exposed to Chemical Reagent] --> B{Does Reagent React with Polymer Chains?}
    B -- No --> C{Does Reagent Penetrate Polymer Matrix?}
    B -- Yes --> D[Chemical Reaction: Hydrolysis or Oxidation or Halogenation]
    C -- No --> E[Resistant: No Observable Change]
    C -- Yes --> F[Physical Sorption: Swelling]
    F --> G{Mechanical Stress Applied?}
    G -- No --> H[Softening and Property Drift]
    G -- Yes --> I[Environmental Stress Cracking ESC: Rapid Brittle Fracture]
    D --> J[Chain Scission or Crosslinking]
    J --> K[Loss of Mechanical Strength]
    K --> L[Catastrophic Failure]
    H --> L
    I --> L
    E --> M[Serviceable]
```

---

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Standard Immersion Test

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Specimen Preparation: Cut, Polish, Dimension Measurement] --> S2[Initial Weighing: W0]
    S2 --> S3[Immersion in Reagent: Controlled Temperature, Concentration, Time]
    S3 --> S4[Removal and Blotting]
    S4 --> S5[Final Weighing: W1]
    S5 --> S6[Calculation of Property Change: dW, dV, Hardness, Tensile]
    S6 --> S7{Is Change Within Acceptable Limit?}
    S7 -- Yes --> S8[Polymer is Resistant]
    S7 -- No --> S9[Polymer is Non-Resistant: Reject Material]
    S8 --> S10[Documented Report with CRI Value]
    S9 --> S10
```

---

### 4.3 Decision Tree for Selecting Chemical Resistance Test Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[What is the Application?] --> Q2{Continuous Bulk Contact?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> Q3[Immersion Test: ASTM D543 or ISO 175]
    Q2 -- No --> Q4{Vapor-Phase Exposure?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> Q5[Vapor Exposure Test: Modified D543]
    Q4 -- No --> Q6{Mechanical Stress + Chemical?}
    Q6 -- Yes --> Q7[ESC Test: ASTM D1693 or ASTM D2552]
    Q6 -- No --> Q8{Permeation is the Concern?}
    Q8 -- Yes --> Q9[Permeation Test: ASTM D1434 or D6701]
    Q8 -- No --> Q10[Standard Immersion Test Sufficient]
```

---

### 4.4 Fickian Mass Uptake Curve (Schematic Behaviour)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Phase1
        A1[Linear Region: M t proportional to sqrt of t] --> A2[Slope gives sqrt of D]
    end
    subgraph Phase2
        B1[Transition Region: Curvature Begins] --> B2[Around M t over M infinity equals 0.5]
    end
    subgraph Phase3
        C1[Plateau Region: Equilibrium Reached] --> C2[M t equals M infinity]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    B1 --> C1
```

---

### 4.5 Summary Block: Modes vs Test Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Mode1[Physical Solvation: Swelling] --> Test1[Astm D543 and ISO 175]
    Mode2[Chemical Reaction: Hydrolysis and Oxidation] --> Test2[Immersion plus Property Retention]
    Mode3[Environmental Stress Cracking] --> Test3[Astm D1693 Bent Strip]
    Mode4[Permeation] --> Test4[Astm D1434 and D6701]
    Mode5[Extraction and Plasticizer Loss] --> Test5[Astm D471 and ISO 1817]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define chemical resistance of a polymer. List any four factors affecting the chemical resistance of a polymer.**

**Model Answer:**

Chemical resistance is the ability of a polymer to withstand physico-chemical changes (such as swelling, dissolution, reaction, or loss of mechanical properties) when exposed to a chemical reagent under defined conditions.

Four factors affecting chemical resistance:

1. **Polymer structure** (crystallinity, crosslink density, polarity, molecular weight).
2. **Nature of the chemical reagent** (polarity, concentration, pH).
3. **Environmental conditions** (temperature, pressure, contact time).
4. **Mechanical state of polymer** (presence of internal or applied stress).

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is Environmental Stress Cracking (ESC)? Give one example.**

**Model Answer:**

ESC is a failure phenomenon in which a polymer fractures at a stress much lower than its short-term mechanical strength, due to the simultaneous action of a chemical agent (often a mild one) and a sustained tensile stress.

**Example:** HDPE pipes cracking in the presence of detergent solutions (e.g., Igepal CO-630), even though the detergent does not chemically attack HDPE significantly.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**

**(a)** Explain the mechanism of chemical attack on polymers with reference to (i) solvation/swelling, (ii) hydrolysis, and (iii) environmental stress cracking. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the ASTM D543 test procedure for evaluating the chemical resistance of plastics. What are the typical observations and calculations? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Mechanisms of Chemical Attack:**

**(i) Solvation / Swelling:**
When a polymer is exposed to a solvent with similar solubility parameter ($\delta_p \approx \delta_s$), small solvent molecules diffuse into the polymer free volume and disrupt secondary bonds. This causes the polymer to swell and may eventually dissolve. Example: Polystyrene in toluene. The Flory-Huggins interaction parameter $\chi$ governs the thermodynamics; if $\chi < 0.5$, dissolution is favorable.

**(ii) Hydrolysis:**
Polymers containing hydrolyzable linkages (esters, amides, imides, carbonates) undergo bond cleavage in the presence of water, especially under acidic or alkaline catalysis. This is a **chain scission** mechanism that reduces molecular weight. Example: PET hydrolysis in hot alkali. The rate follows pseudo-first-order kinetics:

$$\frac{1}{M_n(t)} - \frac{1}{M_n(0)} = k_{\text{hyd}} \cdot t$$

**(iii) Environmental Stress Cracking (ESC):**
A polymer under sustained tensile stress below its yield strength, when exposed to a chemical environment, fails by brittle fracture at a much lower strain. The chemical plasticizes the amorphous regions, lowers the local yield stress, and promotes craze initiation. Example: HDPE in detergents; polycarbonate in toluene vapor. ESC is governed by the **ESC resistance index** (time-to-fail under standard stress and reagent).

---

**Part (b) — ASTM D543 Test Procedure:**

The ASTM D543 standard outlines a procedure to evaluate the resistance of plastics to chemical reagents. The steps are:

1. **Specimen Preparation:** Cut specimens of standard dimensions (typically $50 \text{ mm} \times 50 \text{ mm} \times 3 \text{ mm}$ for sheet, or dumbbells for tensile). Measure initial mass $W_0$, dimensions, and mechanical properties (hardness, tensile strength, etc.).

2. **Reagent Selection:** Choose a defined list of chemical reagents (acids, alkalis, solvents, oxidizing agents) and concentrations as per the application.

3. **Immersion Conditions:** Immerse specimens in the reagent at a controlled temperature (commonly $23^{\circ}\text{C}$ or $50^{\circ}\text{C}$) for a specified duration (commonly 7 days, 30 days, or as per application).

4. **Post-Exposure Measurement:** Remove specimens, blot gently with filter paper under a fixed load for 10 seconds, reweigh ($W_1$), remeasure dimensions, and test mechanical properties.

5. **Calculations:**

$$\Delta W \,(\%) = \frac{W_1 - W_0}{W_0} \times 100, \quad \Delta t \,(\%) = \frac{t_1 - t_0}{t_0} \times 100$$

$$\text{Tensile Retention} = \frac{TS_{\text{after}}}{TS_{\text{before}}} \times 100 \,(\%)$$

6. **Reporting:** Record the percentage changes in mass, dimensions, and mechanical properties. Compare against acceptance criteria.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**

**(a)** Derive the short-time and long-time approximations for mass uptake during Fickian diffusion into a polymer sheet of thickness $L$. Show that the diffusion coefficient $D$ can be obtained from the slope of $\left(M_t/M_\infty\right)^2$ versus $t$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A HDPE sample ($L = 3.0 \text{ mm}$) is immersed in a fuel-oil mixture at $40^{\circ}\text{C}$. The equilibrium uptake is $M_\infty = 0.320 \text{ g}$. After $t = 7200 \text{ s}$, the uptake is $M_t = 0.160 \text{ g}$. Compute the diffusion coefficient using the short-time formula and comment on the suitability of HDPE for this application. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Derivation of Short-Time Approximation:**

Starting from Fick's second law in 1-D:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 C}{\partial x^2}$$

with BCs: $C(\pm L/2, t) = C_s$ and $C(x, 0) = 0$.

By separation of variables, the series solution is:

$$C(x,t) = C_s - \frac{4 C_s}{\pi} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{(-1)^n}{2n+1} \exp\!\left[-\frac{D(2n+1)^2 \pi^2 t}{L^2}\right] \cos\!\left[\frac{(2n+1)\pi x}{L}\right]$$

Integrating over $x \in [-L/2,\, L/2]$ to obtain total mass:

$$\frac{M_t}{M_\infty} = 1 - \frac{8}{\pi^2} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{(2n+1)^2} \exp\!\left[-\frac{D(2n+1)^2 \pi^2 t}{L^2}\right]$$

**Short-time limit:** Retain only the first term of the series for small $t$ (long argument inside $\exp$ dominates); for sufficiently small $t$, the exponential may be expanded and the summation replaced by an integral, leading to:

$$\frac{M_t}{M_\infty} = \frac{4}{L} \sqrt{\frac{D t}{\pi}}$$

Squaring:

$$\left(\frac{M_t}{M_\infty}\right)^2 = \frac{16 D t}{\pi L^2}$$

A plot of $(M_t/M_\infty)^2$ vs $t$ is linear with slope $m = 16 D / (\pi L^2)$, from which:

$$D = \frac{\pi L^2 m}{16}$$

**Long-time limit:** The series is dominated by $n = 0$:

$$\frac{M_t}{M_\infty} = 1 - \frac{8}{\pi^2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{\pi^2 D t}{L^2}\right)$$

A plot of $\ln(1 - M_t/M_\infty)$ vs $t$ is linear with slope $-\pi^2 D / L^2$.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation:**

Given: $L = 3.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$, $M_\infty = 0.320 \text{ g}$, $M_t = 0.160 \text{ g}$, $t = 7200 \text{ s}$.

Step 1 — Compute the ratio:

$$\frac{M_t}{M_\infty} = \frac{0.160}{0.320} = 0.500$$

Since $M_t/M_\infty = 0.5$, we are exactly at the boundary of validity for the short-time approximation; we proceed with caution.

Step 2 — Apply the formula:

$$D = \left(\frac{M_t}{M_\infty} \cdot \frac{L}{4}\right)^2 \cdot \frac{\pi}{t}$$

Step 3 — Substitute:

$$\begin{aligned}
D &= \left(0.500 \times \frac{3.0 \times 10^{-3}}{4}\right)^2 \times \frac{\pi}{7200} \\
  &= \left(0.500 \times 7.50 \times 10^{-4}\right)^2 \times \frac{3.14159}{7200} \\
  &= \left(3.75 \times 10^{-4}\right)^2 \times 4.366 \times 10^{-4} \\
  &= 1.406 \times 10^{-7} \times 4.366 \times 10^{-4} \\
  &= 6.14 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}
\end{aligned}$$

Step 4 — Engineering Comment:

A diffusion coefficient of $\sim 6 \times 10^{-11} \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$ for fuel oil into HDPE is relatively high. Over a service period of, say, 1 year ($\sim 3.15 \times 10^7 \text{ s}$), the characteristic diffusion length is $\sqrt{Dt} = \sqrt{6.14 \times 10^{-11} \times 3.15 \times 10^7} \approx 1.4 \text{ mm}$. For a thin-walled HDPE container (1–2 mm), this means **fuel oil will permeate across the wall within months**. Therefore, **HDPE is unsuitable** for long-term fuel storage; a **fluorocarbon liner (FKM) or multilayer structure with EVOH barrier** is recommended.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Chemical Resistance Problems:**
> 1. **Sign convention errors:** A negative $\Delta W$ means extraction (loss of additives) — many students wrongly report this as "resistance". State explicitly what the sign means.
> 2. **Unit mismatch in $D$:** Always report $D$ in standard SI units ($\text{m}^2/\text{s}$). Mixing $\text{cm}^2/\text{s}$ and $\text{mm}$ is a frequent slip-up.
> 3. **Confusing $W_0$ with $W_d$:** In swelling tests, $W_d$ is the *dry* mass (before any solvent contact), not the initial conditioned mass. The two differ if the specimen is dried in a vacuum oven first.
> 4. **Forgetting to square $M_t/M_\infty$:** When asked to plot $(M_t/M_\infty)^2$ vs $t$, students often plot $M_t/M_\infty$ vs $\sqrt{t}$ instead. The slope interpretation is different.
> 5. **Ignoring temperature in $D$:** Diffusion is Arrhenius-activated. Two $D$ values at two temperatures must be linked via $D = D_0 \exp(-E_a/RT)$.
> 6. **Blunders in ASTM numbers:** ASTM D543 is for plastics; ASTM D471 is for rubber. Mixing them up costs marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Chemical resistance quantifies a polymer's ability to resist physico-chemical change in a chemical environment.
- **Mechanisms:** Physical solvation, chemical reaction (hydrolysis/oxidation), ESC, permeation/extraction.
- **Golden Rule:** *Like dissolves like* — polar polymers dissolve in polar solvents; non-polar in non-polar.
- **Key Metrics:** $\Delta W$, $\Delta V$, $\Delta t$, $PR$, $CRI$.
- **Standards:** ASTM D543 (plastics), ISO 175 (plastics), ASTM D1693 (ESC), ASTM D471 (rubber in liquids).
- **Test Workflow:** Specimen prep $\rightarrow$ initial measurement $\rightarrow$ immersion $\rightarrow$ blotting $\rightarrow$ final measurement $\rightarrow$ calculation.
- **Diffusion Mathematics:**
  - Short time: $(M_t/M_\infty)^2 = (16 D t)/(\pi L^2)$
  - Long time: $M_t/M_\infty = 1 - (8/\pi^2)\exp(-\pi^2 D t / L^2)$
  - Arrhenius: $D = D_0 \exp(-E_a/RT)$
- **Swelling Ratio:** $S_R = M_s/W_d$; crosslink density via Flory-Rehner.
- **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** For hydrocarbon/fuel service, use FKM, PTFE, or multilayer EVOH; for acids/alkalis, use HDPE, PP, PVDF; for oxidizing media, use PTFE.
- **Always report:** Test temperature, concentration, exposure duration, and specimen geometry — these are mandatory fields in KTU board answers.
- **ESC Warning:** Never ignore ESC in stressed applications — it is the *single most common cause* of in-service polymer failures.
- **Graphical Skill:** Practice drawing $M_t$ vs $\sqrt{t}$ plots and extracting $D$ from slopes; KTU frequently asks 3–5 mark numericals on this.
